title: Epic G: Data, Lexical, Decode Pipeline — gbf-data
description: Corpus governance + lexical/interaction/decode contracts. CorpusManifest with source provenance + dedup + contamination check. LexicalSpec (model-semantic, in ArtifactCore) split from InteractionBundle (interaction policy, in ArtifactAux). DecodeMode/DecodePolicy/DecodeCapabilitySet/DecodeTransformSet/SessionProfile.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Epic G: Data, Lexical, Decode Pipeline (`gbf-data`)

### What this is

The corpus governance + lexical/interaction/decode contracts that frame the model's inputs and outputs:
- `gbf-data` corpus pipeline with `CorpusManifest`, source provenance, dedup, contamination check vs `WorkloadManifest`.
- `LexicalSpec` (charset + normalization + control tokens — model-semantic, lives in `ArtifactCore`) and `InteractionBundle` (keyboard layout + transcript spec + session profile — interaction policy, lives in `ArtifactAux`).
- Decode infrastructure: `DecodeMode`, `DecodePolicy`, `DecodeCapabilitySet`, `DecodeTransformSet`, `SessionProfile`.

### Why this matters

Per planv0.md line 703: the old monolithic `InputSpec` is replaced by these split types. `LexicalSpec` is what the model is trained against; `InteractionBundle` is what the runtime renders. Conflating them means "I changed line wrapping, now the model has a new semantic identity hash" — which is silly.

`gbf-data` is also what stops the corpus from contaminating eval workloads silently. Per planv0.md line 2552: data lineage is a reproducibility peer of build lineage.

### Scope (three features)

- **F-G1 — Corpus pipeline**: `CorpusManifest`, source provenance, deterministic normalization, dedup policy, contamination check, deterministic train/val/test splits.
- **F-G2 — Lexical + Interaction**: `LexicalSpec`, `InteractionBundle`, `KeyboardLayoutSpec`, `TranscriptSpec`. Lives in `gbf-artifact` (LexicalSpec in core, InteractionBundle in aux).
- **F-G3 — Decode**: `DecodeMode`, `DecodePolicy`, `DecodeCapabilitySet`, `DecodeTransformSet`, `SessionProfile`. Decode policy is **session-level**, declared in `SessionProfile`; `ArtifactCore` only declares supported modes via `DecodeCapabilitySet`.

### Critical path

```
F-G1 ─┬─→ training (epic bd-1rb) consumes CorpusManifest
      └─→ workload manifests (Epic E) cross-check via contamination

F-G2 ──→ F-A5.5 (keyboard) consumes KeyboardLayoutSpec
F-G2 ──→ F-A5.4 (text) consumes TranscriptSpec

F-G3 ──→ runtime (Epic D) consumes SessionProfile / DecodePolicy
F-G3 ──→ oracles (Epic C) consume DecodeCapabilitySet for canonical decode semantics
```

### Confidence: HIGH

Mostly schema + deterministic pipelines. The hardest part is contamination check — it needs to run efficiently over a multi-GB corpus.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Charset decision — Tier 2 locked in

Decision date: 2026-04-27. Owner: bkase.

The v1 LexicalSpec charset is fixed at **80 tokens**: A–Z + a–z + 0–9 + 13 punctuation symbols (` .,!?-':;()\"/`) + newline + 3 control tokens (<bos>, <eos>, <unk>). Mixed case preserved.

Tier choice rationale: pure-Stross / *Accelerando* register requires mixed case + parens + quotes + semicolons + slash. Pure caps-only Tier 1 (~48 tokens) would push the voice toward Asimov-era pulp / computer-terminal aesthetic which is off-genre. Tier 2 cost vs Tier 1: ~+1 KiB embedding ROM and ~+7 % per-token inference time — small relative to one extra MoE expert (~30 KiB).

Detailed charset + normalization specs live on the child beads:
- bd-2ym0 (F-G2): charset, LexicalSpec / InteractionBundle schema, keyboard layout sketch.
- bd-tmaw (F-G1): deterministic normalization pipeline, drop policy, splits, CorpusManifest.

This decision is identity-hashed once F-G2 lands. Future churn requires a new artifact lineage.

title: T7.1: Define TemporalSwitchDigest and related export types
description: In gbf-artifact: TemporalSwitchDigest { layer: LayerId, same_expert_rate_q8_8: u16, transition_mass: Vec<ExpertTransitionDigest> }. ExpertTransitionDigest { from_expert: ExpertId, to_expert: ExpertId, rate_q8_8: u16 }. ClipSaturationDigest { layer: LayerId, boundary: BoundaryId, saturation_rate_q8_8: u16 }. ExpertPayloadDigest { expert: ExpertId, total_bytes: u32, ternary_bytes: u32, scale_bytes: u32 }. Add these to ExportFacts.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-artifact`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-artifact/src/export_facts.rs` (or `gbf-artifact/src/digest.rs`)

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "TemporalSwitchDigest", "ExpertTransitionDigest", "ClipSaturationDigest", "ExpertPayloadDigest", "ExportFacts".

### Implementation Notes
Define these types in gbf-artifact:
```rust
pub struct TemporalSwitchDigest {
    pub per_layer_switch_rate: Vec<f32>,     // fraction of consecutive tokens that switch expert
    pub mean_switch_rate: f32,
    pub sample_count: u64,
}

pub struct ExpertTransitionDigest {
    pub per_layer_transition_matrix: Vec<Vec<Vec<f32>>>,  // [layer][from_expert][to_expert]
}

pub struct ClipSaturationDigest {
    pub per_layer_clip_rate: Vec<f32>,       // fraction of activations clipped
    pub per_layer_max_activation: Vec<f32>,
}

pub struct ExpertPayloadDigest {
    pub per_expert_bytes: Vec<u32>,
    pub total_expert_bytes: u64,
}
```
- Add all four to `ExportFacts` struct as `Option<T>` fields.
- Derive: `Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize`
- These are training-time observations exported with the artifact for compiler hints.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-artifact                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- export_facts         # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-artifact -- -D warnings        # No warnings
# Verify: ExportFacts can carry all four digest types
# Verify: JSON serialization round-trip
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: Digests are typed structs, not raw JSON blobs
- §VI.1: ExportFacts is the single source of truth for training observations

title: T-A1.1: ISA types and register/operand modeling
description: Define legal LR35902 instruction shapes, registers (Reg8/Reg16), operand modes (Operand8/Operand16), conditions, RST vectors. The type backbone for everything in gbf-asm — encoder, builder, cycle model, layout all consume Instr from here. Make invalid operand combinations unrepresentable (per CONSTITUTION §I.1).
close_reason:
Implemented the canonical LR35902 ISA type backbone in `gbf-asm/src/isa.rs`.

Scope completed:
- Added typed registers, conditions, RST vectors, bit indices, high/direct address operands, operand-mode classification, and instruction-family variants.
- Split instruction operands into narrower types so illegal families such as immediate destinations, `PUSH SP`, `LD [HL], [HL]`, non-`C` high-register addressing, and out-of-range bit/RST values are not representable through public constructors.
- Added `Instr::byte_len()` as the static sizing contract consumed by layout/cycle-model work.
- Added serde validation for constructor-validated newtypes via `#[serde(try_from = ...)]`, plus negative deserialization tests.
- Documented that this is canonical concrete/post-symbol instruction IR. Symbolic labels, relocations, pseudo-ops, branch relaxation, machine effects, and final opcode emission remain with their owning beads.

Reference checked:
- Pan Docs CPU Instruction Set: https://gbdev.io/pandocs/CPU_Instruction_Set.html

Moved / explicit non-goals:
- Symbolic labels, builder DSL, and pseudo-ops: `bd-3p1`.
- Branch relaxation and symbolic-to-concrete branch lowering: `bd-c4s`.
- Final opcode bytes and invalid-opcode rejection: `bd-2o3`.
- Cycle timing beyond byte length: `bd-2k5`.
- MachineEffect / PrivilegeClass typing: `bd-1bw`.
- Section roles, provenance, and symbols: `bd-1e2`.

Subagent review results and fixes:
- Mode 1 found that `Instr` lifecycle and canonical-vs-raw-legal ISA policy were under-specified. Fixed with module docs and explicit non-goals above.
- Mode 2 found serde-derived invalid-state bypass, ambiguous `Operand16::SP` representation, and unclear `Reg16Math` naming. Fixed by serde `try_from`, `Reg16Pair`/`Operand16::Sp`, and `Reg16Data`.
- Mode 3 independently found the serde invalid-state bypass. Fixed and covered with `isa::serde_rejects_invalid_validated_operands`.
- Mode 4 found the same serde gap and noted that filtered tests can pass with zero tests on old code. Fixed the test paths earlier and added AGENTS guidance requiring closure evidence to confirm filtered tests actually ran.

Feedback-harness reflection:

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Constructor-validated tuple newtypes derived `Deserialize` | Private fields are still populated by generated serde code | Do not derive `Deserialize` for validated newtypes unless serde routes through validation | `cargo test -p gbf-asm -- isa::serde_rejects_invalid_validated_operands` | `AGENTS.md` ASM / ISA Beads; `gbf-asm/src/isa.rs` |
| `Instr` lifecycle unclear | Task mixed concrete ISA, symbolic authoring IR, and later relaxation concerns | State whether AsmIR is pre-layout symbolic, post-relaxation concrete, or adapter | Module docs plus moved/non-goal closure entries | `bd-11e`, `bd-3p1`, `bd-c4s` |
| Canonical high-memory direct-address policy looked like raw ISA legality | Task conflated legal CPU encodings with canonical project encodings | Distinguish legal CPU encodings from canonical project encodings | `DirectAddr` constructor boundary and operand classification test | `AGENTS.md` ASM / ISA Beads; `isa::operand_classification` |
| Filtered cargo tests passed with zero tests before patch | Test path was easy to cite without proving selection | Confirm filtered commands run nonzero tests in closure evidence | Closure command output shows 1 or 3 tests run | `AGENTS.md` ASM / ISA Beads; closure matrix |
| Unrelated dirty `history/planv0.md` present | Existing worktree change unrelated to bead | Do not stage unrelated files; call it out at final status | `git status --short` before staging/commit | Session protocol |

Claim-to-gate matrix:

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `gbf-asm` compiles with concrete ISA types | `cargo check -p gbf-asm` | Passed |
| Operand modes cover the intended LR35902 operand classes and constructor boundaries | `cargo test -p gbf-asm -- isa::operand_classification` | Passed; ran 1 test |
| Every `Instr` variant has a known static byte length | `cargo test -p gbf-asm -- isa::instr_size_in_bytes` | Passed; ran 1 test |
| Serde cannot bypass validated `BitIndex` / `DirectAddr` constructors | `cargo test -p gbf-asm -- isa::serde_rejects_invalid_validated_operands` | Passed; ran 1 test |
| All local ISA tests pass together | `cargo test -p gbf-asm -- isa` | Passed; ran 3 tests |
| Formatting and linting are clean | `cargo fmt --check --all`; `cargo clippy -p gbf-asm -- -D warnings` | Passed |

Commands run:

- `cargo test -p gbf-asm -- isa::operand_classification`
- `cargo test -p gbf-asm -- isa::instr_size_in_bytes`

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A1.1: ISA types + register/operand modeling

**Target crate**: `gbf-asm`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-asm/src/isa.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` §"Assembly eDSL" (line 2046).

### Background

This is the type backbone for everything else in `gbf-asm`. The encoder, builder, cycle model, and layout passes all consume `Instr` values defined here. Getting this wrong cascades.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub enum Reg8 { A, B, C, D, E, H, L }
pub enum Reg16 { BC, DE, HL, SP, AF }    // pairs + special
pub enum Cond  { NZ, Z, NC, C }

pub enum Operand8 {
    Reg(Reg8),
    Imm(u8),
    HlInd,                       // (HL)
    Direct(u16),                 // (nn)
    HighDirect(u8),              // ($FF00 + n)
    HighReg(Reg8 /* C */),       // ($FF00 + C)
}

pub enum Operand16 {
    Reg(Reg16),
    Imm(u16),
    Sp,
    SpPlus(i8),
}

pub enum Instr {
    Nop,
    Ld8 { dst: Operand8, src: Operand8 },
    Ld16 { dst: Reg16, src: Operand16 },
    LdAddrReg16 { dst: Reg16Addr, src: Reg8 /* A */ },  // (BC), (DE), (HL+), (HL-)
    Add { src: Operand8 },
    Adc { src: Operand8 },
    Sub { src: Operand8 },
    Sbc { src: Operand8 },
    And { src: Operand8 },
    Or  { src: Operand8 },
    Xor { src: Operand8 },
    Cp  { src: Operand8 },
    Inc8 { dst: Operand8 },
    Dec8 { dst: Operand8 },
    Inc16 { dst: Reg16 },
    Dec16 { dst: Reg16 },
    Add16Hl { src: Reg16 },
    AddSp { off: i8 },
    Bit { bit: u3, src: Operand8 },
    Res { bit: u3, src: Operand8 },
    Set { bit: u3, src: Operand8 },
    Rlca, Rrca, Rla, Rra, Rlc { src: Operand8 }, Rrc { src: Operand8 },
    Rl { src: Operand8 }, Rr { src: Operand8 },
    Sla { src: Operand8 }, Sra { src: Operand8 }, Srl { src: Operand8 },
    Swap { src: Operand8 },
    JpAbs { cond: Option<Cond>, addr: u16 },
    JpHl,
    JrRel { cond: Option<Cond>, off: i8 },
    Call { cond: Option<Cond>, addr: u16 },
    Ret { cond: Option<Cond> },
    Reti,
    Rst { vector: RstVector },           // $00, $08, ..., $38
    Push { src: Reg16 },
    Pop  { dst: Reg16 },
    Halt,
    Stop,
    Di, Ei,
    Ccf, Scf, Cpl, Daa,
}
```

`u3` is a typed three-bit value (use `bilge` crate or local newtype with `Self::new(u8) -> Option<Self>`).

`Operand8::Direct(u16)` is **address-typed** — the encoder rejects reads from `$FF00–$FFFF` through `Direct` because those should use `HighDirect` for the smaller encoding. This is a "make invalid states unrepresentable" call.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-asm                                              # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- isa::operand_classification                 # Operand variants are exhaustive over Pan Docs op modes
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- isa::instr_size_in_bytes                    # Each Instr variant has a known byte length
cargo clippy -p gbf-asm -- -D warnings                               # Clean
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Invalid operand combinations (e.g., `Ld8 { dst: Imm(_), src: ... }`) are unrepresentable.
- §VI.1: ISA shape lives only here; nothing else encodes opcodes.

title: T5.4: Implement ternary zero regularizer
description: Soft L1-style penalty on the pre-threshold expert weights, encouraging sparsity (more zeros in the ternary representation). This is useful for SparseTernaryBitplanes encoding where higher zero density means fewer bytes. For Ternary2, zeros don't save bytes but they do reduce accumulator magnitude, which is still useful. Config-gated.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::sparsity passes; All-zero weights -> regularizer=0; Near-zero weights penalized

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/loss/sparsity.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "ternary zero regularizer", "L1", "sparsity".

### Implementation Notes
- Soft L1-style penalty on pre-threshold expert weights, encouraging sparsity (more zeros in ternary encoding).
- `pub fn ternary_zero_regularizer(weights: &Tensor, threshold: &Tensor) -> Tensor`
- Penalty: `Σ |w| * (1 - sigmoid(alpha * (|w| - threshold)))` — smooth version that penalizes weights near threshold
- Or simpler: `Σ min(|w|, threshold)` — encourages weights to be exactly 0 or above threshold
- Config-gated: only active when `lambda_zero > 0.0`.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::sparsity       # Tests pass
# Verify: all-zero weights -> regularizer = 0
# Verify: penalty increases with number of near-zero weights
# Verify: gradient encourages weights toward 0 or ±scale
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Pure function
- §I.5: Differentiable w.r.t. weights — honest gradient

title: T-A2.3: MBC5 register semantics — RAMG/BANK1/BANK2/RAMB
description: MBC5 register address bands (0x0000-0x1FFF RAMG, 0x2000-0x2FFF BANK1, 0x3000-0x3FFF BANK2, 0x4000-0x5FFF RAMB), canonical RAM-enable value 0x0A (Pan Docs notes other low-nibble-A values are not recommended), 9-bit ROM bank assembly from BANK1+BANK2.

comments.author: bkase

title: T7.3: Implement temporal smoothness regularization in router
description: In Top1RouterQat, maintain a running buffer of the previous token's soft routing distribution within each training sequence. Compute L_switch = (1/LT) sum_{l,t} (1 - dot(p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1})) over contiguous windows. Handle sequence boundaries (mask out cross-sequence penalties). Multiply by lambda_switch. Gradient flows through the router's softmax output. Window size is a config parameter (default: 32 tokens).
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router::temporal passes; Buffer resets at sequence boundaries; Smoothness in [0,1]

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/qat/router.rs` (extend Top1RouterQat)

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "temporal smoothness regularization", "L_switch", "consecutive tokens", "routing distribution buffer".

### Implementation Notes
- In Top1RouterQat, maintain a running buffer of previous token's soft routing distribution.
- Compute `L_switch` (temporal switch loss) over contiguous windows.
- Window size is a config parameter (default: 32 tokens).
- Buffer management:
  - Ring buffer of size `window_size` per layer
  - Reset at sequence boundaries
  - `pub fn compute_temporal_smoothness(&self, current_probs: &Tensor) -> Tensor`
- This is in the MODEL crate (not loss crate) because it requires router-internal state (the distribution buffer).
- The actual loss scalar computation may delegate to `gbf-train/src/loss/switch.rs`.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router::temporal  # Tests pass
# Verify: buffer tracks previous routing distributions
# Verify: window respects sequence boundaries
# Verify: smoothness metric is in [0, 1] range
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Buffer state is isolated in the router; smoothness computation is pure
- §V.2: Buffer is reconstructable — reset at sequence boundaries, no persistent state needed across restarts

title: T10.14: ExportVisitor-to-ArtifactOracle agreement test
description:
Tests that the exported artifact, when evaluated by ArtifactOracle, produces the same outputs as the training model's fake-quant forward pass.

**Forward agreement test:**
- Create tiny model, run forward pass on 4 fixture prompts, record logits L_train
- Export to ModelArtifact
- Load artifact into ArtifactOracle (exact canonical evaluation, no tiling/banking)
- Run ArtifactOracle on same 4 prompts, record logits L_artifact
- ASSERT: L_train == L_artifact (exact equality, not approximate — fake-quant forward should match)

**Checkpoint-level agreement:**
- At each SemanticCheckpointId (PostEmbedding, PostRouter, PostExpertDowncast, PostLogits, PostDecode):
  - Record intermediate values from training forward pass
  - Record intermediate values from ArtifactOracle
  - ASSERT: exact match

**Edge case: different decode modes:**
- Argmax decode: verify token output matches between training model and oracle
- TopKTemperature decode: verify logit distributions match (tokens may differ due to RNG, but pre-sample distributions must agree)

**Quantization gap measurement:**
- Evaluate dense teacher on fixture prompts -> logits L_teacher
- Evaluate ternary artifact on same prompts -> logits L_artifact
- Record: max abs difference, mean abs difference, KL divergence
- Log these as structured metrics for conformance.json
- These are NOT assertions (some gap is expected) — they're measurements

This test is the training-side companion to the oracle suite's differential tests.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- oracle_agreement passes; Forward agreement within atol=1e-5; Checkpoint-level values match; Quantization gap measured

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: EXTERNAL DEPENDENCY NOTE: This test assumes ArtifactOracle (from gbf-oracle) exists and can evaluate a frozen artifact in canonical logical form. ArtifactOracle is part of the compiler/oracle infrastructure (M1-M2 milestone), not part of this training-contract epic. If ArtifactOracle is not yet available when this test is reached, implement the test with a lightweight 'training-side artifact evaluator' that replays the fake-quant forward pass using the exported CanonicalTensors and TernaryWeightPlan, then upgrade to the real ArtifactOracle when it's ready. The key invariant being tested — export agreement with training — is the same either way.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-test`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-test/tests/oracle_agreement.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` §Oracle specifications. Bead description for T10.14.

### Implementation Notes
Tests that exported artifact, when evaluated by ArtifactOracle, produces same outputs as training model's fake-quant forward pass:
- **Forward agreement**: model.forward_hard_quant(input) ≈ ArtifactOracle.eval(exported_artifact, input)
- **Checkpoint-level agreement**: at each SemanticCheckpointId, values match within tolerance
- **Quantization gap measurement**: measure divergence between DenotationalOracle (full precision) and ArtifactOracle (quantized)

EXTERNAL DEPENDENCY NOTE: If ArtifactOracle not available yet, implement a lightweight training-side artifact evaluator that replays the exported CanonicalTensors through the quantized compute graph.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo test -p gbf-test -- oracle_agreement       # Passes
# Verify: forward agreement within atol=1e-5 for hard-quantized outputs
# Verify: checkpoint-level values match
# Verify: quantization gap is measured and logged
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.4: Oracle agreement is THE verifiable correctness criterion
- §III.3: This test verifies stratum 1 vs stratum 2 (denotational vs artifact semantics)

title: F-C3: ScheduleOracle
description: Runs GbSchedIR over named arenas + continuation ABI + reduction plans + SemanticCheckpointSchema. Proves scheduled execution refines artifact semantics. Effectively a Rust simulator of GbSchedIR for verification.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-C3: ScheduleOracle

### What this is

Runs `GbSchedIR` over the same named arenas, continuation ABI, reduction plans, and semantic-checkpoint schema that the ROM will use (`planv0.md` line 296). Proves scheduled execution semantics before assembly + ROM layout.

### Why this matters

`ScheduleOracle` is the third-stratum oracle: operational truth at the scheduled level. If `ScheduleOracle` agrees with `ArtifactOracle` at every checkpoint, scheduling preserved artifact semantics. If they disagree, the bug is in scheduling (Stages 5–11), not lowering or runtime.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct ScheduleOracle {
    sched: SchedulePack,
    arena_geometry: ArenaPlan,
    abi: AbiVersion,
}

impl ScheduleOracle {
    pub fn evaluate(&self, workload: &Workload) -> ScheduleObservations;
    pub fn compare_against(&self, artifact: &ArtifactObservations) -> SemanticDriftReport;
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B11 GbSchedIR
- F-B10 ArenaPlan
- F-A3 abi types
- F-C2 ArtifactOracle (comparison target)

### Confidence: MEDIUM. The implementation is essentially a Rust simulator of `GbSchedIR`.

title: T-B14.4: Per-profile defaults — initial CompileKnobs + bounds + locks + RepairPolicy
description: Resolve initial CompileKnobs from each CompileProfile. Bringup: iters=1, all repair toggles false, locks everything except tile/coarsening. Default: iters=4, all toggles true, locks only ResourcePressure + StageIters. Trace (Invariant): iters=2, all toggles false, locks everything; behavior-affecting knobs resolved from paired Default-compatible build then frozen. Recovery: iters=6, all toggles true, fit-first bounds (PackedExperts/RequiredOnly/AnyOverlayable/PureTokenValues/FitFirstPagedSpills/DuplicateToSatisfyWindow). ResourcePressureThresholds resolved with provenance from TargetProfile + RuntimeChromeBudget + SchedulerPolicy + TraceBudget + CompileObjective + profile defaults.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B14.4: Per-profile defaults — resolve initial `CompileKnobs` + bounds + locks

**Target crate**: `gbf-policy`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/knobs/profile_defaults.rs`, plus extending `gbf-policy/src/profile.rs` (T11.2 / `bd-ymo`)
**Reference**: F-B14 oracle resolution Q2 — per-profile defaults table.

### Background

Each `CompileProfile` (`Bringup`, `Default`, `Trace`, `Recovery`) resolves to a starting `CompileKnobs` configuration: initial knob values, per-knob bounds (max value the loop may advance to), lock-set, and `RepairPolicy` defaults.

This task defines those resolutions. Note that **T11.2 (`bd-ymo`) currently defines only `BringUp`** — production profiles `Default`, `Trace`, `Recovery` are deferred until the F12 sequence-state experiment resolves. This task lands the resolution machinery and ships `Bringup` first; `Default`/`Trace`/`Recovery` are added as their conditions are met.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub fn resolve_initial_knobs(
    profile: &CompileProfile,
    target: &TargetProfile,
    chrome_budget: &RuntimeChromeBudget,
    request: &CompileRequest,
) -> CompileKnobs;

pub fn resolve_repair_policy(profile: &CompileProfile) -> RepairPolicy;
```

#### Bringup defaults

```rust
RepairPolicy {
    max_refinement_iters: 1,
    allow_placement_profile_fallback: false,
    allow_trace_demotion: false,
    allow_overlay_promotion: false,
    allow_recompute_promotion: false,
}
```

Initial knob values:
- placement: `StrictOnePerBank`; max = `StrictOnePerBank`.
- observation: `trace_demotion: None`; max = `None`.
- range: `AllowChunkedI16`; max = `AllowChunkedI16` (NOT `AllowRenormLoop`).
- storage: `recompute_promotion: None`; max = `None`.
- sram: `KeepHotInWram`; max = `KeepHotInWram`.
- rom_window: `ProfileDefault` + `ShareKernels`; max same.
- overlay: `None`; max = `None`.
- schedule: tile classes `{SmallWorkingSet, Balanced}`, slice coarsening `Fine` or `ProfileDefault`.

Lock-set (Bringup): everything except `ScheduleTileSearch` + `ScheduleSliceCoarsening`.

#### Default defaults

```rust
RepairPolicy {
    max_refinement_iters: 4,
    allow_placement_profile_fallback: true,
    allow_trace_demotion: true,
    allow_overlay_promotion: true,
    allow_recompute_promotion: true,
}
```

Initial: `Budgeted` placement, `None` trace demotion, `AllowRenormLoop`, `PureSliceValues` recompute, `BatchSramAccesses`, all tile classes, `ProfileDefault` coarsening. Bounds: max `PackedExperts`, max `DropDiagnosticAndBestEffort`, max `PureResumeWindowValues`, max `AllowColdSpills`, max `KernelsAndLutFragments`, max `CoarseWithinLatency`. Lock-set: only `ScheduleResourcePressure` + `StageIterationCeilings`.

#### Trace (Invariant) defaults

```rust
RepairPolicy {
    max_refinement_iters: 2,
    allow_placement_profile_fallback: false,
    allow_trace_demotion: false,                 // forced false under Invariant
    allow_overlay_promotion: false,
    allow_recompute_promotion: false,
}
```

Resolution rule: resolve behavior-affecting knobs from the paired `Default`-compatible build first, then **freeze** them before trace instrumentation is inserted. If `TraceBudget` cannot fit, fail with a clear diagnostic — do NOT silently demote.

Lock-set (Invariant): everything (every behavior-affecting knob plus tile/coarsening). Flexible-mode Trace opens up like Default but requires `trace_loss_report.json` + `observability.cert.json`.

#### Recovery defaults

```rust
RepairPolicy {
    max_refinement_iters: 6,
    allow_placement_profile_fallback: true,
    allow_trace_demotion: true,
    allow_overlay_promotion: true,
    allow_recompute_promotion: true,
}
```

Initial: `Budgeted` or `PackedExperts`, `AllowRenormLoop`, all tile classes. Bounds: max `PackedExperts`, max `RequiredOnly` trace, max `PureTokenValues`, max `FitFirstPagedSpills`, max `FitFirst` residency, max `DuplicateToSatisfyWindow`, max `AnyOverlayable`, max `CoarseWithinLatency` (rarely `EpochCoalesced`). Lock-set: same as Default.

### Strict-first-fit override

Provide a `BringupFirstFit` config flag that explicitly sets `max_refinement_iters = 0` for users who want zero-tolerance bring-up. Not the canonical Bringup default.

### `ResourcePressureThresholds` resolution

Each pressure threshold is resolved from typed sources with `ConstraintProvenance`:
- `wram_hot.hard` ← `RuntimeChromeBudget.wram_reserved.hot_arena_floor` (PolicySource::ProfileDefault, evidence chrome budget hash).
- `bank_switches_per_token.hard` ← `CompileObjective.max_bank_switches_per_token` or `CompileProfile.max_bank_switches_per_token` (whichever is tighter; provenance records source).
- ...and so on for each PressureLimit field.

This is what stops "what's a sensible threshold?" from becoming pass-private folklore.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-policy
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- profile_defaults::bringup           # Returns expected RepairPolicy + locked sets
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- profile_defaults::default
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- profile_defaults::trace_invariant_locks_everything
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- profile_defaults::recovery
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- profile_defaults::pressure_thresholds_have_provenance
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `RepairPolicy` per profile is structured; no scattered defaults.
- §VI.1: One canonical resolver; profiles do not reach into pass internals.

title: F-B9: OverlayPlan (Stage 8.5)
description: Turns WramOverlay residency into explicit install/layout plan. Replaces existing T11.4 stub (bd-gmw) with the canonical {regions, installs, share_classes} shape per planv0.md. For BringUp v1: regions.len()==1, no share_classes.

## F-B9: OverlayPlan (Stage 8.5)

### What this is

Turns `KernelResidency::WramOverlay` into an explicit install/layout plan (`planv0.md` line 1334). Decides which overlayable objects share a region, when installs may occur, and what WRAM budget must be reserved before arena assignment.

**Replaces existing T11.4 stub (`bd-gmw`)** with the canonical shape.

### Why this matters

The session decision (F11) committed to per-expert-switch overlay reload as the default. Without `OverlayPlan` realizing that policy concretely, the overlay stays a vibe.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct OverlayPlan {
    pub regions: Vec<OverlayRegion>,
    pub installs: Vec<OverlayInstall>,
    pub share_classes: Vec<OverlayShareClass>,
}

pub struct OverlayRegion {
    pub id: OverlayId,
    pub bytes: u16,                                  // = CompileProfile.wram_layout.overlay_bytes for v1
    pub residents: Vec<OverlayResidentId>,
}
```

For BringUp (v1): `regions.len() == 1`, no `share_classes`, single resident per slice. Future profiles open up the rest.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B8 RomWindowPlan
- F11 / bd-1i8 CompileProfile (overlay byte count)
- T11.4 (bd-gmw) — supersede / close after F-B9 lands

### Confidence: HIGH

title: F12: Dual-Path Sequence-State Experimentation — LinearState + BoundedKv
description: The plan keeps both LinearState (recurrent/linear-attention) and BoundedKv as production candidates. gbf-model abstracts over SequenceSemanticsSpec; both blocks are implemented under the same training scaffold (phase scheduler, loss, QAT modules) and run through shadow_compile so the production choice is decided empirically on workloads, not in spec docs. Production CompileProfiles for each variant are defined only after this experiment resolves.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F12: Dual-Path Sequence-State Experimentation

### Background

planv0.md keeps two production candidates open: `SequenceSemanticsSpec::LinearState` (recurrent / linear-attention-like) and `SequenceSemanticsSpec::BoundedKv` (causal bounded-KV). Earlier framings tried to declare one a winner. The 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session committed to the opposite: **decide empirically**. Both must be implementable on the same training scaffold so the comparison is apples-to-apples.

### Why this matters

The runtime memory math is very different between the two:
- **LinearState**: state is small, fits in WRAM ping-pong, no growth with context. SRAM persistence is a checkpoint, not a working set.
- **BoundedKv**: KV slab grows with context; even at small contexts it likely exceeds WRAM, so SRAM paging through `SramPagePlan` is required, with hot tiles streamed into WRAM.

Without F12, the trainer has to pick one before any data exists. With F12, both paths run through the same QAT phases, the same loss terms, the same shadow_compile pipeline, and produce side-by-side `CheckpointFrontierPoint` data. The production `CompileProfile` (`Production-LinearState` or `Production-BoundedKv`) is defined only after T12.4's `SequenceStateComparisonReport` is in.

### The problem this solves

- Eliminates premature commitment that would force a costly later rework.
- Forces the model code to abstract over sequence semantics from day one (this is good architecture; it would be the right shape even if only one variant shipped).
- Gives F11's `CompileProfile.sequence_state` field a concrete enum to refer to.
- Aligns shadow_compile (F8) with the experimentation goal: the same compile pipeline runs both variants on the same workloads.

### Design

`gbf-model` owns `SequenceSemanticsSpec` and a `SequenceBlock` trait:

```rust
pub enum SequenceSemanticsSpec {
    LinearState { state_bytes_per_layer: u16 },
    BoundedKv { max_context: u16, kv_bytes_per_token: u16 },
}

pub trait SequenceBlock {
    fn forward(&self, x: ActivationTile, state: &mut SequenceState) -> ActivationTile;
    fn state_init(&self) -> SequenceState;
    fn state_size(&self) -> SequenceStateSize;
    fn export_facts(&self) -> SequenceExportFacts;
}
```

`ArtifactCore` (in `gbf-artifact`) carries `SequenceSemanticsSpec` directly so artifacts of different sequence-state types are distinguishable.

The training scaffold (`gbf-train`): phase scheduler, loss terms (z-loss, balance, range, switch, distillation), QAT modules (ternary, activation, norm), and shadow_compile all dispatch through `SequenceBlock`. No code outside the block dispatch branches on the variant.

### Confidence: HIGH for the trait/abstraction; MEDIUM for which variant wins

The abstraction is independently good architecture. The actual outcome of LinearState vs BoundedKv depends on workloads we don't have yet — that's exactly what T12.4's report measures.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T12.1–T12.4.

**Target crate(s)**: `gbf-model` (types + blocks), `gbf-artifact` (SequenceSemanticsSpec lands in ArtifactCore), `gbf-train` (scaffold dispatch).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "SequenceSemanticsSpec", "LinearState", "BoundedKv", "fixed-state recurrent", "bounded-KV causal".

### Feature Definition of Done

All child tasks (T12.1–T12.4) closed AND:
```bash
cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence_block               # Both blocks pass shared trait tests
cargo test -p gbf-model -- linear_state                 # LinearState-specific
cargo test -p gbf-model -- bounded_kv                   # BoundedKv-specific
cargo test -p gbf-train -- shadow_compile::dual_path    # Both variants run shadow_compile on a tiny model
cargo test -p gbf-test -- sequence_state_comparison_report  # Report shape is stable
```

title: T10.7: Phase transition integration tests (F4 coverage)
description:
Integration tests for the 5-phase training schedule:

**Phase transition correctness:**
- Create tiny model, configure 5-phase schedule with step boundaries [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
- Step through phases, verify at each boundary:
  - QAT hardness changes to expected values per TrainPhaseSpec
  - Loss term activation changes (λ_balance/λ_zrouter enabled from Phase B, λ_distill from Phase C, etc.)
  - Correct log events are emitted (phase_transition event with before/after hardness)
- Verify model behavior changes: Phase A forward should not ternary-quantize; Phase C forward should

**Teacher freeze and validation:**
- Train for 10 steps (Phase A), freeze teacher
- Verify teacher weights are frozen (no gradient)
- Verify teacher weights != student weights after 10 more steps of QAT
- Export teacher as ReferenceModelBundle, verify it passes ArtifactValidation
- Verify teacher and student produce different but correlated outputs (distillation should work)

**Phase scheduler edge cases:**
- Empty phase list: verify error
- Overlapping phases: verify error
- Skip a phase (e.g., go directly to Phase C): verify correct hardness applied
- Single-step phases: verify transitions fire correctly

**Logging verification:**
- Capture all training events via test logging harness
- Verify: exactly one phase_transition event per boundary
- Verify: loss_step events contain all expected fields with correct types
- Verify: export events contain artifact_core_hash field

All tests use tiny model fixture from T10.1.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- phase_tests passes; 5-phase schedule produces correct hardness; Teacher frozen at A->B boundary; Structured log events verified

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: Removed dep on T10.3 (QAT unit tests). Phase transition tests need the QAT modules to exist (already guaranteed by T4.5 depending on T1.2/T1.3/T1.4), not for the QAT unit test suite to have passed first. This avoids gating integration testing on the full QAT unit test suite.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: Teacher export validation (ReferenceModelBundle) is now covered by T4.3b (export frozen teacher) and tested in T10.11 (E2E pipeline). Phase transition tests focus on: phase transitions, QAT hardness changes, teacher freeze (weights frozen, no gradient), distillation loss computation, and log event verification.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-test`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-test/tests/phase_tests.rs`

**Reference**: Bead descriptions for T4.1 (bd-ptk), T4.2 (bd-cmf), T4.5 (bd-2uw).

### Implementation Notes
Integration tests for the 5-phase training schedule:
- **Phase transition correctness**: verify hardness changes at exact step boundaries
  - Phase A: all QAT Off
  - Phase B: router active, weight QAT Off
  - Phase C: weight QAT Hard, activation QAT Soft
  - Phase D: all QAT Hard
  - Phase E: frozen, export mode
- **Teacher freeze validation**: teacher weights frozen at Phase A -> B boundary
- **Edge cases**: step exactly on boundary, zero-length phase, single-phase schedule
- **Logging verification**: phase transition events captured by TestEventCollector (from T10.2)

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo test -p gbf-test -- phase_tests            # All pass
# Verify: 5-phase default schedule produces correct hardness at each phase
# Verify: teacher is frozen and immutable after Phase A
# Verify: structured log events emitted for each transition
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §III.3: Phase schedule in config IS the spec — tests verify the spec is followed
- §V.1: Transition logging verified through TestEventCollector

title: Epic A: M0 Foundation Stack — gbf-asm, gbf-hw, gbf-abi, BankLease ABI, Bank0 Runtime, gbf-store/migrate
description: The M0 milestone from planv0.md: typed LR35902 eDSL + encoder + ROM builder, verified DMG/MBC5 hardware contract, gbf-abi live-execution types, BankLease/BankGuard ABI as the sole legal MBC-write path, Bank0 cooperative runtime skeleton (boot, ISRs, scheduler with TIMA-armed yield, joypad, text, keyboard, video_commit + UiCommitPlan, panic), and gbf-store + gbf-migrate infrastructure. Without M0, no other milestone (M1 oracles, M2 first ROM, M3 routed FFN, M4 sequence-state, M5 interactive, M6 autotune) can begin.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Epic A: M0 Foundation Stack

### What this is

This epic is the **M0 milestone** from `history/planv0.md` §"What I would build first" (line 2468). It is the foundation under everything: the assembly eDSL, the hardware contract, the live-execution ABI, the bank-write ABI, the Bank0 cooperative runtime skeleton, and the content-addressed storage / migration infrastructure.

Without M0, none of M1–M6 can begin. The Training-Contract Revision Pass (epic `bd-1rb`) decomposed the **training side** into ~75 beads. M0 is the equivalent decomposition for **the foundation that the compiler, runtime, oracle, and bench all build on top of**.

### Why this matters (overarching project goals)

GBLLM compiles a quantized MoE language model into a Game Boy ROM that executes cooperatively alongside a UI. The five cooperating products from `planv0.md` (training, artifact boundary, oracles, compiler, runtime) all share three contracts: `gbf-hw` (target contract), `gbf-artifact` (durable model contract), `gbf-abi` (live execution contract). M0 produces those three contracts in usable form **plus** the assembly eDSL that is the only legal authoring layer for executable code, **plus** the runtime nucleus that everything else schedules onto.

Every architectural invariant in the plan eventually grounds out in M0:

- "All executable code originates from `AsmIR`" → requires `gbf-asm` to exist (F-A1).
- "ISR code/data is Bank0-resident, proven by `ReachabilityValidation`" → requires `MachineEffect` / `PrivilegeClass` typing in AsmIR (F-A1) plus the Bank0 nucleus modules (F-A5).
- "The `BankLease`/`BankGuard` ABI is the only legal path to MBC writes" → requires F-A4.
- "The runtime is a cooperative kernel centered on Bank0" → requires F-A5.
- "Every build is content-addressed and lazy-loadable" → requires F-A6 (`gbf-store`).

### Scope

Six features:

- **F-A1 — `gbf-asm`**: typed LR35902 eDSL, ISA shapes, `MachineEffect`/`PrivilegeClass`, builder, cycle model, layout/relax, encoder, listing, ROM builder.
- **F-A2 — `gbf-hw`**: `TargetProfile`, verified DMG/MBC5 memory map, MBC5 register semantics, LCD/timing/interrupt constants, joypad, calibration schema (types, not data).
- **F-A3 — `gbf-abi`**: live-execution `#[repr(C)]` types — `AbiVersion`, `InferenceState` with liveness, harness command/result blocks, faults, interrupts, `ResourceLease`, semantic-checkpoint ids, trace events.
- **F-A4 — `BankLease`/`BankGuard` ABI**: the sole legal path to MBC writes, with HRAM bank shadow registers and interrupt-safe acquire/release primitives.
- **F-A5 — Bank0 cooperative runtime skeleton**: boot + IRQ vectors, scheduler with TIMA-armed deadline yielding, joypad, text renderer, keyboard, `video_commit` queue with `UiCommitPlan`, panic screen.
- **F-A6 — `gbf-store` + `gbf-migrate`**: content-addressed blob store, two-level `StageCache`, pinsets/GC, archive transport, host-side migration DAGs.

### What is **not** in scope (deferred to other epics)

- The compiler IR pipeline (Stages 1–12) → Epic B.
- The three oracles (`DenotationalOracle`, `ArtifactOracle`, `ScheduleOracle`) → Epic C.
- The runtime persistence protocol, harness control plane, drift monitor, fault-policy table, `SchedulePack` mode switching → Epic D.
- Calibration *bundle production* (the schema is here in F-A2; producing real bundles is Epic E).
- Build reports, certificates, slow reference impls → Epic F.
- Corpus / lexical / decode pipelines → Epic G.
- Kernel families, emulator adapters → Epic H.

### Decomposition rationale

Why six features and not one giant blob?

- Each feature corresponds to a single crate (or one tightly coupled pair) in `gbf-foundation`/`gbf-asm`/`gbf-hw`/`gbf-abi`/`gbf-runtime`/`gbf-store`+`gbf-migrate`. That makes PR review and test isolation natural.
- Crate boundaries are already drawn by `bd-1bi` (T0.1) which scaffolded the workspace. Every module file currently exists as `//! Module stub.` — these features fill them in.
- The features have a real DAG (F-A1, F-A2, F-A3, F-A6 are independent; F-A4 needs F-A1 + F-A2; F-A5 needs everything earlier). That parallelism matters for staffing this work.

### Critical-path analysis

```
F-A1 (asm) ─────────────────────────────┐
F-A2 (hw) ──────────────────┐           │
F-A3 (abi)                  ├─→ F-A4 ───┤
F-A6 (store/migrate)        │           ├─→ F-A5 (Bank0 runtime)
                            │           │
                       (mbc5 + memory)  │
                                  (BankLease + InterruptPolicy + AsmIR)
```

The longest path is F-A1 + F-A2 → F-A4 → F-A5. F-A3 and F-A6 can land independently.

### Milestone DoD (when this epic is done)

All six features complete AND:

```bash
cargo test --workspace                                    # Everything green
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings                   # Clean
cargo run -p gbf-cli -- demo-bank0-rom                    # A bring-up ROM exists
```

The bring-up ROM:
1. Boots on a real DMG/MBC5 emulator (SameBoy or BGB).
2. Shows a text screen and keyboard prompt rendered from `gbf-runtime::text` + `keyboard`.
3. Cooperatively yields per the `gbf-runtime::scheduler` + TIMA deadline ABI.
4. Performs at least one bank switch through `BankLease` and one safe-mode panic via `gbf-runtime::panic`.
5. Has a `runtime_nucleus_hash` consistent with the `RuntimeChromeBudget` pinned in the synthetic reference budget (T2.6, `bd-ros`).

That ROM is what M1 (oracles) and M2 (first scheduled inference) build against.

### How this connects to existing beads

- **T0.1 (`bd-1bi`)**, **T0.2 (`bd-yb3`)**, **T0.3 (`bd-1z3`)** are CLOSED and provide the workspace + pre-commit + foundation types this epic builds on.
- **F2 (`bd-1hv`)** `RuntimeChromeBudget` and its children (T2.1–T2.7) consume **F-A2** (`TargetProfileId`, memory map) and **F-A3** (`runtime_nucleus_hash` provenance), and the synthetic reference budget (T2.6, `bd-ros`) is what F-A5's bring-up ROM hash-pins against.
- **F11 (`bd-1i8`)** `CompileProfile + WRAM Layout` defines the `BringUp` profile (T11.2, `bd-ymo`) that the F-A5 runtime imports for WRAM layout constants.
- **F12 (`bd-144`)** `Dual-Path Sequence-State Experimentation` does not depend on M0 directly but its M2 implementation will run on M0's runtime.

### Constitution checkpoints (epic-level)

- **§I.1 Correctness by Construction**: every module here lands typed and exhaustive — no opaque numbers, no untyped MBC writes, no untyped ABI.
- **§II.1 Latency is Tech Debt**: the feature set is sized so a developer can implement one feature per week and still keep the workspace test suite under 2 minutes.
- **§III.1 Compiler-first**: most M0 invariants are **types + tests**. Runtime/encoder bugs surface as failing `cargo test`, not field debugging.
- **§IV.3 Reproducible Builds**: `gbf-store` (F-A6) is content-addressed; `gbf-asm` encoder (F-A1) is bit-stable.
- **§VI.1 Single Source of Truth**: `gbf-hw` is the *only* place hardware constants live. `gbf-asm` is the *only* authoring layer for executable code. `gbf-abi` is the *only* live-execution contract.

### Confidence: HIGH on shape, MEDIUM on Bank0 runtime sizing

The plan specifies F-A1, F-A2, F-A3, F-A4, F-A6 quite tightly — they're mostly schema/types/encoder work. F-A5 (Bank0 nucleus) involves real cooperative-runtime engineering on a constrained 16 KiB Bank 0; the byte budget is tight and may force a second pass. The reference shell module set (Boot, Interrupts, Scheduler, Banking, Joypad, Text, Keyboard, VideoCommit — pinned in T2.4 `bd-37r`) anchors the F-A5 sizing constraint.

### Future-self note

If you (later me) are wondering "why not skip M0 and start at M1 with a stub runtime?": because M1 oracles need a *real* `gbf-abi`, M2 `RomWindowPlan` needs *real* MBC5 register semantics from `gbf-hw`, and the very first compiled ROM needs `gbf-asm`'s encoder. Cutting M0 corners shows up as silent contract drift between the compiler and the runtime later — exactly the failure mode planv0.md was written to prevent (`runtime_nucleus_hash` exists for this reason).

The thirty-eight tasks below are sized so each is one focused PR. Pick them in dependency order with `br ready`.

title: F-B8: RomWindowPlan (Stage 8)
description: The most important architectural correction in the plan. Computes simultaneously visible ROM set under single-switchable-window rule; selects KernelResidency (Bank0Fixed / WramOverlay / CoResidentSwitchable). Rejects impossible placements before layout. Emits certs/window.cert.json.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B8: RomWindowPlan (Stage 8)

### What this is

Essential on MBC5 (`planv0.md` line 1318). Computes which ROM objects must be simultaneously visible under the single-switchable-window rule, selects kernel + LUT residency from `StoragePlan`'s materializations, duplicates or stages tiny hot tables when legal, **rejects impossible code/data placements** before layout.

`KernelResidency` is `Bank0Fixed | WramOverlay | CoResidentSwitchable`. Per planv0.md line 1320, this pass resolves the contradiction: you cannot have "shared micro-kernels in common banks" *and* "expert-local data in expert banks" be the hot-path default simultaneously, because there's only one switchable ROM window.

### Why this matters

The single most important architectural correction in the whole plan was adding this pass (planv0.md line 2554). Without it, bank thrash is silent and deferred to runtime; with it, illegal placements fail loudly at compile time.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub enum KernelResidency {
    Bank0Fixed,
    WramOverlay,
    CoResidentSwitchable,
}

pub struct RomWindowPlan {
    pub kernel_residency: BTreeMap<KernelInstanceId, KernelResidency>,
    pub lut_residency: BTreeMap<LutId, KernelResidency>,
    pub residency_epochs: Vec<ResidencyEpoch>,
    pub bank_switch_cost: BTreeMap<TransitionId, EstimatedCostDelta>,
}
```

Emits `certs/window.cert.json`.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B6 StoragePlan
- F11 / bd-1i8 CompileProfile (overlay reload policy + max_bank_switches_per_token)
- F-A2.2 Memory map predicates

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T-A5.7: Panic screen
description: Disable interrupts, dump FaultCode + FaultSnapshot to known SRAM, render minimal halted-code screen via direct VRAM write (audited bypass — video_commit may itself be broken). Section is exempt from no-direct-VRAM rule, recognized by ReachabilityValidation.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A5.7: Panic screen

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-runtime/src/panic.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 1646 (panic/debug screen in Bank 0 nucleus).

### Background

When the runtime takes an unrecoverable fault, it disables interrupts, dumps `FaultSnapshot` (T-A3.4) to a known SRAM address (so a power-cut and reboot can read it), renders a "Game Boy halted: <fault code>" screen via direct VRAM write (since normal video_commit may itself be broken), and halts.

Panic is in the reference shell budget reservation but not in the included module set initially — yet bring-up needs at least a basic panic so faults don't silently freeze. **Compromise**: ship a minimal panic in M0 (just halt + on-screen code dump); defer the full FaultSnapshot+SRAM persistence to Epic D.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub fn build_panic_section() -> Section;

/// Emit AsmIR for: disable interrupts, dump FaultCode to a known VRAM tile, halt.
pub fn emit_panic<B: Builder>(b: &mut B, code: FaultCode);

/// Bypass video_commit (since it may be broken); write directly to VRAM.
pub fn emit_panic_screen_render<B: Builder>(b: &mut B);
```

Bypassing `video_commit` is one of the rare audited cases where direct VRAM write is legal. The section is `PrivilegeClass::Privileged + InterruptDisabled`. ReachabilityValidation will recognize the panic section as exempt from the "no-direct-VRAM-write" rule.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-runtime
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- panic::emits_di_then_halt
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- panic::renders_fault_code_glyphs
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- panic::section_marked_exempt              # Annotation for ReachabilityValidation
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §V.3: Panic dumps fault code to a visible location; not silent.
- §I.1: FaultCode is structured.

title: T2.5: runtime_nucleus_hash CI drift gate between training and production shell
description: CI step that: (1) on every PR touching gbf-runtime, re-emits RuntimeChromeBudget; (2) compares runtime_nucleus_hash to the pinned training-time hash recorded in the active training run manifest (or the canonical reference budget); (3) fails the PR unless the bump is intentional and tagged with a release note that promotes the new hash. Trainer also self-checks at every step that the captured pin is unchanged.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target**: CI workflow + `gbf-runtime` + `gbf-train`.
**Target file(s)**: `.github/workflows/runtime-nucleus-drift.yml` (new) or equivalent CI config; `gbf-train/src/preflight.rs` (self-check); a reusable `tools/check-nucleus-drift` binary if needed.

**Reference**: bd-1hv 2026-04-26 session comment ("`runtime_nucleus_hash` is load-bearing"), T2.1 refinement (bd-yfv), T2.4 (bd-37r).

### Implementation Notes

Two layers of drift detection, both required:

1. **CI gate (production-shell drift).**
   - Trigger: any PR that modifies `gbf-runtime/`, `gbf-policy/src/budget.rs`, `gbf-policy/src/profile.rs`, `gbf-policy/src/reference_shell.rs`, or anything that ends up in the runtime-nucleus binary.
   - Action: re-emit `RuntimeChromeBudget` from the post-PR shell. Compare `runtime_nucleus_hash` to the value pinned in `artifacts/calibration/pinned_reference_budget.json` (or whatever path the project chooses).
   - Failure mode: PR fails with a diagnostic listing both hashes and per-slot byte deltas. The PR author either (a) reverts the budget-affecting change, or (b) explicitly bumps the pin in the same PR with a release note that promotes the new hash.

2. **Trainer self-check (active-run drift).**
   - At training start, the trainer captures the pinned `runtime_nucleus_hash` into the run manifest.
   - At every checkpoint commit and every export step, the trainer re-reads the active `RuntimeChromeBudget` (synthetic or real) and asserts the hash is unchanged.
   - On drift: trainer halts with a clear error pointing to (i) the pinned hash, (ii) the current hash, (iii) which file change likely caused it (via `git log -p <budget_file>` summary).

### Release-note convention for promoting a new hash

Promotion is a deliberate, reviewable act. The mechanism:
- A dedicated marker file `artifacts/calibration/PINNED_HASH_HISTORY.md` records every pinned hash with date, the modules included, and the reason for promotion.
- CI reads the most recent entry's hash; PRs that change the runtime nucleus must add a new entry to be merged.

### What this task does NOT do

It does not specify the byte-level encoding of `runtime_nucleus_hash` (that's T2.1 / bd-yfv). It does not implement the synthetic budget (that's T2.6 / bd-ros). It composes those.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
# Locally:
cargo run -p tools-check-nucleus-drift                         # Exit 0 when in sync
cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight::nucleus_self_check        # Trainer self-check enforces drift

# CI:
# Verify: a PR adding a new file to gbf-runtime/src/ that ends up in the nucleus fails CI without a pin promotion
# Verify: a PR that promotes the pin (updates PINNED_HASH_HISTORY.md) passes
# Verify: the diagnostic on failure lists both hashes and the offending file paths
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.4: CI gate is the verifiable reward — exit 0 means no silent drift.
- §IV.3: Reproducible budget — same source produces same hash; CI catches deviations.
- §V.3: Loud failure with structured diff (old hash, new hash, byte deltas) on drift.
- §VI.1: PINNED_HASH_HISTORY.md is the single source of truth for hash promotion.

title: T-A5.1: Boot + IRQ vector dispatch
description: Bank 0 boot section (cartridge header + Nintendo logo + init), IRQ dispatch table at 0x0040..0x0060, ISR entry stubs (save regs / call handler / restore regs / RETI). Initializes HRAM shadow registers.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A5.1: Boot + IRQ vector dispatch

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-runtime/src/boot.rs`, `gbf-runtime/src/interrupts.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 1626, line 1646 (boot/header/vectors as part of Bank 0 nucleus).

### Background

Boot is the first executable code on the cartridge. After the cartridge header (`$0100`) and the Nintendo logo verification dance, control jumps to the runtime's `main` entry point. From there the runtime initializes:
- HRAM shadow registers (zero them).
- IRQ vectors (point each `INT_VECTOR_*` at the matching ISR entry stub).
- LCD power-up state.
- Joypad register read.
- Cooperative scheduler initial state.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
/// Build the Bank 0 boot section with cartridge header, init sequence, and IRQ vectors.
pub fn build_boot_section() -> Section;

/// Build the IRQ dispatch section. Each vector is a 16-byte slot; they jump to ISR stubs in Bank 0.
pub fn build_irq_vectors_section() -> Section;

/// IRQ entry stubs: minimal AsmIR that saves regs, calls into the corresponding ISR handler,
/// restores regs, RETI.
pub fn build_isr_stubs_section() -> Section;
```

The vector layout (per gbf-hw T-A2.5):
- `$0040` VBlank
- `$0048` LCD STAT
- `$0050` Timer (used by scheduler for yield deadline)
- `$0058` Serial
- `$0060` Joypad

Each ISR stub is `Privileged + InterruptHandler` per T-A1.4; reachability is `Bank0/HRAM/fixed-WRAM`.

The cartridge header bytes are emitted via T-A1.9's `assemble_rom`; this task wires the runtime into those slots.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-runtime
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- boot::cartridge_header_layout         # Bytes at $0100-$014F
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- boot::irq_vector_jumps                 # Vectors jump to ISR stubs
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- boot::shadow_registers_zeroed_at_init
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- interrupts::isr_stubs_are_isr_marked   # Privilege class
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Boot is structured AsmIR sections, not raw bytes.
- §VI.1: Single boot module.

title: T-A2.1: TargetProfile + ConsoleModel + CartridgeProfile + CapabilitySet
description: Top-level type bundling Game Boy + cartridge + capabilities for a build. Includes ConsoleModel (Dmg/Mgb/Sgb/Cgb), CartridgeProfile (mbc + max sizes + battery + rtc), CapabilitySet. dmg_mbc5_8mib_128kib() is the bring-up profile.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A2.1: `TargetProfile` + `ConsoleModel` + `CartridgeProfile` + `CapabilitySet`

**Target crate**: `gbf-hw`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-hw/src/target.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 213.

### Background

`TargetProfile` is the top-level type that bundles "which Game Boy + which cartridge + which capabilities" for a given build. Compile requests (`CompileRequest::target: TargetProfileId`) resolve into a `TargetProfile` early in compilation. This is what tells the rest of the system whether to assume DMG, CGB, or some specific MBC variant.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct TargetProfile {
    pub id: TargetProfileId,                  // from gbf-foundation
    pub family: TargetFamilyId,
    pub console: ConsoleModel,
    pub cartridge: CartridgeProfile,
    pub timing: TimingProfile,                // populated in T-A2.4
    pub capabilities: CapabilitySet,
}

pub enum ConsoleModel {
    Dmg,                                       // original Game Boy
    Mgb,                                       // Game Boy Pocket — same architecture, different timing trim
    Sgb,                                       // Super Game Boy — DMG mode only
    Cgb,                                       // Game Boy Color
    // Cgb-in-DMG-mode is a CompileProfile choice, not a separate ConsoleModel.
}

pub struct CartridgeProfile {
    pub mbc: MbcType,
    pub max_rom_kib: u32,
    pub max_sram_kib: u32,
    pub has_battery: bool,
    pub has_rtc: bool,
}

pub enum MbcType {
    None,
    Mbc1,
    Mbc2,
    Mbc3,
    Mbc5,
    // future: Mbc6, Mbc7, etc.
}

pub struct CapabilitySet {
    pub double_speed_mode: bool,               // CGB only
    pub vram_dma: bool,                        // CGB
    pub rtc_present: bool,
}
```

Provide a known-good `pub fn dmg_mbc5_8mib_128kib() -> TargetProfile { ... }` constructor for the bring-up profile.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-hw
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- target::dmg_mbc5_constructor              # Bring-up profile is well-formed
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- target::serde_round_trip                  # Profile round-trips through JSON
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- target::capability_set_exhaustive          # Each capability has a known semantics
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `MbcType` and `ConsoleModel` are enumerated.
- §VI.1: `TargetProfile` is the single place that bundles target identity.

title: T10.15: Full regression test script with diagnostic summary
description:
A single entry-point script (cargo test -p gbf-test -- --training-contract or a dedicated binary) that runs ALL training-contract tests in the correct order and produces a structured diagnostic summary.

**Test execution order:**
1. Infrastructure: tiny model fixtures, logging setup
2. Unit tests: QAT modules, byte math, model config, loss terms, router, budget, Pareto
3. Integration tests: phase transitions, temporal smoothness boundaries
4. E2E tests: train-to-export, export determinism, oracle agreement, shadow compile
5. Regression checks: compare against golden values from last known-good run

**Output artifacts:**
- test_results.json: per-test pass/fail/skip with timing, failure messages, and structured diagnostics
- test_summary.txt: human-readable one-line-per-test with [PASS]/[FAIL]/[SKIP] and total counts
- training_metrics.json: loss curves from E2E training test (useful for detecting training regressions)
- artifact_hashes.json: content hashes of all exported artifacts (useful for detecting export regressions)
- byte_counts.json: per-expert byte counts (useful for detecting budget formula regressions)

**CI integration:**
- Exit code 0 if all tests pass, 1 if any fail
- Failed test output includes: test name, assertion that failed, expected vs actual, full structured context
- Summary line: 'Training-contract tests: 47/47 passed, 0 failed, 0 skipped (12.3s)'
- Optionally: upload test_results.json and training_metrics.json as CI artifacts

**Golden value management:**
- Golden values stored in fixtures/golden/training_contract/
- When REGENERATE_GOLDEN=1, tests overwrite golden values instead of comparing
- Golden values include: artifact hashes, byte counts, phase transition sequences, loss term values for known inputs

**Performance guard:**
- Total test suite should complete in < 60 seconds on CI (tiny model, CPU only)
- Individual slow tests (E2E) are marked with a time budget and warn if exceeded
acceptance_criteria: ./scripts/test_all.sh exits 0; test_results.json lists ALL tests; Total runtime <60s; Golden hash comparison works; Exit 1 on any failure
