title: T10.10: Pareto frontier selection correctness tests (F8 coverage)
description:
Unit tests for Pareto frontier tracking and checkpoint selection:

**Dominance relation tests:**
- Point A (quality=0.9, fit=true, cost=100) dominates B (quality=0.8, fit=true, cost=150): verify A dominates B
- Point A (quality=0.9, fit=true, cost=100) vs C (quality=0.85, fit=true, cost=80): verify neither dominates (Pareto-incomparable)
- Point with fit=false is never selected regardless of quality/cost

**Frontier tracking tests:**
- Insert 5 known points, verify frontier contains exactly the non-dominated ones
- Verify frontier size never exceeds keep_frontier limit
- Verify oldest non-frontier points are evicted first when at capacity

**Selection algorithm tests:**
- Given frontier: [(quality=0.9,cost=100), (quality=0.85,cost=80), (quality=0.95,cost=200)]
- After filtering fit=true, verify selected = highest quality point (0.95, cost=200)
- If best-quality point has fit=false, verify next-best is selected
- If all points have fit=false: verify selection returns None with appropriate error

**training_selection.json output:**
- Run selection on known frontier, verify JSON contains: frontier array, selected checkpoint_id, domination relationships, timestamp
- Verify JSON is valid and parseable

**Edge cases:**
- Single point frontier: verify it's selected
- All identical quality: verify any one is selected (deterministic tiebreak)
- Empty frontier: verify error, not crash
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- pareto_tests passes; Dominance is transitive; Frontier bounded; Selection never picks hard-failed checkpoint

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-test`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-test/tests/pareto_tests.rs`

**Reference**: Bead description for T8.4 (bd-2gp).

### Implementation Notes
- **Dominance relation tests**: A dominates B iff A is better on ALL metrics
- **Frontier tracking tests**: adding dominated point doesn't change frontier; adding non-dominated point evicts dominated ones
- **Selection algorithm tests**: hard failures filtered; best quality selected from remaining frontier
- **training_selection.json output**: valid JSON with selected checkpoint id and quality metrics
- **Edge cases**: empty frontier, single point, all points failing, ties

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo test -p gbf-test -- pareto_tests           # All pass
# Verify: dominance is transitive and antisymmetric
# Verify: frontier size stays bounded
# Verify: selection never picks a hard-failed checkpoint
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Dominance check is a pure function — test it independently
- §III.2: Property tests for dominance relation properties

title: F-B13: Backend — AsmIR codegen + ReachabilityValidation + PlacedRom + EncodedRom (Stage 12)
description: Lower GbSchedIR slices to AsmIR sections; ReachabilityValidation computes ISR-reachable / yield-resume-reachable / fault-path-reachable / harness-entry-reachable / bank-lease-protected / normal-only classes; PlacedRom does layout + relax + far-call thunks under PlacementProfile; EncodedRom emits .gb/.sym/.lst.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B13: Backend — AsmIR codegen + ReachabilityValidation + PlacedRom + EncodedRom (Stage 12)

### What this is

The headline backend stage with four sub-passes (`planv0.md` line 1496):

1. **AsmIR codegen**: lower `GbSchedIR` slices into `AsmIR` sections via `gbf-asm` builders. Compiler-generated code uses `BankLease` pseudo-ops only — no raw MBC writes.
2. **ReachabilityValidation**: compute (not trust) ISR-reachable / yield-resume-reachable / fault-path-reachable / harness-entry-reachable / bank-lease-protected / normal-only classes. Validate ISR locality, no forbidden MBC writes on privileged paths, no switchable-bank dep on ISR or resume paths, no illegal reentrancy through bank guards, no fault path on non-resident data. Emits `certs/reachability.cert.json`.
3. **PlacedRom**: layout via `PlacementProfile::{StrictOnePerBank, Budgeted, PackedExperts}`, branch relax, far-call thunks, bank-switch coalescing, deterministic section ordering, residency enforcement.
4. **EncodedRom**: bytes only. `.gb` + `.sym` + `.lst`. Tiny encoder.

### Why this matters

Backend is where structural correctness becomes a real ROM. ReachabilityValidation is the safety pass that catches bugs that would otherwise survive until cartridge testing.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub fn codegen_asm(sched: &GbSchedIR, kernels: &KernelRegistry) -> Vec<Section>;
pub fn validate_reachability(sections: &[Section]) -> Result<ReachabilityReport, ReachabilityError>;
pub fn place_rom(sections: Vec<Section>, profile: PlacementProfile) -> PlacedRom;
pub fn encode_rom(placed: PlacedRom, header: CartridgeHeader) -> EncodedRom;
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A1 (gbf-asm) for AsmIR + encoder
- F-B11 GbSchedIR
- F-A4 BankLease ABI (validated by reachability)
- F-H1, F-H2 KernelRegistry + kernel impls

### Confidence: HIGH on structure, MEDIUM on reachability completeness — getting all six privilege classes right is the engineering challenge.

title: T2.7: Per-export RuntimeChromeBudget re-validation in export pipeline
description: Export pipeline (Phase E HardenAndSelect + F8 shadow_compile) re-runs the preflight from T2.3 against the CURRENT RuntimeChromeBudget. Compares to the training-time pinned hash; emits a clear diagnostic showing both hashes and per-slot byte deltas. Hard fits failure (fits_envelope=false) blocks export. Risk-only flags surface but do not block. Adds the re-validation step as a stage in the export pipeline so it is not skippable.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train` (export pipeline) + relies on T2.3 (bd-27n) preflight.
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/export.rs` (extend or new), `gbf-train/src/shadow.rs` (re-validation hook).

**Reference**: bd-1hv 2026-04-26 session ("Per-export re-validation"), T2.3 refinement (bd-27n), T2.5 (bd-177).

### Implementation Notes

Add an explicit re-validation stage to the export pipeline that runs the same preflight from T2.3 against the **current** `RuntimeChromeBudget` (not the training-time pinned one).

1. **Inputs.**
   - The just-exported `ArtifactCore` + lowering artifacts.
   - The training-time pinned `RuntimeChromeBudget` (from the run manifest).
   - The current production `RuntimeChromeBudget` (re-emitted from the live shell).

2. **Action.**
   - Compare the two `runtime_nucleus_hash`es. If equal, skip the byte-delta comparison and run the standard export-time preflight against the pinned budget.
   - If they differ:
     - Compute per-slot byte deltas.
     - Re-run the preflight against the **current** budget (this is the authoritative check for what production will actually receive).
     - Emit `BudgetDriftReport`:
       ```rust
       pub struct BudgetDriftReport {
           pub training_hash: Hash256,
           pub current_hash: Hash256,
           pub training_modules: BTreeSet<RuntimeShellModule>,
           pub current_modules: BTreeSet<RuntimeShellModule>,
           pub slot_deltas: Vec<RomBudgetSlotDelta>,
           pub wram_delta: WramReservedDelta,
           pub sram_delta: i32,
       }
       ```
   - If `fits_envelope == false` against the current budget, **block export**. Hard failure.
   - If only `risk_flags` fire, surface them but do not block.

3. **Diagnostic.**

   ```
   Budget drift detected:
     Training-time:  runtime_nucleus_hash=0xAB12...  modules={Boot,…,VideoCommit}
     At export:      runtime_nucleus_hash=0xCD34...  modules={Boot,…,VideoCommit,Persistence}
     ExpertBank.usable_bytes: 15488 -> 15104 (delta -384, persistence module added).
     Re-running preflight against current budget...
     Expert payload demand=14976 bytes. PASS (margin 128 bytes).
   ```

4. **Where it runs.**
   - Phase E (HardenAndSelect) export pipeline.
   - F8 shadow_compile pipeline (so drift is caught early during training, not only at final export).

### Coordination with T2.5

T2.5 prevents the production shell from drifting silently. T2.7 handles the case where drift is intentional and the trainer needs to react. They are complementary, not redundant.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- export::revalidation_no_drift        # Same hash: standard preflight runs
cargo test -p gbf-train -- export::revalidation_drift_passing   # Drift but fits: warning, export proceeds
cargo test -p gbf-train -- export::revalidation_drift_blocking  # Drift and breaks fit: export blocked
cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings                        # No warnings
# Verify: BudgetDriftReport is emitted as JSON to artifacts/runs/<run_id>/budget_drift.json
# Verify: blocked export returns a non-zero exit code with a structured error
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.4: Re-validation is the verifiable reward at export — exit 0 means production fit is confirmed.
- §V.1: BudgetDriftReport is JSON.
- §V.3: Diagnostic on block lists both hashes, modules, slot deltas, and the offending preflight failure.
- §III.1: Re-validation runs at shadow_compile too, so drift is caught well before final export.

title: T4.4: Implement logit distillation from frozen teacher
description: During phases C and D, compute KL divergence between the student's logits and the frozen teacher's logits on the same input. Weight by lambda_distill. The teacher forward pass is run in no-grad mode. This provides a stable gradient signal that compensates for the noisy STE gradients from ternary projection. The distillation loss should be logged separately from the LM loss so quality vs distillation tradeoffs are visible.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::distillation passes; KL divergence non-negative; Identical logits produce loss near 0; Gradient through student only
close_reason:
Implemented logit distillation primitives for `gbf-train`.

Scope completed:
- Added `gbf_train::loss::distillation`.
- Implemented raw scalar KL distillation: `KL(softmax(teacher / T) || softmax(student / T)) * T^2`.
- Implemented batched scalar semantics: logits are partitioned by `class_count`; KL is summed over the class axis and averaged over the remaining rows.
- Added explicit weighted helpers that apply caller-supplied `lambda_distill` without owning global loss config defaults.
- Added feature-gated Burn tensor distillation using the same class-axis semantics and detaching teacher logits.
- Added frozen-teacher helpers that call `FrozenTeacher::forward_no_grad` before computing scalar/Burn distillation loss.
- Added phase convenience helpers for C/D activation: `ExpertTernaryQat` and `FullNumericQat`.
- Added `burn_log_softmax` to the existing Burn adapter boundary.

Non-goals and moved ownership:
- Loss TOML defaults, total-loss composition, and durable `lambda_distill` config remain bd-1c6.
- Real training-loop adoption, phase-boundary invocation, and per-step `LossBreakdown` emission remain bd-14k / future training-loop integration.
- Exporting the frozen teacher as `ReferenceModelBundle` remains bd-7lu.

## Support Matrix

| Behavior | Status | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Raw one-row KL distillation formula | supported | `distillation_loss_matches_manual_kl_with_temperature_scaling` |
| Correct KL orientation | supported | `distillation_loss_orientation_is_teacher_distribution_to_student_distribution` |
| Identical logits near-zero | supported | `distillation_loss_is_zero_for_identical_logits` |
| Batch/class reduction semantics | supported: class-axis sum, row mean | `batched_distillation_loss_means_rows_after_class_axis_sum`; `burn_distillation_loss_matches_scalar_oracle_for_batched_logits` |
| Explicit lambda weighting | supported, no default config ownership | `weighted_distillation_loss_applies_explicit_lambda_distill`; `burn_weighted_distillation_loss_applies_non_default_lambda` |
| Teacher gradients blocked in Burn loss | supported | `burn_distillation_loss_flows_gradient_only_to_student_logits` under `burn-adapter` |
| FrozenTeacher no-grad forward entrypoint | supported helper | `distillation_loss_can_call_frozen_teacher_forward_no_grad` |
| Phase C/D predicate | supported convenience helper | `distillation_is_enabled_only_for_phases_c_and_d` |
| Full training loop computes/logs distillation during C/D | moved | bd-14k / future training-loop integration |
| Loss config defaults and total-loss composition | moved | bd-1c6 |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Target crate compiles | `cargo check -p gbf-train` | none | Passed |
| Distillation tests pass | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::distillation` | none | Passed |
| Burn autodiff gradient proof passes | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- loss::distillation` | `burn-adapter` | Passed; this is the acceptance gate for student-gradient/teacher-no-gradient |
| Clippy clean | `cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings`; `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --all-features -- -D warnings` | none / all features | Passed |
| Full gbf-train tests clean | `cargo test -p gbf-train`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --all-features` | none / all features | Passed |
| Formatting clean | `cargo fmt --check --all` | none | Passed |

## Review Feedback Addressed

| Feedback item | Resolution | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Plain acceptance test did not prove Burn gradient/no-grad behavior | Added and cited the feature-enabled Burn distillation gate | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- loss::distillation` |
| Reduction semantics were underspecified | Added class-axis/row-mean semantics in scalar and Burn paths | `batched_distillation_loss_means_rows_after_class_axis_sum`; `burn_distillation_loss_matches_scalar_oracle_for_batched_logits` |
| Local default lambda overlapped bd-1c6 config ownership | Removed local default lambda/config; weighting is explicit | `weighted_distillation_loss_applies_explicit_lambda_distill` |
| Raw `distill_loss` should be separable from weighted total contribution | Burn/scalar primary functions now return raw KL; separate weighted helpers apply lambda | scalar and Burn weighting tests |
| Teacher no-grad forward was only assumed from precomputed logits | Added helpers that call `FrozenTeacher::forward_no_grad` | `distillation_loss_can_call_frozen_teacher_forward_no_grad` |
| Tiny temperature could overflow and scalar clamp could mask NaN | Added scaled-logit overflow and finite-loss validation; no broad NaN clamp | `distillation_loss_validates_inputs` |
| Shape mismatch error hid same-element-count shape differences | Added Burn `ShapeMismatch` carrying full shapes | `burn_distillation_loss_reports_shape_not_flattened_count` |

## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Gradient proof lived behind optional Burn feature but plain test was the only acceptance command | Acceptance text did not name the feature gate needed for autodiff evidence | Added AGENTS training-loss guide: Burn autodiff claims must cite a feature-enabled gate | `burn_distillation_loss_flows_gradient_only_to_student_logits` | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- loss::distillation` |
| Loss helper mixed raw diagnostic loss with weighted contribution | Formula owner blurred with total-loss composition owner | Added AGENTS guide: log raw per-term losses; apply configured loss weights in composition owner | raw/weighted scalar and Burn tests | bd-1c6 owns `lambda_distill` and global composition |
| Batch/token/vocab reduction was implicit | 1D oracle was enough to pass while real logits are batched | Added AGENTS guide: define class/vocab axis and reduction semantics for loss beads | batched scalar/Burn parity tests | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- loss::distillation` |
| Standalone helper could overclaim training-loop phase adoption/logging | No production training loop caller exists in this bead | Added AGENTS guide: do not claim phase-boundary adoption from a standalone loss helper | closure support matrix names moved owners | bd-14k / future training-loop integration |

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/loss/distillation.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "logit distillation", "KL divergence", "frozen teacher", "lambda_distill".

### Implementation Notes
- During Phases C and D, compute KL divergence between student and frozen teacher logits.
- `pub fn distillation_loss(student_logits: &Tensor, teacher_logits: &Tensor, temperature: f32) -> Tensor`
- Standard KL distillation: `KL(softmax(teacher/T) || softmax(student/T)) * T^2`
- Teacher runs in no-grad mode (already frozen from T4.3).
- Weighted by `lambda_distill` from loss config.
- Temperature is a config parameter (default: 2.0).

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                          # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::distillation      # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings           # No warnings
# Verify: KL divergence is non-negative
# Verify: identical logits produce loss ≈ 0
# Verify: gradient flows through student logits but NOT teacher logits
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Pure function — takes tensors, returns tensor. No side effects.
- §III.2: Unit test numerical correctness against known KL values

title: Epic F: Reports & Verify — gbf-report + gbf-verify
description: The trust layer. 13+ build report files, machine-checkable certificates (range/arena/window/reachability/resource_state) consumed by gbf-verify, independent slow reference implementations (pack/unpack, LUT eval, decode/RNG) used in nightly randomized cross-checks, FailureCapsule with minimization/reduction_log, RunManifest + ReproducibilityManifest.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Epic F: Reports & Verify (`gbf-report`, `gbf-verify`)

### What this is

The trust layer: the 13+ build report files (`map.json`, `provenance.json`, `budget.json`, `slice_report.json`, `trace_schema.json`, `oracle_vectors.bin`, `semantic_checkpoint_schema.json`, `operational_probe_schema.json`, `hint_consumption.json`, `compiler_feedback.json`, `reachability_report.json`, etc.), the machine-checkable certificates (`certs/{range,arena,window,reachability,resource_state}.cert.json`), the slow reference algorithms in `gbf-verify`, and the structured failure capsule + minimization machinery.

### Why this matters

Reports turn "the build worked" into "the build worked, and here's the structured evidence." Certificates make safety-critical passes auditable. `gbf-verify`'s independent slow reference impls prevent the failure mode where a shared bug becomes self-validating (engineering rule 17). `FailureCapsule` + `minimized_workload` makes failures bisectable instead of ephemeral.

### Scope (five features)

- **F-F1 — Build reports**: emit the structured JSON + binary report set per `planv0.md` line 1577. One JSON schema per report; CI tests round-trip them.
- **F-F2 — Certificates**: `certs/range.cert.json`, `certs/arena.cert.json`, `certs/window.cert.json`, `certs/reachability.cert.json`, `certs/resource_state.cert.json`. Each consumed by `gbf-verify` as machine-checkable proofs.
- **F-F3 — Slow reference impls in `gbf-verify`**: pack/unpack, logical LUT eval, decode/RNG — independent re-implementations used in nightly randomized cross-checks against production.
- **F-F4 — `FailureCapsule` + minimization**: capture failed runs with structured state + reduction log; cross-build replay support.
- **F-F5 — `RunManifest` + `ReproducibilityManifest`**: scientific reproducibility plumbing — toolchain pin, lockfile, host triple, deterministic seeds, `study_tag`.

### Critical path

```
F-A6 (gbf-store) ──→ F-F1..F-F4 (reports + capsules go through CAS)
F-B (compiler stages) ──→ F-F1..F-F2 (each stage emits its report + cert)
F-C (oracles) ──→ F-F3 cross-checks; F-F4 captures oracle failures
```

### Confidence: HIGH

Mostly schema + JSON serialization + per-stage emit hooks. The verify reference impls are real engineering — they must be deliberately written to fail differently from production code (e.g., naive LUT eval vs production's tiled SIMD-ish lookup).

title: F-D4: RuntimeDriftMonitor + automatic mode demotion
description: RuntimeDriftMonitor + DriftTrigger + DriftAction (ShrinkSlices / DropTrace / DemoteMode). Tracks observed DriftEnvelope (slice/UI/trace/persist p95s) against expected; fires actions past threshold.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-D4: RuntimeDriftMonitor

### What this is

Per `planv0.md` correction: `RuntimeDriftMonitor` + `DriftTrigger` + `DriftAction` (`ShrinkSlices` / `DropTrace` / `DemoteMode`). Tracks observed `DriftEnvelope` (slice_cycles_p95, ui_commit_cycles_p95, trace_drop_rate_pct, persist_overrun_rate_pct) against expected; fires actions past threshold.

### Why this matters

Production runtime experiences load it wasn't trained on. Drift monitoring + automatic mode demotion is what keeps the cooperative budget honored even when reality disagrees with calibration.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct DriftEnvelope {
    pub slice_cycles_p95: Option<u32>,
    pub ui_commit_cycles_p95: Option<u32>,
    pub trace_drop_rate_pct: Option<u8>,
    pub persist_overrun_rate_pct: Option<u8>,
}

pub enum DriftAction {
    ShrinkSlices,
    DropTrace,
    DemoteMode(RuntimeMode),
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A3 abi InterruptPolicy + ResourceLease
- F-D6 SchedulePack mode switching (DemoteMode target)

### Confidence: MEDIUM (depends on real workload data)

title: T10.4: TernaryWeightPlan byte math and pack/unpack round-trip tests (F3 coverage)
description:
Unit tests for TernaryWeightPlan types and byte computations:

**Byte cost computation tests:**
- PerTensor Q8_8, 128x224: verify exact byte count = ceil(128*224/4) + 2 = 7170
- PerOutputRow Q8_8, 128x224: verify = ceil(128*224/4) + 224*2 = 7616
- PerOutputRow Q8_8, 224x128: verify = ceil(224*128/4) + 128*2 = 7424
- PerGroup(16) Q8_8, 128x224: verify = ceil(128*224/4) + ceil(128*224/16)*2 = 10720
- Full two-matrix expert (128,224) PerOutputRow Q8_8: verify total = 15040 + metadata
- Verify all byte costs are deterministic across runs

**Pack/unpack round-trip tests:**
- Create known float tensor, apply ternary projection, pack via production packer, unpack via production unpacker, verify lossless round-trip
- Test all WeightEncoding variants: Ternary2, SparseTernaryBitplanes, Binary1
- Test boundary cases: all-zero tensor, all-positive tensor, alternating pattern
- Test scale formats: Q8_8, Q4_4, Pow2 round-trip through quantize->dequantize

**Independent reference agreement test:**
- Generate 100 random tensors of varying sizes (4x4 to 256x256)
- Pack with production packer AND with gbf-verify independent reference packer
- Verify byte-identical output for all tensors
- If any diverge: log tensor index, first divergent byte position, expected vs actual in hex

**Artifact hash stability:**
- Export same tiny model twice, verify ArtifactCore content hash is identical
- Export, modify non-semantic field in ArtifactAux, verify ArtifactCore hash unchanged

All failures include: dimensions, encoding, scale granularity, expected byte count, actual byte count, hex dump of first divergent region.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- ternary_tests passes; Byte cost covers all variants; 100 random round-trip tests pass; Production vs reference packer agreement

title: F7: Router Switch-Awareness — Temporal Smoothness and Export Diagnostics
description: Make the router explicitly aware of bank-switch cost via temporal smoothness regularization, z-loss, expert dropout, and low-rank projection. Export TemporalSwitchDigest/ClipSaturationDigest/ExpertPayloadDigest in ExportFacts. Add ExpertSlotAffinity to CompilePreferences.
acceptance_criteria: All T7.x tasks closed; Digest types tested; Router features tested; Integration tests pass

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F7: Router Switch-Awareness — Temporal Smoothness and Export Diagnostics

### Background

On Game Boy with MBC5, a bank switch costs real cycles: the runtime must write to the MBC5 ROM bank register (at $2000-$3FFF), update the HRAM shadow, and potentially flush/restore any state that depended on the previous bank's data. Calibration measurements will quantify this cost precisely, but even the lower bound (a few dozen M-cycles per switch) means that high switch rates kill throughput.

The router doesn't know this. A standard MoE router optimizes for per-token quality, not for temporal coherence between consecutive tokens. Two adjacent tokens in the same sentence might route to completely different experts in the same layer, causing a bank switch every token.

### The temporal switch penalty

L_switch = (1/LT) Σ_{l,t} (1 - ⟨p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1}⟩)

This directly penalizes the inner product gap between consecutive routing distributions. When the same expert is chosen for consecutive tokens, the dot product is close to 1 and the penalty is near 0. When different experts are chosen, the penalty increases.

This is much more informative than a vague "locality" term because:
1. It directly models the deployment cost (bank switches)
2. It's differentiable through the router's softmax
3. It can be measured during training and compared against deployment measurements

### Export diagnostics

TemporalSwitchDigest records per-layer temporal switch statistics:
- same_expert_rate_q8_8: what fraction of consecutive tokens use the same expert (in Q8.8 fixed point)
- transition_mass: per expert-pair transition probability (top-K transitions that account for most mass)

ClipSaturationDigest records how often activations hit the clipping bounds (early warning for range issues).
ExpertPayloadDigest records the actual exported byte count per expert (for budget verification).
ExpertSlotAffinity in CompilePreferences: hints to the compiler about which experts should be co-located based on transition patterns.

### Low-rank router

The router projects d_model -> n_experts, where n_experts is typically 8-32. A full-rank router is d_model × n_experts parameters. A low-rank router factorizes this as d_model → r → n_experts where r << n_experts, saving common-bank bytes and providing implicit regularization. For d_model=128, n_experts=16, rank=4: full-rank is 2048 params, low-rank is 128*4 + 4*16 = 576 params — 3.5x smaller.

### z-loss and expert dropout

These are standard MoE training techniques, not novel to this project:
- z-loss penalizes large router logits before softmax, preventing confident but fragile routing decisions
- Expert dropout randomly drops expert outputs during training, forcing the model to not over-rely on any single expert

They're included here because they interact with the temporal switch penalty: z-loss keeps router distributions softer, which makes the temporal switch penalty more effective (softer distributions have more room for gradients to shift routing toward temporal coherence).

### Confidence: HIGH

The temporal switch penalty is the most directly useful contribution. The export diagnostics are straightforward. The z-loss/expert dropout/low-rank are standard practice. Mild reservation: the specific interaction between z-loss temperature and temporal switch lambda will require tuning.

| F4 Off/Soft/Hard hardness was implied but not implemented. | Local execution modes were treated as phase-hardness completion. | Either implement exact F4 `Off`/`Soft`/`Hard` or move it to the phase-hardness owner bead. | Guide only; full implementation remains a bead-level concern. | `bd-2uw`; `.agents/skills/qat-bead-closure/SKILL.md` |
| Router balance term was a single-token proxy, not the standard batch load-balance loss. | Scalar-core aux loss name overclaimed training-loss semantics. | Proxy losses must be named as proxies and cite the standard loss owner. | Harness now flags unqualified router balance/load-balance claims unless they name the proxy and cite `bd-1b3`. | `bd-1b3`; `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` |
| Top-1 tie break and default rank clamp were undocumented. | Deterministic conventions were left implicit. | Document deterministic router conventions that affect traces/artifacts. | Guide only; model tests now cover tie break. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router`; `.agents/skills/qat-bead-closure/SKILL.md` |
| The original bead was too large and hard to review. | Split suggestion existed but was not enforced before implementation. | No automated action in this patch; use bead comments and ready-queue hygiene to split future bundled work when owners/paths differ. | No low-false-positive sensor; decomposition remains manual project judgment. | Future bead triage |
| `expert_id: usize` could be a newtype. | Type-level dispatch contract is still loose. | No action in this patch; useful design follow-up but not directly part of the reviewed behavioral regression. | No sensor; file a design bead if this becomes a recurring source of bugs. | Future design bead |

title: T5.2: Implement router z-loss and expert load-balance loss
description: Standard MoE losses. z-loss = (1/N) sum log(sum exp(router_logits))^2 — penalizes large pre-softmax logits. Load-balance = N * sum(fraction_tokens_per_expert * average_gate_per_expert) — penalizes uneven expert utilization. These are well-studied and should be included from Phase B (RouterWarmup) onward, not deferred to M6.

Gates passed: cargo check -p gbf-train; cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::router; cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- loss::router; cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings; cargo clippy -p gbf-train --all-features -- -D warnings; cargo test -p gbf-train; cargo test -p gbf-train --all-features; cargo fmt --check --all. comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/loss/router.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "z-loss", "expert load-balance loss", "standard MoE losses".

### Implementation Notes
- Two standard MoE loss terms:
  1. **Router z-loss**: `L_z = (1/B) Σ_i log(Σ_j exp(z_{i,j}))^2` — penalizes large router logits
  2. **Expert load-balance loss**: `L_balance = n_experts * Σ_j f_j * P_j` where f_j = fraction of tokens routed to expert j, P_j = mean routing probability for expert j
- Both included from Phase B (RouterWarmup) onward — these are NOT M6-deferred.
- `pub fn router_z_loss(logits: &Tensor) -> Tensor`
- `pub fn load_balance_loss(routing_probs: &Tensor, expert_assignments: &Tensor, n_experts: usize) -> Tensor`

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::router         # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings        # No warnings
# Verify: z-loss is 0 when all logits are 0
# Verify: z-loss increases with logit magnitude
# Verify: load-balance loss is minimized when routing is uniform
# Verify: gradient flows through both terms
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Pure functions — tensors in, scalars out
- §III.2: Property tests for known analytical values

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B1: QuantGraph (Stage 1)

### What this is

The canonical artifact graph (`planv0.md` line 1156): frozen canonical tensors only, explicit quant formats, explicit norm plans, explicit sequence-state semantics, explicit decode spec, explicit provenance back to exported tensor ids. **Not a training graph.** All weights are already frozen, all quant schemes are explicit. Physical packings, reorders, and bank-chunked lowerings are NOT here — those are `TargetDataLoweringArtifact`s. Tensor shapes, quant ranges, sequence-state semantics, routing tables, expert sections, decode plan all live here.

### Why this matters

Every later pass operates on `QuantGraph`. Getting the boundary right here (no concrete bytes, no scheduling) is what lets `ArtifactOracle` (Epic C F-C2) run against the same graph the compiler sees.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct QuantGraph {
    pub tensors: Vec<QuantTensorRef>,           // CanonicalTensor + quant format
    pub norm_plans: Vec<NormPlan>,
    pub routing_table: Option<RoutingTable>,
    pub expert_sections: Vec<ExpertSection>,
    pub decode_spec: DecodeSpec,
    pub sequence_semantics: SequenceSemanticsSpec,    // from F12 (bd-144)
    pub provenance: BTreeMap<TensorId, ExportTensorId>,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B0 (validated artifact)
- gbf-artifact ArtifactCore types
- F12 / bd-ctl SequenceSemanticsSpec

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T-A1.4: MachineEffect + PrivilegeClass typing on instructions/sections
description: Recent revision pass addition (planv0.md correction 9): MachineEffect classifies each instruction's side effect (LoadFromBank0/Switchable/Wram/Hram/Sram/Vram/Oam/Io, StoreToMbcRegister, InterruptControl, branches). PrivilegeClass = Normal | Privileged | InterruptHandler. ReachabilityValidation (Epic B) walks these annotations.
close_reason:
Implemented MachineEffect + PrivilegeClass typing for gbf-asm.

Summary:
- Added gbf-asm/src/effect.rs with explicit MachineEffect, MachineEffectKind, PrivilegeClass, memory-region, MBC register, interrupt-control, and pseudo-op classification APIs.
- Wired SectionPrivilege into Section with default Normal policy, explicit Privileged/InterruptHandler policies, allowed-effect allowlists, and revalidation when privilege changes after items exist.
- Added SectionItem::machine_effect so typed instructions, pseudo-ops, and raw bytes expose effect metadata. Raw bytes are modeled as OpaqueBytes and require privileged sections.
- Updated Builder to validate typed instruction, pseudo-op, and raw-byte effects at the emission boundary, while preserving try_* APIs for recoverable errors.
- Strengthened tests for stack effects, high-memory/full-address boundaries, mixed-region SP stores, MBC register ranges, ISR policy, raw-byte rejection, and privilege downgrade revalidation.

Review agents:
- Mode 1 Intent: flagged one-effect lossy framing, raw-byte bypass, underspecified section tagging, weak oracles, and privilege/context assumptions.
- Mode 2 Design: flagged mutable privilege not being an invariant, raw bypass, dynamic address writes treated as fixed-safe, singular effects, high-page store misclassification, and stale builder docs.
- Mode 3 Semantic: flagged conditional RET as branch instead of return, PUSH/POP as pure compute, two-byte/high-page LD (a16),SP issues, and raw bypass.
- Mode 4 Evidence: flagged missing closure evidence, weak builder-boundary proof, non-exhaustive effect fixture, missing MBC/ISR coverage.

Fixes applied from review:
- PUSH/POP now classify as stack store/load effects; conditional RET now classifies as Return.
- LD (a16),SP uses full 16-bit memory-map classification and reports mixed static regions across two-byte boundaries.
- MBC writes are table-tested across all MBC windows and remain Privileged.
- Raw bytes are OpaqueBytes, rejected in Normal sections, and tested.
- Section privilege updates revalidate existing items; downgrade rejection is tested.
- Dynamic-address effects expose requires_dynamic_address_proof() and are tested as a reachability obligation rather than a fixed-region proof.
- Builder docs now state local privilege/effect validation and leave dynamic-address proof to reachability.
- AGENTS.md ASM guidance updated via feedback-harness-reflection.

Feedback-harness reflection:
| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Stack-touching instructions marked PureCompute | Classifier grouped register and stack opcodes too broadly | Effect classifiers must not collapse stack-touching instructions into pure compute | effect::classify_exhaustive covers PUSH/POP stack effects | bd-1bw / cargo test -p gbf-asm -- effect::classify_exhaustive |
| Raw bytes bypassed privilege validation | Raw escape hatch was outside SectionItem effect model | Raw bytes are opaque privileged effects unless explicitly narrowed | builder::builder_rejects_raw_bytes_in_normal_sections | bd-1bw / cargo test -p gbf-asm -- builder::builder_rejects_raw_bytes_in_normal_sections |
| Section privilege could be downgraded after privileged emission | Privilege setter did not revalidate existing items | Section privilege is a durable invariant | builder::builder_revalidates_existing_items_when_privilege_changes | bd-1bw / cargo test -p gbf-asm -- builder::builder_revalidates_existing_items_when_privilege_changes |
| High-page and two-byte SP stores misclassified | DirectAddr helper was reused for full u16 addresses | Full-address classifiers must test memory-map boundaries and cross-region stores | effect::classify_exhaustive includes $FF80 and $9FFF cases | bd-1bw / cargo test -p gbf-asm -- effect::classify_exhaustive |
| Dynamic writes looked safe rather than unresolved | Dynamic address variants lacked a named proof obligation | Dynamic-address effects must name reachability obligations | effect::dynamic_memory_effects_name_reachability_obligation | bd-1bw / cargo test -p gbf-asm -- effect::dynamic_memory_effects_name_reachability_obligation |
| MBC/ISR policy under-tested | Tests pinned one MBC address and no ISR behavior | MBC and ISR policy need table/policy tests | effect::mbc_writes_are_privileged; section::privilege_inheritance | bd-1bw acceptance gates |

Non-goals / moved boundaries:
- This bead does not prove concrete dynamic-address residency; ReachabilityValidation in Epic B consumes requires_dynamic_address_proof() effects.
- This bead does not assign product-level runtime/ISR section tags; F-A4/bd-371 owners consume SectionPrivilege for BankLease/BankGuard/runtime sections.
- This bead does not lower pseudo-ops, far-call thunks, relocation, branch relaxation, or final bytes.
- Db/Dw remain data declarations with no machine effect. Raw bytes are the only opaque executable-byte escape hatch and now require privileged sections.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A1.4: `MachineEffect` + `PrivilegeClass` typing

**Target crate**: `gbf-asm`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-asm/src/section.rs` (or new `effect.rs` re-exported from `section.rs`)
**Reference**: `planv0.md` §"AsmIR" line 1500 + recent revision pass note ("(9) add `MachineEffect` and `PrivilegeClass` typing to `gbf-asm` so `ReachabilityValidation` validates against explicit intent").

### Background

`ReachabilityValidation` (Epic B Stage 12 sub-pass) needs to compute residency / privilege classes over `AsmIR`. It must be able to ask: *"does this instruction have a side effect that's only legal from a non-ISR context?"* and *"does this load depend on a switchable bank?"* These are properties of operations, not of programs. They have to live on the instruction-level types.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub enum MachineEffect {
    PureCompute,                          // ALU, register-only
    LoadFromBank0,                        // load addressed in fixed Bank 0
    LoadFromSwitchableRom,                // load through $4000-$7FFF
    LoadFromWram,
    LoadFromHram,
    LoadFromSwitchableSram,               // through $A000-$BFFF
    LoadFromVram,
    LoadFromOam,
    LoadFromIo { reg: IoRegister },
    StoreToWram,
    StoreToHram,
    StoreToSwitchableSram,
    StoreToVram,
    StoreToOam,
    StoreToIo { reg: IoRegister },
    StoreToMbcRegister { reg: MbcRegisterClass },   // **privileged**
    InterruptControl(InterruptControlOp),            // EI, DI, HALT, STOP
    UnconditionalBranch,
    ConditionalBranch,
    Call,
    Return,
    Reti,
    Rst { vector: RstVector },
    SystemCall(SystemCallKind),                       // far-call, yield, panic
}

pub enum PrivilegeClass {
    Normal,
    Privileged,                                       // legal only via BankLease/BankGuard ABI
    InterruptHandler,                                 // legal from ISR context
}

pub fn classify_effect(instr: &Instr) -> MachineEffect;
pub fn privilege_of(effect: &MachineEffect) -> PrivilegeClass;
```

Section-level annotations:

```rust
pub struct SectionPrivilege {
    pub default_privilege: PrivilegeClass,
    pub allows_interrupt_disabled: bool,
    pub allowed_effects: Option<BTreeSet<MachineEffectKind>>,    // None = unrestricted
}
```

`gbf-runtime::banking` (F-A4) sections are tagged `default_privilege = Privileged`. Generated inference code is `Normal`. ISR sections are `InterruptHandler`.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-asm
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- effect::classify_exhaustive       # Every Instr variant has a MachineEffect
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- effect::mbc_writes_are_privileged  # StoreToMbcRegister maps to Privileged
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- section::privilege_inheritance     # Section privilege is inherited unless overridden
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `PrivilegeClass` is exhaustive; ReachabilityValidation can rely on a closed set.
- §III.1: Privilege violations become compile errors at Builder boundaries (Builder rejects emitting a `Privileged` op into a `Normal` section).

title: T5.6: Loss term config with sensible defaults and logging
description: All loss terms config-gated in training TOML. Default config for Phase B onward: lambda_balance=0.01, lambda_zrouter=0.001, all others 0.0 until M6. Each loss term logged separately to metrics (not just the total loss). Training dashboard should show: lm_loss, distill_loss, balance_loss, zrouter_loss, switch_loss, range_loss, zero_loss individually.

title: T-A5.2: Cooperative scheduler with TIMA + yield_requested HRAM flag
description: Main scheduler loop. Arms TIMA before slice dispatch; Timer ISR sets HRAM yield_requested=1; generated code polls at safe points (compiler-emitted via Yield pseudo-op); on yield save continuation, run UI work, sleep until next frame. Bumps liveness counters; raises LivelockDetected past threshold.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A5.2: Cooperative scheduler with TIMA + yield_requested HRAM flag

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-runtime/src/scheduler.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 1790–1796 (yield mechanism + liveness).

### Background

Inference runs cooperatively with UI. The scheduler:
1. Arms TIMA before dispatching a slice (or relies on the regular timer interrupt).
2. The Timer ISR (via T-A5.1) sets `HRAM[HRAM_OFF_YIELD_REQUESTED] = 1`.
3. Generated inference code polls that byte at safe points (compiler-inserted via the `Yield` pseudo-op).
4. On observed flag: code saves continuation, returns to scheduler.
5. Scheduler runs UI work (joypad read, video commit, text), then either resumes inference or sleeps until next frame.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
/// Build the scheduler section. Includes:
/// - main scheduler loop (Bank 0)
/// - TIMA arming helper
/// - yield handling (saves continuation pointer to InferenceState)
/// - resume entry (from continuation)
pub fn build_scheduler_section() -> Section;

/// Helper for compiler-emitted code: emit AsmIR that polls HRAM yield flag and falls through to a
/// save-and-yield sequence if set.
pub fn emit_yield_check<B: Builder>(b: &mut B, kind: YieldKind);

pub struct SchedulerPolicy {
    pub frame_budget_m_cycles: u32,                // <= FRAME_M_CYCLES from gbf-hw
    pub max_interrupt_latency_m_cycles: u16,
    pub timer_period_m_cycles: u16,                 // TIMA reload period
    pub default_yield_kind: YieldKind,
}
```

The scheduler must satisfy:
- Frame budget includes UI work — typically ~14000 M-cycles for inference and ~3000 for UI per 17556-cycle frame.
- TIMA period chosen so soft deadlines arrive every ~500 M-cycles inside a slice (configurable).
- After a yield, scheduler re-checks `yield_requested` is cleared; bug if not.

Liveness: scheduler bumps `LivenessCounters::no_progress_frames` if no checkpoint advance occurs in a frame; raises `FaultCode::LivelockDetected` past threshold (via `FaultPolicy`).

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-runtime
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- scheduler::yield_round_trip            # Build a tiny program that yields, run on emu, assert resume
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- scheduler::tima_arming
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- scheduler::livelock_detection           # Frame counter bumps; threshold fires
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- scheduler::max_interrupt_latency        # Critical sections respect bound
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: SchedulerPolicy is typed.
- §V.3: Liveness fault structured-logged with reason.

title: T-A5.6: video_commit + UiCommitPlan — sole VRAM/OAM writer
description: UiCommitPlan + commit queue + LCD-mode-gated drain at HBlank/VBlank ISR. UiCommitOp covers PutGlyph/SetTilePalette/PutOamSprite/ClearRegion. Illegal-mode-write attempts raise FaultCode::UiCommitOutsideLegalMode.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A5.6: `video_commit` + `UiCommitPlan`

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-runtime/src/video_commit.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 117 ("VRAM accessible during modes 0, 1, 2; OAM during modes 0, 1"), line 1635 (video_commit module owns UiCommitPlan and the commit queue).

### Background

VRAM and OAM are only accessible during specific PPU modes. Writing during mode 3 (Drawing) corrupts the display. The `video_commit` module is the *single* writer to VRAM/OAM; all other modules (text, keyboard, panic) stage `UiCommitOp`s into the queue, and the commit module drains the queue at HBlank/VBlank/OAMSearch boundaries.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct UiCommitPlan {
    pub max_ops_per_frame: u16,
    pub max_bytes_per_hblank: u16,
    pub vblank_priority_ops: Vec<UiCommitOpKind>,
}

pub enum UiCommitOp {
    PutGlyph { x: u8, y: u8, glyph: u8 },
    SetTilePalette { ... },
    PutOamSprite { ... },
    ClearRegion { x: u8, y: u8, w: u8, h: u8 },
}

pub fn build_video_commit_section() -> Section;

pub fn emit_queue_op<B: Builder>(b: &mut B, op: UiCommitOp);

/// Emit the commit-drain helper that runs at HBlank/VBlank ISR.
pub fn emit_commit_drain<B: Builder>(b: &mut B);
```

The commit queue lives in WRAM as a small ring buffer (~16-32 ops). `emit_commit_drain` is called from the LCD STAT ISR (HBlank handler) and the VBlank ISR. It checks `STAT_REG`'s mode bits, dequeues an op, executes it. If a queued op would be illegal in the current mode, it's deferred to the next.

`UiCommitPlan` is a build-time policy (which kinds of ops prefer VBlank vs HBlank). For v1, default policy: PutGlyph at HBlank (small writes), bulk ClearRegion at VBlank.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-runtime
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- video_commit::queue_full_handling
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- video_commit::no_writes_in_mode_3       # Verify drain code path checks mode
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- video_commit::vblank_priority            # vblank_priority_ops fire first
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: UiCommitOp enumerated.
- §VI.1: Sole VRAM/OAM writer.
- §V.3: Illegal-mode-write attempt becomes FaultCode::UiCommitOutsideLegalMode.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A1.2: Section, provenance, symbol table

**Target crate**: `gbf-asm`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-asm/src/section.rs`, `gbf-asm/src/provenance.rs`, `gbf-asm/src/symbols.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` §"Assembly eDSL" (line 2046), §"Engineering rules" rule 3 (every instruction and data directive carries provenance).

### Background

Sections are the named ROM/RAM regions that the layout pass places into banks. Provenance answers "which compiler stage emitted this byte?" — a debugging aid that is non-negotiable per the engineering rules. Symbols are the labels the linker resolves and the harness uses (rule 7: "harness uses symbols and `SemanticCheckpointId`s, not magic addresses").

### Implementation Notes

`section.rs`:

```rust
pub enum SectionRole {
    Bank0Nucleus,           // boot, ISRs, scheduler, UI
    CommonBank,             // co-resident shared kernels + data
    ExpertBank,             // expert-local code/data
    WramHotArena,           // ArenaPlan-managed
    WramOverlay,            // OverlayPlan-managed
    HramFastFlags,
    SramPersistent,
    VramOwnedByUi,
    OamOwnedByUi,
    HeaderCartridge,
}

pub struct SectionId(u32);   // monotone ids assigned by host

pub struct Section {
    pub id: SectionId,
    pub role: SectionRole,
    pub name: SymbolName,
    pub items: Vec<SectionItem>,         // Instr or Db/Dw with provenance
    pub align: u16,
    pub size_hint_bytes: Option<u32>,
}
```

`provenance.rs`:

```rust
pub struct InstrProvenance {
    pub stage: PlanningStage,           // QuantGraph | StoragePlan | RomWindowPlan | OverlayPlan | ArenaPlan | GbSchedIR | Backend
    pub source_node: Option<ValueId>,
    pub source_op: Option<&'static str>,
    pub note: Option<&'static str>,
}
```

`PlanningStage` is the same enum used in `RepairProposal::source` (Epic B). Define it here; Epic B re-uses.

`symbols.rs`:

```rust
pub struct SymbolName(pub Cow<'static, str>);
pub struct SymbolTable { ... }            // resolves names <-> addresses post-layout
```

Provide a stable canonical naming scheme: `kernel.<family>.<id>`, `expert.<layer>.<id>`, `runtime.<module>.<sym>`, etc.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-asm
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- section::role_exhaustive            # All planv0.md residency classes are covered
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- provenance::stage_enum_stable        # PlanningStage ordering is stable across versions
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- symbols::canonical_naming            # No two distinct symbols share a canonical name
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `SectionRole` enum is exhaustive; later passes match on it and a missing variant is a compile error.
- §VI.1: Provenance is recorded structurally, not as a free-text comment.

title: T-A6.3: gbf-store — pinsets + GC
description: Named Pinsets (latest builds, current calibration cohort, etc). Transitive GC walks pinsets, follows BlobReferences trait for outgoing refs, removes unreferenced. dry_run + max_remove_per_run for safety. GcReport is JSON.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A6.3: `gbf-store` — pinsets + GC

**Target crate**: `gbf-store`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-store/src/pinset.rs`, `gbf-store/src/gc.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 138 (pinsets/GC/eviction).

### Background

Without GC, the blob store grows without bound. Pinsets are named sets of blobs that survive GC (e.g., "the latest 5 builds", "the current calibration cohort"). GC walks pinsets, collects all referenced blobs (transitively, since blobs can reference other blobs by hash), and removes the unreferenced.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct PinsetName(pub Cow<'static, str>);

pub struct Pinset {
    pub name: PinsetName,
    pub roots: BTreeSet<Hash256>,
    pub annotation: Option<String>,
}

pub trait BlobReferences {
    fn referenced_blobs(&self) -> Vec<Hash256>;             // transitive references inside this blob's logical content
}

pub struct GcOptions {
    pub dry_run: bool,
    pub max_remove_per_run: Option<usize>,
}

pub struct GcReport {
    pub pinsets_walked: u32,
    pub blobs_kept: u32,
    pub blobs_removed: u32,
    pub bytes_freed: u64,
    pub removed: Vec<Hash256>,
}

pub fn run_gc(store: &BlobStore, pinsets: &[Pinset], opts: &GcOptions) -> GcReport;
```

The transitive reachability requires every blob format to implement `BlobReferences`. For unknown blob types, conservatively treat as "no outgoing refs" (won't pull dependent blobs into the keep set, but won't crash either).

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-store
cargo test -p gbf-store -- gc::pinned_blobs_survive
cargo test -p gbf-store -- gc::transitive_refs                    # A pinned blob's refs are kept
cargo test -p gbf-store -- gc::dry_run_removes_nothing
cargo test -p gbf-store -- gc::report_counts_correct
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §V.1: GcReport is structured JSON.
- §I.1: Pinset is typed.

title: T8.3: Implement shadow compile pipeline
description: Shadow compile: (1) export EMA weights to temp ModelArtifact, (2) for each CompileRequest in ShadowCompilePolicy.requests: run ArtifactValidation -> StaticBudgetReport -> (optionally full compile), (3) for each workload: run ArtifactOracle on a few prompts, compare against ReferenceModelBundle, (4) if full compile: run ScheduleCostAnalysis. StaticBudgetReport alone catches most fit failures. Full compile is config-gated because it's expensive. Output: one CheckpointFrontierPoint per checkpoint.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- shadow::pipeline passes; Produces CheckpointFrontierPoint; Budget failure caught without crash; Timeout works

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/shadow.rs` (extend with pipeline)

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "shadow compile pipeline", "ArtifactValidation", "StaticBudgetReport", "conformance".

### Implementation Notes
Shadow compile pipeline (runs at intervals defined by ShadowCompilePolicy):
1. Export EMA weights via ExportVisitor -> ModelArtifact
2. Run ArtifactValidation (schema check)
3. Run StaticBudgetReport (does it fit?)
4. Optionally: full compile (if budget permits and compile is fast enough)
5. Run small conformance suite on specified workloads
6. Output: `CheckpointFrontierPoint` with quality/conformance/fit metrics

- Pipeline should be runnable both inline (blocking training) and async (background thread).
- Timeout: if shadow compile takes >5 minutes, abort and log warning.
- Output goes to `shadow_results/step_{N}/` directory.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- shadow::pipeline     # Tests pass
# Verify: pipeline produces CheckpointFrontierPoint
# Verify: budget failure is caught (fits=false) without crashing training
# Verify: timeout works
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §II.1: Shadow compile must not add >5min latency to training
- §V.1: All pipeline steps emit structured log events
- §V.2: Shadow compile is crash-safe — partial results are fine, training continues

title: F-B15: StageCache integration across all stages
description: Wire every transformative compiler stage into the two-level StageCache from gbf-store. Each stages output is content-addressed; --resume-from layered on top. Mostly mechanical wrapper work once individual stages exist.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B15: StageCache integration across all stages

### What this is

Wire every transformative compiler stage into the two-level `StageCache` from `gbf-store` (`planv0.md` engineering rule 20). Each stage's input includes a stable hash of its predecessors and inputs; output is content-addressed and stored. `--resume-from <stage>` is layered on top.

### Why this matters

Iteration speed. A typical training run shadow-compiles dozens of checkpoints; without stage caching, each does the full pipeline. With caching, only the parts that actually changed re-run.

### Design sketch

For each stage:
1. Compute `StageKey` from typed inputs (no untyped `Display` / `format!`-based hashing).
2. `StageCache::get(key)` — return cached output if present.
3. Run the stage.
4. `StageCache::put(key, output)`.

Stage-by-stage integration; each pass implementer adds the caching wrapper.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A6.2 StageCache (infrastructure)
- F-B0..F-B13 (each stage individually)

### Confidence: HIGH. Mostly mechanical wrapper work once stages exist.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime` (or wherever runtime shell build lives)
**Target file(s)**: Runtime build pipeline — search for runtime/UI shell assembly code. Output: `chrome_budget.json`.

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "runtime shell", "chrome budget", "Bank0", "RuntimeNucleus".

### Implementation Notes
- After the runtime/UI shell is assembled (boot, interrupts, scheduler, joypad, text, keyboard, video_commit, panic, trace, harness, persistence modules), measure how much ROM space each bank class has consumed.
- Emit a `RuntimeChromeBudget` artifact as `chrome_budget.json`:
  - Bank0Free: bytes remaining in bank 0 after runtime nucleus
  - CommonBank: bytes remaining in each common bank after shared kernels/embeddings
  - ExpertBank: full capacity of each expert bank (minus bank header/stubs)
  - wram_reserved: WRAM bytes used by runtime
  - sram_reserved: SRAM bytes used by runtime
- This is the **build-specific** budget that the trainer needs for preflight.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check --workspace                           # Compiles
# Verify: chrome_budget.json is emitted after runtime build
# Verify: budget reflects actual runtime nucleus size, not hardcoded values
# Verify: JSON deserializes into RuntimeChromeBudget from gbf-policy
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §V.1: Structured JSON output, not human-readable text
- §IV.3: Reproducible — same runtime code produces same budget

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: EXTERNAL DEPENDENCY WARNING: This task requires the runtime/UI shell (gbf-runtime) to be assembled first, which is a separate subsystem not tracked in the training-contract beads. Options: (1) Stub with a hardcoded chrome_budget.json for testing, implement real emission when gbf-runtime is ready. (2) Defer until gbf-runtime work is scoped. Recommend option 1 — create a mock/fixture budget for now.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Refinement from 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session

### Reference shell composition (pinned)

The first `RuntimeChromeBudget` emitted from a real runtime shell build must use the **minimal + UI** module set:

- Included: `boot`, `interrupts`, `scheduler`, `banking`, `joypad`, `text`, `keyboard`, `video_commit`.
- Excluded (do not link into the reference shell): `persistence`, `trace`, `harness`, `panic`.

This matches the configuration the trainer pins against (see F2 / bd-1hv decisions and T2.4 `ReferenceShellSpec`). The emitter records the included module set in `RuntimeChromeBudget.reference_shell_modules` (see T2.1 refinement comment) so drift is detectable.

### Hash contract

`runtime_nucleus_hash` covers:
- Linked module set (`reference_shell_modules`).
- Encoded section bytes for Bank0 nucleus (boot + ISR + scheduler + banking + UI + text + keyboard + video_commit).
- `CompileProfile` id (so a profile change forces a new hash).
- The build's `gbf-runtime` source version stamp.

The hash must match what `KernelCalibrationBundle.runtime_nucleus_hash` records, otherwise calibration is invalidated against this budget.

### Slack accounting

`RomBudgetSlot.reserved_slack` for Bank0Free includes a named reservation for each future module the reference shell does not yet ship (`persistence`, `trace`, `harness`, `panic`). T2.4 defines those reserved byte counts. The emitter must subtract them from `usable_bytes` and record the breakdown in `BuildReports` so it is auditable.

### Pre-runtime: synthetic budget path

Until the real runtime shell exists, training uses a synthetic reference `RuntimeChromeBudget` (T2.6) under a stable, explicitly-marked hash. T2.2 (this task) is the *real* emission path; T2.6 is the pre-runtime stand-in. The two must produce the same `RuntimeChromeBudget` schema so trainer code is not branchy.

### EXTERNAL DEPENDENCY note (unchanged)

This task still requires the runtime/UI shell to exist. The earlier comment's option (1) "stub with hardcoded chrome_budget.json" is now formalized as T2.6 (synthetic reference budget) and is no longer ad hoc.

title: T-A1.9: ROM builder — .gb assembler with cartridge header
description: Assembles encoded sections into a real .gb file: cartridge header at 0x100-0x14F (Nintendo logo, MBC type, ROM/RAM size, header + global checksums), ROM padded to power-of-two, bank packing (bank N at N*0x4000). Output must boot in SameBoy/BGB.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A1.9: ROM builder — `.gb` assembler with cartridge header

**Target crate**: `gbf-asm`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-asm/src/rom.rs` (new)
**Reference**: `planv0.md` §"EncodedRom" (line 1565), Pan Docs cartridge header layout.

### Background

The output of M0 needs to be **a real `.gb` file** that boots in SameBoy/BGB. That requires:
1. A valid cartridge header at `$0100–$014F` (Nintendo logo, title, MBC type, ROM size, RAM size, header checksum, global checksum).
2. ROM padded to a power-of-two size (32 KiB minimum, MBC5 supports up to 8 MiB).
3. Bank packing — each `PlacedSection` written at its bank's offset within the ROM blob.

This task is small (mostly transcription) but load-bearing: every M0 demo and every later compiled ROM goes through it.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct CartridgeHeader {
    pub title: [u8; 16],                      // padded with zeros
    pub mbc_type: MbcType,                    // MBC5 for our targets
    pub rom_size_kib: u32,                    // 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192
    pub ram_size_kib: u32,                    // 0, 8, 32, 128, 256
    pub destination_code: u8,
    pub old_licensee_code: u8,                // 0x33 — use new licensee
    pub new_licensee_code: [u8; 2],
    pub mask_rom_version: u8,
}

pub fn assemble_rom(
    encoded_sections: &[(EncodedSection, PlacedSection)],
    header: &CartridgeHeader,
    target: TargetProfileId,
) -> Result<Vec<u8>, RomAssemblyError>;
```

Header checksum (`$014D`) is the standard `(x = 0; for i in $0134..=$014C: x = x - rom[i] - 1) & 0xFF`. Global checksum at `$014E–$014F` is bigendian sum of all bytes except those two.

The Nintendo logo at `$0104–$0133` is fixed bytes (well-known). Hardcode it.

Sections placed in bank 0 go to `0x0000..=0x3FFF` of the rom blob. Sections in bank N go to `N * 0x4000`. Unused regions are filled with `0xFF` (canonical).

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-asm
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- rom::header_checksum                    # Matches Pan Docs algorithm
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- rom::global_checksum
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- rom::power_of_two_size                  # ROM size always 2^n
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- rom::nintendo_logo_present              # Bytes at $0104-$0133 are correct
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- rom::section_placement                  # Section in bank N appears at N*0x4000
cargo run -p gbf-asm --example tiny_rom                          # Produces a .gb that boots in SameBoy
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `MbcType` and `RomAssemblyError` are enumerated; bad inputs fail loudly.
- §IV.3: Bit-stability — same inputs -> identical .gb bytes.

title: F2: RuntimeChromeBudget — Build-Specific Bank Capacity Contract
description: Define RuntimeChromeBudget/RomBudgetSlot/BudgetSlotClass so the trainer reasons about actual free bank capacity after the UI/runtime shell build, not a stale 16 KiB constant. Wire into gbf-train preflight alongside DeployabilityEnvelope.
acceptance_criteria: All T2.x tasks closed; cargo test -p gbf-policy -- budget passes; cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight passes

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F2: RuntimeChromeBudget — Build-Specific Bank Capacity Contract

### Background

The plan repeatedly talks about "bank-sized experts" and "15.5 KiB budget" but this number is a hand-waved approximation. In reality, the free space in each bank class depends on the specific runtime shell build: how much Bank0 space the scheduler/ISR/UI/keyboard/text renderer/video commit queue consume, how much common-bank space embeddings/router/classifier/shared LUTs occupy, and how much expert-bank slack is reserved for stubs and metadata.

### The problem

Without RuntimeChromeBudget, the trainer has two bad options:
1. Use a static constant (e.g., 15.5 KiB) that's wrong for every actual build
2. Parse the build's map.json to reverse-engineer available space

Both are fragile. The first silently produces experts that don't fit. The second couples the trainer to the compiler's output format.

### Design

RuntimeChromeBudget is emitted as a structured artifact from the runtime/UI shell build. It contains:
- `target` and `profile` (so the trainer knows which build this is for)
- `runtime_nucleus_hash` (so stale budgets are detectable)
- `rom_slots`: a Vec of `RomBudgetSlot`, each describing a bank class (Bank0Free, CommonBank, ExpertBank) with `usable_bytes`, `reserved_slack`, and supported `placement_caps`
- `wram_reserved` and `sram_reserved` for hot/persistent memory budgets

The trainer's preflight checks expert payload sizes against the smallest ExpertBank slot's usable_bytes, not a hardcoded constant. This automatically adapts as the runtime shell grows or shrinks.

### Why "chrome" in the name

"Chrome" is the browser-industry term for the UI frame around content. Here it means: the runtime shell is the chrome, and the model inference is the content. The budget tells you how much room the content has after the chrome is installed.

### Confidence: HIGH

This is a clean, simple contract that prevents a real failure mode. The only reservation is timing — you need a working runtime shell build before you can emit it, so this becomes practical around M2-M3.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Architecture decisions from 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session

The model/runtime interface for respecting the GB memory map was reviewed against planv0.md. Four decisions were committed; this comment records them so future agents do not re-litigate.

### 1. Reference runtime shell composition (pinned for pre-training)

The shell used to emit the canonical pre-training `RuntimeChromeBudget` is **minimal + UI**:

- Included: `boot`, `interrupts`, `scheduler`, `banking`, `joypad`, `text`, `keyboard`, `video_commit`.
- **Excluded** from the reference shell: `persistence`, `trace`, `harness`, `panic`.

When any excluded module ships in production, `RuntimeChromeBudget` changes — Bank0Free shrinks, `wram_reserved` and `sram_reserved` grow. Models trained against the current reference shell must be re-preflighted (or retrained) at that point. This is captured in T2.4 (`ReferenceShellSpec` with explicit module set) and T2.5 (CI drift gate on `runtime_nucleus_hash`).

### 2. Slack reservation policy

`reserved_slack` and `wram_reserved` / `sram_reserved` carry **named** reservations for known-future modules so the budget does not silently shift when those modules land. T2.4 defines `ReferenceShellSpec` with a per-module slack table covering `persistence`, `trace`, `harness`, `panic`. The shell emitter records "future-reserved" bytes alongside actually-consumed bytes; preflight subtracts both.

This is option (a) of "reserve named slack now" vs "retrain on shell expansion." The intent is to absorb at least one shell expansion without invalidating training.

### 3. Preflight cadence

Two checkpoints, both required:

- **Pre-training pin.** Trainer captures `runtime_nucleus_hash` from the chosen reference `RuntimeChromeBudget` at training start. Drift mid-training is a hard failure (T2.5 — CI gate).
- **Per-export re-validate.** Export pipeline re-runs preflight against the *current* `RuntimeChromeBudget`. If the production shell has drifted from the training-time reference, export refuses with diagnostic listing both hashes (T2.7).

Pre-runtime training is supported via a deterministic synthetic reference budget under a stable, marked hash (T2.6) so F2 is useful at M1–M2 before a real runtime shell exists.

### 4. `runtime_nucleus_hash` is load-bearing

The hash field in `RuntimeChromeBudget` is the single drift detector between training-time and production-time. Any change to the runtime nucleus binary (or its config) bumps the hash. Drift is gated by CI (T2.5), not informally tracked.

### Cross-references

- WRAM layout (fixed overlay region + ArenaPlan-managed remainder) and the BringUp `CompileProfile` (with hard switch cap) are tracked under F11.
- Sequence-state experimentation (LinearState vs BoundedKv on the same training scaffold) is tracked under F12.
- `wram_reserved: u16` becomes structured `WramReserved { overlay, hot_arena_floor, total }` — see T11.1 and the comment on T2.1.

title: F11: CompileProfile + WRAM Layout — BringUp, Overlay Reload, Switch Cap
description: Define the CompileProfile registry and the WRAM layout contract: WramLayoutPolicy (fixed overlay region + ArenaPlan-managed remainder), OverlayReloadPolicy (PerExpertSwitch default), and the named BringUp profile with a hard max_bank_switches_per_token cap. Production profiles are deferred until F12 sequence-state experimentation resolves.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F11: CompileProfile + WRAM Layout Contract

### Background

The plan (planv0.md) repeatedly refers to `CompilePreferences`, `BuildConstraints`, and a "WRAM hot arena" without ever pinning down the *layout contract* that bounds them. Two specific gaps surfaced in the 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session:

1. The default residency strategy for expert hot kernels is **WRAM overlay** (per-expert-switch reload). Overlay competes with the hot arena for 8 KiB of WRAM, so the split must be a contract, not a vibe.
2. There is no named `CompileProfile` in the codebase. Preflight, switch-cap enforcement, and policy gating all need named profiles to refer to. Decision from the session: define **`BringUp` only for now**; production profiles wait until F12 (sequence-state experimentation) resolves.

### The problem this solves

Without F11:
- Preflight can't tell the trainer how much WRAM is actually free for the activation/accumulator/scratch arena, because overlay size is implicit.
- The "hard switch cap per profile" decision has nowhere to live. `max_bank_switches_per_token` exists as a `BuildConstraints` field (planv0.md) but is not bound to a named profile.
- `OverlayPlan` (planv0.md compiler-pipeline section 8.5) has no input shape — the compiler can't know how big to size the overlay region.

### Design

Add three policy types to `gbf-policy`:

```rust
pub struct WramLayoutPolicy {
    pub overlay_bytes: u16,
    pub continuation_bytes: u16,
    pub stack_bytes: u16,
    pub hot_arena_bytes_min: u16,
}

pub enum OverlayReloadPolicy {
    PerExpertSwitch,  // default
    PerSlice,
    PerLayerStep,
}

pub struct CompileProfile {
    pub id: CompileProfileId,
    pub wram_layout: WramLayoutPolicy,
    pub overlay_reload: OverlayReloadPolicy,
    pub max_bank_switches_per_token: u16,
    pub sequence_state: SequenceSemanticsRef,
}
```

`RuntimeChromeBudget.wram_reserved` is restructured into `WramReserved { overlay, hot_arena_floor, total }` so preflight can subtract overlay independently from runtime-internal use.

`OverlayPlan` (T11.4) becomes the IR slot that consumes `WramLayoutPolicy.overlay_bytes` and produces a residents list.

### Decisions committed in 2026-04-26 session

- WRAM overlay is the **default** expert-kernel residency strategy.
- Overlay reload policy: **per expert switch**.
- WRAM split: **fixed overlay (per profile constant) + computed remainder via `ArenaPlan`**.
- Switch cap: **hard per-profile constraint**, not just a soft training prior.
- Named profiles: **`BringUp` only**; production profiles deferred until F12 settles.

### Confidence: HIGH for types, MEDIUM for BringUp constants

The type design is straightforward and tightly constrained by F2's existing budget contract. The concrete `BringUp` constants (overlay byte size, switch cap value) are starting hand-picked numbers that calibration (gbf-bench) and shadow_compile data will refine. Provenance must be recorded in T11.2's profile definition.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T11.1–T11.4.

**Target crate(s)**: `gbf-policy` (types + BringUp), `gbf-train` (preflight integration), `gbf-codegen` (OverlayPlan stub).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "OverlayPlan", "WRAM hot arena", "WRAM overlay", "CompileProfile", "max_bank_switches_per_token". Also see the 2026-04-26 session comment on bd-1hv.

### Feature Definition of Done

All child tasks (T11.1–T11.4) closed AND:
```bash
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- profile               # CompileProfile + BringUp tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- wram_layout            # WramLayoutPolicy round-trip tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight::wram         # WRAM preflight checks pass
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- overlay_plan          # OverlayPlan IR slot exists
```

title: T10.6a: Standard loss term gradient flow tests (z-loss, balance, range, zero)
description:
Per-loss-term gradient flow verification for the standard (non-temporal) loss terms:

**For each of: λ_balance, λ_zrouter, λ_range, λ_zero:**
- Create a tiny model + synthetic input where the term should produce non-zero loss
- Compute the loss term in isolation (all other λ=0)
- Backward pass
- Verify: gradient is non-zero on the expected parameters
- Verify: gradient is zero on parameters the term should NOT affect
- Log: term name, loss value, grad norm per parameter group

**Specific gradient flow tests:**
- λ_balance: gradient should flow only through router logits, NOT through expert weights
- λ_zrouter: gradient should flow only through router logits
- λ_range: gradient through activations at expert boundaries, proportional to distance outside safe range
- λ_zero: gradient through pre-threshold expert weights, proportional to absolute value (L1-style)

**Independence test:**
- Enable each term one at a time, verify total loss equals that term
- Enable all four terms, verify total loss equals sum (linearity)

**Numerical stability tests:**
- Very large router logits (>100): verify z-loss doesn't overflow
- Very small expert usage (one expert gets 99%): verify balance loss is finite and large
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- loss_tests passes; Each loss term has gradient flow test; No NaN/Inf on extreme inputs; Independence verified

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-test`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-test/tests/loss_tests.rs`

**Reference**: Bead descriptions for T5.2 (bd-1b3), T5.3 (bd-1p3), T5.4 (bd-11s).

### Implementation Notes
Per-loss-term gradient flow verification:
- **z-loss**: gradient w.r.t. router logits is non-zero, correct direction (reduces logit magnitude)
- **load-balance loss**: gradient encourages uniform routing
- **activation range penalty**: zero gradient within bound, non-zero outside
- **ternary zero regularizer**: gradient pushes weights toward 0 or ±scale

Also:
- **Independence test**: each loss term's gradient only affects its expected parameters
- **Numerical stability tests**: extreme inputs (very large/small values) don't produce NaN/Inf

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo test -p gbf-test -- loss_tests             # All pass
# Verify: each loss term has gradient flow test
# Verify: no NaN/Inf on extreme inputs
# Verify: independence — z-loss gradient doesn't affect weights, only router logits
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §III.2: Gradient flow tests are the unit-level verification for loss terms
- §I.4: Verifiable — gradient is non-zero (or zero) as expected, exit 0/1

title: T12.3: Implement BoundedKv sequence block under SequenceSemanticsSpec
description: Implement causal bounded-KV sequence block satisfying SequenceBlock. KV slab paged through SRAM via SramPagePlan; hot tiles WRAM-resident. QAT-compatible numerics matching LinearState.
