comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/sequence/bounded_kv.rs` (new).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "BoundedKv", "bounded-KV causal", "SramPagePlan". Also F12 (bd-144) and T12.1 (bd-ctl).

### Implementation Notes

Implement a `BoundedKvBlock` that satisfies `SequenceBlock`:

- **State shape**: KV slab sized by `(max_context, kv_bytes_per_token)`. Grows with token position up to `max_context`; bounded above.
- **Update rule**: causal attention with bounded KV (sliding-window or fixed-context truncation). Per-token compute: O(context).
- **Deploy memory**: KV slab is paged through SRAM via `SramPagePlan` (planv0.md compiler-pipeline section 7). Hot tiles get copied into WRAM, worked on there, written back at commit boundaries. This block must not assume the whole KV fits in WRAM.
- **Numerics**: same QAT compatibility as LinearState. Use F1 QAT modules at expert input/output, residual boundaries, and deployable norm.
- **Export facts**: populate `SequenceExportFacts` with `bytes_per_token = kv_bytes_per_token`, `bytes_per_layer = max_context * kv_bytes_per_token`, and the SequenceSemanticsSpec::BoundedKv variant.

### Loss / phase compatibility

The same loss terms (z-loss, balance, range, switch, distillation) and phase scheduler must work without code branching outside the block dispatch — this is the F12 contract. If a loss term needs sequence-state-specific behavior, it goes through a `SequenceBlock`-trait method, not a match statement on the variant.

### What "good enough for v1" looks like

- Tiny tests instantiate it with `max_context=32, kv_bytes_per_token=8`.
- Gradient flows through bounded attention (smoke test).
- ExportVisitor (T1.6) produces CanonicalTensors for its parameters.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                                   # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::bounded_kv             # Forward + grad smoke tests
cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence_block::bounded_kv       # Implements SequenceBlock trait
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings                    # No warnings
# Verify: state_size().bytes_per_token > 0 (state grows with tokens)
# Verify: state_size().bytes_per_layer == max_context * kv_bytes_per_token
# Verify: A tiny BoundedKvBlock instantiates under the same TrainPhaseSpec config as LinearState
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Implementing `SequenceBlock` enforces parity with LinearState at the type level.
- §III.1: Compile error if BoundedKv accidentally bypasses the SequenceBlock trait.
- §VI.1: Block lives in `gbf-model::sequence::bounded_kv`.

title: T3.1: Define TernaryWeightPlan types in gbf-artifact
description: Define TernaryWeightPlan, ScaleGranularity (PerTensor/PerOutputRow/PerGroup(u16)), ScaleFormat (Q8_8/Q4_4/Pow2), ThresholdPlan (FixedQ8_8/AnnealedGlobalThenPerOutputRow/LearnedPerGroup(u16)) in gbf-artifact as part of the QuantSpec extension. These are pure data types with serde support. Include a compute_byte_cost(&self, rows: u32, cols: u32) -> ByteCost method.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-artifact exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- weight_plan passes; compute_byte_cost returns correct values for PerTensor, PerOutputRow, PerGroup
close_reason: TernaryWeightPlan, scale/threshold plans, weight encodings, and byte-cost tests implemented in gbf-artifact.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-artifact`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-artifact/src/weight_plan.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "TernaryWeightPlan", "ScaleGranularity", "ScaleFormat", "ThresholdPlan", "compute_byte_cost".

### Implementation Notes
Define these types:
```rust
pub struct TernaryWeightPlan {
    pub encoding: WeightEncoding,
    pub scale_granularity: ScaleGranularity,
    pub scale_format: ScaleFormat,
    pub threshold: ThresholdPlan,
}

pub enum ScaleGranularity { PerTensor, PerOutputRow, PerGroup(u16) }
pub enum ScaleFormat { Q8_8, Q4_4, Pow2 }
pub enum ThresholdPlan { FixedQ8_8, AnnealedGlobalThenPerOutputRow, LearnedPerGroup(u16) }
```

Key method:
```rust
impl TernaryWeightPlan {
    pub fn compute_byte_cost(&self, rows: u32, cols: u32) -> ByteCost { ... }
}
```

Byte math (from the epic comment):
- Ternary2 base: `ceil(rows * cols / 4)` bytes (2 bits per weight)
- Scale overhead depends on granularity:
  - PerTensor: 2 bytes (one Q8_8 scale)
  - PerOutputRow: `rows * size_of(scale_format)` bytes
  - PerGroup(g): `ceil(rows * cols / g) * size_of(scale_format)` bytes
- Total = ternary_bytes + scale_bytes

Derive: `Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize`

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-artifact                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- weight_plan            # Unit tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-artifact -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: compute_byte_cost returns correct values for known inputs
# Test: PerOutputRow with 64 rows, 128 cols => ternary=2048, scales=128 (Q8_8=2 bytes each -> 64*2=128)
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: ScaleGranularity/ScaleFormat/ThresholdPlan as enums — no stringly-typed configs
- §III.2: Property tests for byte cost monotonicity (more rows/cols => more bytes)

title: Epic D: Runtime Beyond M0 — Persistence, Harness, Trace, Drift, Fault, SchedulePack
description: Runtime modules beyond the M0 reference shell: persistence (versioned SRAM record protocol), full harness control plane (StepSlice/RunUntilCheckpoint/DumpArena/InjectFault/PowerCut), trace pipeline, RuntimeDriftMonitor with automatic mode demotion, fault policy table + recovery + BootValidationPlan, SchedulePack mode switching. F-D6 multi-mode cadence is BLOCKED on oracle question.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Epic D: Runtime Beyond M0

### What this is

The runtime modules **not** in the M0 reference shell: persistence, harness control plane, trace pipeline, drift monitor, fault policy, and `SchedulePack` mode switching. These are the modules that turn the M0 cooperative skeleton into a production runtime.

### Why this matters

M0 (Epic A, F-A5) ships a runtime nucleus that boots and yields. M1 oracles + M2 first ROM run on it. But long-running interactive use needs:
- **Persistence** (so save state survives a power-cut).
- **Harness** (so tests can `StepSlice` / `RunUntilCheckpoint` / `InjectFault` / `PowerCut` from the host).
- **Trace** (so observability is structured and bounded).
- **Drift monitor** (so the runtime can demote modes under unexpected load).
- **Fault policy** (so faults trigger graded recovery rather than freezing).
- **SchedulePack mode switching** (so a build can carry multiple `RuntimeMode`s and switch between them at safe boundaries).

Per the F2 reference shell decision (T2.4 `bd-37r`), Persistence / Trace / Harness / Panic each have *reserved slack* in the chrome budget but are NOT in the pinned reference shell. When this epic's features land, the reference shell must be re-evaluated.

### Scope (six features)

- **F-D1 — Persistence**: `PersistHeader`, `PersistGroupCommit`, `PersistKind`, `PageState`, double-buffered SRAM record protocol with explicit commit boundaries. Per `planv0.md` lines 1718–1788.
- **F-D2 — Harness Control Plane**: full implementation of `HarnessOp` set (`StepSlice`, `RunUntilCheckpoint`, `DumpArena`, `InjectFault`, `PowerCut`, `SetSession`, `GetState`).
- **F-D3 — Trace Pipeline**: trace pages in SRAM, ring-buffer drain, host-side parser, `TraceBudget` enforcement, `trace_loss_report.json`.
- **F-D4 — Drift Monitor**: `RuntimeDriftMonitor` + `DriftTrigger` + `DriftAction` (`ShrinkSlices`/`DropTrace`/`DemoteMode`).
- **F-D5 — Fault Policy Table + Recovery**: per-`FaultDomain` action mapping + `BootValidationPlan` runner + cold-start logic.
- **F-D6 — SchedulePack Mode Switching**: runtime side of `SchedulePack` — `ModeSwitchPolicy`, legal-switch-point gating, `Safe` mode, `SafeModeTrigger`s.

### Open question for the oracle (the second one we drafted)

How many `RuntimeMode`s should `SchedulePack` carry across milestones? Single-mode in v1 (just `Steady`)? Two-mode (`Steady` + `Debug`)? Three (`Interactive` + `Steady` + `Trace`)? Plus `Safe` as a fallback. F-D6 sizing depends on the answer.

### Critical path

```
F-A5 (Bank0 nucleus) ──→ all of F-D1..F-D6
F-A3 (gbf-abi types) ──→ F-D1 (PersistKind), F-D2 (HarnessOp), F-D3 (TraceEvent), F-D4 (DriftAction), F-D5 (FaultPolicy)
F-B11 (GbSchedIR) ────→ F-D6 (SchedulePack consumer)
```

### Confidence: HIGH on F-D1/F-D2/F-D3/F-D5, MEDIUM on F-D4/F-D6

Drift monitor + mode switching depend on production workload data we don't have yet. F-D6 specifically blocked on the SchedulePack-mode oracle question.

title: F-E6: Constrained autotune over tile/residency/slice knobs
description: Explicit knob set (tile sizes, kernel residency, slice ceilings). Pareto frontier across CompileObjective axes. Selection by objective + risk policy. Overlaps with F-B14 — autotune feeds CompileKnobs.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-E6: Constrained autotune

### What this is

Explicit knob set (tile sizes, kernel residency, slice ceilings) per `planv0.md` line 318. Pareto frontier across `CompileObjective` axes. Selection by objective + risk policy.

### Why this matters

The plan limits autotune to a small explicit knob surface to keep tuning honest. Random hyperparameter sweeps are excluded.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct AutotuneKnobs {
    pub tile_sizes: BTreeMap<KernelInstanceId, Vec<TileShape>>,
    pub residency_choices: BTreeMap<KernelInstanceId, Vec<KernelResidency>>,
    pub slice_ceiling_choices: Vec<u32>,
}

pub fn run_autotune(knobs: &AutotuneKnobs, objective: &CompileObjective)
    -> ParetoFrontier;
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B12 ScheduleCostAnalysis
- F-B14 FeasibilityRefinementLoop (overlaps; autotune feeds CompileKnobs)

### Confidence: MEDIUM. Requires F-B14 oracle answer.

title: T-A3.6: SemanticCheckpointId + CompactCheckpointId + SemanticCheckpointSchema
description: Stable durable checkpoint ids (e.g., layer.3.router.post_top1) + compact u16 mapping for trace events. SemanticCheckpointSchema is exported as semantic_checkpoint_schema.json (planv0.md line 1240); harness, oracles, runtime, and reports translate via it. SemanticStratum enum (Denotation/Artifact/Operational).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A3.6: `SemanticCheckpointId` + `CompactCheckpointId` + `SemanticCheckpointSchema`

**Target crate**: `gbf-abi`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-abi/src/checkpoint.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 271, line 1209 (ObservationPlan consumes SemanticCheckpointSchema), engineering rule 7 ("harness uses SemanticCheckpointId, not magic addresses").

### Background

`SemanticCheckpointId` is the durable id used across the harness, oracles, runtime, and reports to refer to a logical checkpoint in the model's execution (e.g., "after layer 3 router decision"). `CompactCheckpointId` is the smaller fixed-width id used in trace events and InferenceState for space efficiency. `SemanticCheckpointSchema` is the exported mapping between them (`planv0.md` line 1240).

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct SemanticCheckpointId(pub Cow<'static, str>);

#[repr(transparent)]
pub struct CompactCheckpointId(pub u16);

pub struct SemanticCheckpointSchema {
    pub schema_version: u16,
    pub checkpoints: Vec<CheckpointEntry>,
}

pub struct CheckpointEntry {
    pub semantic: SemanticCheckpointId,        // e.g. "layer.3.router.post_top1"
    pub compact: CompactCheckpointId,
    pub stratum: SemanticStratum,              // Denotation | Artifact | Operational
    pub source_op: Option<&'static str>,
}

pub enum SemanticStratum {
    Denotation,
    Artifact,
    Operational,
}
```

The schema is exported by the build (it's part of `BuildReports` per `planv0.md` line 1577 — `semantic_checkpoint_schema.json`). The harness, oracles, and runtime read it to translate between compact and semantic ids.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-abi
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- checkpoint::serde_round_trip
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- checkpoint::compact_id_unique           # Schema validation: no two entries share a compact id
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- checkpoint::stratum_exhaustive          # All 3 strata legal
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: SemanticStratum is exhaustive; matches the three-stratum framing in planv0.md.
- §VI.1: One schema type, used by oracles + harness + runtime + reports.

title: T5.3: Implement activation range penalty
description: Soft penalty for activations that exceed the safe integer range at expert input/output boundaries. The range target comes from the declared accumulator format (typically i16). penalty = max(0, |activation| - safe_bound)^2 averaged over the batch. Works in conjunction with ActFakeQuant — the penalty nudges activations toward the clipping range while the fake-quant simulates the clip.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::range passes; Within bound -> penalty=0; Exceeding -> quadratic penalty
close_reason:
Implemented activation range penalty in gbf-train.

Summary:
- Added gbf-train/src/loss/range.rs with SafeActivationBound, ActivationRangeBatch, scalar raw loss, weighted helper, and Burn tensor helpers behind burn-adapter.
- Reduction contract is explicit: per expert-boundary sample mean, then batch mean; rectangular tensors are equivalent to elementwise mean.
- Burn helper validates scalar config and tensor shape before math, then validates computed loss finiteness after math; it no longer host-copies the full activation tensor for routine validation.
- Added non-default safe-bound tests, finite weighted Burn behavior, Burn gradient proof, and f64 scalar accumulation to avoid finite-mean overflow from intermediate f32 sums.
- Did not claim training-loop config/logging adoption; bd-1c6 owns composition/logging, and bd-1j7 owns broader per-term parameter-group gradient tests.

Review modes:
- Intent: requested explicit reduction oracle and feature-enabled Burn gradient gate; addressed in docs/tests/closure.
- Design: requested named batch/sample boundary, no full tensor host validation in Burn helper, and separation from unrelated planv0 changes; addressed, with planv0 left unstaged.
- Semantic: flagged f32 accumulation overflow for finite means; addressed with f64 accumulation and regression test.
- Evidence: requested non-default safe-bound tests, Burn weighted success test, and honest red-before-green wording; addressed. No pre-patch red-before-green is claimed because the filtered loss::range target was introduced by this patch.

Feedback reflection table:
| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Range loss reduction was ambiguous about batch/sample axes. | Flat helper hid expert-boundary sample width. | AGENTS Training Loss Beads now requires activation/range losses to name batch and per-sample axes and use a checked value object when flat slices hide semantics. | ActivationRangeBatch plus activation_range_penalty_uses_per_sample_then_batch_mean_reduction. | bd-1p3 / cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::range |
| Safe-bound behavior only used bound 1.0. | Tests could pass with a hard-coded default bound. | AGENTS now requires non-default/non-1.0 tests for scalar hyperparameters. | activation_range_penalty_honors_non_default_safe_bound; burn_activation_range_penalty_matches_scalar_oracle uses safe_bound 2.0. | bd-1p3 / cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- loss::range |
| Burn helper copied all activations to host before differentiable loss math. | Validation boundary copied production tensors instead of validating shape/config and computed loss. | AGENTS now says Burn loss helpers should not host-copy entire differentiable tensors for routine validation. | Code path now validates Burn shape and computed scalar loss only. | bd-1p3 / cargo clippy -p gbf-train --all-features -- -D warnings |
| New filtered test target could be reported as green without red-before-green proof. | New module filters select zero tests before module exists. | AGENTS now requires test counts and forbids red-before-green claims unless actually run pre-patch. | Closure reports 8 scalar range tests and 13 feature-enabled range tests; no red-before-green claim. | bead closure / pre-commit review |
| Scalar f32 sum could overflow before a finite mean. | Mean was implemented as f32 sum then divide. | No separate guide; this is a normal semantic regression handled by a unit test. | activation_range_penalty_uses_f64_accumulation_for_finite_large_means. | bd-1p3 / cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::range |

Commands run:
- cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::range (8 tests)
- cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- loss::range (13 tests)
- cargo check -p gbf-train
- cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings
- cargo clippy -p gbf-train --all-features -- -D warnings
- cargo test -p gbf-train --all-features (131 lib tests + 4 structured logging tests)
- cargo test -p gbf-train (71 lib tests + 3 structured logging tests)
- cargo fmt --check --all

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/loss/range.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "activation range penalty", "safe integer range", "expert boundaries".

### Implementation Notes
- Soft penalty for activations exceeding safe integer range at expert boundaries.
- `penalty = Σ max(0, |activation| - safe_bound)^2`
- `pub fn activation_range_penalty(activations: &Tensor, safe_bound: f32) -> Tensor`
- safe_bound comes from the target's accumulator bit width (e.g., i16 -> 32767 scaled down by accumulator chain length).
- Applied at expert input/output boundaries — works alongside ActFakeQuant.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::range          # Tests pass
# Verify: activations within safe_bound -> penalty = 0
# Verify: activations exceeding bound -> quadratic penalty
# Verify: gradient is zero within bound, linear outside bound
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Pure function
- §I.5 (Honest Loss): This term is differentiable w.r.t. activations — a legitimate training signal

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Activation fake-quant dependency clarification

`bd-pk3` provides the activation fake-quant module and fixed-range Burn STE proof. This bead remains the owner of `activation_range_penalty`, including the loss implementation, safe-bound parameterization, and gradient tests. Do not treat `bd-pk3` closure as implementing or gating the loss term.

title: F-F3: Slow reference impls in gbf-verify
description: Independent slow reference re-implementations of pack/unpack, logical LUT eval, decode/RNG, ternary projection. Used in nightly randomized cross-checks. Deliberately written DIFFERENTLY from production so a shared bug cannot become self-validating.

comments.author: bkase
bkase
comments.text:
## Moved acceptance owner from bd-22o

`bd-22o` now proves that exported canonical ternary tensors are reference-packable, but it cannot prove production packed-byte agreement because `ExportVisitor` currently emits canonical `I8`/`U16` tensors rather than target packed bytes.

This feature owns the future production/reference packed ternary byte agreement once a production materializer emits comparable ternary `weight_bytes` and `scale_bytes`. The expected gate should compare production bytes against the independent `gbf-verify` reference packer and report the tensor id, first divergent byte offset, and hex context on mismatch.

title: T1.7b: Burn adapters for Top1RouterQat and ExpertBlockQat
description: Implement differentiable gbf-train Burn QAT adapters for gbf-model Top1RouterQat and ExpertBlockQat. This owns the moved Burn training path that bd-ubm originally pointed at bd-1mv; bd-1mv only owns the generic Burn adapter layer plus existing ternary/activation/norm adapter containment. Add router/expert Burn Module wrappers, tensor shape validation, gradient-flow tests, and export-from-trained-state handoff hooks without adding Burn dependencies to gbf-model.
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

**Artifact contract:** This bead implements the pre-export Burn adapter handoff for `Top1RouterQat` and `ExpertBlockQat`. The contract is: trainable Burn params live in `gbf-train`, forward paths remain differentiable under `--features burn-adapter`, and `to_core_from_trained_state` reconstructs scalar `gbf-model` QAT cores from Burn-owned tensors. It does not claim ArtifactCore materialization, compiler lowering, runtime lowering, or ArtifactOracle agreement; those remain with bd-g90, bd-12c, and bd-22o.

**Differentiable Burn path:** `gbf-train/src/qat/router.rs` adds `Top1RouterBurnQat`; `gbf-train/src/qat/expert.rs` adds `ExpertBlockBurnQat`. The expert adapter composes existing `TernaryLinearBurnQat` and `ActFakeQuantBurnQat`; router/expert modules are exported from `gbf-train/src/qat/mod.rs` behind `burn-adapter`.

**Tests proving it:** See claim-to-gate matrix below. The acceptance commands pass, plus the QAT harness and architecture boundary tests pass.

**Acceptance movement:** None of the listed bd-1ptv acceptance criteria were moved. Artifact/oracle agreement is explicitly out of scope for this bead.

## Review Feedback Addressed

- Intent review: clarified closure scope as pre-export Burn handoff, not full loss policy/scheduler/artifact agreement.
- Design/semantic review: fixed router z-loss to use stable max-subtracted logsumexp, added non-finite input rejection for router/expert/ternary adapter boundaries, and made empty expert batches return a `[0, d_model]` tensor.
- Evidence review: strengthened export handoff tests by mutating Burn-owned params before reconstruction; changed router gradient proof to flow through `routing_weights`; added expert full-precision and eval activation-mode coverage.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `Top1RouterQat` forward: logits, soft probabilities, hard/soft top-1 routing weights | supported | supported | pre-export handoff supported | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::router` |
| Router aux outputs: z-loss, token-balance proxy, temporal smoothness | supported | supported | pre-export handoff supported | `burn_router_z_loss_uses_stable_scalar_logsumexp_oracle` |
| Router hard top-1 gradient behavior | supported as hard dispatch | supported through STE over soft probabilities | pre-export handoff supported | `burn_router_soft_mode_is_differentiable_through_projection_params` |
| Router finite input and previous-distribution boundary | supported | supported/rejected with scalar error variants | pre-export handoff supported | `burn_router_rejects_shape_and_option_mismatches_before_forward` |
| `ExpertBlockQat` hard-quantized selected expert plus shared branch | supported | supported | pre-export handoff supported | `burn_expert_forward_matches_scalar_expert_block` |
| Expert full-precision projection mode and eval activation mode | supported | supported | pre-export handoff supported | `burn_expert_forwards_full_precision_and_eval_activation_options` |
| Expert batch routing, including empty batch | supported | supported | pre-export handoff supported | `burn_expert_batch_forward_routes_each_row` |
| Fixed activation fake-quant ranges | supported | supported | pre-export handoff supported | `burn_expert_forwards_full_precision_and_eval_activation_options` |
| Learned or EMA activation ranges | scalar config supported | rejected at Burn adapter boundary | not claimed here | `burn_expert_rejects_shape_and_dynamic_activation_contracts` |
| Burn-trained router/expert scalar reconstruction | supported | supported from Burn-owned params | pre-export handoff supported | `burn_router_export_handoff_uses_burn_owned_tensors`, `burn_expert_export_handoff_uses_burn_owned_tensors` |
| ArtifactCore / ExportVisitor materialization | supported by bd-g90 | not applicable | outside this bead | bd-g90 |
| ArtifactOracle or cross-backend artifact agreement | not applicable | not applicable | outside this bead | bd-12c, bd-22o |

## Claim-To-Gate Matrix

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `gbf-train` compiles with router/expert Burn adapters | `cargo check -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter` | `burn-adapter` | Acceptance command passed |
| Router Burn forward matches scalar logits/probabilities/routing and validates options | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::router` | `burn-adapter` | Includes scalar comparison, non-finite rejection, stable z-loss oracle, routing-weight gradient, and trained-state reconstruction |
| Expert Burn forward matches scalar behavior and routes selected/shared branches | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::expert` | `burn-adapter` | Includes batch, empty batch, full-precision, eval activation, gradients, dynamic range rejection, and trained-state reconstruction |
| Existing ternary adapter remains compatible and rejects non-finite direct inputs | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::ternary` | `burn-adapter` | Needed because expert composes ternary adapters |
| All QAT Burn adapter tests pass together | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat` | `burn-adapter` | 31 tests passed |
| Clippy is clean for the target crate | `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- -D warnings` | `burn-adapter` | Acceptance command passed |
| Burn remains confined to `gbf-train::adapter` | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Architecture boundary passed |
| QAT closure and moved-acceptance harness still pass | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` | none | Passed before close; rerun after close |

## No-Future Variant Acceptance

No future QAT variants are accepted by the Burn adapter path. `RouterTrainMode::SoftTop1` and `RouterTrainMode::HardTop1` are the only router modes implemented here; hard top-1 uses explicit STE behavior and does not claim scheduler or F4 phase-hardness completion. The router term exposed here is a single-token token-balance proxy, not the standard batch load-balance objective. Expert activation ranges are accepted only for fixed ranges through `ActFakeQuantBurnQat`; learned and EMA ranges are rejected with a focused test. Artifact lowering and oracle agreement are not claimed by this bead.

title: F9: Adaptive Expert Shapes — M6 Research Mode via ExpertShapePolicy
description: ExpertShapePolicy::StructuredWidthGates allows training experts as a supernet and exporting after hardening/pruning into bank-fitting widths. This is where lambda_shape/lambda_overflow become meaningful. M6-scoped research mode, not part of the default training path.
acceptance_criteria: All T9.x tasks closed; ExpertShapePolicy enum defined; M6 tasks stubbed or implemented

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F9: Adaptive Expert Shapes — M6 Research Mode via ExpertShapePolicy

### Background

The default training path uses fixed-shape experts: d_model -> d_ff -> d_model with fixed dimensions chosen before training. The byte cost is deterministic from the architecture parameters + TernaryWeightPlan, and bank fit is verified at preflight time. This is simple, predictable, and sufficient for M0-M5.

However, there's a research question: could experts learn to be different sizes? Some experts might need more capacity (wider d_ff) while others could be smaller. If the system could learn per-expert widths and then prune/harden them to fit banks, it might achieve better quality for the same total ROM budget.

### ExpertShapePolicy

- Fixed: all experts have the same d_ff. Default. Used M0-M5.
- StructuredWidthGates { row_group, col_group }: experts are trained as a supernet with max d_ff, but each row_group x col_group block has a learnable gate. During training, gates close gradually (annealing). At export, closed gates are pruned and the expert is exported with its actual (possibly smaller) width. This is the regime where λ_shape and λ_overflow become meaningful training signals.

### Why this is M6-scoped

1. It requires a working fixed-shape training pipeline first (M0-M5 worth of infrastructure)
2. The quality benefit is uncertain — it might not be worth the complexity
3. The compiler needs to handle variable-width experts (different byte costs per expert, different bank packing)
4. The training dynamics of width gates + ternary quantization + routing are not well-studied

### Why it's tracked now despite being M6

The plan's loss function (F5) includes λ_shape and λ_overflow terms that are gated on this enum. The enum needs to exist (even if only the Fixed variant is implemented) so that the gating logic is correct from the start. Adding it later would require touching the loss function config, the model config, the export logic, and the compiler's budget calculations.

### Confidence: LOW-MEDIUM

This is the most speculative feature in the entire training-contract revision. The Fixed variant is trivial and definitely correct. The StructuredWidthGates variant is a research idea that may or may not pan out. The value of tracking it now is: (a) it makes the λ_shape/λ_overflow gating logic explicit, (b) it leaves a clean extension point for M6, (c) it costs almost nothing to define the enum with one variant now.

## T-B14.5: Rename `RepairPolicy::allow_profile_fallback` → `allow_placement_profile_fallback`

**Target crate**: `gbf-policy`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/repair.rs` (existing); call-sites in `gbf-train` preflight + planv0.md references.

### Background

`RepairPolicy::allow_profile_fallback` is ambiguous: `RiskPolicy::fallback_profile` already exists for "try a different `CompileProfile` after this one fails." The repair-loop field controls only the **placement-profile ladder** (`StrictOnePerBank → Budgeted → PackedExperts`). Rename for clarity.

### Implementation Notes

1. Rename the field. Add a doc comment:
   ```rust
   /// PlacementProfile fallback only:
   /// StrictOnePerBank -> Budgeted -> PackedExperts.

   /// Full CompileProfile fallback remains RiskPolicy::fallback_profile.
   pub allow_placement_profile_fallback: bool,
   ```


title: F-E1: Workload manifest pipeline
description: WorkloadManifest + WorkloadClass (Conformance/Performance/Endurance/Ui/Quality/Interactive) + AcceptanceMatrix + ExecutionMatrix + ObservationPolicy in gbf-workload. Bench runner consumes them.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-E1: Workload manifest pipeline

### What this is

`WorkloadManifest` + `WorkloadClass` (`Conformance`/`Performance`/`Endurance`/`Ui`/`Quality`/`Interactive`) + `AcceptanceMatrix` (per-stratum gates) + `ExecutionMatrix` (which paths the workload runs against) + `ObservationPolicy` in `gbf-workload`. Plus the bench runner that consumes them.

### Why this matters

Workloads are typed by purpose. A conformance workload wants deterministic decode + rich checkpoint collection + tight numerical envelope; a performance workload wants representative prompts + limited probe overhead; an endurance workload wants long duration + power-cut injection. Treating them as the same thing makes CI noisy.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct WorkloadManifest {
    pub id: WorkloadId,
    pub class: WorkloadClass,
    pub prompts: Vec<PromptCase>,
    pub observation_policy: ObservationPolicy,
    pub execution_matrix: ExecutionMatrix,
    pub acceptance_matrix: AcceptanceMatrix,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A3.6 SemanticCheckpointId
- F-G3 DecodeMode / SessionProfile

### Confidence: HIGH

title: Training-Contract Revision Pass: Make the Training Side Implementable
description: Nine training-contract corrections that transform the plan's training/model side from hand-wavy aspirations into implementable engineering. Covers: Burn reframing, RuntimeChromeBudget, TernaryWeightPlan, phased QAT training with dense teacher, honest loss function, MoE architecture restrictions, router switch-awareness, shadow export/compile, and M6 adaptive expert shapes.
acceptance_criteria: ./scripts/test_all.sh passes; cargo check --workspace exits 0; All 10 features closed

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Training-Contract Revision Pass — Master Tracking Issue

### What this is

The architecture plan (planv0.md) was comprehensive on the compiler, runtime, oracle, and artifact boundary sides but left the training/model side underspecified. This epic tracks nine corrections that make the training contract implementable rather than aspirational.

### Why this matters

Without these corrections, the first person to actually implement training would hit a cascade of unresolved questions:
- "Burn owns QAT" — but Burn doesn't have QAT. Who owns the ternary projection code?
- "Bank-friendly expert sizing" — but the loss function penalizes bank overflow for fixed-shape experts where bank overflow is a geometry property, not a differentiable training signal.
- "Use 16 KiB experts" — but 16 KiB of what? Raw ternary bits? Plus scales? Plus metadata? The byte formula was never specified.
- "Router locality loss" — but locality of what kind? Spatial? Temporal? The switch cost on GB is primarily temporal (consecutive tokens hitting different banks).
- "Export a ReferenceModelBundle" — from what? The full-precision model? A dense teacher? When in training?

### The nine corrections

1. **Burn as front-end, not numerics owner** — Burn provides autodiff/optimizers/checkpoints; gbf-model owns deployed numerics (ternary projection, activation fake-quant, norm approximation, export visitation)
2. **RuntimeChromeBudget** — The trainer needs to know how many bytes are free in each bank class AFTER the runtime shell is built, not just a static 16 KiB constant
3. **TernaryWeightPlan** — WeightEncoding::Ternary2 alone doesn't specify scale granularity, scale format, threshold schedule, or exported byte cost
4. **Phased training with dense teacher** — Training doesn't go from random init to hard ternary in one shot; there's a 5-phase schedule with a frozen dense teacher for distillation and denotational reference
5. **Honest loss function** — The old λ_bank/λ_locality terms were phantom gradients for fixed-shape experts; replaced with terms that are actually differentiable w.r.t. the quantities they claim to optimize
6. **MoE architecture restrictions** — MoE on FFN path only, two-matrix experts (not GLU), tied embeddings, optional shared dense branch, selected blocks not every block
7. **Router switch-awareness** — Temporal switch cost is the real deployment bottleneck; router training gets z-loss, expert dropout, temporal smoothness regularization, and TemporalSwitchDigest in export facts
8. **Shadow export/compile during training** — Don't wait until end of training to discover the model doesn't fit; periodic EMA export + compile + conformance check with Pareto frontier selection
9. **Adaptive expert shapes (M6)** — StructuredWidthGates as an explicit M6 research mode where λ_shape/λ_overflow actually become meaningful

### Dependency structure

The features form a DAG:
- F1 (Burn reframing) is the foundation — almost everything depends on gbf-model owning the QAT modules
- F6 (MoE arch) is structurally independent but defines the model that F3/F5/F7 must support
- F3 (TernaryWeightPlan) depends on F1
- F7 (Router switch-awareness) depends on F6
- F4 (Phased training) depends on F1 and F3
- F5 (Loss function) depends on F3 and F7
- F2 (RuntimeChromeBudget) has external deps on gbf-hw/gbf-policy but no internal feature deps
- F8 (Shadow compile) depends on F2 and F4
- F9 (Adaptive shapes) depends on F3 and F5, is M6-deferred

### Milestone mapping

- M1-M2: F1 (Burn reframing), F3 (TernaryWeightPlan basics), F6 (MoE arch decisions)
- M3: F6 (implementation), F7 (router switch-awareness)
- M3-M4: F4 (phased training), F2 (RuntimeChromeBudget)
- M4-M5: F8 (shadow compile)
- M6: F5 (full loss function), F9 (adaptive shapes)

### Assessment notes

Revisions 1, 3, 5, 8, 2 are wholeheartedly endorsed — they fix genuine holes that would cause implementation failures. Revisions 4 and 6-7 are endorsed with mild reservations about specificity (the exact phase ordering and block selection may need revision once training data exists). Revision 9 is the most speculative — it's harmless but premature as a first-class type.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Dependency Restructuring Note

Removed 8 overly conservative feature-level deps that were blocking pure type-definition and config work from appearing in `br ready`. The task-level deps (which are more granular) were already correct and provide the real implementation ordering constraints.

**Removed feature-level deps:**
- F3->F1, F4->F1, F4->F3: Type definitions in gbf-artifact/gbf-train don't need gbf-model QAT code
- F5->F3, F5->F7: Standard MoE loss terms (z-loss, load-balance, config) don't need TernaryWeightPlan or router switch code
- F7->F6: Router code is parameterized by n_experts from config, not by F6 MoE architecture implementation
- F9->F3, F9->F5: ExpertShapePolicy enum definition is independent

**Preserved task-level deps provide the real constraints:**
- T3.3->T1.6 (scale tensor export needs ExportVisitor)
- T4.3->T1.6 (teacher export needs ExportVisitor)
- T4.5->T1.2/T1.3/T1.4 (QAT hardness control needs QAT modules)
- T5.1->T7.3 (temporal switch penalty needs router temporal smoothness)
- T5.5->T9.1 (λ_shape gating needs ExpertShapePolicy enum)
- T6.4->T1.2 (two-matrix expert needs TernaryLinearQat)
- T7.5->T1.5 (low-rank router needs Top1RouterQat)

**Added task-level deps for F9 M6 tasks:**
- T9.2->T3.1, T5.1, T5.3, T5.4 (supernet training needs ternary types + loss terms)

**Result:** `br ready` now shows 20 items instead of 9, correctly reflecting that type definitions, standard loss terms, and config work can start in parallel with F1/F6 implementation work.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Session 2026-04-26: model/runtime interface decisions added two new features

This epic now includes two additional features that emerged from the session reviewing how the model/runtime interface respects the Game Boy memory map:

- **F11: CompileProfile + WRAM Layout Contract** — defines `WramLayoutPolicy`, `OverlayReloadPolicy`, the `CompileProfile` registry, and the named `BringUp` profile (single profile for now; production profiles deferred until F12 experimentation resolves). Captures the WRAM split (fixed overlay + ArenaPlan-managed remainder) and the hard switch cap convention.
- **F12: Dual-Path Sequence-State Experimentation** — `gbf-model` abstracts over `SequenceSemanticsSpec`; both `LinearState` and `BoundedKv` are implemented under the same training scaffold and shadow_compile so the production choice is settled empirically rather than declaratively.

F2 (`bd-1hv`) gained four new child tasks (T2.4–T2.7) covering the reference-shell spec, runtime_nucleus_hash CI gate, synthetic pre-runtime budget, and per-export re-validation. These are documented on F2's bead.

### Key commitments from the session (also recorded on F2)

1. Reference runtime shell for pre-training = minimal + UI: `boot, interrupts, scheduler, banking, joypad, text, keyboard, video_commit`.
2. WRAM overlay is the default residency strategy for expert hot kernels; reload is per-expert-switch.
3. WRAM split: fixed overlay region per `CompileProfile`; remainder managed by `ArenaPlan`.
4. `RuntimeChromeBudget` is pinned at training start (real or synthetic) and re-validated at export; drift gated by CI on `runtime_nucleus_hash`.
5. Switch cap is a hard `CompileProfile` constraint (not just a training prior). Only `BringUp` is defined now.

### Milestone updates

- M2: F12 (sequence-state types + LinearState/BoundedKv blocks).
- M3: F11 (compile profile + WRAM layout types, BringUp defaults), and F2's new T2.4–T2.7 tasks.
- M3-M4: T2.7 per-export re-validation (rides with F8 shadow_compile).

title: F-C1: DenotationalOracle
description: Runs ReferenceProgram from ReferenceModelBundle. Owns ReferenceNumericProfile, DeterminismClass, ReductionOrderPolicy. Slow reference impls live in gbf-verify, not here. Defines target-independent reference meaning.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-C1: DenotationalOracle

### What this is

Runs the `ReferenceProgram` from `ReferenceModelBundle` to produce reference observations at semantic-checkpoint granularity. Owns `ReferenceNumericProfile`, `DeterminismClass`, `ReductionOrderPolicy`. Per planv0.md correction: slow reference impls live in `gbf-verify`, not here.

### Why this matters

Denotational truth is the quality baseline. Without `DenotationalOracle`, "did quantization regress quality?" has no ground-truth answer.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct DenotationalOracle {
    bundle: ReferenceModelBundle,
    numeric_profile: ReferenceNumericProfile,
}

impl DenotationalOracle {
    pub fn evaluate(&self, workload: &Workload, observation_policy: &ObservationPolicy)
        -> ReferenceObservations;
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- gbf-artifact ReferenceModelBundle
- F-A3.6 SemanticCheckpointId
- F-F3 gbf-verify slow reference impls

### Confidence: MEDIUM. Hardest part is making determinism class match operationally.

title: T-A3.4: FaultCode + FaultDomain + FaultSnapshot + FaultPolicy + BootValidationPlan
description: Typed faults per planv0.md correction. FaultCode enumerates failures (AbiVersionMismatch, PersistChecksum, BankShadowDivergence, UnauthorizedMbcWrite, LeaseUnbalanced, YieldTooLong, LivelockDetected, UiCommitOutsideLegalMode, InternalAssertion, ...). FaultPolicy maps FaultDomain -> RecoveryAction (ColdStart/DemoteToSafeMode/AbortAndPanic/BootValidationOnly).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A3.4: `FaultCode` + `FaultDomain` + `FaultSnapshot` + `FaultPolicy`

**Target crate**: `gbf-abi`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-abi/src/fault.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 271, line 2554 (rev pass: fault domains and graded recovery actions).

### Background

Faults are typed; they are not magic numbers. `FaultDomain` says where the fault originated (storage, schedule, ABI, persistence). `FaultPolicy` decides what to do (cold-start, return to safe mode, panic, etc.). `FaultSnapshot` is the on-cartridge structured record written to SRAM on fault.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
#[repr(u16)]
pub enum FaultCode {
    None                       = 0,
    AbiVersionMismatch         = 0x0001,
    PersistChecksum            = 0x0010,
    PersistTornWrite           = 0x0011,
    PersistSchemaUnknown       = 0x0012,
    BankShadowDivergence       = 0x0020,
    UnauthorizedMbcWrite       = 0x0021,
    LeaseUnbalanced            = 0x0022,
    YieldTooLong               = 0x0030,
    InterruptLatencyExceeded   = 0x0031,
    LivelockDetected           = 0x0032,
    UiCommitOutsideLegalMode   = 0x0040,
    UnknownChecksumKind        = 0x0050,
    UnknownPersistKind         = 0x0051,
    HarnessProtocolError       = 0x0060,
    InternalAssertion          = 0xFF00,
}

pub enum FaultDomain {
    Boot,
    Persistence,
    Banking,
    Scheduling,
    Liveness,
    Ui,
    Harness,
    Internal,
}

pub fn classify_fault(code: FaultCode) -> FaultDomain;

#[repr(C)]
pub struct FaultSnapshot {
    pub code: FaultCode,
    pub domain: FaultDomain,
    pub at_pc: u16,
    pub at_bank: u8,
    pub at_checkpoint: SemanticCheckpointId,
    pub regs: RegisterSnapshot,                       // A, F, B, C, D, E, H, L, SP
    pub liveness: LivenessCounters,
}

pub enum RecoveryAction {
    ColdStart,
    DemoteToSafeMode,
    AbortAndPanic,
    BootValidationOnly,                                // only validate, don't auto-fix
}

pub struct FaultPolicy {
    pub by_domain: BTreeMap<FaultDomain, RecoveryAction>,
    pub default_action: RecoveryAction,
}

pub struct BootValidationPlan {
    pub validate_persistence: bool,
    pub validate_runtime_nucleus_hash: bool,
    pub validate_artifact_core_hash: bool,
}
```

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-abi
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- fault::code_to_domain_mapping         # Every FaultCode has a domain
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- fault::policy_default_action          # FaultPolicy.default_action is non-trivial
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- fault::snapshot_layout                 # FaultSnapshot has expected size
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: FaultCode and FaultDomain are enumerated.
- §V.3: FaultSnapshot carries enough state to debug after the fact.

title: F-A4: BankLease/BankGuard ABI in gbf-runtime::banking
description: The sole legal path to MBC writes per planv0.md engineering rule 15. BankLease/BankGuard types, HRAM shadow registers (current_rom_bank, current_sram_bank, sram_enabled, yield_requested, last_fault), acquire/release primitives in AsmIR builders, and interrupt-safe scaffolding (annotations consumed later by ReachabilityValidation in Epic B).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-A4: `BankLease` / `BankGuard` ABI in `gbf-runtime::banking`

### Background

Per `planv0.md` line 1716, all MBC5 register handling is centralized in `gbf-runtime::banking`. Compiler-generated code may **never** emit raw MBC writes (rule 15, line 2506); it must go through the `BankLease` / `BankGuard` ABI. This feature defines that ABI.

The reason for centralization: software bank shadow registers in HRAM must stay synchronized with hardware bank state. Any divergence — especially across an interrupt boundary — causes the runtime to lock up "after twenty minutes on cartridge" (`planv0.md` line 1583). Centralizing through one type-checked ABI lets `ReachabilityValidation` (Epic B) compute residency/privilege classes and prove the invariant.

### How this serves overarching project goals

- The compiler's generated code (Epic B) calls `BankLease` pseudo-ops; the encoder lowers those to the runtime's `acquire_*` primitives.
- The Bank0 nucleus (F-A5) holds bank shadow state in HRAM and arbitrates access.
- `ReachabilityValidation` proves "no ISR-reachable code depends on the switchable bank" by walking `BankLease` lifetimes.
- The shadow-register convention is one of the things `runtime_nucleus_hash` covers — it must be deterministic.

### The problem this solves

Without F-A4:
- Compiler can emit raw MBC writes that bypass the shadow.
- Two slices can disagree about which bank is active at a yield boundary.
- Interrupt handlers can sample stale shadow state.

### Design

Three layers:

1. **Type-level lease**: `BankLease` is acquired against an `MbcBankClass` (`Rom4000`, `SramA000`) for a `LeaseLifetime` (`Slice`, `ResumeWindow`, `Token`, `Manual`). Acquisition returns a `BankGuard` (RAII-ish in the eDSL — drop emits `BankRelease`).
2. **Shadow registers**: HRAM holds `current_rom_bank: u8`, `current_sram_bank: u8`, `sram_enabled: bool`. The shadow is *authoritative for software*; hardware writes must go through the ABI to update it.
3. **Acquire/release primitives**: `acquire_rom_bank(n)` / `acquire_sram_bank(n)` / `enable_sram(true|false)` written in `AsmIR` builders. Each performs (a) a short critical section if the runtime configures one, (b) the MBC write (`$2000`/`$3000`/`$4000`/`$0000`), (c) the shadow update.

### Interrupt safety

ISR code/data must never depend on the switchable bank, period. `InterruptPolicy::ShortCriticalSection` is the only legal way to acquire a lease that crosses an ISR boundary; otherwise the lease must be released before the slice yields.

### Children (T-A4.1 ... T-A4.4)

1. **T-A4.1** `BankLease` / `BankGuard` types + `MbcBankClass` + `LeaseLifetime`.
2. **T-A4.2** HRAM shadow registers + RAM-enable state.
3. **T-A4.3** Acquire / release primitives in AsmIR builders.
4. **T-A4.4** Interrupt-safe scaffolding — declarative residency annotations consumed by ReachabilityValidation later.

### Dependencies on other Epic A features

- F-A1.4 (`MachineEffect` + `PrivilegeClass`): the lease ops carry `PrivilegeClass::Privileged` so ReachabilityValidation can spot violations.
- F-A2.3 (MBC5 register addresses): the primitive writes use those addresses.
- F-A2.2 (HRAM constants): shadow registers live at well-known HRAM addresses.
- F-A3.5 (`InterruptPolicy` + `ResourceLease`): the type-level lease here is one kind of `ResourceLease`.

### Confidence: HIGH on shape, MEDIUM on critical-section semantics

The bigger architectural risk is whether to model the critical section as a generic disable-IF/enable-IF block (simple, works for short writes) or as a more elaborate IRQ-deferral mechanism. Default: simple disable/enable around the four-instruction MBC write sequence. Revisit if measured ISR latency under bank pressure exceeds the `SchedulerPolicy::max_interrupt_latency` budget.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T-A4.1–T-A4.4.

### Feature Definition of Done

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- banking                                  # All module tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- banking::shadow_consistency               # Shadow stays consistent across acquire/release
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- banking::no_raw_mbc_writes                # Grep test: outside banking.rs, no $2000/$3000/$4000 stores
cargo clippy -p gbf-runtime -- -D warnings                             # Clean
```

title: T10.9: RuntimeChromeBudget and preflight tests (F2 coverage)
description:
Unit and integration tests for the chrome budget contract:

**Type construction tests:**
- Create RuntimeChromeBudget with known slot capacities, verify serialization round-trip
- Verify BudgetSlotClass variants correctly classify Bank0Free, CommonBank, ExpertBank

**Preflight pass/fail tests:**
- Model config with d_ff=16, ExpertBank usable=512 bytes: verify PASS
- Model config with d_ff=224, ExpertBank usable=100 bytes: verify FAIL with clear diagnostic
- Model config with unsupported NormPlan: verify FAIL on DeployabilityEnvelope check
- Model config passing all checks: verify DeployabilityReport.fits_envelope = true, no hard_failures

**Stale budget detection:**
- Create budget with runtime_nucleus_hash=A, then check against a build with hash=B: verify WARNING about stale budget
- Budget with matching hash: verify no warning

**Preflight diagnostic output:**
- Capture preflight log events, verify each check emits: check_name, status, detail, numeric_value, threshold
- Verify failing checks include: which dimension failed, how much by, and suggested fix (e.g., 'reduce d_ff from 224 to 208')

**Integration with DeployabilityEnvelope:**
- DeployabilityEnvelope says NormPlan::TileRmsThenAffineClip is supported, model uses it: PASS
- DeployabilityEnvelope says only AffineClipLut, model uses TileRmsThenAffineClip: FAIL
- Both envelope and budget must pass for overall PASS
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- budget_tests passes; Pass case -> fits=true; Fail case -> hard_failures with details; Stale detection warns on hash mismatch

title: F10: Training-Contract Test Suite and Diagnostic Logging
description: Comprehensive unit tests, integration tests, and E2E pipeline tests for all training-contract features. Includes structured logging infrastructure. Every implementation feature (F1-F9) has corresponding test coverage here. Tests use tiny model fixtures for fast iteration and produce detailed diagnostic output for debugging failures.
acceptance_criteria: All T10.x tasks closed; ./scripts/test_all.sh passes; Total runtime <60s

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F10: Training-Contract Test Suite and Diagnostic Logging

### Why this feature exists

The training-contract revision adds 47 implementation tasks across 9 features. Without a corresponding test suite, the first integration attempt will surface all the subtle disagreements between features simultaneously — a debugging nightmare. Each feature's contracts (type invariants, numeric agreements, gradient flow, byte formulas) need to be verified independently before integration.

### Test philosophy

1. **Tiny fixtures**: All tests use a micro MoE model (2 experts, d_model=8, d_ff=16, 2 layers, vocab=32). This trains in seconds on CPU and exercises every code path. Real-scale testing happens in gbf-bench, not here.

2. **Layered testing**: Unit tests verify individual modules in isolation. Integration tests verify feature interactions. E2E tests verify the full pipeline. Each layer catches different classes of bugs.

3. **Diagnostic output**: Every test emits structured logs that can be used to diagnose failures. Test failures include: which assertion failed, what the expected vs actual values were, the full state context (model config, TernaryWeightPlan, phase, step), and suggested investigation steps.

4. **Regression safety**: Snapshot/golden tests for byte formulas, export formats, and artifact hashes prevent silent regressions when any component changes.

### Test task dependency structure

- T10.1 (fixtures) and T10.2 (logging) have no deps — they're infrastructure
- Unit test tasks (T10.3-T10.10) each depend on the implementation tasks they test + T10.1
- Integration tests (T10.11-T10.12) depend on unit tests passing first
- E2E tests (T10.13-T10.15) depend on integration tests
- The regression script (T10.15) depends on everything

### Logging architecture

Structured logging using `tracing` crate with:
- Span-per-phase (DenseTeacherWarmup, RouterWarmup, etc.)
- Per-step loss term breakdown (each λ term logged individually)
- Phase transition events with before/after QAT hardness
- Export events with artifact hash, byte counts, TernaryWeightPlan summary
- Shadow compile events with fit/fail status, frontier point details
- Preflight events with per-check pass/fail and detailed diagnostics

All events use structured key-value fields, not free-form strings, so they can be queried programmatically in CI.

title: T10.12: E2E shadow compile pipeline test
description:
End-to-end test: shadow export/compile during training with frontier selection.

**Test steps:**
1. Use the micro model from T10.11
2. Configure ShadowCompilePolicy: every_n_steps=20, 1 CompileRequest (default profile), smoke workload, keep_frontier=3
3. Run training for 60 steps (through phases A-D, into E)
4. Shadow compile should fire at steps 20, 40, 60

**Verification per shadow compile point:**
- EMA weights are exported (not raw weights)
- ArtifactValidation passes on the exported artifact
- StaticBudgetReport runs and produces a valid budget.json
- If full compile runs: schedule_cost is populated in the frontier point
- CheckpointFrontierPoint has non-null quality, conformance, projected_fit

**Frontier tracking verification:**
- After 3 shadow compiles: frontier has ≤ 3 points
- Points are correctly ordered (later checkpoints generally have better quality on tiny data)
- No point with projected_fit.hard_failures is selected

**training_selection.json output:**
- Verify file is emitted after the training run completes
- Verify it contains the selected checkpoint, frontier, and reasoning
- Verify selected checkpoint is the best quality among fit-passing points

**Compile feedback loop:**
- If compiler_feedback.json is emitted by shadow compile, verify it can be parsed
- Verify feedback contains scope.artifact_core_hash matching the exported artifact

**Logging verification:**
- Verify 3 shadow_compile events in the log stream
- Each event has: checkpoint_id, compile_profile, fit_status, quality_summary
- Verify 1 frontier_selection event at end with selected_checkpoint_id
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- e2e_shadow_compile passes in <60s; Shadow compile produces CheckpointFrontierPoint; Pareto selection works

title: T10.11: E2E tiny-model train-to-export pipeline test
description:
End-to-end test: train a micro MoE model through all 5 phases, export artifact, verify correctness.

**Test steps:**
1. Create micro model: d_model=8, d_ff=16, n_experts=2, n_layers=2, vocab=32, alternating MoE
2. Configure 5-phase schedule with 10 steps per phase (50 steps total)
3. Create tiny training corpus (T10.1 fixtures)
4. Run full training loop through all 5 phases
5. At Phase A end: freeze dense teacher, verify teacher checkpoint exists
6. At Phase E: export ModelArtifact via ExportVisitor

**Verification after export:**
- ArtifactCore has correct structure: 2 expert sections, each with up/down CanonicalTensors + scale tensors
- ArtifactManifest has valid content hash
- TernaryWeightPlan matches config
- QuantSpec references the correct TernaryWeightPlan
- All tensor byte counts match TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost()
- ReferenceModelBundle exists and contains the dense teacher weights
- ReferenceProgram evaluates correctly on fixture prompts (compare against saved teacher forward pass)
- HintBundle contains ExportFacts with non-trivial values (expert_usage, temporal_switch, activation_ranges populated)
- ExportFacts.expert_payloads matches compute_expert_bytes()

**Artifact hash stability:**
- Run the entire pipeline twice with the same seed
- Verify ArtifactCore content hash is identical both times

**Logging verification:**
- Verify structured log stream contains: 5 phase_transition events, 50 loss_step events, 1 teacher_freeze event, 1 export_complete event
- Verify loss_step events show decreasing lm_loss trend (model is learning, even on tiny data)

This is the single most important test in the suite — if this passes, the basic training pipeline works.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- e2e_train_export passes in <30s; All 5 phases complete; Exported artifact valid; Deterministic (same seed = same hash)

title: F-B4: ObservationPlan (Stage 4)
description: Consumes SemanticCheckpointSchema; decides which semantic checkpoints/probes/metrics this build emits. Re-emits semantic_checkpoint_schema.json and operational_probe_schema.json.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B4: ObservationPlan (Stage 4)

### What this is

`ObservationPlan` (`planv0.md` line 1209) consumes the exported `SemanticCheckpointSchema` and decides "which semantic checkpoints are mandatory for this build, in what numerical domain, with what encoding, and which additional operational probes / metrics are enabled?" Explicitly, instead of letting checkpoint behavior be emergent from scheduler/backend/harness.

### Why this matters

Two wins: semantic checkpoint stability across schedule changes, and profile-tunable debug instrumentation that doesn't change semantic comparison contracts. Re-emits `semantic_checkpoint_schema.json` and a build-specific `operational_probe_schema.json` as part of the report pack.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct ObservationPlan {
    pub semantic: Vec<SemanticObservation>,
    pub probes: Vec<OperationalProbe>,
    pub metrics: Vec<MetricProbe>,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B3 GbInferIR (probe attachment points)
- F-A3.6 SemanticCheckpointId, SemanticCheckpointSchema
- F-A3.7 TraceProbeId, ProbeBudgetClass

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T-A6.4: gbf-store — archive / directory transport
description: Sequential (hash, length, bytes) records with header (magic GBLM/ARCH, pinset_count, blob_count, total_bytes). create/extract/list operations. Round-trippable for build/calibration transport across machines.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A6.4: `gbf-store` — archive / directory transport

**Target crate**: `gbf-store`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-store/src/archive.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 138.

### Background

To move a build (or a calibration set) between machines, you serialize the relevant blobs into one file. Archive format: sequential `(hash, length, bytes)` records with a header that lists pinsets included.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct ArchiveHeader {
    pub magic: [u8; 8],                                      // "GBLM\0ARCH"
    pub version: u8,
    pub pinset_count: u16,
    pub blob_count: u32,
    pub total_bytes: u64,
}

pub fn create_archive(
    store: &BlobStore,
    pinsets: &[Pinset],
    out: &mut dyn Write,
) -> io::Result<ArchiveHeader>;

pub fn extract_archive(
    in_: &mut dyn Read,
    store: &BlobStore,
) -> io::Result<ArchiveHeader>;

pub fn list_archive(in_: &mut dyn Read) -> io::Result<ArchiveContents>;

pub struct ArchiveContents {
    pub header: ArchiveHeader,
    pub pinsets: Vec<Pinset>,
    pub blobs: Vec<(Hash256, u64)>,                          // hash + length
}
```

Archives are round-trippable: `create_archive` then `extract_archive` produces the same blobs and pinsets. Test with a few-megabyte fixture.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-store
cargo test -p gbf-store -- archive::round_trip                # Create then extract preserves blobs + pinsets
cargo test -p gbf-store -- archive::list_without_extract       # Can inspect contents without writing
cargo test -p gbf-store -- archive::magic_bytes                # Header magic bytes correct
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §IV.3: Archive is bit-stable for the same inputs.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Moved acceptance owner from bd-22o

`bd-22o` now proves byte-stable JSON serde for the in-memory `ExportedQatArtifact` boundary. Directory/CAS transport for an `ExportedQatArtifact` or its successor artifact envelope is owned here, together with the store/archive layer: write artifact bytes into the `blobs/sha256` layout, load them through the artifact/store loader, re-export them, and compare the resulting directory blobs byte-for-byte.

comments.author: bkase

title: T0.3: Define core shared types in gbf-foundation
description:
Define the core shared types that many crates reference but nobody explicitly creates. Without these, downstream tasks (T1.2, T3.1, T6.1, T7.1, T2.1, etc.) will each need to invent their own placeholder types, leading to inconsistency.

Types to define in gbf-foundation/src/lib.rs (or submodules):
- Hash256: [u8; 32] newtype with hex display, from_bytes, as_bytes
- ByteCost: u64 newtype with Display, Add, Sub, comparison
- TargetProfileId, CompileProfileId, TargetFamilyId: typed string wrappers
- LayerId, ExpertId: u16 newtypes with From<u16>
- BudgetSlotId: typed id
- CheckpointId, WorkloadId, CalibrationSetRef ids
- SemVer: simple semver struct
- Common derives: Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize on all

These are deliberately minimal newtypes. The point is to prevent raw u16/String misuse across crate boundaries (CONSTITUTION §I.1: make invalid states unrepresentable).
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-foundation exits 0; All newtypes have Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize; Hash256 displays as hex; ByteCost supports Add/Sub; LayerId/ExpertId prevent raw u16 misuse at compile time; cargo test -p gbf-foundation passes
close_reason: Core foundation hash, cost, typed id, and semver primitives implemented with tests.

comments.author: bkase

title: Implement Burn-backed learned and EMA activation range state
description: ActFakeQuantBurnQat currently accepts only Fixed activation ranges. Implement Burn-owned learned clip-bound params and/or live EMA observation updates for ActivationRangeMode::Learned and ActivationRangeMode::Ema, with autodiff tests proving learned range gradients and boundary tests proving EMA update/export behavior. Until this closes, Burn adapter constructors must reject non-Fixed range modes.

title: F-D3: Trace Pipeline
description: TraceEvent ring buffer in SRAM, host drain channel, TraceBudget enforcement, trace_loss_report.json + trace_perturbation_report.json. Drop policy honored under budget pressure.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-D3: Trace Pipeline

### What this is

`TraceEvent` ring buffer in SRAM, drain-to-host channel, `TraceBudget` enforcement, `trace_loss_report.json`, `trace_perturbation_report.json`. ROM emits structured trace events; host harness drains them.

### Why this matters

Observability per `§V.1`. Without structured traces, debugging a long inference run on cartridge means cycle-counter staring.

### Design sketch

`gbf-runtime/src/trace.rs` (currently a stub). Lock-free ring buffer (single-producer, single-consumer) in SRAM. Drain via harness's `DumpArena`. Drop policy honored under budget pressure. Perturbation reports under `ObservabilityMode::Invariant` builds.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A3.7 TraceEvent + TraceBudget
- F-D2 harness (drain channel)
- F-F1 build reports (trace_loss_report.json, trace_perturbation_report.json)

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T3.3: Export scale tensors as first-class CanonicalTensors
description: Scale tensors (per-row or per-group) must be exported as named CanonicalTensors in ArtifactCore, not silently baked into packed representations or left as float metadata. The ExportVisitor (T1.6) must emit: one ternary weight CanonicalTensor + one scale CanonicalTensor per projection. Scale tensors themselves are fake-quantized during training (to the declared ScaleFormat) and exported in that quantized form.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export passes; Separate CanonicalTensor for weights and scales; Deterministic tensor naming
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

- Artifact contract: ternary scale data is exported as a named `CanonicalTensor` with `CanonicalTensorKind::TernaryScale`, `TensorElementType::Q8_8`, deterministic `<projection>.scale` identity, and a `TernaryQuantEntry::scale` reference from the projection. `ArtifactCore::new` validates scale kind, element type, and shape against the quant plan.
- Differentiable Burn path: no new Burn path in this bead. Current scale state is owned by `TernaryLinearQat` and Burn adapter tests already prove export-from-trained-state for the supported Q8_8 path. Non-Q8_8 scale tensor formats are moved to `bd-38om`.
- Tests proving it: `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export`, `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core`, `cargo check -p gbf-model`, `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings`, `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`, `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`.
- Acceptance movement: bd-g90 already introduced the basic separate scale tensor export. bd-209 adds the stronger regression oracle requested by its acceptance: deterministic scale naming, raw Q8_8 scale payload checks, and reconstruction of projected quantized weights from exported weight plus scale tensors. Non-Q8_8 ScaleFormat encodings are rejected today and moved to `bd-38om`.

## Review Feedback Disposition

- Intent review: agreed that bd-209 is now a proof/regression bead because bd-g90 introduced the base ExportVisitor behavior. Closure names bd-g90 as prior owner and bd-209 as the stronger oracle.
- Intent/design/evidence review: agreed that the task wording over-implied all `ScaleFormat` values. Added `artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_q8_8_scale_formats_until_tensor_encoding_exists` and opened `bd-38om` for Q4_4/Pow2 tensor encodings.
- Semantic review: no code findings. It confirmed the current visitor emits separate `projection.weight` and `projection.scale` tensors and artifact validation checks kind/type/shape.
- Evidence review: agreed evidence only covers current per-output-row Q8_8 export. Closure marks that as supported today and moves non-Q8_8 support to `bd-38om`.

## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| bd-209 duplicated bd-g90's base export claim | The ready bead lagged behind implementation introduced by an earlier bead | Added QAT closure guide: proof beads must name the earlier owner and add a stronger regression oracle | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` now reconstructs projected weights from exported tensors | `.agents/skills/qat-bead-closure/SKILL.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| Non-Q8_8 scale formats could be implied without tensor support | Public `ScaleFormat` variants outpaced `TensorElementType` encodings | Added QAT closure guide: declared quant plan must match artifact tensor encoding, reject unsupported variants | `artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_q8_8_scale_formats_until_tensor_encoding_exists` | `.agents/skills/qat-bead-closure/SKILL.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core` |
| Round-trip oracle was vague | Acceptance did not define what reconstruction must match | Closure narrows oracle to current Q8_8 path: exported weight + scale tensors reconstruct `export_canonical().projected_weights()` | Direct reconstruction assertion in `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| Training fake-quant wording exceeded export target files | The bead mixed export representation with training scale schedule/format support | Mark non-Q8_8 training/export format support moved to `bd-38om`; current Burn path remains existing Q8_8 export-from-trained-state | No new Burn sensor in bd-209; existing hook runs Burn adapter suite | `bd-38om`; `cargo test --workspace --all-features` hook |

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Per-output-row Q8_8 ternary scales | supported | supported by existing Burn adapter export tests | supported as first-class `CanonicalTensor` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export`; hook `cargo test --workspace --all-features` |
| Deterministic `<projection>.scale` naming | supported through `ArtifactPath::join("scale")` | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` |
| Reconstruct projected weights from exported tensors | supported for Q8_8 per-row | not applicable | supported in export regression test | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` |
| Q4_4/Pow2 scale formats | rejected/moved | moved to `bd-38om` | rejected/moved to `bd-38om` | `artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_q8_8_scale_formats_until_tensor_encoding_exists`; `bd-38om` |
| Per-group Q8_8 artifact shape validation | not produced by current scalar exporter | moved to future non-default plan support | schema validation supported if supplied directly | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core` |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Export emits separate named weight and scale tensors per ternary projection | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` | none | Checks `projection.weight` and `projection.scale` as separate canonical tensors |
| Scale tensor stores quantized Q8_8 payload, not float metadata | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` | none | Checks raw Q8_8 scale payload and element type |
| Exported weight plus scale tensors reconstruct deployed quantized projection | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` | none | Reconstructs projected weights from exported tensors and compares to `export_canonical()` |
| ArtifactCore rejects unsupported declared scale formats | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core` | none | Rejects Q4_4/Pow2 until `bd-38om` adds encodings |
| gbf-model remains Burn-free and QAT backend seams stay contained | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Architecture invariant still passes |
| QAT closure/harness rules stay satisfied | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` | none | Harness passed before closure and will be rerun after sync |

## No-Future Variant Rule

bd-209 accepts and proves only the current per-output-row Q8_8 scale export path. Non-Q8_8 scale formats are rejected at the artifact boundary today and owned by `bd-38om`. Per-group production export is not claimed by this bead.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model` (export path), `gbf-artifact` (tensor types)
**Target file(s)**:
- `gbf-model/src/qat/export.rs` (modify ExportVisitor)
- `gbf-artifact/src/tensor.rs` (if CanonicalTensor needs updates)

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "scale tensors as first-class CanonicalTensors", "CanonicalTensor".

### Implementation Notes
- Scale tensors MUST be exported as **named CanonicalTensors** in ArtifactCore.
- NOT baked into packed representations — they are first-class citizens.
- ExportVisitor emits per projection layer:
  1. One ternary weight CanonicalTensor (e.g., `"layer.0.expert.0.up.weight"`)
  2. One scale CanonicalTensor (e.g., `"layer.0.expert.0.up.scale"`)
- Naming convention must be deterministic and stable across exports.
- This ensures the compiler can independently reason about weight data vs scale data.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export            # Unit tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: export produces separate CanonicalTensor for weights and scales
# Verify: tensor names follow a deterministic naming convention
# Verify: round-trip — exported scales can reconstruct the quantized representation
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: Tensor naming should use structured ids, not ad-hoc strings
- §IV.3: Byte-identical output for same weights — naming is part of the identity
