title: F-B6: StoragePlan (Stage 6) — the bridge
description: Bridge between value/effect semantics and spatial scheduling. Picks Materialization (Recompute / Materialize{class, lifetime} / Persist{page, commit_group}) per value. Recompute-vs-spill becomes an explicit decision.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B6: StoragePlan (Stage 6) — the bridge

### What this is

The bridge between value/effect semantics and spatial scheduling (`planv0.md` line 1256). Decides which values are materialized, which are recomputed, what their lifetime class is (`Slice` / `ResumeWindow` / `Token` / `Session` / `Persistent`), and which storage class they require (`WramHot` / `HramHot` / `SramPaged` / `RomConst`). Does **not** assign concrete byte offsets — that's `ArenaPlan`.

### Why this matters

Recomputation-vs-spill becomes an explicit decision rather than a side effect of buffer lowering. Schedule equivalence becomes easier to reason about.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub enum Materialization {
    Recompute,
    Materialize { class: StorageClass, lifetime: LifetimeClass },
    Persist { page: PersistPageId, commit_group: CommitGroupId },
}

pub struct StorageBinding {
    pub value: ValueId,
    pub materialization: Materialization,
    pub alias_class: AliasClassId,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B3 GbInferIR
- F-B5 RangePlan (different reductions imply different scratch)
- F-B14 FeasibilityRefinementLoop consumes recompute_promotion proposals from here

### Confidence: HIGH

title: F-A3: gbf-abi — Live Execution Contract Types
description: The repr(C) live-execution contract per planv0.md line 271: AbiVersion, CompatibilityEnvelope, BuildIdentityBlock, InferenceState with liveness counters, harness command/result blocks, fault types, InterruptPolicy + ResourceLease, SemanticCheckpointId, TraceEvent. Compiler, runtime, harness, emulator adapters, and ScheduleOracle all meet here.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-A3: `gbf-abi` — Live Execution Contract Types

### Background

`gbf-abi` owns the **shared runtime/compiler contract that is not durable model data** (`planv0.md` line 271): `InferenceState` (with liveness fields), `HarnessCommandBlock`, `HarnessResultBlock`, `FaultCode` (including liveness faults), `SemanticCheckpointId`, `CompactCheckpointId`, `InterruptPolicy`, `TraceEvent`, `AbiVersion`, `CompatibilityEnvelope`, `BuildIdentityBlock`, `FaultSnapshot`.

`gbf-artifact` is the durable offline contract; `gbf-abi` is the **live execution contract**. Having a separate crate with `#[repr(C)]` layouts and compile-time layout assertions reduces drift between compiler, runtime, harness, emulator adapters, and `ScheduleOracle`.

### How this serves overarching project goals

- Compiler-emitted code uses `gbf-abi` types as the calling convention boundary into the runtime.
- `ScheduleOracle` (Epic C) asserts execution semantics against the same `SemanticCheckpointId`s the runtime emits.
- The harness mode (Epic D) marshals `HarnessCommandBlock` / `HarnessResultBlock` from host to ROM.
- Emulator adapters (Epic H) parse `TraceEvent`s from a memory-mapped trace buffer using these layouts.

### The problem this solves

Without `gbf-abi`:
- Three crates each invent their own struct for "the thing that survives a yield" (continuation), and the layouts diverge silently across `#[repr(C)]` boundaries.
- `FaultCode` becomes a magic `u8` with three different lookup tables.
- Harness command IDs collide between test code and ROM code.

### Design

Modules (matching the existing scaffold):

```
gbf-abi/src/
  version.rs      # AbiVersion, CompatibilityEnvelope, BuildIdentityBlock
  continuation.rs # InferenceState (continuation record stored in WRAM/SRAM)
  liveness.rs     # progress_epoch, last_checkpoint, no_progress_frames + LivenessFault
  harness.rs      # HarnessCommandBlock, HarnessResultBlock, HarnessOp, HarnessResultKind
  fault.rs        # FaultCode, FaultDomain, FaultSnapshot, FaultPolicy
  interrupt.rs    # InterruptPolicy, ResourceLease, ResourceLeaseKind
  checkpoint.rs   # SemanticCheckpointId, CompactCheckpointId, SemanticCheckpointSchema
  trace.rs        # TraceEvent, TraceProbeId, ProbeBudgetClass, ProbeLevel
```

All types `#[repr(C)]` where the runtime sees them as memory layouts; `serde::{Serialize, Deserialize}` for host-side tooling. `gbf-abi` is `no_std + alloc` capable.

### A note on liveness

`planv0.md` line 1795 establishes that "a cooperative runtime can be locally safe and globally broken (livelock, oscillation between tiny slices with no semantic progress, repeatedly revisiting the same checkpoint, starving generation while the UI runs smoothly). Liveness is not optional." This is why `InferenceState` carries `progress_epoch`, `last_checkpoint`, and `no_progress_frames`. Liveness faults are first-class `FaultCode`s.

### Children (T-A3.1 ... T-A3.7)

1. **T-A3.1** `AbiVersion` + `CompatibilityEnvelope` + `BuildIdentityBlock`.
2. **T-A3.2** `InferenceState` + liveness counters.
3. **T-A3.3** Harness command/result blocks.
4. **T-A3.4** `FaultCode` family + `FaultSnapshot` + `FaultPolicy`.
5. **T-A3.5** `InterruptPolicy` + `ResourceLease` + `ResourceLeaseKind`.
6. **T-A3.6** `SemanticCheckpointId` + `CompactCheckpointId` + `SemanticCheckpointSchema`.
7. **T-A3.7** `TraceEvent` + probe ids.

### Confidence: HIGH

The shapes come straight from the plan. The main risk is missing a layout-stability assertion (e.g., a `#[repr(C)]` struct that's not actually layout-stable across host/target). Use `static_assertions` or explicit `size_of` tests.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T-A3.1–T-A3.7.

### Feature Definition of Done

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-abi                                           # All module tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- layout                                  # repr(C) sizes match expected (see per-task tests)
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- version::compatibility_envelope         # AbiVersion / CompatibilityEnvelope semantics
cargo clippy -p gbf-abi -- -D warnings                           # Clean
```

title: T-A1.5: Cycle model — per-instruction M-cycle costs
description: Per-Instr-variant M-cycle cost (taken/not-taken for branches). Consumed by ScheduleCostAnalysis (Epic B), gbf-bench (Epic E), and the cooperative scheduler slice budgets (F-A5.2). Spot-check ~30 instructions vs Pan Docs.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A1.5: Cycle model

**Target crate**: `gbf-asm`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-asm/src/cycle_model.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` §"Assembly eDSL" (line 2046), Pan Docs LR35902 timing tables.

### Background

The cycle model is consumed by `ScheduleCostAnalysis` (Epic B), `gbf-bench` (Epic E), and the cooperative scheduler (F-A5.2 — slice cycle budgets). Every `Instr` variant must have a deterministic M-cycle cost (with branch-taken vs not-taken disambiguated where applicable).

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub enum CycleCost {
    Fixed(u8),                                    // M-cycles
    Branch { taken: u8, not_taken: u8 },           // for conditional ops
}

pub fn cycle_cost(instr: &Instr) -> CycleCost;
```

Reference: Pan Docs lists every opcode's M-cycle count. Some highlights:
- `Nop` = 1 M-cycle.
- `Ld8 { dst: Reg, src: Reg }` = 1 M-cycle.
- `Ld8 { dst: Reg, src: Imm }` = 2 M-cycles.
- `Ld8 { dst: HlInd, src: Reg }` = 2 M-cycles.
- `JpAbs { cond: None, ... }` = 4 M-cycles.
- `JrRel { cond: Some(_), ... }` = 3 / 2 (taken / not-taken).
- `Call { cond: Some(_), ... }` = 6 / 3.

Use a single static lookup table indexed by `Instr` discriminant (or compute structurally). Tests fail if any variant returns `CycleCost::Fixed(0)` (a typo trap).

The cycle model is **target-cycle accurate at the M-cycle level**, not at the T-state level. Multiply by 4 for T-states if needed externally.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-asm
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- cycle_model::known_instructions    # Spot-check ~30 instructions vs Pan Docs
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- cycle_model::no_zero_cost           # No instruction has zero cost
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- cycle_model::branch_invariant       # Branch costs satisfy taken >= not_taken (always +1 cycle for taking the jump)
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `CycleCost` enum makes "this instruction has variable cost" explicit.
- §V.3: Cycle model is the load-bearing input for slice budgets and bench reports — wrong values = silently wrong cycle budgets.

title: F-D6: SchedulePack Mode Switching (BLOCKED on oracle)
description: Runtime side of SchedulePack: ModeSwitchPolicy (legal switch points, UI-pressure thresholds, drift triggers, safe-mode triggers), runtime mode-state, transition logic. BLOCKED on RuntimeMode-cadence oracle question.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-D6: SchedulePack Mode Switching (runtime side)

### What this is

The runtime side of `SchedulePack`: `ModeSwitchPolicy` (legal switch points, UI-pressure thresholds, drift triggers, safe-mode triggers), runtime mode-state, transition logic. Switches modes only at declared safe boundaries.

### **Open question for the oracle**

How many `RuntimeMode`s should `SchedulePack` carry? The plan defines four (`Interactive`, `Steady`, `Trace`, `Safe`) but doesn't say which are required at which milestone. Drafted oracle question pending.

### Why this matters

A single `CompiledBuild` carrying multiple modes lets the runtime adapt — interactive typing wants short slices; steady-state generation wants throughput; trace mode wants probes; safe mode is the fallback under fault. Without F-D6 this is just a typed shell.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct ModeSwitchPolicy {
    pub legal_switch_points: Vec<SemanticCheckpointId>,
    pub legal_epoch_boundaries: Vec<EpochId>,
    pub ui_pressure_thresholds: Vec<UiPressureThreshold>,
    pub safe_mode_triggers: Vec<SafeModeTrigger>,
    pub drift_triggers: Vec<DriftTrigger>,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B11 GbSchedIR / SchedulePack
- F-D4 RuntimeDriftMonitor (consumer of DriftAction::DemoteMode)
- F-D5 fault policy (Safe mode triggers)

### Confidence: BLOCKED on RuntimeMode-cadence oracle question

title: F5: Honest Loss Function — Only Penalize What Is Differentiable
description: Replace the old lambda_bank/lambda_locality phantom-gradient terms with an honest loss: lambda_distill, lambda_balance, lambda_zrouter, lambda_switch, lambda_range, lambda_zero. Gate lambda_shape/lambda_overflow on ExpertShapePolicy since they are meaningless for fixed-shape Ternary2. M6-scoped.
acceptance_criteria: All T5.x tasks closed; All loss terms tested with gradient flow verification

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F5: Honest Loss Function — Only Penalize What Is Differentiable

### Background

The original plan included:
```
loss = lm_loss + λ_zero * sparsity_regularizer + λ_bank * expert_budget_penalty + λ_locality * router_locality_penalty + λ_range * activation_range_penalty
```

This looks reasonable but is mathematically dishonest in two ways:

1. **λ_bank * expert_budget_penalty**: For fixed-shape Ternary2 experts (the default), the byte cost of an expert is `ceil(rows*cols/4) + scale_bytes + metadata_bytes`. This is a function of the architecture dimensions, NOT of the learned weights. No gradient can flow through it. The penalty is a constant during training — it's either satisfied or not at architecture-definition time. Making it a loss term is cargo-cult optimization.

2. **λ_locality * router_locality_penalty**: "Locality" is vague. Spatial locality (nearby tokens use nearby experts)? Temporal locality (consecutive tokens reuse the same expert)? The deployment cost is temporal — a bank switch happens when token t routes to a different expert than token t-1 in the same layer. The old term didn't specify which kind.

### The corrected loss

```
loss = lm_loss + λ_distill * logit_distillation_loss + λ_balance * expert_load_balance_loss + λ_zrouter * router_z_loss + λ_switch * temporal_switch_penalty + λ_range * activation_range_penalty + λ_zero * ternary_zero_regularizer + λ_shape * structured_shape_penalty + λ_overflow * bank_overflow_penalty
```

Each term is honest about what it can influence:

- **λ_distill**: KL divergence from frozen dense teacher — always meaningful during QAT phases
- **λ_balance**: Standard expert load-balance loss (entropy-based) — prevents expert collapse
- **λ_zrouter**: Router z-loss (penalizes large logits before softmax) — stabilizes training
- **λ_switch**: L_switch = (1/LT) Σ_{l,t} (1 - ⟨p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1}⟩) — directly penalizes the deployment cost (bank switches), and the gradient flows through the router
- **λ_range**: Penalizes activations outside the safe integer range — gradient flows through the activations
- **λ_zero**: Penalizes non-zero ternary weights — gradient flows through the pre-threshold weights
- **λ_shape** (DISABLED for fixed-shape): Penalizes non-bank-fitting expert widths — only meaningful for StructuredWidthGates
- **λ_overflow** (DISABLED for fixed-shape): Penalizes total byte cost exceeding bank capacity — only meaningful when byte cost depends on learned structure

### The temporal switch penalty specifically

L_switch = (1/LT) Σ_{l,t} (1 - ⟨p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1}⟩)

where p_{l,t} is the soft expert distribution for layer l, token t. When consecutive tokens pick the same expert, the inner product is 1 and the penalty is 0. When they pick different experts, the inner product decreases and the penalty increases. This is a differentiable proxy for the actual bank-switch count that will be seen at deployment.

### M6 scope

All these loss terms are M6-scoped except λ_balance and λ_zrouter which are standard MoE training practice and should be included from Phase B onward. The other terms are tuning features that require a working model + compiler to evaluate.

### Confidence: VERY HIGH

The mathematical argument is airtight. The old loss had phantom gradients; the new one doesn't.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
IMPORTANT NOTE FOR IMPLEMENTERS: Although F5 is P3 and labeled M6, two of its tasks are needed much earlier:

- T5.2 (z-loss + load-balance, P2): Standard MoE training losses needed from Phase B (RouterWarmup) onward. This is M3-level work. It's ready NOW with no blockers.
- T5.6 (loss config, P2): Config infrastructure for all loss terms. Also M3-level. Ready NOW.

Don't skip F5 entirely based on its P3 feature priority. Grab T5.2 and T5.6 early alongside F1/F6 implementation work. The truly M6-scoped tasks are T5.1 (temporal switch, P3), T5.3 (range penalty, P3), T5.4 (zero regularizer, P3), and T5.5 (shape gating, P4).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T5.1 through T5.6.

**Target crate(s)**: `gbf-train` (loss functions)
**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "honest loss function", "phantom gradient", "differentiable".

### Feature Definition of Done
All child tasks (T5.1-T5.6) closed AND:
```bash
cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss                    # All loss tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-test -- loss_tests               # Gradient flow tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-test -- switch_loss_tests        # L_switch tests pass
```

### IMPORTANT: T5.2 and T5.6 are M3-needed despite F5 being P3
The z-loss, load-balance, and loss config tasks are needed for M3 training.
Only the M6-specific terms (L_switch, range, zero, shape, overflow) are truly deferred.

title: T-A3.3: Harness command/result blocks
description: repr(C) HarnessCommandBlock + HarnessResultBlock with HarnessOp (Nop/StepSlice/RunUntilCheckpoint/DumpArena/InjectFault/PowerCut/SetSession/GetState) and HarnessResultKind. Harness writes commands at known SRAM offset; ROM polls + writes results.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A3.3: Harness command/result blocks

**Target crate**: `gbf-abi`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-abi/src/harness.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 271, the harness control plane note in line 2554 (rev pass: "(8) turn harness mode into a real control plane with `StepSlice`, `RunUntilCheckpoint`, `DumpArena`, `InjectFault`, `PowerCut` ops").

### Background

The harness is the host-side test driver for ROMs running under emulator (or real hardware over a serial cable). It writes commands to a known SRAM block, polls the result block. This task defines those blocks. The full harness implementation (Epic D) consumes them.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
#[repr(C)]
pub struct HarnessCommandBlock {
    pub magic: [u8; 4],                                           // "HCMD"
    pub seq: u32,                                                 // monotonic command sequence number
    pub op: HarnessOp,
    pub args: [u8; 32],
}

#[repr(C)]
pub struct HarnessResultBlock {
    pub magic: [u8; 4],                                           // "HRES"
    pub seq: u32,                                                 // matches command seq
    pub kind: HarnessResultKind,
    pub data: [u8; 32],
}

#[repr(u16)]
pub enum HarnessOp {
    Nop                 = 0,
    StepSlice           = 1,
    RunUntilCheckpoint  = 2,
    DumpArena           = 3,
    InjectFault         = 4,
    PowerCut            = 5,                                      // simulated power-loss
    SetSession          = 6,
    GetState            = 7,
}

#[repr(u16)]
pub enum HarnessResultKind {
    Ok                  = 0,
    Done                = 1,
    Fault               = 2,
    NotImplemented      = 3,
    InvalidArgs         = 4,
}
```

These blocks live at a known SRAM offset (set in T-A3.6 / build product). Harness writes command, sets some "doorbell" bit; ROM polls the doorbell, executes, writes result, clears doorbell.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-abi
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- harness::layout                       # HarnessCommandBlock / ResultBlock have expected size
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- harness::op_kind_complete              # HarnessOp covers all the planv0.md op set
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- harness::magic_bytes                   # "HCMD" / "HRES"
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: HarnessOp / HarnessResultKind are enumerated.
- §VI.1: Single source of harness ABI.

title: T-A1.7: Encoder — the only path from Instr to bytes
description: Per planv0.md engineering rule 2: the only module that translates legal instructions to bytes. Lowers pseudo-ops (BankLease -> banking primitives, FarCall -> in-bank or thunk, Yield -> scheduler call, TraceProbe -> trace emit, AssertBank -> debug-only). Bit-stable across runs.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A1.7: Encoder

**Target crate**: `gbf-asm`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-asm/src/encoder.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` §"Assembly eDSL", §"Engineering rules" rule 2 ("Only the encoder translates legal instructions to bytes").

### Background

The encoder is the **only** module that turns `Instr` into bytes. It must be deterministic, bit-stable across runs (so blob hashes are stable per `§IV.3`), and exhaustive over the ISA defined in T-A1.1.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct EncodedSection {
    pub id: SectionId,
    pub bytes: Vec<u8>,
    pub instr_offsets: Vec<(InstrIdx, u16)>,    // for listings + provenance lookup
}

pub fn encode_section(
    section: &Section,
    placed: &PlacedSection,
    symbols: &SymbolTable,
) -> Result<EncodedSection, EncodeError>;

pub fn encode_instr(instr: &Instr, here: u16, symbols: &SymbolTable) -> Result<SmallVec<[u8; 4]>, EncodeError>;
```

Pseudo-ops are lowered here:
- `BankLease { ... }` → calls into the F-A4 acquire primitives (the actual bytes are the runtime's banking module entry-point + arguments).
- `FarCall { target, lease_chain }` → either an in-bank `CALL` or a far-call thunk (depending on T-A1.6 relaxation result).
- `Yield { kind }` → call into `gbf-runtime::scheduler::yield_with(kind)`.
- `TraceProbe { id, level }` → call into trace-emit primitive (no-op when trace is disabled).
- `AssertBank` → debug-build-only; release build emits no bytes.
- `Db(u8)` / `Dw(u16)` → straight bytes.

Determinism: every encoding decision must be data-dependent only — no hash-map iteration order, no SystemTime, no thread-local state.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-asm
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- encoder::known_opcodes               # ~30 spot-checks vs Pan Docs
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- encoder::round_trip                   # encode -> decode (where decoder exists) preserves Instr
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- encoder::bit_stable                   # Encoding the same Section twice yields identical bytes
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- encoder::pseudo_op_lowering           # BankLease/FarCall/Yield/TraceProbe lower correctly
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- encoder::assert_bank_release_noop     # AssertBank emits 0 bytes in release builds
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `EncodeError` enumerates exact failure modes.
- §IV.3: Bit-stability is asserted by test.
- §VI.1: The encoder is the only path to bytes.

title: T9.2: Implement StructuredWidthGates supernet training (M6)
description: M6 research: train experts with max d_ff, but each (row_group x col_group) block has a learnable sigmoid gate. During training, the gate values are annealed from 1.0 toward 0.0 using a temperature schedule. The lambda_shape penalty encourages gates to close (producing sparser experts). The lambda_overflow penalty penalizes total exported bytes exceeding the bank budget. The gradient flows through the gate values and the STE-projected ternary weights.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check passes; Gates are learnable parameters; Sigmoid annealing sharpens over steps; Pruning produces valid smaller experts

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model` + `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**:
- `gbf-model/src/qat/expert.rs` (extend with width gates)
- `gbf-train/src/supernet.rs` (new — supernet training logic)

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "StructuredWidthGates", "supernet", "learnable sigmoid gates", "M6 research".

### Implementation Notes
- M6 research mode: train experts with max d_ff, with learnable sigmoid gates per block group.
- Gates: `sigmoid(alpha_k)` per group of `col_group` columns; during training, multiply expert activations by gate values.
- Annealing: gradually sharpen sigmoid (increase temperature) during training to force gates toward 0/1.
- Loss terms: lambda_shape (penalize non-binary gates), lambda_overflow (penalize experts exceeding bank budget).
- After training: prune blocks with gate < threshold, yielding variable-size experts.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                       # Compiles
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -- supernet                          # Tests pass
# Verify: gates are learnable parameters
# Verify: sigmoid annealing sharpens over steps
# Verify: pruning produces valid smaller experts
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: ExpertShapePolicy::StructuredWidthGates is the only entry point for this behavior
- §II.3: This is M6 research — keep it isolated from the main training path

title: F-B2: StaticBudgetReport (Stage 2)
description: Pre-lowering sanity check: per-expert payload fits, projected WRAM/SRAM/HRAM, accumulator maxima, predicted bank-switch counts per token, common-bank footprint. Absorbs existing T3.4 (bd-w80) byte math.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B2: StaticBudgetReport (Stage 2)

### What this is

Pre-lowering sanity check (`planv0.md` line 1169). Answers: does each expert fit under the requested placement profile? What are projected WRAM/SRAM/HRAM needs? Predicted accumulator maxima? Predicted bank-switch counts per token? Likely common-bank footprint?

### Why this matters

If a budget is busted here, you get a useful error before committing to lowering. This is the second shift-left filter (after `gbf-train` preflight against `DeployabilityEnvelope` + `RuntimeChromeBudget`).

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct StaticBudgetReport {
    pub per_expert_payload: BTreeMap<ExpertId, ByteBudget>,
    pub projected_wram: ProjectedSize,
    pub projected_sram: ProjectedSize,
    pub projected_hram: ProjectedSize,
    pub accumulator_maxima: AccumulatorBounds,
    pub projected_bank_switches_per_token: u16,
    pub common_bank_footprint: ByteBudget,
    pub fits: bool,
    pub failures: Vec<BudgetFailure>,
}
```

Existing bead `bd-w80` (T3.4) updates byte math for `TernaryWeightPlan`; absorb into this feature.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B1 QuantGraph (consumed)
- F11 / bd-1i8 CompileProfile (consumed for switch cap)
- F2 / bd-1hv RuntimeChromeBudget (compared against)

### Confidence: HIGH

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: bd-w80 handoff: full compiler StaticBudgetReport remains owned by this Stage 2 feature. bd-w80 only proves model-owned canonical expert byte math and a single selected byte-limit fit report. Multi-expert reports, total ROM usage, per-bank margins, slot identity, placement caps, projected WRAM/SRAM/HRAM, accumulator maxima, and projected bank-switch counts belong here once QuantGraph, CompileProfile, and RuntimeChromeBudget surfaces exist.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: bd-33q review handoff: optional shared dense FFN common-bank footprint/residency is owned here as part of StaticBudgetReport common_bank_footprint, not by the F6 model-core closure. When Stage 2 is implemented, include shared dense up/down/alpha common-bank bytes and distinguish them from per-expert slot bytes; tied embedding/classifier export aliasing remains bd-3bf1.

title: T10.8: Router and temporal smoothness tests (F7 coverage)
description:
Unit tests for router training features:

**Temporal smoothness boundary handling:**
- Sequence of 8 tokens, all routing to expert 0: verify L_switch = 0
- Sequence of 8 tokens, alternating expert 0 and 1: verify L_switch ≈ 1.0
- Two sequences in same batch (tokens [0-3] = seq A, [4-7] = seq B): verify no cross-sequence penalty
- First token of each sequence: verify no penalty (no previous token to compare against)
- Gradient: verify gradient flows through the router softmax for tokens 1+ but not token 0 of each sequence

**Expert dropout tests:**
- Set dropout rate 0.5, run forward 100 times: verify each expert is dropped ~50% of the time (within 3σ)
- Dropout rate 0.0: verify no experts dropped
- Dropout disabled at eval time: verify eval() forward never drops experts
- Gradient through dropped experts: verify gradient is zero for dropped, non-zero for active

**Low-rank factorization tests:**
- Full-rank router (d_model=8 -> n_experts=4): compute output
- Low-rank router (d_model=8 -> rank=2 -> n_experts=4): verify shape is correct
- Verify low-rank param count < full-rank param count
- Gradient flow: verify gradients reach both low-rank matrices

**Temporal switch digest collection:**
- Run model on tiny corpus, collect TemporalSwitchDigest
- Verify same_expert_rate is between 0 and 256 (Q8.8 format)
- Verify transition_mass entries sum to ≤ 256
- Verify n_layers digests are produced

All tests log: n_experts, routing distribution per token, switch rate, dropout mask when applicable.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- router_tests passes; Temporal buffer handles boundaries; Dropout matches config; Low-rank matches full-rank within tolerance

title: T10.5: Model config and instantiation tests (F6 coverage)
description:
Unit tests for model architecture construction:

**Block structure tests:**
- Config with alternating(start=0, stride=2) on 4 layers: verify blocks 0,2 have MoE FFN, blocks 1,3 have shared dense FFN
- Config with middle(first=1, last=2) on 4 layers: verify blocks 1,2 have MoE, others don't
- Config with explicit([1,3]) on 4 layers: verify exactly blocks 1,3 have MoE
- All configs: verify sequence-state path is always shared (never MoE)

**Tied embedding tests:**
- Create model with tied=true: verify embed.weight and classifier.weight are the same Tensor object (not just equal values)
- Forward through embed then classify: verify gradient flows to both paths through a single weight
- Gradient test: compute loss, backward, verify embed.weight.grad is non-zero and reflects both embedding and classifier contributions
- Untied config: verify two separate weight tensors, different gradients

**Two-matrix expert structure tests:**
- Create ExpertBlockQat: verify it contains exactly up + down projections (no gate projection)
- Forward shape: input [batch, d_model] -> output [batch, d_model]
- GLU config with warning: verify GLU=true emits a log warning about bank fit

**Expert byte budget formula tests:**
- compute_expert_bytes(d_model=128, d_ff=224, Ternary2, PerOutputRow, Q8_8): verify = 15040 + metadata
- compute_expert_bytes must equal TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost(up) + compute_byte_cost(down) + metadata_bytes
- Cross-check: the two independent paths to computing byte cost must agree

All tests log: model config summary, block structure, parameter counts, byte costs.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- model_tests passes; MoE on FFN path only; Tied embedding verified; Two-matrix structure verified; Byte budget cross-check
close_reason:
Implemented `bd-2qj` as F6 model config and instantiation coverage.

Scope owned here:

- `gbf-train::embeddings` adds a small Burn-backed embedding/classifier adapter so the tied/untied gradient acceptance is tested with real `.backward()` and `.grad()` behavior instead of manual arithmetic.
- `gbf-test::fixtures::tiny_expert` is public so the integration tests reuse the T10.1 tiny fixture construction instead of duplicating expert builders.

QAT Closure Checklist

Export/artifact tied embedding aliasing remains owned by `bd-3bf1`; final ArtifactOracle agreement remains owned by `bd-12c`.

The implementation owner `bd-x75` exposes this as a structured config warning event, not a tracing logger side effect; this bead asserts the structured event contract and does not claim logging adoption. The 50-byte expert metadata is an estimate from `bd-3bb`, not final packed-layout truth.

Claim-To-Gate

| Untied embeddings keep embedding and classifier gradients separate. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- model_tests` | `burn-adapter` through `gbf-test` dependency | Embedding row gradient is `[1.0, 1.0]`; classifier row gradient is `[2.0, -3.0]`. |

No-future

title: T-A3.2: InferenceState + liveness counters
description: repr(C) InferenceState (continuation across yields) with LivenessCounters (progress_epoch, last_checkpoint, no_progress_frames, livelock_threshold_frames). Liveness contract per planv0.md line 1795 — cooperative runtimes can be locally safe and globally broken; liveness is not optional.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A3.2: `InferenceState` + liveness counters

**Target crate**: `gbf-abi`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-abi/src/continuation.rs`, `gbf-abi/src/liveness.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 1795 (liveness contract), line 2507 (every slice participates in liveness).

### Background

`InferenceState` is the continuation record — the durable state that carries across yields. It lives in WRAM during a session and is checkpointed to SRAM at semantic checkpoints. The liveness counters (`progress_epoch`, `last_checkpoint`, `no_progress_frames`) are how the runtime detects livelock at the slice/checkpoint level.

### Implementation Notes

`liveness.rs`:

```rust
#[repr(C)]
pub struct LivenessCounters {
    pub progress_epoch: u32,                  // monotonic; bumped on each successful checkpoint
    pub last_checkpoint: SemanticCheckpointId,
    pub no_progress_frames: u16,              // number of UI frames with no checkpoint advance
    pub livelock_threshold_frames: u16,       // configurable threshold
}

impl LivenessCounters {
    pub fn record_progress(&mut self, cp: SemanticCheckpointId);
    pub fn note_idle_frame(&mut self);
    pub fn is_livelocked(&self) -> bool;
}

pub struct LivenessFault {
    pub at_checkpoint: SemanticCheckpointId,
    pub no_progress_frames: u16,
}
```

`continuation.rs`:

```rust
#[repr(C)]
pub struct InferenceState {
    pub abi: AbiVersion,
    pub schema: u16,                          // versioned per-build state schema
    pub session_id: u32,
    pub token_count: u32,
    pub slice_id: SliceId,
    pub liveness: LivenessCounters,
    pub last_fault: Option<FaultCode>,
    // followed by build-specific tail of sequence-state bytes; opaque here.
}
```

`InferenceState` is a *layout* exposed to the runtime; concrete shape (especially the build-specific tail) is decided per-build. Tests assert at-least invariants (size, alignment, field offsets) for the fixed prefix.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-abi
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- continuation::layout                  # InferenceState prefix is layout-stable
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- liveness::livelock_threshold          # Threshold check fires correctly
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- liveness::progress_advance             # record_progress bumps epoch and resets idle frames
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Liveness is a typed counter, not a comment.
- §VI.1: Single InferenceState type.

title: F-A5: Bank0 Cooperative Runtime Skeleton — boot, ISRs, scheduler, UI
description: The pinned reference shell per T2.4 (bd-37r): boot + IRQ vectors, cooperative scheduler with TIMA-armed deadline + HRAM yield_requested, joypad reader, text renderer + font, on-screen keyboard, video_commit + UiCommitPlan (sole VRAM/OAM writer, LCD-mode-gated), and a minimal panic screen. Bank 0 budget is 16 KiB; sizing within budget is the load-bearing constraint.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-A5: Bank0 Cooperative Runtime Skeleton

### Background

Per `planv0.md` line 1626, `gbf-runtime` is authored as Rust builders over `AsmIR`. This feature implements the Bank0 nucleus modules: boot, interrupts, scheduler, joypad, text, keyboard, video_commit, panic. These are exactly the modules the **pinned reference shell** (T2.4 `bd-37r`) commits to: `{Boot, Interrupts, Scheduler, Banking, Joypad, Text, Keyboard, VideoCommit}`, the minimal+UI set.

Persistence, trace, harness — the runtime modules NOT in the reference shell — are deferred to Epic D.

### How this serves overarching project goals

- Bank 0 is 16 KiB and contains the scheduler, ISRs, and UI. The model's compiled inference program runs *cooperatively alongside* this nucleus — it does not own the CPU.
- `runtime_nucleus_hash` is computed over the encoded Bank 0 nucleus + `CompileProfile` + version stamp. Models trained against a synthetic budget pin against this hash; CI catches drift (T2.5 `bd-177`).
- The `RuntimeChromeBudget` (F2 / `bd-1hv`) is *emitted from this feature's compiled output*. Sizing F-A5 within Bank 0 budget is the load-bearing constraint.

### The problem this solves

Without F-A5:
- Nothing boots. The compiler has no nucleus to schedule against.
- Cooperative yielding has no `yield_requested` flag location, no TIMA arming, no scheduler return path.
- The model has no way to render a single character of output.
- The pinned-reference-shell concept is vacuous — there's no shell.

### Design

Module-level layout:

```
gbf-runtime/src/
  boot.rs          # cartridge header, boot-time IRQ vector wiring, init sequence
  interrupts.rs    # IRQ dispatch table, ISR entry points, IF acknowledgement
  scheduler.rs     # cooperative scheduler, TIMA-armed deadline, HRAM yield_requested
  joypad.rs        # button-state polling
  text.rs          # font + glyph blitting + scrollback
  keyboard.rs      # on-screen keyboard layout + chord input
  video_commit.rs  # UiCommitPlan + commit queue + LCD-mode-gated stages
  panic.rs         # panic screen + fault-code dump
```

### Cooperative-yield mechanism (planv0.md line 1790)

> The compiler inserts safe points; the runtime may arm a soft deadline using TIMA, and the timer ISR sets a `yield_requested` flag in HRAM. Generated code polls that flag only at declared safe points.

So:
1. Scheduler arms TIMA before dispatching an inference slice.
2. TIMA ISR (in `interrupts.rs`) sets `yield_requested = 1` in HRAM.
3. Inference code polls that flag at every safe point (instruction emitted by the compiler, inside `Yield` pseudo-op handling).
4. On observed yield: save continuation, return to scheduler.

### Bank 0 budget realism

Bank 0 has 16384 bytes. The reference shell modules must fit within that budget MINUS the slack reserved for future modules (Persistence, Trace, Harness, Panic — ROM bytes per `T2.4`'s `FutureReservation` table). The bring-up estimate is ~8 KiB for the minimal+UI nucleus, leaving ~8 KiB for compiled "Bank0Free" use (expert hot kernels, etc.). If F-A5 lands above 8 KiB:
- First, audit text/font for size (fonts are typically the biggest contributor).
- Second, consider moving font/glyph data to a CommonBank (relocates UI bytes out of Bank 0).
- Third, only then revisit reserved_slack.

### Children (T-A5.1 ... T-A5.7)

1. **T-A5.1** Boot + IRQ vector dispatch.
2. **T-A5.2** Cooperative scheduler with TIMA + yield_requested HRAM flag.
3. **T-A5.3** Joypad reader.
4. **T-A5.4** Text renderer + font.
5. **T-A5.5** Keyboard input + layout.
6. **T-A5.6** `video_commit` + `UiCommitPlan`.
7. **T-A5.7** Panic screen.

### Dependencies on other Epic A features

- F-A1: every F-A5 module emits AsmIR.
- F-A2: hardware constants for IRQ vectors, LCD modes, joypad register, etc.
- F-A3: `InterruptPolicy`, `FaultCode`, `SemanticCheckpointId` (ISR/panic flow).
- F-A4: `BankLease` is required for any cross-bank far-call from the runtime.

### Confidence: MEDIUM

Cooperative runtime sizing is the real risk. We can absorb a 30% overshoot of Bank 0 budget in v1 (by relocating glyph data to a CommonBank), but a 2× overshoot would force feature reductions or a second pass on text/keyboard.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T-A5.1–T-A5.7.

### Feature Definition of Done

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-runtime                                              # All module tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- scheduler::yield_round_trip               # Inference slice yields, scheduler returns
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- video_commit::lcd_mode_gating             # No VRAM/OAM commit outside legal LCD modes
cargo run -p gbf-cli -- demo-bank0-rom                                 # Boots in SameBoy/BGB; renders text + keyboard
# Bank 0 size assertion (set as an explicit test):
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- nucleus_fits_bank0_budget                 # Encoded nucleus + slack <= 16384
```

Non-goals: bd-3mrj already covers preflight expert-slot producer adoption and scalar_metric sink subscriber proof.

comments.author: bkase

title: T-B14.7: Reports — extend policy_resolution.json + emit repair_report.json
description: Add compile_knobs section to policy_resolution.json (global/bounds/locks/overrides/provenance with PolicySource per-knob). New repair_report.json schema records every proposed repair (accepted + rejected) with DeltaRejection reason for rejections, before/after KnobDeltaSummary for acceptances, per-stage iteration counts, and TerminalStateRecord. Rejected proposals kept because they are often the fastest explanation of why a build failed. Both reports schema-versioned; gbf-migrate identity + bump-minor scaffolding.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B14.7: Reports — extend `policy_resolution.json` + emit `repair_report.json`

**Target crate**: `gbf-codegen` (emit) + `gbf-report` (schemas) + `gbf-store` (archival)
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-codegen/src/reports/policy_resolution.rs`, `gbf-codegen/src/reports/repair_report.rs`, `gbf-report/src/schemas/`
**Reference**: F-B14 oracle resolution Patch 4 + Patch 5; engineering rule 25.

### Background

Two report files capture compile-policy + refinement-loop outcomes:

1. `policy_resolution.json` (existing — F-B0 emits) gains a new `compile_knobs` section with `global` / `bounds` / `locks` / `overrides` / `provenance`.
2. `repair_report.json` (new) captures every proposed repair (accepted + rejected), the lock/bound/policy reason for every rejection, the before/after `CompileKnobs` delta for each accepted proposal, per-stage iteration counts, and the loop's termination reason.

Rejected proposals are kept because they are often the fastest explanation of why a build failed.

### Implementation Notes

#### policy_resolution.json — extension

Add to F-B0's emit:

```json
{
  "compile_knobs": {
    "global": { "placement": ..., "observation": ..., ... },
    "bounds": { "max_placement_profile": "PackedExperts", ... },
    "locks":  { "locked": ["ScheduleResourcePressure", "StageIterationCeilings"] },
    "overrides": { "disabled_optional_probes": [], "forced_kernel_residency": {}, ... },
    "provenance": {
      "PlacementProfile":  { "source": "ProfileDefault", "evidence": [] },
      "ScheduleResourcePressure": {
        "source": "TargetDefault",
        "evidence": [
          { "kind": "RuntimeChromeBudget", "hash": "0xAB12..." },
          { "kind": "TargetProfile", "id": "dmg_mbc5_8mib_128kib" }
        ]
      },
      ...
    }
  }
}
```

Coordinate with F-B0 / `bd-2fj` (its body has an amendment comment from this session noting the extension).

#### repair_report.json — new schema

```rust
pub struct RepairReport {
    pub initial_knobs: CompileKnobs,
    pub final_knobs: CompileKnobs,
    pub proposals: Vec<RepairProposalRecord>,
    pub stage_iteration_counts: BTreeMap<PlanningStage, u8>,
    pub termination: TerminalStateRecord,
    pub global_iters_used: u8,
}

pub struct RepairProposalRecord {
    pub id: RepairProposalId,
    pub source_stage: PlanningStage,
    pub reason: RepairReason,
    pub delta: ConstraintDelta,
    pub estimated_cost: EstimatedCostDelta,
    pub outcome: ProposalOutcome,
}

pub enum ProposalOutcome {
    Accepted { applied_at_iter: u8, knobs_delta: KnobDeltaSummary },
    Rejected { reason: DeltaRejection },
}

pub enum TerminalStateRecord {
    Converged,
    GlobalBudgetExhausted,
    StageBudgetExhausted { stage: PlanningStage },
    StagedFailureUnrepairable { stage: PlanningStage, last_error: String },
}
```

Stored to `artifacts/builds/<build_id>/repair_report.json` and round-tripped through `gbf-store` content-addressed (same as other reports).

#### Schema versioning

Both reports get version stamps. `gbf-migrate` registers identity + bump-minor migrators (default scaffolding from T-A6.5).

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check --workspace
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- reports::policy_resolution_emits_knobs
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- reports::repair_report_round_trip
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- reports::repair_report_includes_rejected
cargo test -p gbf-report -- schemas::repair_report_versioned
cargo test -p gbf-test  -- e2e_repair_report                  # end-to-end build emits repair_report.json
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §V.1: Reports are JSON, not human-readable text.
- §V.3: Rejected proposals carry full `DeltaRejection` for postmortem.
- §VI.1: Single source of truth for the "what did the compiler decide" answer.

title: F-F5: RunManifest + ReproducibilityManifest
description: Engineering rule 24: every run emits ReproducibilityManifest (toolchain pin, lockfile, host triple, deterministic seeds, study_tag). RunManifest carries artifact + build + calibration + workload + adapter + seeds for the run.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-F5: RunManifest + ReproducibilityManifest

### What this is

`RunManifest` carries train/export/compile/bench run identity: artifact + build + calibration + workload + adapter + seeds. `ReproducibilityManifest` is the minimal CONSTITUTION-§IV.3 set: pinned toolchain, lockfile, host triple, deterministic seeds, `study_tag`.

### Why this matters

Engineering rule 24: "every train/export/compile/bench run emits a minimal `ReproducibilityManifest`." Without it, "this build was flaky" has nowhere to go.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct ReproducibilityManifest {
    pub toolchain: SemVer,
    pub lockfile_hash: Hash256,
    pub host_triple: String,
    pub seeds: BTreeMap<String, u64>,
    pub study_tag: Option<String>,
}

pub struct RunManifest {
    pub run_id: RunId,
    pub artifact_core_hash: Hash256,
    pub build_id: Option<BuildId>,
    pub calibration: Option<CalibrationSetRef>,
    pub workload: WorkloadId,
    pub adapter: Option<EmulatorAdapter>,
    pub reproducibility: ReproducibilityManifest,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- gbf-foundation ids
- F-F1 build reports

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T-A3.1: AbiVersion + CompatibilityEnvelope + BuildIdentityBlock
description: Pinned semver AbiVersion. CompatibilityEnvelope declares backward-compatible versions. BuildIdentityBlock is the repr(C) ROM-resident identity block (magic GBLM, abi, build_hash, artifact_core_hash, runtime_nucleus_hash, compile_request_hash, timestamp). runtime_nucleus_hash ties to F2 RuntimeChromeBudget drift gate (T2.5).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A3.1: `AbiVersion` + `CompatibilityEnvelope` + `BuildIdentityBlock`

**Target crate**: `gbf-abi`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-abi/src/version.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 271 (gbf-abi ownership), line 2533.

### Background

These three types are the identity handshake between every subsystem (planv0.md line 2552). `AbiVersion` is a pinned semver. `CompatibilityEnvelope` declares which past versions a current build remains compatible with. `BuildIdentityBlock` is a ROM-resident block (placed by the compiler in Bank 0) that the harness/emulator can read to identify the build at runtime.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct AbiVersion {
    pub major: u8,
    pub minor: u8,
    pub patch: u8,
}

pub const CURRENT_ABI: AbiVersion = AbiVersion { major: 0, minor: 1, patch: 0 };

pub struct CompatibilityEnvelope {
    pub current: AbiVersion,
    pub backward_compatible_with: Vec<AbiVersion>,
    pub forward_handshake: Vec<AbiVersion>,                     // versions whose newer features we tolerate
}

#[repr(C)]
pub struct BuildIdentityBlock {
    pub magic: [u8; 4],                                          // "GBLM"
    pub abi: AbiVersion,
    pub build_hash: [u8; 32],                                    // sha256 of the encoded ROM core
    pub artifact_core_hash: [u8; 32],                            // ArtifactCore hash
    pub runtime_nucleus_hash: [u8; 32],
    pub compile_request_hash: [u8; 32],
    pub timestamp_unix: u64,
}
```

The `BuildIdentityBlock` is `#[repr(C)]` because the ROM stores it at a known address and host tooling parses it directly. Add `static_assertions::const_assert_eq!(size_of::<BuildIdentityBlock>(), <expected>)`.

`runtime_nucleus_hash` ties into the F2 (`bd-1hv`) `RuntimeChromeBudget` drift gate (T2.5).

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-abi
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- version::current_constant_set            # CURRENT_ABI is non-zero
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- version::compatibility_envelope_no_self  # current not duplicated in backward_compatible_with
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- version::build_identity_layout            # BuildIdentityBlock has expected size + alignment
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: AbiVersion is structured, not a free string.
- §VI.1: Single source of ABI version truth.

title: T4.5: Implement per-phase QAT hardness control
description: Each QAT module (TernaryLinearQat, ActFakeQuant, NormApproxQat, Top1RouterQat) must support three hardness levels: Off (bypass, use original weights), Soft (fake-quant with temperature-scaled STE), Hard (full quantization with STE). The phase scheduler calls model.set_hardness() at each phase transition. During Off, the QAT module's forward is identity. During Soft, a temperature parameter (annealed during the phase) controls the STE slope.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::hardness passes; Off=identity, Hard=quantized; Transitions idempotent
close_reason: Implemented per-phase QAT hardness control with live transition proof; see closure comment.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model` (hardness API), `gbf-train` (phase integration)
**Target file(s)**:
- `gbf-model/src/qat/mod.rs` (add `set_hardness` trait/method)
- `gbf-model/src/qat/ternary.rs`, `activation.rs`, `norm.rs` (implement per module)

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "QuantHardness", "per-phase QAT hardness", "Off, Soft, Hard".

### Implementation Notes
- Each QAT module supports three hardness levels:
  - `Off`: passthrough (no quantization effect)
  - `Soft`: smooth approximation (differentiable but not exact)
  - `Hard`: exact quantization with STE
- Phase scheduler calls `model.set_hardness(expert_qat, activation_qat, norm_qat)` at each phase transition.
- Trait: `pub trait QatHardnessControl { fn set_hardness(&mut self, level: QuantHardness); }`
- TernaryLinearQat: Off=identity, Soft=soft threshold, Hard=hard ternary+STE
- ActFakeQuant: Off=passthrough, Soft=smooth clamp, Hard=round+clamp+STE
- NormApproxQat: Off=exact norm, Soft=blend(exact, approx), Hard=approx only

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::hardness          # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: Off mode produces same output as the unwrapped operation
# Verify: Hard mode produces quantized output
# Verify: transitions are idempotent (setting same hardness twice = no-op)
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: QuantHardness enum — three states, no stringly-typed levels
- §V.2: Crash-only — hardness state is reconstructable from phase schedule + current step

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: Moved-acceptance owner note from bd-ptk: this bead owns executable handling for `QuantHardness::{Off,Soft,Hard}` when phase config is applied to QAT modules. It also participates in applying `RouterTrainMode::{SoftTop1,HardTop1}` together with the phase scheduler once the scheduler calls the model hardness/router boundary.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## QAT Closure Checklist

**Artifact contract:** This bead implements the pre-export executable phase-hardness contract for QAT model/training paths. `QuantHardness::{Off,Soft,Hard}` is now model-owned by `gbf-model::qat` and re-exported by `gbf-train::phase`, so training phase config and executable QAT modules share one enum. Artifact materialization and oracle agreement are not claimed here; export/artifact agreement remains with bd-g90/bd-12c.

**differentiable Burn path:** Implemented for Burn adapter paths that exist today. `TernaryLinearBurnQat` supports live `Off` full-precision, `Soft` sigmoid soft ternary, and `Hard` hard ternary with STE. `ActFakeQuantBurnQat` supports live `Off`, `Soft` clamped-input/hard-output blend, and `Hard` round/clamp fake quant. `NormApproxBurnQat` supports live `Off`, `Soft`, and `Hard` for affine-LUT and tile-RMS plans. `ExpertBlockBurnQat` propagates phase hardness to already-constructed expert/shared branches.

**Tests proving it:** See Support Matrix and Claim-To-Gate below. The critical post-review additions are live transition tests on already-constructed Burn adapters and expert blocks, plus scalar/Burn tests for activation Soft and tile-RMS norm hardness.

**Acceptance movement:** Router dispatch remains on `RouterTrainMode::{SoftTop1,HardTop1}` rather than `QuantHardness`, per the bd-ptk owner note already on this bead. Scheduler-level soft-temperature/progress annealing is moved to bd-cmf, which now explicitly owns exact-step phase progress and annealed `QuantHardness::Soft` updates when the training loop applies `TrainPhaseSpec` rows.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `QuantHardness::{Off,Soft,Hard}` shared between model and phase schema | supported | supported | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::hardness`; `cargo test -p gbf-train -- phase`; export identity remains bd-g90/bd-12c |
| `TernaryLinearQat` hardness | supported | supported | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::hardness`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- ternary` |
| `ActFakeQuant` hardness, including distinct `Soft` | supported | supported | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::hardness`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- activation` |
| `NormApproxQat::AffineClipLut` hardness | supported | supported | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::hardness`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- norm` |
| `NormApproxQat::TileRmsThenAffineClip` hardness | supported | supported | moved | `qat_hardness_controls_tile_rms_norm_forward`; `burn_norm_hardness_controls_tile_rms_forward` |
| Expert block phase application via `set_hardness(expert_qat, activation_qat)` | supported | supported | moved | `qat_hardness_applies_to_expert_block_phase_transitions`; `burn_expert_hardness_can_change_after_adapter_construction` |
| LinearState/BoundedKv scalar phase-hardness propagation | supported | moved | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence`; Burn adapters remain bd-2bm4/bd-3arn |
| Router phase behavior | not applicable | not applicable | moved | Controlled by `RouterTrainMode`, not numeric `QuantHardness`; scheduler boundary remains bd-cmf and integration proof bd-14k |
| Scheduler-level Soft temperature/progress annealing | moved | moved | moved | bd-cmf owns phase progress and any annealed Soft parameter update |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Model-owned `QuantHardness` has no train/model vocabulary drift | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- phase` | none | Phase TOML round-trip uses the re-exported model enum |
| Off/Soft/Hard ternary scalar behavior is implemented and idempotent | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::hardness` | none | Checks Off full precision, Soft smooth projection, Hard ternary projection, repeated Off |
| Activation Soft is distinct from Hard and Off | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::hardness`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- activation` | `burn-adapter` for Burn | Guards the review finding where Soft had collapsed to Hard |
| Norm affine-LUT and tile-RMS plans honor hardness | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::hardness`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- norm` | `burn-adapter` for Burn | Guards both public norm plan variants |
| Already-constructed Burn ternary adapters can transition hardness | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- ternary` | `burn-adapter` | Includes `burn_ternary_hardness_can_change_after_adapter_construction` |
| Already-constructed Burn expert blocks can transition hardness | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- expert` | `burn-adapter` | Includes expert/shared branch propagation after adapter construction |
| Sequence scalar blocks receive phase hardness for owned QAT leaves | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence` | none | Burn sequence adapters remain moved to bd-2bm4/bd-3arn |
| Target crate compiles | `cargo check -p gbf-model` | none | Acceptance command passed |
| Clippy is clean for model and Burn training path | `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings`; `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- -D warnings` | `burn-adapter` for train | Both passed |
| Architecture and QAT closure harnesses still pass | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`; `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` | none | Both passed before close; harness will be rerun after close |

## No-future Acceptance

No-future unsupported hardness paths are accepted silently. Existing numeric QAT modules either implement `Off`/`Soft`/`Hard` today or are marked moved with a live owner. Router behavior is intentionally not represented as `QuantHardness`; it remains `RouterTrainMode` because it controls dispatch selection rather than numeric quantization. Schedule-level annealing/progress is not claimed by this bead and is moved to bd-cmf.

## Feedback Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Leaf-only hardness did not prove phase transitions | Initial tests set hardness before adapter construction | Updated QAT skill: phase hardness/mode beads must prove live transitions on already-constructed model/Burn state | `burn_ternary_hardness_can_change_after_adapter_construction`; `burn_expert_hardness_can_change_after_adapter_construction` | `.agents/skills/qat-bead-closure/SKILL.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- ternary`; `-- expert` |
| Activation `Soft` collapsed to `Hard` | No explicit Soft behavior or test | Support matrix now requires each public mode/path to be explicit | `burn_activation_soft_forward_matches_core_scalar_projection`; scalar hardness test | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- activation`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::hardness` |
| Tile-RMS norm ignored hardness | Tests only covered affine-LUT norm | Public plan variants must be enumerated in support matrix | `qat_hardness_controls_tile_rms_norm_forward`; `burn_norm_hardness_controls_tile_rms_forward` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::hardness`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- norm` |
| Router hardness wording conflicted with `RouterTrainMode` design | Bead text mixed numeric hardness with dispatch mode | Updated QAT skill: keep router dispatch mode and numeric hardness separate unless API unifies them | Phase module docs and support-matrix row; bd-cmf owner comment | bd-cmf; bd-14k future integration proof |
| Unrelated `history/planv0.md` dirty work appeared in review scope | Dirty worktree was wider than bd-2uw | No guide change; existing workflow already says do not stage unrelated user changes | Commit staging excludes `history/planv0.md`, `oracle-context*` | `git status` before staging/commit |

title: T6.3: Implement tied input/output embeddings
description: For char/byte models (vocab_size <= 256), tie the input embedding and output classifier by default. Implementation: a single EmbeddingTied module that provides both embed(token_id) -> d_model vector and classify(d_model_vector) -> logits via weight transposition. Config option to untie when vocab is large. This halves the embedding parameter count and common-bank cost, which is significant for small models.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- embedding passes; Tied mode shares weight between embed and classify; Untied mode has separate weights
close_reason: Implemented backend-independent tied/untied embedding semantics with review fixes; see closure comment.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/embedding.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "tied input/output embeddings", "EmbeddingTied", "char/byte models", "vocab <= 256".

### Implementation Notes
- For char/byte models (vocab_size <= 256), tie input embedding and output classifier by default.
- Single `EmbeddingTied` module that:
  - Holds one weight matrix of shape `[vocab_size, d_model]`
  - `embed(&self, token_ids: Tensor<Int>) -> Tensor` — forward embedding lookup
  - `classify(&self, hidden: Tensor) -> Tensor` — `hidden @ weight.T` to produce logits
- Config flag: `tie_embeddings: bool` (default true when vocab_size <= 256)
- Untied mode: separate embedding and classifier matrices (for future large-vocab use)

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                        # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- embedding             # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings         # No warnings
# Verify: tied mode — embed and classify share the same weight
# Verify: untied mode — separate weights
# Verify: classify output shape is [batch, vocab_size]
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: Tied vs untied is a type-level choice, not a runtime flag that could be mismatched
- §II.3: Default tied for byte-level; minimal code for the common case

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
Implemented bd-2v4 as backend-independent embedding/classifier semantics in `gbf-model/src/embeddings.rs`.

Scope owned by this bead:
- `EmbeddingConfig` with enum-backed `EmbeddingTieMode::{Tied, Untied}`.
- Default tie mode: `EmbeddingConfig::new` uses tied embeddings when `vocab_size <= 256`, untied otherwise.
- Named constructors `EmbeddingConfig::tied` and `EmbeddingConfig::untied` for explicit policy.
- `EmbeddingTied`, which stores one `[vocab_size, d_model]` weight matrix and uses it for both embedding lookup and classifier logits via `hidden @ weight.T`.
- `EmbeddingUntied`, which stores separate embedding and classifier matrices.
- Explicit `EmbeddingOutput` and `ClassifierOutput` wrappers exposing `[tokens, d_model]` and `[batch, vocab_size]` shapes.
- Constructor/runtime validation for nonzero dimensions, shape overflow, weight length, finite weights, token bounds, hidden shape, and finite hidden values.

Scope explicitly not claimed:
- No Burn module, Tensor object identity, or gradient accumulation proof. Those model-instantiation tests remain in existing bd-2qj.
- No artifact/export representation of tied classifier aliases. Created bd-3bf1 to track the export/artifact sharing contract.
- No common-bank measured budget claim. This bead proves structural parameter count at the scalar model layer only.
- No top-level model config boundary connecting embedding `d_model` to block `d_model`; bd-2qj covers model construction tests.

Subagent review disposition:
- Mode 1 intent review flagged that the patch must not claim Burn Tensor sharing, gradient behavior, export aliasing, or common-bank savings. Agreed; closure scope was narrowed and bd-3bf1 was created for export/artifact sharing.
- Mode 2 design review flagged export/artifact sharing and top-level model shape integration as out of scope, and recommended reducing the bool API. Agreed; `with_tie_embeddings(bool)` was removed, named constructors are primary, model integration is covered by bd-2qj, and artifact sharing is tracked by bd-3bf1.
- Mode 3 semantic review found the scalar behavior correct and noted residual model/export gaps. Agreed; those gaps are explicitly not claimed here.
- Mode 4 evidence review requested positive `from_config` tests and a clear non-performance caveat. Agreed; added default-config construction tests and parameter-count assertions, and closure limits common-bank claims to structural scalar counts.

Support matrix:

| Behavior | Status | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Byte/char default ties embeddings for `vocab_size <= 256` | Supported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- embedding` |
| Large vocab defaults to untied | Supported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- embedding` |
| Explicit tied or untied policy | Supported through named constructors and `EmbeddingTieMode` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- embedding` |
| Tied embed/classify share one scalar weight allocation | Supported | `embedding_tied_reuses_one_weight_matrix_for_embed_and_classify` |
| Untied embed/classify use separate scalar matrices | Supported | `embedding_untied_uses_separate_embedding_and_classifier_matrices` |
| Classifier logits shape is `[batch, vocab_size]` | Supported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- embedding` |
| Burn Tensor object identity and gradients | Not claimed here | Existing bd-2qj |
| Export/artifact tied alias representation | Not claimed here | New bd-3bf1 |
| Measured common-bank savings | Not claimed here | bd-3bf1 or later budget/export bead |

Feedback harness reflection:

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Scalar model module could be overclaimed as Burn Tensor/gradient implementation | Bead wording used Tensor-like language while current `gbf-model` layer is backend-independent scalar semantics | Added `AGENTS.md` guidance requiring scalar model closures to name unsupported Burn/export/artifact/budget boundaries | Closure support matrix separates scalar behavior from bd-2qj Burn/model tests | `AGENTS.md`; bd-2qj |
| Parameter sharing/common-bank saving could be claimed without artifact support | Local pointer aliasing does not imply exported artifact aliasing or bank materialization | Added `AGENTS.md` guidance to pair sharing/count claims with owned-layer sensors and named follow-up owners | Added scalar parameter-count assertions; created bd-3bf1 for export/artifact sharing | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- embedding`; bd-3bf1 |
| Bool API weakened enum-backed mode choice | Initial public constructor accepted a raw bool despite the type-level-choice checkpoint | Added `AGENTS.md` guidance that enum/named constructors should be primary APIs for model modes | Removed `with_tie_embeddings(bool)` and added named constructors/tests | `gbf-model/src/embeddings.rs`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- embedding` |
| Positive default-to-module construction was untested | Tests checked default enum values and wrong-mode errors but not the happy path from default config into modules | No new guide needed; this is a local coverage gap | Added `embedding_from_default_config_constructs_expected_tie_mode` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- embedding` |
| Export/artifact aliasing had no bead owner | Existing exporter has separate embedding/classifier paths and no tied alias contract | No closure movement without an owner | Created bd-3bf1 with dependency on bd-2v4 | `br show bd-3bf1` |

Claim-to-gate matrix:

title: F-E2: PlatformCalibrationBundle production
description: Measure bank_switch_cost, sram_page_cost, timer_isr_cost via emulator (F-H3) or hardware. CycleDistribution (mean/p50/p90/p99) + CalibrationConfidence. Layered separately because platform facts are stable across kernel/runtime changes.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-E2: PlatformCalibrationBundle production

### What this is

Measure `bank_switch_cost`, `sram_page_cost`, `timer_isr_cost` on a target via emulator (Epic H F-H3 adapters) or hardware. Run instrumented micro-benchmarks; record `CycleDistribution` (mean / p50 / p90 / p99) plus `CalibrationConfidence` (sample_count + class).

### Why this matters

These are *platform-level* facts that don't change with kernel implementations or compiler internals. Layered separately so they don't get invalidated when only kernel impls change.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub fn measure_platform(target: &TargetProfile, adapter: &dyn EmulatorAdapter)
    -> PlatformCalibrationBundle;
```

Bench runners write the bundle to `gbf-store` for compile-time consumption.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A2.7 calibration schema
- F-H3 emulator adapters

### Confidence: HIGH

title: F-B5: RangePlan (Stage 5)
description: Purely logical: per hot reduction picks SingleI16 / ChunkedI16 / RenormLoop. Proves i16 accumulator safety. Emits certs/range.cert.json.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B5: RangePlan (Stage 5)

### What this is

Purely logical (`planv0.md` line 1242). Chooses a `ReductionPlan` per hot reduction without needing materialization or residency decisions: `SingleI16 | ChunkedI16 { chunk_len } | RenormLoop { tile_len }`. Proves "i16 accumulators are safe here" precisely instead of guessing.

### Why this matters

Reduction structure feeds `StoragePlan` (different scratch needs per plan) and `GbSchedIR` (tile shapes change). Doing it logically before storage decisions keeps the dependency graph clean.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub enum ReductionPlan {
    SingleI16,
    ChunkedI16 { chunk_len: u16 },
    RenormLoop { tile_len: u16 },
}

pub fn plan_ranges(g: &GbInferIR, ranges: &RangeFacts) -> Vec<(NodeId, ReductionPlan)>;
```

Emits `certs/range.cert.json`.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B3 GbInferIR
- F-B4 ObservationPlan

### Confidence: HIGH

title: F-G2: Lexical + Interaction split
description: LexicalSpec (charset + normalization + control tokens) lives in ArtifactCore (model-semantic). InteractionBundle (KeyboardLayoutSpec + TranscriptSpec + default SessionProfile) lives in ArtifactAux (interaction policy). Splitting preserves identity through interaction-policy changes.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-G2: Lexical + Interaction

### What this is

`LexicalSpec` (charset + normalization + control tokens) lives in `ArtifactCore` (model-semantic). `InteractionBundle` (`KeyboardLayoutSpec`, `TranscriptSpec`, default `SessionProfile`) lives in `ArtifactAux` (interaction policy).

### Why this matters

Per planv0.md line 703: changing line wrapping shouldn't bump the model's semantic identity hash. Splitting these two cleanly preserves identity through interaction-policy changes.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct LexicalSpec {
    pub charset: Charset,
    pub normalization: NormalizationPolicy,
    pub control_tokens: BTreeSet<TokenId>,
}

pub struct InteractionBundle {
    pub keyboard: KeyboardLayoutSpec,
    pub transcript: TranscriptSpec,
    pub default_session: SessionProfile,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A5.4 text renderer (TranscriptSpec)
- F-A5.5 keyboard (KeyboardLayoutSpec)
- gbf-artifact (LexicalSpec lives in core, InteractionBundle in aux)

### Confidence: HIGH

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Locked-in v1 charset (Tier 2 — "Accelerando voice")

Decision date: 2026-04-27. Owner: bkase. Genre intent: dense Stross / *Accelerando*-style prose. Caps-only and script-style dialogue conventions explicitly rejected as off-genre.

### Charset (76 printable + newline = 77; +3 control = 80 tokens)

- A–Z (26), a–z (26), 0–9 (10) — **case-preserving**
- Punctuation (13): space . , ! ? - ' : ; ( ) " /
- Newline (1): \n
- Control tokens (3): <bos> <eos> <unk> at IDs 80, 81, 82

Vocab ≤ 256, so tied embeddings apply (see gbf-model/src/embeddings.rs:6 BYTE_LEVEL_TIED_VOCAB_LIMIT).

### LexicalSpec (lives in ArtifactCore — load-bearing for identity hash)

```rust
pub struct LexicalSpec {
    pub charset: Charset,             // the 80-token set above
    pub normalization: NormalizationSpec,
    pub control_tokens: ControlTokenSpec,
}
```

### InteractionBundle (lives in ArtifactAux — NOT part of identity hash)

Three-page on-screen keyboard:
- Page 1: lowercase letters + space + common punctuation + caps-toggle + 123-page button
- Page 2: uppercase letters (same layout)
- Page 3: digits 0–9 + symbol punctuation + back-to-letters

B button = one-shot shift on lowercase page. ~30 visible keys per page → 5×6 grid fits screen with text area above.

Keyboard layout is iterable without artifact reissue. Lives in InteractionBundle::KeyboardLayoutSpec, not LexicalSpec.

### Cost vs the Tier 1 caps-only sketch

| | Tokens | Embedding bytes (d_model=128 ternary) | Per-token output cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 caps-only | 48 | ~1.5 KiB | baseline |
| **Tier 2 (locked)** | **80** | **~2.5 KiB** | **+74% on output projection (~7% total)** |

~1 KiB extra ROM and ~7% per-token slowdown buys mixed-case + parens + quotes + semicolons + slash. Cheap relative to one extra MoE expert (~30 KiB).

### Acceptance for closure

- Charset constant exposed (e.g. `gbf_artifact::lexical::TIER2_CHARSET`) with byte-id mapping pinned by an exact-equality test.
- LexicalSpec serde shape stable; JSON round-trip test.
- Charset and normalization rules contribute to LexicalSpec hash; identity-hash regression test pins it.
- Negative test: an unmappable codepoint either routes to <unk> or rejects the example, never silently dropped.
- Keyboard layout placeholder accepted; full keyboard UX is a follow-up under InteractionBundle (does NOT block this bead's closure).

### Moved boundaries

- Normalization implementation (NFC, accent strip, smart-quote/em-dash/ellipsis fold, whitespace collapse, unmappable handling) is owned by F-G1 (bd-tmaw), not this bead. Schema lives here; deterministic pipeline lives there.
- TranscriptSpec mappings (`--` → em-dash, etc. on render) live in InteractionBundle and are NOT identity-hashed. Already covered by this bead's InteractionBundle scope.
- Keyboard rendering / input handler is owned by F-A5.5 (bd-t0y).

comments.author: bkase

title: T-A3.5: InterruptPolicy + ResourceLease + ResourceLeaseKind
description: Per-slice InterruptPolicy (Enabled/ShortCriticalSection/Disabled). Typed ResourceLease with kind (RomWindow/SramPage/Overlay/InterruptMask) used by ResourceStateValidation (Epic B Stage 10.5) to prove leases are balanced and yield-safety holds.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A3.5: `InterruptPolicy` + `ResourceLease` + `ResourceLeaseKind`

**Target crate**: `gbf-abi`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-abi/src/interrupt.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 1368 (`ResourceLeaseKind`), line 1417 (`InterruptPolicy`), line 1476 (`ResourceStateValidation`).

### Background

`InterruptPolicy` is per-slice: `Enabled | ShortCriticalSection | Disabled`. `ResourceLease` formalizes the typed "we have this MBC bank / SRAM page / overlay region for this duration" claim that gets validated by `ResourceStateValidation` (Epic B). `gbf-runtime::banking` (F-A4) implements one kind of resource lease (`RomWindow` / `SramPage`).

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub enum InterruptPolicy {
    Enabled,
    ShortCriticalSection,                     // EI/DI window for ~10 M-cycles
    Disabled,                                 // entire slice runs IF-disabled — only legal at boot
}

pub struct LeaseId(pub u32);

pub enum ResourceLeaseKind {
    RomWindow(RomWindowBinding),
    SramPage(SramPageBinding),
    Overlay(OverlayId),
    InterruptMask(InterruptPolicy),
}

pub struct ResourceLease {
    pub id: LeaseId,
    pub kind: ResourceLeaseKind,
    pub acquired_in: SliceId,
    pub released_in: SliceId,
    pub yield_safe: bool,                     // can a yield cross this lease?
}

pub struct RomWindowBinding {
    pub bank: u16,
}

pub struct SramPageBinding {
    pub page: u8,
    pub enabled: bool,
}

pub struct OverlayId(pub u16);
pub struct SliceId(pub u32);
```

`yield_safe = false` for `RomWindow` (yielding while holding a switchable-bank lease is illegal), `true` for `SramPage` (the runtime can save+restore SRAM bank state).

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-abi
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- interrupt::policy_enum_complete          # All 3 variants
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- interrupt::lease_yield_safety_table       # RomWindow=false, SramPage=true, Overlay=false, InterruptMask depends
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- interrupt::lease_id_uniqueness            # LeaseId u32 has full range
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: ResourceLeaseKind exhaustive; ResourceStateValidation can rely on it.

title: T6.2: Implement block selection config for alternating/middle MoE
description: MoE block selection defined in model config TOML. Options: all_blocks, alternating(start, stride), middle(first_moe, last_moe), explicit(list_of_block_indices). Default for first serious model: alternating(start=2, stride=2) meaning blocks 2, 4, 6, etc. Non-MoE blocks use a shared dense FFN of the same d_ff, which lives in common banks. Block selection is an architecture parameter, not a training hyperparameter — it's fixed before training begins.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- config::moe passes; Alternating(2,2) with 12 blocks yields 2,4,6,8,10; Empty selection rejected
close_reason: Implemented validated MoE block selection config

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/config.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "block selection", "alternating", "middle", "MoE block".

### Implementation Notes
- Define MoE block selection strategy in config:
```rust
pub enum MoeBlockSelection {
    AllBlocks,
    Alternating { start: usize, stride: usize },
    Middle { first_moe: usize, last_moe: usize },
    Explicit(Vec<usize>),
}
```
- Default: `Alternating { start: 2, stride: 2 }`
- TOML config: `[model.moe] block_selection = { type = "alternating", start = 2, stride = 2 }`
- Implement `MoeBlockSelection::contains(&self, block_idx: usize, total_blocks: usize) -> bool`
- Validation: ensure at least one MoE block, indices in range

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- config::moe            # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: Alternating(2,2) with 12 blocks -> blocks 2,4,6,8,10
# Verify: Middle(3,8) with 12 blocks -> blocks 3,4,5,6,7,8
# Verify: empty selection rejected at construction time
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §II.3: One canonical way to express block selection
