- Scope/topology: owns validated MoE block-selection config and expansion into ModelTopologyConfig. Block indices are zero-based; middle(first_moe, last_moe) is inclusive. This does not implement Burn forward math, router behavior, export/bank allocation, or artifact weight aliasing.
- Implemented: MoeBlockSelection variants all_blocks/alternating/middle/explicit; default alternating(2,2); constructor validation; custom serde/TOML deserialization for [model.moe] block_selection inline tables through the same constructors; selected_blocks/contains with total_blocks validation; topology helper requiring one dense fallback only when non-MoE blocks remain and same d_ff as the MoE FFN.
- Review fixes: made variant payloads validated private-field structs; contains now returns Result instead of swallowing validation failures; all-MoE selections do not require unused dense config; added TOML, all_blocks, topology-mask, missing-dense, zero-depth, and out-of-range tests.
- Evidence: cargo check -p gbf-model; cargo test -p gbf-model -- config::moe (10 tests); cargo test -p gbf-model -- block; cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings; cargo test -p gbf-model (120 tests); git diff --check.
- Non-goals/deferred: common-bank runtime/storage aliasing is not proven here. This config represents a single dense fallback prototype for non-MoE blocks; full model instantiation/export proof belongs to bd-2qj/F6 integration, not this config bead.

title: T-B14.6: FeasibilityRefinementLoop driver — admissibility + monotonicity + iteration ceilings + termination
description: Bounded monotone repair loop wrapping stages 5-11 (RangePlan/StoragePlan/SramPagePlan/RomWindowPlan/OverlayPlan/ArenaPlan/GbSchedIR). Only the controller applies ConstraintDelta; passes propose only. Six admissibility checks: not locked / policy toggle / bounds / monotone / pure-effect (for ForceRecompute, walks GbInferIR effect edges) / Invariant ObservabilityMode. TerminalState: Converged | GlobalBudgetExhausted | StageBudgetExhausted | StagedFailureUnrepairable. Per-stage RefinementIterBudget enforced as min(global_remaining, stage_remaining).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B14.6: `FeasibilityRefinementLoop` driver

**Target crate**: `gbf-codegen`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-codegen/src/refinement_loop.rs` (new)
**Reference**: F-B14 oracle resolution; planv0.md §"FeasibilityRefinementLoop"; engineering rule 25 (new).

### Background

The bounded monotone repair loop wrapping stages 5–11 (`RangePlan → StoragePlan → SramPagePlan → RomWindowPlan → OverlayPlan → ArenaPlan → GbSchedIR`). Passes propose `RepairProposal`s; **only this driver** applies `ConstraintDelta`s.

### Implementation Notes

#### Driver shape

```rust
pub struct LoopState {
    pub knobs: CompileKnobs,
    pub repair_policy: RepairPolicy,
    pub observability: ObservabilityMode,
    pub global_iters_remaining: u8,
    pub stage_iters_remaining: StageIterationCeilings,
    pub history: RepairHistory,                       // for repair_report.json (T-B14.7)
}

pub fn run_refinement_loop(
    initial_state: LoopState,
    pipeline: &mut CompilerPipeline,
) -> Result<TerminalState, RefinementLoopError>;

pub enum TerminalState {
    Converged,                                        // all stages successful, no proposals
    GlobalBudgetExhausted,
    StageBudgetExhausted { stage: PlanningStage },
    StagedFailureUnrepairable { stage: PlanningStage, last_error: String },
}
```

#### Per-iteration algorithm

1. Run the wrapped stages in order. Each stage may either succeed or emit a `RepairProposal`.
2. If all stages succeed: `Converged` — emit `repair_report.json`, exit.
3. If a stage emits a proposal:
   a. Run admissibility checks (T-B14.3 `check_delta_admissible`):
      - Knob not locked? Else `KnobLocked`.
      - `RepairPolicy.allow_*` toggle? Else `PolicyToggleDisabled`.
      - Within `CompileKnobBounds`? Else `BeyondBounds`.
      - Monotone in declared order? Else `NotMonotone`.
      - For `ForceRecompute`: target value is **pure** (effect-free walk against `GbInferIR` effect edges)? Else `EffectfulRecompute`.
      - Under `ObservabilityMode::Invariant`: deltas affecting `ObservationKnobs` are rejected? Else `InvariantObservabilityViolation`.
   b. Record outcome in `history` (accepted or rejected with reason).
   c. If accepted: apply delta to `knobs`, decrement budgets, restart from step 1.
   d. If rejected: bubble up; the originating stage gets the rejection reason and may fail or emit a different proposal.
4. If global or per-stage iter budget exhausted: terminate with `*BudgetExhausted`.

#### Monotonicity enforcement

For ordered enums: `MonotoneKnob::is_advance(from, to)` from T-B14.1.

For `tile_class_overrides` (subset removal): `new ⊆ old`.

For `disabled_optional_probes` (superset addition): `new ⊇ old`.

For `forced_recompute` / `forced_kernel_residency`: insert-only or move-to-later.

#### Per-stage iteration ceilings

Effective bound at any moment for stage S:
```
min(global_iters_remaining, stage_iters_remaining.<S>)
```

Decrement both when S runs (whether the run succeeded or proposed a repair).

#### Effectful recompute check

Walk `GbInferIR`. For each `ValueSelector` in a `ForceRecompute` proposal:
- Trace the value's producer chain back through `effects_in` edges.
- If any traversed node has a non-empty `effects_out` containing `EffectClass::SequenceWrite | RngAdvance | PersistenceCommit | TraceEmit`, reject as `EffectfulRecompute`.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-codegen
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- refinement_loop::convergence_no_proposals
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- refinement_loop::placement_fallback_advance
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- refinement_loop::overlay_promotion_advance
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- refinement_loop::effectful_recompute_rejected
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- refinement_loop::invariant_blocks_trace_demotion
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- refinement_loop::stage_budget_exhausted
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- refinement_loop::global_budget_exhausted
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.4: Loop produces a verifiable terminal state (Converged/Exhausted/Unrepairable).
- §V.3: Every rejection carries `DeltaRejection` with actionable info.
- §III.1: Loop logic catchable by tests; no need to run a ROM to check semantics.

title: F6: MoE Architecture Restrictions — FFN-Only, Two-Matrix, Tied Embeddings
description: MoE applies only to FFN path of selected (alternating/middle) blocks. Default two-matrix expert MLP. Input/output embeddings tied for char/byte models. Optional shared dense FFN branch as stability profile. No default GLU.
acceptance_criteria: All T6.x tasks closed; cargo test -p gbf-model -- block passes; cargo test -p gbf-model -- embedding passes; cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget passes; cargo test -p gbf-test -- model_tests passes
close_reason: Completed F6 model-core architecture restriction closure

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F6: MoE Architecture Restrictions — Make the First Model Concrete

### Background

The plan describes an MoE (Mixture of Experts) architecture but leaves several critical decisions underspecified:
- Which blocks have MoE? All of them? Some?
- What's the expert internal structure? Two-matrix (standard MLP) or three-matrix (GLU)?
- Are embeddings and classifier separate or tied?
- Is the sequence-state path (attention/recurrent) also MoE, or shared?

These aren't tuning knobs — they fundamentally change the byte budget, the bank layout, the routing semantics, and the training dynamics.

### Key decisions and their justifications

**MoE on FFN path only, not sequence-state**: The sequence-state update (attention, recurrent, linear-attention) is the most numerically sensitive part of the model. It benefits from shared, higher-precision weights in common banks. Making it MoE would mean routing for both FFN and state update, doubling the router complexity and bank-switch cost. The FFN path is the natural place for MoE because experts are relatively independent computations.

**Alternating or middle blocks, not every block**: The first and last blocks tend to learn more universal features (embedding projection, pre-classifier features). MoE in these blocks wastes bank capacity on experts that all produce similar output. Starting MoE at block 2 and using it in every other block (or only in the middle N blocks) is standard practice in efficient MoE designs.

**Two-matrix experts, not GLU**: A GLU expert has three projections: gate, up, and down. For bank-sized ternary:
- Two-matrix (128→224→128): ~15 KiB → fits in one bank with margin
- GLU (128→224→128 with separate gate): ~22.5 KiB → does NOT fit in one bank
The three-matrix GLU would require either larger banks (not available), smaller d_ff (quality hit), or packed multi-expert banks (complexity). Two-matrix with a simple clipped nonlinearity (ReLU, GELU clip) is the right default.

**Tied input/output embeddings**: For a char/byte-level model with a small vocabulary (128-256 tokens), the embedding matrix is tiny (~32-64 KiB). Tying input and output embeddings halves this already-small cost and is standard practice for character models. The embedding lives in a common bank.

**Optional shared dense FFN branch**: A stability technique where each MoE block also has a tiny shared (non-expert) dense FFN in common banks. The sparse expert output is added to the shared output as a delta. This improves training stability because the shared branch provides a stable gradient path even when routing is noisy. It costs a few KiB of common-bank space but may significantly improve quality.

### Confidence: HIGH with mild reservation

The byte math is solid. The "alternating blocks" recommendation is well-supported by the literature but the specific block selection will depend on the model's actual size and the quality/latency experiments that happen during training. The types and structure are right; the defaults may shift.

title: T10.6b: Temporal switch loss gradient flow tests (L_switch)
description:
Gradient flow tests specifically for the temporal switch penalty L_switch:

**Basic gradient flow:**
- Sequence of 8 tokens, all routing to expert 0: verify L_switch = 0, gradient is zero
- Sequence of 8 tokens, alternating expert 0 and 1: verify L_switch ≈ 1.0, gradient is non-zero on router
- Gradient flows through router softmax for tokens 1+ but NOT for token 0 (no previous token)

**Sequence boundary handling:**
- Two sequences in same batch (tokens [0-3] = seq A, [4-7] = seq B): verify NO cross-sequence penalty
- First token of each sequence: verify zero penalty contribution
- Contiguous window size=4: verify penalty computed only within windows

**Interaction with z-loss:**
- Enable both λ_switch and λ_zrouter: verify gradients combine correctly (router receives gradients from both terms)
- The z-loss should soften routing distributions, which should reduce the temporal switch penalty — verify this qualitative behavior on a short training run

**λ_distill interaction:**
- Enable λ_switch + λ_distill: verify student is pushed toward both teacher quality AND temporal smoothness
- If teacher has high switch rate and student learns smooth routing, verify conformance gap is logged

All tests log: n_tokens, n_experts, routing distributions, L_switch value, gradient norms per router parameter.
acceptance_criteria: cargo test -p gbf-test -- switch_loss_tests passes; Gradient encourages same-expert routing; Sequence boundary handling correct

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-E4: RuntimeCalibrationBundle production

### What this is

Measured `scheduler_overhead`, `overlay_install_cost`, `trace_event_cost`. Tied to `runtime_nucleus_hash`.

### Why this matters

Cooperative-runtime overheads aren't free. The scheduler adds cycles per yield; overlay installs eat WRAM bandwidth; trace events cost something. Measuring keeps the cost model honest.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub fn measure_runtime(target: &TargetProfile, runtime_nucleus_hash: Hash256)
    -> RuntimeCalibrationBundle;
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A5 (Bank0 nucleus must exist to measure)
- F-H3 emulator adapters

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T-A3.7: TraceEvent + probe ids + TraceBudget + drop policy
description: repr(C) TraceEvent with seq/timestamp/probe/slice/checkpoint/data. TraceProbeId, ProbeLevel (Always/OnError/Verbose), ProbeBudgetClass (PerSlice/PerFrame/PerSession). TraceBudget with TraceDropPolicy (DropOldest/DropNewest/HaltAndFault). Consumed by Epic D trace pipeline.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A3.7: `TraceEvent` + probe ids

**Target crate**: `gbf-abi`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-abi/src/trace.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 271, line 1226 (`OperationalProbe` + `TraceProbeId`), line 2142 (`TraceBudget`).

### Background

The runtime emits structured `TraceEvent`s into a ring of trace pages in SRAM (or a memory-mapped buffer in emulator mode). The harness reads them via the harness control plane. `TraceProbeId` is the compact id used inside trace pages; `ProbeBudgetClass` and `ProbeLevel` constrain how often a probe can fire under `TraceBudget`.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
#[repr(transparent)]
pub struct TraceProbeId(pub u16);

pub enum ProbeLevel {
    Always,                                   // unconditional, even in non-Trace builds
    OnError,
    Verbose,                                  // only enabled in Trace profile
}

pub enum ProbeBudgetClass {
    PerSlice,                                 // counted against slice probe budget
    PerFrame,                                 // counted against frame probe budget
    PerSession,
}

#[repr(C)]
pub struct TraceEvent {
    pub seq: u32,
    pub timestamp_m_cycles: u32,              // wall M-cycles since boot
    pub probe: TraceProbeId,
    pub slice: SliceId,
    pub checkpoint: CompactCheckpointId,
    pub data: [u8; 16],                       // payload — interpretation depends on probe
}

pub struct TraceBudget {
    pub max_events_per_slice: u16,
    pub max_bytes_per_frame: u16,
    pub drop_policy: TraceDropPolicy,
}

pub enum TraceDropPolicy {
    DropOldest,
    DropNewest,
    HaltAndFault,                             // for invariant-mode builds
}
```

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-abi
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- trace::event_layout                   # TraceEvent has expected size + alignment
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- trace::probe_level_exhaustive
cargo test -p gbf-abi -- trace::budget_drop_policy_exhaustive
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: ProbeLevel, ProbeBudgetClass, TraceDropPolicy enumerated.
- §V.1: Trace events are structured; never raw printf.

title: F-C4: ConformanceEnvelope + conformance.json regression gate
description: First-class regression gate per engineering rule 18. Records quality/degradation between Denotation (F-C1) and Artifact (F-C2) under named workloads. Hierarchical (per-checkpoint, per-workload, aggregate). conformance.json is what CI gates against.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-C4: ConformanceEnvelope + conformance.json

### What this is

The first-class regression gate (`planv0.md` engineering rule 18). Records quality/degradation between Denotation (F-C1) and Artifact (F-C2) under named workloads, with hierarchical envelope structure (per-checkpoint, per-workload, aggregate).

### Why this matters

Without `conformance.json` as a gate, "the artifact is acceptable" is informal. With it, CI rejects builds whose conformance regresses past declared tolerances — even if all unit tests still pass.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct ConformanceEnvelope {
    pub workload: WorkloadId,
    pub stratum: SemanticStratum,            // Denotation vs Artifact
    pub per_checkpoint: BTreeMap<SemanticCheckpointId, ConformanceMetric>,
    pub aggregate: ConformanceMetric,
    pub thresholds: ConformanceThresholds,
}

pub struct ConformanceMetric {
    pub mean_abs_diff: f32,
    pub max_abs_diff: f32,
    pub disagreement_rate: f32,
    pub samples: u32,
}
```

Emits `conformance.json` per build.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-C1, F-C2 (sources)
- F-F1 build reports (consumes conformance.json)

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T-A4.1: BankLease/BankGuard types + MbcBankClass + LeaseLifetime
description: Type backbone for bank-lease ABI. MbcBankClass (Rom4000 | SramA000), LeaseLifetime (Slice | ResumeWindow | Token | Manual), BankLeaseSpec, BankGuard (host-side RAII enforcement that release is always emitted).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A4.1: `BankLease` / `BankGuard` types + `MbcBankClass` + `LeaseLifetime`

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-runtime/src/banking.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 1631 (banking owns BankLease/BankGuard ABI — only legal MBC write path), line 1716 (centralized MBC handling).

### Background

This is the type definition layer for the bank-write ABI. The actual acquire/release machinery is T-A4.3; the HRAM shadow is T-A4.2. This task defines what a lease *is*.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub enum MbcBankClass {
    Rom4000,                                   // switchable ROM bank at $4000-$7FFF
    SramA000,                                  // switchable SRAM bank at $A000-$BFFF
}

pub enum LeaseLifetime {
    Slice,                                     // released at slice boundary
    ResumeWindow,                              // released when continuation is resumed
    Token,                                     // released at next TokenReady checkpoint
    Manual,                                    // explicit release required
}

pub struct BankLeaseSpec {
    pub class: MbcBankClass,
    pub bank_n: u16,                           // up to 9-bit (MBC5)
    pub lifetime: LeaseLifetime,
    pub yield_safe: bool,                      // only true if lifetime != Manual and runtime saves bank state
}

pub struct BankLease {
    pub id: LeaseId,                           // from gbf-abi
    pub spec: BankLeaseSpec,
}

pub struct BankGuard<'a> {
    pub(crate) lease: &'a BankLease,
    pub(crate) released: bool,
}

impl Drop for BankGuard<'_> {
    fn drop(&mut self) { /* enforce release */ }
}
```

The `BankGuard` is RAII at the host-side (compiler-time) — it ensures the encoder emits a `BankRelease` for every `BankLease` in well-typed compiler output. It is not a runtime construct (the ROM has no destructors).

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-runtime
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- banking::lease_spec_invariants       # bank_n fits in 9 bits for Rom4000, 4 bits for SramA000
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- banking::lifetime_yield_safety        # Slice + ResumeWindow are yield_safe; Manual is not
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `MbcBankClass`, `LeaseLifetime` are enumerated.
- §VI.1: BankLease lives only here.

title: T2.4: Define ReferenceShellSpec and named-future-module slack reservations in gbf-policy
description: Define ReferenceShellSpec { included: BTreeSet<RuntimeShellModule>, future_reservations: BTreeMap<RuntimeShellModule, FutureReservation> } where FutureReservation { rom_bytes_per_bank0: u16, wram_bytes: u16, sram_bytes: u32 }. The reference shell is the minimal+UI set: {Boot, Interrupts, Scheduler, Banking, Joypad, Text, Keyboard, VideoCommit}. Future reservations cover persistence, trace, harness, panic. The shell emitter subtracts these from usable_bytes so production-shell expansion does not silently invalidate trained models.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-policy` (types) + `gbf-runtime` (consumer).
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/reference_shell.rs` (new), `gbf-runtime/src/lib.rs` (record present modules).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "runtime nucleus", "Bank0", "minimal", "chrome". Also bd-1hv 2026-04-26 session comment, T2.2 refinement (bd-1g9).

### Implementation Notes

Define `ReferenceShellSpec` and the `RuntimeShellModule` enum (the latter is referenced by `RuntimeChromeBudget.reference_shell_modules` per T2.1 refinement):

```rust
pub enum RuntimeShellModule {
    Boot,
    Interrupts,
    Scheduler,
    Banking,
    Joypad,
    Text,
    Keyboard,
    VideoCommit,
    Panic,
    Trace,
    Harness,
    Persistence,
}

pub struct ReferenceShellSpec {
    pub included: BTreeSet<RuntimeShellModule>,
    pub future_reservations: BTreeMap<RuntimeShellModule, FutureReservation>,
}

pub struct FutureReservation {
    pub rom_bytes_per_bank0: u16,
    pub wram_bytes: u16,
    pub sram_bytes: u32,
    pub justification: &'static str,
}

pub fn pinned_reference_shell() -> ReferenceShellSpec {
    use RuntimeShellModule::*;
    ReferenceShellSpec {
        included: [Boot, Interrupts, Scheduler, Banking, Joypad, Text,
                   Keyboard, VideoCommit].into_iter().collect(),
        future_reservations: [
            (Persistence, FutureReservation {
                rom_bytes_per_bank0: 768,    // SRAM record protocol code + headers
                wram_bytes: 64,               // active page header cache in HRAM-adjacent WRAM
                sram_bytes: 512,              // per-page commit metadata
                justification: "Reserve for SRAM record protocol per planv0.md \
                                Persistent record protocol section.",
            }),
            (Trace, FutureReservation {
                rom_bytes_per_bank0: 256,
                wram_bytes: 0,
                sram_bytes: 4096,
                justification: "Dev trace pages live primarily in SRAM.",
            }),
            (Harness, FutureReservation {
                rom_bytes_per_bank0: 384,
                wram_bytes: 0,
                sram_bytes: 256,              // command/result blocks
                justification: "Harness command/result blocks per gbf-abi.",
            }),
            (Panic, FutureReservation {
                rom_bytes_per_bank0: 192,
                wram_bytes: 16,
                sram_bytes: 0,
                justification: "Panic screen + panic ISR landing pad.",
            }),
        ].into_iter().collect(),
    }
}
```

The shell emitter (T2.2) reads `pinned_reference_shell()`, subtracts each `FutureReservation`'s bytes from the relevant `RomBudgetSlot.usable_bytes` / `wram_reserved` / `sram_reserved`, and records the breakdown in `BuildReports`.

### Concrete byte values

The numbers above are starting estimates. They MUST appear in `BuildReports` so reviewers can see what is being reserved and why. They can be refined when each module is actually implemented and its real footprint becomes measurable.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-policy                                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- reference_shell                      # pinned_reference_shell() returns expected set
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings                          # No warnings
# Verify: pinned_reference_shell().included == minimal+UI set
# Verify: pinned_reference_shell().future_reservations covers Persistence, Trace, Harness, Panic
# Verify: every FutureReservation has a non-empty justification
# Verify: no reservation present for an `included` module
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `RuntimeShellModule` enum is exhaustive; missing a module is a compile error at consumers.
- §VI.1: Reference shell module set lives in code (gbf-policy), not in a config file.
- §V.3: Slack reservations carry justifications so failures can name them.

title: T10.2: Set up structured logging framework for training pipeline
description: Set up tracing-based structured logging across gbf-model, gbf-train, and gbf-data. Requirements: (a) Span-per-training-phase with phase name, step range, and QAT hardness levels as structured fields. (b) Per-step loss breakdown: each λ-weighted term logged as a named f32 field (lm_loss, distill_loss, balance_loss, zrouter_loss, switch_loss, range_loss, zero_loss, shape_loss, overflow_loss, total_loss). (c) Phase transition events: info-level with before/after hardness, step number, checkpoint saved (bool). (d) Export events: info-level with artifact_core_hash, total_bytes, n_experts, ternary_weight_plan summary, scale_bytes_total. (e) Preflight events: info-level per-check with check_name, status (pass/warn/fail), detail string, numeric_value, threshold. (f) Shadow compile events: info-level with checkpoint_id, compile_profile, fit_status, quality_summary, frontier_size. (g) All events use structured key=value fields, never free-form format! strings for load-bearing data. (h) Test mode: capture events into a Vec<TestEvent> for assertion in unit tests. (i) Configurable via TOML: log level per module, output format (json/human), file/stderr routing.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- logging passes; TestEventCollector captures structured events; TOML config controls log level
close_reason:
Implemented bd-3aq as a scoped gbf-train structured logging framework.

Summary:
- Added `gbf_train::logging` typed logging config for `[training.logging]`, JSON/human output, stderr/file routing, module-level filters, and `init_logging`.
- Added canonical structured event constants and typed emitters for `training_phase` spans, `loss_step`, `phase_transition`, `export_complete`, `preflight`, `shadow_compile`, and scalar metrics.
- Added `TestEventCollector` for unit assertions plus a subscriber-level tracing capture test that verifies actual emitted fields and absence of load-bearing `message` fields.
- Updated the Burn `TracingMetricSink` to emit a canonical structured `scalar_metric` event instead of an ad hoc message string.
- Added follow-up `bd-3mrj` for real producer adoption across gbf-train/gbf-model/gbf-data and for the logging-overhead measurement gate.

Review feedback addressed:
| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Collector tests could pass while real tracing fields drifted. | Test collector was a mirrored side channel only. | Logging bead tests must include subscriber-level capture. | `actual_tracing_subscriber_observes_structured_event_and_span_fields` records real `tracing` fields. | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- logging` |
| Existing Burn metric sink emitted a message string and no event name. | New logging contract was not used by the existing tracing call site. | Canonical event names must be constants; avoid ad hoc direct event names/messages. | `structured_logging_source_avoids_message_strings_on_required_events` scans the Burn source for the old bad pattern. | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- logging`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --all-features` |
| Bead text could overclaim cross-crate producer adoption and <1% overhead. | The task mixed schema/framework setup with future producer integration and performance gates. | Closure must distinguish helper/schema contract from producer adoption and measured performance. | Follow-up bead `bd-3mrj` owns producer adoption and overhead gate. | `bd-3mrj` |
| `[training]` TOML without `[training.logging]` rejected unrelated config. | Parser fell back to treating the root TOML as logging config. | Missing logging config in a larger training config should default. | `logging_config_defaults_when_training_toml_omits_logging_section`. | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- logging` |

Claim-to-gate matrix:
| Claim | Gate |
| --- | --- |
| gbf-train logging API compiles | `cargo check -p gbf-train` |
| Logging config parses TOML and validates routing | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- logging` |
| Test collector captures typed event fields | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- logging` |
| Actual tracing subscriber sees structured fields, not message payloads | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- logging` |
| Burn adapter metric sink compiles with canonical structured metric event | `cargo test -p gbf-train --all-features` |
| No clippy warnings in gbf-train with Burn adapter enabled | `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --all-features -- -D warnings` |
| Formatting and whitespace are clean | `cargo fmt --check --all`; `git diff --check` |

Checks run:
- `cargo check -p gbf-train`
- `cargo check -p gbf-model -p gbf-data`
- `cargo test -p gbf-train -- logging` (10 passed)
- `cargo test -p gbf-train`
- `cargo test -p gbf-train --all-features` (41 passed)
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --all-features -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --check --all`
- `git diff --check`

Four-mode review:
- Mode 1 identified scope overclaim risk and unstable event taxonomy. Fixed taxonomy to `loss_step`/`export_complete`; moved producer adoption and overhead proof to `bd-3mrj`.
- Mode 2 identified ad hoc metric logging and collector-as-side-channel. Fixed the metric sink and added subscriber-level proof.
- Mode 3 identified the same metric path and a TOML missing-section edge case. Fixed both.
- Mode 4 identified evidence gaps around real tracing output and performance proof. Added the tracing subscriber test; explicitly did not claim performance proof.

Non-claims:
- This does not wire every real training/data/model producer to the logging framework; `bd-3mrj` owns that.
- This does not prove logging overhead <1%; `bd-3mrj` owns the measurement gate.
- This does not implement the E2E training log stream, teacher-freeze producer, or phase scheduler integration; downstream phase/E2E beads consume the schema.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`

- §II.1: Logging overhead < 1% of training step time

title: T-B14.1: Define core CompileKnobs types — values, bounds, locks, provenance
description: CompileKnobs core: CompileKnobValues with 8 sub-knobs (placement, observation, range, storage, sram, rom_window, overlay, schedule). Each has a declared monotone order (e.g., placement: StrictOnePerBank -> Budgeted -> PackedExperts). Plus CompileKnobBounds, KnobLockSet, CompileKnobId enum, ConstraintProvenance with PolicySource (TargetDefault/ProfileDefault/CompileRequestOverride/HintBundle/Calibration/RepairProposal). MonotoneKnob trait so admissibility checks are type-driven. Schema only.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B14.1: Define core `CompileKnobs` types — values + bounds + locks + provenance

**Target crate**: `gbf-policy`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/knobs/mod.rs` (new), `gbf-policy/src/knobs/values.rs`, `gbf-policy/src/knobs/bounds.rs`, `gbf-policy/src/knobs/locks.rs`, `gbf-policy/src/knobs/provenance.rs`
**Reference**: F-B14 oracle resolution comment on `bd-3ix`; planv0.md §"FeasibilityRefinementLoop".

### Background

The repair-mutable policy surface that the bounded refinement loop is allowed to advance through. Eight typed sub-knobs (placement, observation, range, storage, sram, rom_window, overlay, schedule) each with a declared monotone order. Schema only; loop logic and admissibility checks are T-B14.6.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct CompileKnobs {
    pub global: CompileKnobValues,
    pub bounds: CompileKnobBounds,
    pub overrides: CompileKnobOverrides,         // T-B14.2
    pub locks: KnobLockSet,
    pub provenance: BTreeMap<CompileKnobId, ConstraintProvenance>,
}

pub struct CompileKnobValues {
    pub placement: PlacementKnobs,
    pub observation: ObservationKnobs,
    pub range: RangeKnobs,
    pub storage: StorageKnobs,
    pub sram: SramKnobs,
    pub rom_window: RomWindowKnobs,
    pub overlay: OverlayKnobs,
    pub schedule: ScheduleKnobs,
}
```

Define the eight sub-knobs and their inner enums/structs verbatim from the oracle answer:

- `PlacementKnobs { profile: PlacementProfile }` — order `StrictOnePerBank → Budgeted → PackedExperts`.
- `ObservationKnobs { trace_demotion, optional_probe_floor }` with `TraceDemotionLevel` (None / DropBestEffort / DropDiagnosticAndBestEffort / RequiredOnly) and `ProbeBudgetClass` (Required / Important / Diagnostic / BestEffort).
- `RangeKnobs { reduction_ceiling }` with `ReductionPlanCeiling` (SingleI16Only / AllowChunkedI16 / AllowRenormLoop).
- `StorageKnobs { recompute_promotion }` with `RecomputePromotionLevel` (None / PureSliceValues / PureResumeWindowValues / PureTokenValues).
- `SramKnobs { page_aggression, spill_policy }` with `SramPageAggression` (KeepHotInWram / BatchSramAccesses / AllowColdSpills / FitFirstPagedSpills).
- `RomWindowKnobs { kernel_residency_bias, kernel_duplication_bias }` with `KernelResidencyBias` (ProfileDefault / PreferCoResident / PreferBank0Streaming / PreferWramOverlay / FitFirst) and `KernelDuplicationBias` (ShareKernels / DuplicateEntryStubs / DuplicateTinyKernels / DuplicateToSatisfyWindow).
- `OverlayKnobs { promotion }` with `OverlayPromotionLevel` (None / KernelsOnly / KernelsAndLutFragments / AnyOverlayable).
- `ScheduleKnobs { tile_search, slice_coarsening, pressure, stage_iters }` with `TileSearchKnobs { allowed_classes: BTreeSet<TileCandidateClass> }`, `TileCandidateClass` (SmallWorkingSet / Balanced / SwitchAmortized), `SliceCoarseningLevel` (Fine / ProfileDefault / CoarseWithinLatency / EpochCoalesced), `ResourcePressureThresholds` with `PressureLimit<T> { soft, hard }`, and `StageIterationCeilings` with per-stage `RefinementIterBudget(u8)`.

Plus the bound + lock types:

```rust
pub struct CompileKnobBounds {
    pub max_placement_profile: PlacementProfile,
    pub max_trace_demotion: TraceDemotionLevel,
    pub max_reduction_ceiling: ReductionPlanCeiling,
    pub max_recompute_promotion: RecomputePromotionLevel,
    pub max_sram_page_aggression: SramPageAggression,
    pub max_kernel_residency_bias: KernelResidencyBias,
    pub max_kernel_duplication_bias: KernelDuplicationBias,
    pub max_overlay_promotion: OverlayPromotionLevel,
    pub allowed_tile_classes: BTreeSet<TileCandidateClass>,
    pub max_slice_coarsening: SliceCoarseningLevel,
    pub stage_iters: StageIterationCeilings,
}

pub struct KnobLockSet { pub locked: BTreeSet<CompileKnobId> }

pub enum CompileKnobId {
    PlacementProfile,
    ObservationTraceDemotion,
    ObservationProbeSelection,
    RangeReductionCeiling,
    StorageRecomputePromotion,
    StorageMaterializationOverrides,
    SramPageAggression,
    SramSpillPolicy,
    RomKernelResidencyBias,
    RomKernelDuplicationBias,
    RomKernelResidencyOverrides,
    OverlayPromotion,
    ScheduleTileSearch,
    ScheduleSliceCoarsening,
    ScheduleResourcePressure,
    StageIterationCeilings,
}
```

`ConstraintProvenance { source: PolicySource, evidence: Vec<EvidenceRef> }` with `PolicySource` covering `TargetDefault | ProfileDefault | CompileRequestOverride | HintBundle | Calibration | RepairProposal(RepairProposalId)`. Define `RepairProposalId` here as a typed newtype.

### Monotone-order trait

Provide a small trait so T-B14.6's admissibility check is type-driven, not match-statement-driven:

```rust
pub trait MonotoneKnob: Eq {
    fn rank(&self) -> u8;
    fn is_advance(from: &Self, to: &Self) -> bool { from.rank() <= to.rank() }
}
```

Implement for each ordered enum.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-policy
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- knobs::serde_round_trip
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- knobs::monotone_orders            # rank() is dense and in declared order
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- knobs::knob_id_exhaustive          # All sub-knobs are reachable via CompileKnobId
cargo clippy -p gbf-policy -- -D warnings
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Every knob is an enum or typed struct; no opaque numeric tuning fields.
- §VI.1: Repair-mutable surface lives only in `gbf-policy::knobs`.

title: T6.6: Update expert byte budget formula for two-matrix + scales
description: The canonical expert byte formula for Ternary2 PerOutputRow Q8_8: bytes = ceil(d_model*d_ff/4) + d_ff*sizeof(ScaleFormat) + ceil(d_ff*d_model/4) + d_model*sizeof(ScaleFormat) + metadata_bytes. For d_model=128 d_ff=224 Q8_8: 7168+448+7168+256+~50 = ~15090 bytes. Add this as a compute_expert_bytes() function in gbf-model that the preflight and StaticBudgetReport both call. Must be consistent with TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost().
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget passes; Delegates to TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost; Known-value test for d_model=256 d_ff=512
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

- Artifact contract: this bead does not change artifact tensor identity. It defines a target-independent pre-lowering byte estimate for a two-matrix expert by delegating both projections to `TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost()`. The metadata overhead is explicitly named as estimated, not final packed-layout truth.
- differentiable Burn path: not applicable. This bead adds budget arithmetic and preflight/report helpers, not QAT forward math or autodiff behavior.
- Tests proving it: see the Claim-To-Gate matrix below.
- Acceptance movement: the available `gbf-train` preflight and compiler report modules are stubs. This bead adds the reusable checked preflight/report APIs now; full command/stage wiring is tracked by bd-t7x6.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |

comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/budget.rs` (new file)

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "expert byte budget", "compute_expert_bytes", "two-matrix", "bank budget". Also see bd-1jl (T3.1) for TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost.

### Implementation Notes
- Canonical expert byte formula for the default two-matrix Ternary2 PerOutputRow Q8_8 expert:
```rust
pub fn compute_expert_bytes(plan: &TernaryWeightPlan, d_model: u32, d_ff: u32) -> ByteCost {
    let up = plan.compute_byte_cost(d_model, d_ff);   // d_model -> d_ff
    let down = plan.compute_byte_cost(d_ff, d_model);  // d_ff -> d_model
    up + down  // plus any metadata overhead
}
```
- Must be **consistent** with `TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost()` — uses it, doesn't reimplement it.
- Include metadata overhead estimate (tile headers, alignment padding).
- This function is used by both the trainer (preflight) and compiler (StaticBudgetReport).

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                          # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget                  # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings           # No warnings
# Verify: compute_expert_bytes delegates to TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost
# Verify: known example — d_model=256, d_ff=512, Ternary2/PerOutputRow/Q8_8:
#   up: ceil(256*512/4) + 256*2 = 32768 + 512 = 33280 bytes
#   down: ceil(512*256/4) + 512*2 = 32768 + 1024 = 33792 bytes
#   total ~= 67072 bytes + metadata
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.4: Byte budget is a verifiable gate — fits or doesn't, exit 0/1
- §VI.2: The byte formula IS documentation — keep it in sync with planv0.md

title: F-E5: Cycle-model drift reports
description: Compare gbf-asm static cycle model to measured kernel runs; emit drift reports per kernel. Drift past threshold triggers cycle model revision.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-E5: Cycle-model drift reports

### What this is

Compare `gbf-asm` static cycle model predictions to measured kernel runs; emit drift reports per kernel. When drift exceeds threshold, the cycle model needs revision.

### Why this matters

The static cycle model (T-A1.5) is the load-bearing input for every cost estimate. Drift goes silent unless we test for it.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct CycleModelDriftReport {
    pub kernel: KernelSpecId,
    pub configuration: KernelConfigKey,
    pub predicted_cycles: u32,
    pub measured_cycles_p50: u32,
    pub drift_pct: f32,
    pub samples: u32,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A1.5 cycle model
- F-E3 KernelCalibrationBundle

### Confidence: HIGH

title: Represent tied embedding/classifier sharing in export artifact
description: Define and test how a tied input embedding/output classifier is represented after export. The bd-2v4 scalar model module proves one in-memory weight matrix can serve both embed and classify, but ArtifactCore/ExportVisitor currently model embedding and classifier tensors as separate weight entries. Add an explicit artifact/export contract so tied embeddings do not silently duplicate classifier payload/common-bank cost, and update export tests to prove the deployed artifact preserves the sharing semantics.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: bd-33q review handoff: F6 scalar/Burn tied embeddings do not claim export/artifact sharing. Keep this bead as the deployable tied embedding/classifier alias contract so export does not silently duplicate classifier payload/common-bank cost.

title: F-B10: ArenaPlan (Stage 9)
description: Concrete byte ranges for materialized values (StoragePlan output) plus WRAM region reservation (OverlayPlan). Named arenas: ping-pong activations, accum scratch, route scratch, decode scratch, continuation, persistent state pages, trace pages, harness blocks. Emits certs/arena.cert.json.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B10: ArenaPlan (Stage 9)

### What this is

Concrete byte ranges to materialized values selected by `StoragePlan`, plus reservation of WRAM regions selected by `OverlayPlan` (`planv0.md` line 1346). Allocates only `StoragePlan`-materialized values, not pure expression nodes directly.

Named arenas: ping-pong activations, accum scratch, route scratch, decode scratch, continuation record (with liveness), persistent sequence-state pages, trace pages, harness command/result blocks.

### Why this matters

Arenas are named so later stages — scheduling, placement, trace, runtime, `ScheduleOracle` — agree on the same storage geometry. Without that, debugging a slice means reverse-engineering byte offsets.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct ArenaPlan {
    pub wram_arenas: Vec<WramArena>,
    pub sram_arenas: Vec<SramArena>,
    pub hram_assignments: Vec<HramAssignment>,
    pub overlay_reservation: Option<OverlayReservation>,
}
```

Emits `certs/arena.cert.json`.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B6 StoragePlan
- F-B9 OverlayPlan
- F11 / bd-h04 WramReserved (hot_arena_floor must be respected)

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T5.5: Gate lambda_shape and lambda_overflow on ExpertShapePolicy
description: lambda_shape and lambda_overflow must be automatically disabled (set to 0.0) when ExpertShapePolicy::Fixed is active (the default). They should only activate when StructuredWidthGates is configured, because that's the only regime where exported byte cost depends on learned structure. Add a config validation check that errors if lambda_shape > 0 with Fixed expert shapes.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; Fixed policy + lambda_shape>0 -> warning + forced to 0; StructuredWidthGates + lambda_shape>0 -> allowed

description: Bounded monotone repair loop wrapping stages 5-11. comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B14: FeasibilityRefinementLoop + RepairPolicy + CompileKnobs

### What this is

The bounded monotone repair loop wrapping `RangePlan` → `StoragePlan` → `SramPagePlan` → `RomWindowPlan` → `OverlayPlan` → `ArenaPlan` → `GbSchedIR` (`planv0.md` line 1063). Passes don't call earlier passes recursively; they emit `RepairProposal`s against an explicit `CompileKnobs` surface. `ScheduleCostAnalysis` is the single objective oracle used by the loop.

### **BLOCKED on oracle question**

The `CompileKnobs` surface is **named but never defined** in planv0.md (line 1066). Per-profile `RepairPolicy` defaults are also unspecified. Both are out for oracle review (drafted in conversation 2026-04-26). When the oracle returns:
1. Land `CompileKnobs` definition.
2. Land BringUp / Default / Trace / Recovery profile defaults.
3. Implement loop driver.

### Why this matters

Without F-B14, the transform pipeline is brittle: any sub-pass failure aborts the whole compile. Real builds need to absorb fits-but-tight situations by autonomously promoting a kernel to overlay or a value to recompute.

### Design sketch (provisional)

```rust
pub struct RepairPolicy {
    pub max_refinement_iters: u8,
    pub allow_profile_fallback: bool,
    pub allow_trace_demotion: bool,
    pub allow_overlay_promotion: bool,
    pub allow_recompute_promotion: bool,
}

pub struct RepairProposal {
    pub source: PlanningStage,
    pub reason: RepairReason,
    pub tighten: ConstraintDelta,                    // writes against CompileKnobs (TBD)
    pub estimated_cost: EstimatedCostDelta,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B5..F-B11 (the wrapped stages)
- F-B12 ScheduleCostAnalysis (cost oracle)
- gbf-policy ResolvedCompilePolicy

### Confidence: BLOCKED. Cannot land before oracle question resolves.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Oracle resolution (2026-04-26): UNBLOCKED — full CompileKnobs design

The oracle question on `CompileKnobs` + per-profile `RepairPolicy` defaults was answered comprehensively. F-B14 is no longer blocked. The full design is recorded below; child tasks T-B14.1..T-B14.7 implement it.

### Headline shape

`CompileKnobs` becomes the **only repair-mutable policy surface** for the bounded refinement loop. Four sub-structures:

1. **`global: CompileKnobValues`** — the profile-resolved starting point.
2. **`bounds: CompileKnobBounds`** — how far repair may move each knob.
3. **`overrides: CompileKnobOverrides`** — typed targeted overrides inserted by accepted `RepairProposal`s.
4. **`locks: KnobLockSet`** — knobs frozen for this profile (initialized but not repair-mutable).

Plus `provenance: BTreeMap<CompileKnobId, ConstraintProvenance>` for `policy_resolution.json` audit.

### Naming fix

`RepairPolicy::allow_profile_fallback` is **renamed** to `allow_placement_profile_fallback` to disambiguate from `RiskPolicy::fallback_profile` (which remains the separate "try another `CompileProfile` after this one fails" mechanism). T-B14.5 owns this rename.

### Eight knob sub-structures

Each is a small typed value with a declared monotone repair order:

| Sub-knob | Type | Monotone order |
|---|---|---|
| `placement: PlacementKnobs` | `PlacementProfile` | `StrictOnePerBank → Budgeted → PackedExperts` |
| `observation: ObservationKnobs` | `TraceDemotionLevel`, `optional_probe_floor: ProbeBudgetClass` | `None → DropBestEffort → DropDiagnosticAndBestEffort → RequiredOnly` |
| `range: RangeKnobs` | `ReductionPlanCeiling` | `SingleI16Only → AllowChunkedI16 → AllowRenormLoop` |
| `storage: StorageKnobs` | `RecomputePromotionLevel` | `None → PureSliceValues → PureResumeWindowValues → PureTokenValues` |
| `sram: SramKnobs` | `SramPageAggression`, `SpillPolicy` | `KeepHotInWram → BatchSramAccesses → AllowColdSpills → FitFirstPagedSpills` |
| `rom_window: RomWindowKnobs` | `KernelResidencyBias`, `KernelDuplicationBias` | residency: `ProfileDefault → PreferCoResident → PreferBank0Streaming → PreferWramOverlay → FitFirst`; duplication: `ShareKernels → DuplicateEntryStubs → DuplicateTinyKernels → DuplicateToSatisfyWindow` |
| `overlay: OverlayKnobs` | `OverlayPromotionLevel` | `None → KernelsOnly → KernelsAndLutFragments → AnyOverlayable` |
| `schedule: ScheduleKnobs` | `TileSearchKnobs`, `SliceCoarseningLevel`, `ResourcePressureThresholds`, `StageIterationCeilings` | tile classes narrow monotonically; coarsening: `Fine → ProfileDefault → CoarseWithinLatency → EpochCoalesced` |

**Critical rule on recompute promotion**: only legal for **pure value/effect-free** computations. Never for RNG advancement, sequence writes, persistence commits, trace effects, or any value whose recomputation would duplicate an observable effect.

**Critical rule on tile search**: monotone means **narrowing candidate classes** (removing variants from the allowed set), never bouncing between arbitrary dimensions.

### Targeted overrides — typed selectors

```rust
pub struct CompileKnobOverrides {
    pub disabled_optional_probes: BTreeSet<TraceProbeId>,         // semantic observations NEVER listed here
    pub forced_kernel_residency: BTreeMap<KernelSelector, KernelResidency>,
    pub forced_recompute: BTreeSet<ValueSelector>,
    pub reduction_ceiling_overrides: BTreeMap<ReductionSelector, ReductionPlanCeiling>,
    pub tile_class_overrides: BTreeMap<TileSelector, BTreeSet<TileCandidateClass>>,
}
```

New typed selectors (added under gbf-foundation/gbf-policy as appropriate): `KernelSelector`, `ValueSelector`, `ReductionSelector`, `TileSelector`, `SliceClass`, `ReductionSiteId`, `SectionId`. They exist precisely so repair proposals don't use untyped strings or pass-local indices.

### `ConstraintDelta` — typed knob mutations

```rust
pub enum KnobDelta {
    AdvancePlacementProfile { to: PlacementProfile },
    SetTraceDemotion { to: TraceDemotionLevel },
    DisableOptionalProbes { probes: BTreeSet<TraceProbeId> },
    RaiseReductionCeiling { selector: Option<ReductionSelector>, to: ReductionPlanCeiling },
    PromoteRecomputeLevel { to: RecomputePromotionLevel },
    ForceRecompute { values: BTreeSet<ValueSelector> },
    AdvanceSramPageAggression { to: SramPageAggression },
    AdvanceKernelResidencyBias { to: KernelResidencyBias },
    AdvanceKernelDuplicationBias { to: KernelDuplicationBias },
    ForceKernelResidency { selector: KernelSelector, to: KernelResidency },
    PromoteOverlay { to: OverlayPromotionLevel },
    NarrowTileClasses { selector: TileSelector, remaining: BTreeSet<TileCandidateClass> },
    SetSliceCoarsening { to: SliceCoarseningLevel },
    UpdatePressureThreshold { update: ResourcePressureUpdate },
}
```

### Loop controller admissibility (six checks)

A proposed `ConstraintDelta` is admissible only when:

1. The target knob is **not locked** in `KnobLockSet`.
2. The corresponding `RepairPolicy.allow_*` toggle **permits** the mutation.
3. The new value is within `CompileKnobBounds`.
4. The mutation is **monotone** in the knob's declared order.
5. The mutation has **provenance** (records the originating `RepairProposalId`).
6. The mutation does **not violate** `ObservabilityMode` (`Invariant` mode forces `allow_trace_demotion = false`).

Only the **loop controller** applies `ConstraintDelta`s. Passes propose; passes do not mutate.

### Per-profile `RepairPolicy` defaults

| Profile | iters | `allow_placement_profile_fallback` | `allow_trace_demotion` | `allow_overlay_promotion` | `allow_recompute_promotion` |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| **Bringup** | **1** | false | false | false | false |
| **Default** | **4** | true | true | true | true |
| **Trace** (Invariant) | **2** | false | false | false | false |
| **Recovery** | **6** | true | true | true | true |

Crucial nuances:
- **Bringup defaults to 1, not 0.** One iteration absorbs deterministic local tile/slice fixes while still failing loudly on placement / overlay / recompute / trace policy. A separate `BringupFirstFit` / `--strict-first-fit` mode sets iters=0 explicitly.
- **`Trace` defaults to `ObservabilityMode::Invariant`.** Behavior-affecting knobs are resolved from a paired Default-compatible build, then frozen before trace instrumentation. Failing to fit under Invariant Trace is a **hard failure with a clear diagnostic**, not a silent demotion. `ObservabilityMode::Flexible` opens up Default-like repair but must emit `trace_loss_report.json` + `observability.cert.json`.
- **`Recovery` is fit-first.** May advance to `PackedExperts`, demote optional tracing to required-only, promote to `AnyOverlayable`, recompute up to `PureTokenValues`, advance SRAM to `FitFirstPagedSpills`, residency to `FitFirst`, duplication to `DuplicateToSatisfyWindow`. Does NOT bypass semantic observations, persistence correctness, ResourceStateValidation, ReachabilityValidation, interrupt-latency bounds, or liveness bounds.

### Lock-sets per profile

`Bringup`: locks everything except `ScheduleTileSearch` + `ScheduleSliceCoarsening`.
`Default`: locks only `ScheduleResourcePressure` + `StageIterationCeilings`.
`Trace` (Invariant): locks everything (behavior-affecting + tile/coarsening, since invariant must be resolved before instrumentation).
`Recovery`: locks only `ScheduleResourcePressure` + `StageIterationCeilings` (same as Default).

### Per-stage iteration ceilings

Single global `max_refinement_iters` is too coarse — one stage can consume the whole budget. Add per-stage `RefinementIterBudget` (range / storage / sram_page / rom_window / overlay / arena / schedule). Effective bound:
```
min(max_refinement_iters_remaining, per_stage_remaining)
```
These ceilings are **profile policy** (resolved at policy resolution), not repair-mutable.

### Resource pressure thresholds

`ResourcePressureThresholds` carries soft/hard `PressureLimit<T>` per resource (WRAM hot, HRAM hot, Bank0 ROM, switchable ROM window, SRAM window, slice cycles, interrupt latency, trace bytes/frame, persist bytes/frame, overlay installs/frame, bank switches/token, SRAM page switches/token).

Resolved once from `TargetProfile` + `RuntimeChromeBudget` + `SchedulerPolicy` + `TraceBudget` + `CompileObjective` + profile defaults + explicit request overrides. Each value carries `ConstraintProvenance::PolicySource`. Usually locked after resolution.

### What CompileKnobs intentionally does NOT contain

- Calibration constants (those live in `PlatformCalibrationBundle` / `KernelCalibrationBundle` / `RuntimeCalibrationBundle`).
- Target constants (those live in `TargetProfile`).
- Objective weights (those live in `CompileObjective`).
- Pass-private heuristics (those stay inside their pass).
- Opaque numeric tuning fields with no semantic meaning.

This keeps the repair surface finite, typed, profile-bounded, and auditable.

### Reports

- `policy_resolution.json` extended with `compile_knobs.{global,bounds,locks,overrides,provenance}` — every value has a `PolicySource` (TargetDefault / ProfileDefault / CompileRequestOverride / HintBundle / Calibration / RepairProposal).
- `repair_report.json` (new): proposed repairs (accepted + rejected), the lock/bound/policy reason for every rejection, before/after `CompileKnobs` delta for every accepted proposal, per-stage iteration counts, termination reason.

Rejected proposals are often the fastest explanation of why a build failed — that's why they're recorded.

### Engineering rule 25 (new)

> `CompileKnobs` is the only mutable repair surface for the bounded feasibility loop. Passes may emit `RepairProposal`s, but only the loop controller may apply `ConstraintDelta`s. Every repair mutation must be typed, monotone in a declared finite order, bounded by profile policy, rejected if locked, and recorded in `policy_resolution.json` / `repair_report.json` with provenance.

### Patches to planv0.md

The oracle provided 6 git-style patches to planv0.md (Patch 1: types; Patch 2: monotone repair semantics; Patch 3: profile defaults table; Patch 4: extended policy_resolution.json + new repair_report.json; Patch 5: report list; Patch 6: engineering rule 25). Application of those patches to `history/planv0.md` is a **separate documentation change** to be made when the user is ready — not a bead-level operation.

### Decomposition into child tasks

- **T-B14.1** Define core `CompileKnobs` types — `CompileKnobValues` + 8 sub-knob structs/enums + their declared monotone orders (gbf-policy/src/knobs/values.rs).
- **T-B14.2** Define `CompileKnobOverrides` + selector types (`KernelSelector`, `ValueSelector`, `ReductionSelector`, `TileSelector`, `SliceClass`, plus new `ReductionSiteId` / `SectionId` if not already in gbf-asm).
- **T-B14.3** Define `ConstraintDelta` + `KnobDelta` + `ResourcePressureUpdate` + admissibility-check primitives.
- **T-B14.4** Per-profile defaults — resolve initial `CompileKnobs` from each `CompileProfile` (Bringup / Default / Trace / Recovery) including bounds, lock-sets, and `ResourcePressureThresholds`.
- **T-B14.5** Rename `RepairPolicy::allow_profile_fallback` → `allow_placement_profile_fallback`. Small but important fix.
- **T-B14.6** `FeasibilityRefinementLoop` driver — admissibility checks, monotonicity guard, per-stage iteration ceilings, termination, integration with stages 5–11.
- **T-B14.7** Reports — extend `policy_resolution.json` to include `CompileKnobs`; emit new `repair_report.json` schema with proposed/accepted/rejected proposals.

### F-D6 status

Note: F-D6 (`SchedulePack` mode-switching cadence) was **not** part of this oracle answer and remains BLOCKED on its own outstanding question.

title: T12.4: Wire LinearState and BoundedKv into training scaffold and shadow_compile A/B
description: gbf-train phase scheduler, loss terms, and shadow_compile pipeline run for either SequenceSemanticsSpec without code branching outside of the SequenceBlock dispatch. shadow_compile produces side-by-side CheckpointFrontierPoints for both variants on the same workloads. Emit a SequenceStateComparisonReport (quality, schedule cost, projected bank-switch rate per token) so the production choice is data-backed.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate(s)**: `gbf-train` (scaffold dispatch + report), `gbf-test` (E2E coverage).
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/scaffold.rs` (new or refactor), `gbf-train/src/shadow.rs` (extend), `gbf-train/src/reports/sequence_state_comparison.rs` (new).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "shadow_compile", "CheckpointFrontierPoint", "training selection". Also F12 (bd-144), T12.2 (bd-tnb), T12.3 (bd-1jk).

### Implementation Notes

1. **Scaffold dispatches via `SequenceBlock` trait, not variant matching.**
   - `gbf-train` phase scheduler, loss terms, QAT module wiring, and checkpoint orchestration all call methods on a `dyn SequenceBlock` (or generic `SequenceBlock`) — not `match` on `SequenceSemanticsSpec`.
   - This is the testable contract: a grep for `SequenceSemanticsSpec::` outside `gbf-model` should return zero matches in training code paths.

2. **shadow_compile runs both variants.**
   - `ShadowCompilePolicy.requests` (planv0.md F8) is extended to optionally carry a `dual_sequence_state: bool` flag (or two parallel `CompileRequest`s). When enabled, every selected checkpoint is exported and compiled twice — once as LinearState, once as BoundedKv — under matching `CompileProfile`s (BringUp for both during experimentation).
   - Both produce `CheckpointFrontierPoint`s.

3. **`SequenceStateComparisonReport`.**

   ```rust
   pub struct SequenceStateComparisonReport {
       pub workload: WorkloadId,
       pub linear_state: SequenceVariantSummary,
       pub bounded_kv: SequenceVariantSummary,
       pub generated_at: DateTime<Utc>,
   }

   pub struct SequenceVariantSummary {
       pub quality: QualitySummary,
       pub schedule_cost: Option<EstimatedCostDelta>,
       pub projected_bank_switches_per_token: f32,
       pub projected_state_bytes: u32,
       pub fits_envelope: bool,
   }
   ```

   - Emitted under `artifacts/runs/<run_id>/sequence_state_comparison.json`.
   - Acts as the empirical input that justifies defining `Production-LinearState` or `Production-BoundedKv` `CompileProfile`s later.

4. **No production CompileProfile is created by this task.**
   - That decision waits for the report to actually contain data on a real workload. T12.4 ends when the report is generated for at least one tiny-model run.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- scaffold::dispatch                   # Scaffold uses trait, not variant match
cargo test -p gbf-train -- shadow::dual_path                    # Both variants run on a tiny model
cargo test -p gbf-test -- sequence_state_comparison_report      # Report shape stable
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings                         # No warnings
# Verify: grep for `SequenceSemanticsSpec::` outside gbf-model shows zero hits in training code
# Verify: shadow_compile run produces sequence_state_comparison.json with both variants populated
# Verify: report fits_envelope is computed against the BringUp profile and pinned RuntimeChromeBudget
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.4: `SequenceStateComparisonReport` is the verifiable reward that justifies adding a production CompileProfile.
- §V.1: Report is JSON, not human-readable text.
- §III.1: The "no variant matches outside gbf-model" rule is enforceable by a grep test.

title: F-A6: gbf-store + gbf-migrate Infrastructure
description: Content-addressed blob store + two-level StageCache + pinsets/GC + archive transport in gbf-store; host-side migration DAG + epochs + report types in gbf-migrate. Per planv0.md line 138 + line 1126: makes the stage cache work and makes contract evolution survivable. Independent of F-A1/A2/A3/A4/A5 (uses gbf-foundation only).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-A6: `gbf-store` + `gbf-migrate` Infrastructure

### Background

Two infrastructure crates that are crate-scaffolded but module-stubbed.

`gbf-store` is the dedicated CAS (content-addressed storage) crate. Per `planv0.md` line 138 + the latest revision pass: it owns content-addressed object store, two-level `StageCache`, archive/directory transport, pinsets/GC/eviction, integrity verification. It exists separate from `gbf-artifact` so the artifact crate stays schema-only and `gbf-codegen` does not grow its own blob store.

`gbf-migrate` owns host-side upgrade DAGs, deprecation windows, artifact/report/workload/calibration migrators (`planv0.md` line 1126). Per the engineering rules, "core crates define current schemas only; host-side migration logic lives in `gbf-migrate` so the compiler, oracles, and report consumers can assume 'current schema in memory.'"

### How this serves overarching project goals

- `gbf-store` is what makes the StageCache (`planv0.md` rule 20) work. Most of the compiler's intermediate artifacts get hashed and stored; recompilation reuses what's still legal.
- Lazy loading via `BlobRef` is also `gbf-store`'s job — large packed tensors do not have to be materialized eagerly.
- `gbf-migrate` is what makes contract evolution survivable. Without it, every schema change is a flag day.

### The problem this solves

Without F-A6:
- Stage outputs get re-derived on every compile.
- Artifacts can't be transported across machines without a custom container format.
- Schema changes require manual migration scripts that drift from the actual schemas.

### Design

`gbf-store` modules (already scaffolded):

```
gbf-store/src/
  blob.rs          # blobs/sha256/<hash> path layout, write/read primitives
  integrity.rs     # CRC + hash verification
  stage_cache.rs   # two-level: shard-local digests + global build hash
  pinset.rs        # named pin sets (which blobs survive GC)
  gc.rs            # eviction policy + dry-run + reporting
  archive.rs       # directory <-> single-file archive transport
```

`gbf-migrate` modules (already scaffolded):

```
gbf-migrate/src/
  dag.rs           # version-pair DAG of migrators
  epochs.rs        # CompatibilityEpochs (artifact, abi, calibration, reports)
  report.rs        # MigrationReport + lossy_fields + warnings
```

### Children (T-A6.1 ... T-A6.5)

1. **T-A6.1** `gbf-store` blob store + integrity.
2. **T-A6.2** `gbf-store` two-level `StageCache`.
3. **T-A6.3** `gbf-store` pinsets + GC.
4. **T-A6.4** `gbf-store` archive/directory transport.
5. **T-A6.5** `gbf-migrate` scaffolding — DAG types + epochs + reports.

### Dependencies on other Epic A features

None directly (uses `gbf-foundation::Hash256` + `BlobRef`, both already CLOSED in T0.3).

### Confidence: HIGH

Pure infrastructure. The key correctness invariant is content-addressed determinism: `hash(write(blob)) == hash(blob)`.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T-A6.1–T-A6.5.

### Feature Definition of Done

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-store                                          # All module tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-store -- blob::round_trip                       # write -> read returns identical bytes
cargo test -p gbf-store -- stage_cache::shard_invalidation        # Touching one component invalidates only its shard
cargo test -p gbf-store -- gc::pinset_protection                  # Pinned blobs survive GC
cargo test -p gbf-migrate                                         # All module tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-migrate -- dag::transitive_migration            # Multi-step upgrades chain correctly
cargo clippy -p gbf-store -p gbf-migrate -- -D warnings           # Clean
```

title: T11.3: Wire CompileProfile through CompileRequest and gbf-train preflight
description: Thread CompileProfileId through CompileRequest into preflight. Preflight enforces: ArenaPlan demand <= WramReserved.hot_arena_floor; largest expert kernel size <= WramLayoutPolicy.overlay_bytes; declared MoE topology satisfies max_bank_switches_per_token (soft pre-training, hard at export). Emit WramFitReport and SwitchBudgetReport blocks in DeployabilityReport.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate(s)**: `gbf-policy` (CompileRequest extension), `gbf-train` (preflight integration).
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/compile.rs` (extend `CompileRequest`), `gbf-train/src/preflight.rs` (extend).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "CompileRequest", "preflight", "WramFitReport", "SwitchBudgetReport". Also bd-27n's 2026-04-26 refinement comment.

### Implementation Notes

1. **Thread `CompileProfileId` through `CompileRequest`.**
   - `CompileRequest` already (per planv0.md) carries fields like `target`, `objectives`, `calibration`. Add `profile: CompileProfileId`.
   - Resolve to a concrete `CompileProfile` from the registry (T11.2) early in preflight.

2. **Extend `gbf-train` preflight checks.**
   Add three checks driven by `CompileProfile`:

   ```
   WramFitReport {
       overlay_demand_bytes: u16,        // largest expert kernel size
       overlay_capacity_bytes: u16,      // CompileProfile.wram_layout.overlay_bytes
       hot_arena_demand_bytes: u16,      // ArenaPlan estimate (or upper-bound estimate pre-ArenaPlan)
       hot_arena_floor_bytes: u16,       // RuntimeChromeBudget.wram_reserved.hot_arena_floor
       overlay_fits: bool,
       hot_arena_fits: bool,
   }

   SwitchBudgetReport {
       cap: u16,                         // CompileProfile.max_bank_switches_per_token
       projected_min: u16,               // sum over MoE layers of 1 (best case: same expert across all layers)
       projected_typical: u16,           // estimated from declared MoE topology
       fits: bool,
       severity: CheckSeverity,          // Soft pre-training, Hard at export
   }
   ```

3. **Cadence.**
   - Pre-training (training-start pin, see T2.3 / bd-27n): WramFitReport is a HARD check; SwitchBudgetReport is a SOFT (warning) check because no schedule exists yet.
   - At export (per-export re-validate, see T2.7): both reports are HARD checks. Export refuses on either failure.

4. **Diagnostic output.**
   Match the existing diagnostic style on T2.3 (bd-27n) — keep it copy-pasteable. Example:
   ```
   WRAM split: overlay=512, hot_arena_floor=4096 (BringUp profile).
   ArenaPlan demand: hot_arena=3712 -> PASS with 384 bytes margin.
   Overlay fit: largest expert kernel=380 bytes -> PASS with 132 bytes margin.
   Switch budget: cap=8, projected_typical=6 -> PASS with 2 switches margin.
   ```

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-policy                                  # Compiles
cargo check -p gbf-train                                   # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight::wram                  # WramFitReport produced and checked
cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight::switch_budget         # SwitchBudgetReport produced and checked
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings                    # No warnings
# Verify: preflight with overlay demand > capacity returns fits_envelope=false at export
# Verify: preflight with overlay demand > capacity returns risk_flag (not hard fail) pre-training
# Verify: missing CompileProfile in CompileRequest is a compile-time error (or a clear runtime error)
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.4 Verifiable Rewards: WramFitReport + SwitchBudgetReport produce exit-0/exit-1 outcomes.
- §V.3: Hard-failure diagnostics include profile id, demand, capacity, margin, and the originating CompileProfileId.
- §III.1: Preflight catches WRAM/switch failures before training/export costs are incurred.

title: Wire structured logging into training producers and add overhead gate
description: Adopt bd-3aq structured logging contract in real producer paths across gbf-train, gbf-model, and gbf-data where training emits events. Replace direct or ad hoc tracing call sites with canonical event names (loss_step, phase_transition, teacher_freeze, export_complete, preflight, shadow_compile, scalar_metric), prove subscriber-captured fields in integration tests, and add an explicit logging-overhead gate before claiming CONSTITUTION §II.1 <1% overhead. This owns the bd-3aq review feedback that the framework landed before full producer adoption.
close_reason:
Implemented structured logging adoption follow-up for the producer paths that exist today, with explicit non-claims for future paths.

Scope completed:
- Added canonical `teacher_freeze` event support to `TrainingLogEmitter` and test collector plumbing.
- Added `ExpertSlotPreflightEvent` and `TrainingLogEmitter::expert_slot_preflight` so the real preflight expert-slot path logs unit-bearing byte fields: `expert_bytes`, `expert_slot_usable_bytes`, `slack_bytes`, `over_by_bytes`, plus `budget_computed`.
- Wired `ExpertBudgetPreflightReport::check_expert_slot_with_logging` as the concrete preflight producer adoption path, including structured fail events for invalid budget inputs.
- Added `LoggingOverheadMeasurement` / `LoggingOverheadGate` as an explicit measured-value gate for the CONSTITUTION §II.1 `<1%` check. This is not a benchmark and does not make a measured overhead claim.
- Added `gbf-train/tests/structured_logging.rs` subscriber-capture integration tests for canonical helper fields, the real preflight producer path, and the Burn scalar metric sink.
- Reparented `bd-3mrj` under F10 (`bd-1uj`).

Support matrix:

| Event | Real producer status | Evidence / follow-up |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `preflight` | Adopted for expert-slot budget preflight via `ExpertBudgetPreflightReport::check_expert_slot_with_logging` | `cargo test -p gbf-train --test structured_logging`; `preflight_producer_is_captured_by_tracing_subscriber`; unit tests cover pass/fail/invalid budget input |
| `scalar_metric` | Existing Burn metric producer path uses canonical event name | `cargo test -p gbf-train --all-features --test structured_logging`; `scalar_metric_sink_uses_canonical_event_name` |
| `loss_step` | Schema/helper only; no real training loop producer exists yet | Follow-up `bd-2sd7` |
| `phase_transition` | Schema/helper only; no phase-transition producer exists yet | Follow-up `bd-2sd7` |
| `teacher_freeze` | Schema/helper added; no dense-teacher freezer producer exists yet | Follow-up `bd-2sd7` |
| `export_complete` | Schema/helper only; no training export orchestration producer exists yet | Follow-up `bd-2sd7` |
| `shadow_compile` | Schema/helper only; `gbf-train/src/shadow.rs` is still a stub | Follow-up `bd-2sd7` |
| Logging overhead `<1%` | Gate type added; no measured overhead claim made | Measured benchmark/gate follow-up `bd-zy3j` |

Review feedback and fixes:
- Intent review found the bead scope broader than current producer surfaces. Fixed with support matrix, `bd-2sd7`, `bd-zy3j`, and reparenting under `bd-1uj`.
- Design review found typed byte data was squeezed into generic `numeric_value` / `threshold`. Fixed with unit-bearing preflight byte fields.
- Semantic review found invalid preflight-budget inputs were not logged. Fixed with structured fail event emission before returning `PreflightLoggingError::Budget`.
- Evidence review found helper tests could be mistaken for producer adoption and overhead proof was synthetic. Fixed test names/scope, added full-field subscriber assertions, and explicitly moved measured overhead proof to `bd-zy3j`.

Feedback-harness reflection:

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Broad logging bead text exceeded current producer surfaces | Task assumed future training/export/shadow producers existed | Closure must include event-by-event support matrix and moved-to beads for missing producers | `bd-2sd7`; closure support matrix | `logging-bead-closure` (already covers this) |
| Overhead gate confused with overhead proof | A comparator over synthetic values looked like a performance claim | Separate explicit measured-value gate from benchmark ownership; no `<1%` claim without measured workload | `bd-zy3j`; doc comments on `LoggingOverheadMeasurement` / `LoggingOverheadGate` | `logging-bead-closure` (already covers this) |
| Preflight budget fields lost units | Generic preflight numeric schema was reused for byte budget data | Use unit-bearing structured fields when producer data has meaningful units | `preflight_producer_is_captured_by_tracing_subscriber` asserts byte fields | `gbf-train::preflight` / logging tests |
| Helper subscriber tests overclaimed adoption | Tests drove `TrainingLogEmitter` directly while bead asked for producer adoption | Name helper tests as schema/helper evidence; producer-adoption tests must drive real entrypoints | `preflight_producer_is_captured_by_tracing_subscriber`; scalar sink test | `gbf-train/tests/structured_logging.rs` |

No skill patch was needed: `logging-bead-closure` already requires distinguishing schema/helpers from producer adoption, moved-to beads for incomplete adoption, subscriber capture, and no overhead percentage without benchmark/gate evidence.

Commands run:
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `cargo test -p gbf-train`
- `cargo test -p gbf-train --all-features --test structured_logging`
- `cargo test -p gbf-train --all-features`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --all-features -- -D warnings`

title: T9.3: Implement hardening/pruning export for adaptive widths (M6)
description: M6 research: after supernet training, harden by: (a) prune blocks whose gate < threshold, (b) recompute TernaryWeightPlan with actual (pruned) dimensions, (c) export with variable-size expert payloads. The compiler's StaticBudgetReport and placement logic must handle variable expert sizes. The ExportVisitor must emit the actual d_ff per expert, not the max d_ff.

comments.author: bkase
