title: F-B7: SramPagePlan (Stage 7)
description: SRAM analogue of RomWindowPlan. Active SRAM working sets, page-switch batching, cold-spill residency, commit ordering. Single 8 KiB SRAM window means SRAM pressure deserves first-class planning.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B7: SramPagePlan (Stage 7)

### What this is

The SRAM analogue of `RomWindowPlan` (`planv0.md` line 1303). Plans active SRAM working sets, page-switch batching, cold-spill residency, commit ordering for all `SramPaged` materializations before byte ranges are assigned.

### Why this matters

Only one 8 KiB SRAM window is visible at a time. SRAM-page planning needs first-class status the same way ROM-window planning does, otherwise SRAM pressure becomes a blind spot spread across `StoragePlan`, persistence rules, and scheduling.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct SramPagePlan {
    pub active_sets: Vec<SramWorkingSet>,
    pub page_bindings: Vec<SramPageBinding>,
    pub commit_boundaries: Vec<CommitBoundary>,
    pub spill_policy: SpillPolicy,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B6 StoragePlan
- F-A3.5 SramPageBinding (resource lease type)

### Confidence: HIGH

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: Owner clarification from bd-1jk: this SramPagePlan feature owns deploy memory lowering for BoundedKv KV slabs, including SRAM paging, WRAM hot-tile residency, page-switch batching, and commit-boundary writeback. bd-1jk only implements the scalar gbf-model byte-slab semantics and does not prove SramPagePlan lowering.

title: F-D5: Fault Policy Table + Recovery + BootValidationPlan
description: FaultPolicy maps FaultDomain -> RecoveryAction (ColdStart/DemoteToSafeMode/AbortAndPanic/BootValidationOnly). Boot runs BootValidationPlan and decides recovery. Default policy table.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-D5: Fault Policy Table + Recovery + BootValidationPlan

### What this is

`FaultPolicy` maps `FaultDomain` → `RecoveryAction` (`ColdStart` / `DemoteToSafeMode` / `AbortAndPanic` / `BootValidationOnly`). Boot runs `BootValidationPlan` (validate persistence, validate runtime_nucleus_hash, validate artifact_core_hash) and decides recovery.

### Why this matters

Faults are graded; not every fault should cold-start the entire session. Liveness fault → demote mode; persistence checksum → cold-start that record family but keep others; ABI version mismatch → AbortAndPanic with structured fault snapshot.

### Design sketch

`gbf-runtime/src/fault_policy.rs` (new). Default `FaultPolicy` table; consumed by F-A5.7 panic, F-D1 persistence, F-D4 drift monitor.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-A3.4 FaultCode + FaultPolicy + RecoveryAction
- F-D1 persistence (validates record families)
- F-A5.7 panic

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T-A1.3: Builder eDSL — typed instruction emitter, Db/Dw, pseudo-ops
description: The Builder API surface used by runtime authors and compiler-generated code. Emits Instr with provenance, supports Db/Dw, and lowers pseudo-ops (BankLease, BankRelease, FarCall, Yield, TraceProbe, AssertBank). Raw(Vec<u8>) is an audited escape hatch; lint discourages use.
close_reason:
Implemented the `gbf-asm` builder eDSL for typed section authoring.

Scope completed:
- Added `Builder` in `gbf-asm/src/builder.rs` with typed `emit`, `db`, `dw`, `label`, `align`, `raw`, provenance scoping, and pseudo-op methods.
- Extended `SectionItem` with label, align, pseudo-op, and raw item variants; all item variants carry `InstrProvenance`.
- Added pseudo-op marker types for BankLease, BankRelease, FarCall, Yield, TraceProbe, and AssertBank authoring intent.
- Added basic builder-side lease lifecycle/range validation for duplicate leases, unknown releases, stale far-call lease chains, SRAM assertion ranges, and MBC5 ROM/SRAM lease ranges.
- Kept raw bytes behind `Builder::raw`; the raw item constructor is crate-private and `Section` mutation is builder-mediated.
- Replaced misleading zero-byte symbolic sizing with `fixed_byte_len()` / `fixed_item_bytes() -> Option<u32>` so align and pseudo-op widths remain unknown until their owning lowering/layout pass.
- `with_provenance` restores the previous provenance on normal return and on caught panic.

IR lifecycle and moved boundaries:
- `Builder` emits symbolic pre-layout section IR containing concrete `Instr` items plus labels, alignment directives, pseudo-op markers, and audited raw byte items.
- Branch relocation and branch relaxation remain owned by `bd-c4s`.
- Final opcode bytes and pseudo-op byte lowering remain owned by `bd-2o3`.
- BankLease runtime ABI implementation and concrete MBC writes remain owned by downstream banking/runtime beads such as `bd-19j`.
- MachineEffect and PrivilegeClass typing remain owned by `bd-1bw`; this bead only preserves provenance and authoring intent.
- Final align padding, far-call thunk insertion, and reachability validation are not claimed here.

Subagent review results and fixes:
- Mode 1 found the builder lifecycle and pseudo-op ownership under-specified. Fixed with module docs and closure boundaries.
- Mode 2 found missing symbolic reference lifecycle, porous raw bypasses, missing effect/privilege ownership, under-validated pseudo-op state, and misleading size accounting. Fixed by narrowing scope, locking raw mutation to builder paths, adding lease validation, and making symbolic widths unknown.
- Mode 3 found align/pseudo sizing as the highest-risk semantic bug, plus lease lifecycle/range gaps and provenance restoration on panic. Fixed with `Option` sizing, lifecycle/range tests, and panic-safe provenance restoration.
- Mode 4 found weak pseudo-op/provenance evidence and raw-audit ambiguity. Fixed by asserting exact pseudo-op payload sequences and provenance across label/align/pseudo/raw/instr/db/dw paths.

Feedback-harness reflection:

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Builder lifecycle blurred symbolic pre-layout items with concrete `Instr` lowering | `Instr` and builder docs described different stages without naming the bridge | Builder beads must state they emit symbolic pre-layout section IR and move relocation/lowering to owner beads | Module docs and closure moved-boundary section | `AGENTS.md` ASM / ISA Beads; `bd-3p1` |
| Raw byte escape hatch was porous through public section mutation and raw item construction | Raw policy lived only in `Builder::raw` prose | Keep raw constructors crate-private or guarded, and keep section mutation behind builder APIs | `Section` fields/private `push`; crate-private `SectionItem::raw`; `builder::provenance_recorded` covers raw provenance | `gbf-asm/src/section.rs`; `gbf-asm/src/builder.rs` |
| Align/pseudo-op width was encoded as zero | Fixed-size API tried to account for symbolic items too early | Unknown-width symbolic items must return `None`/unknown, not zero | `section::section_items_carry_provenance_and_size`; `builder::roundtrip`; `builder::pseudo_ops_dont_panic` | `gbf-asm` tests |
| Pseudo-op test only checked variant class, not payloads | Evidence tested non-panics instead of semantic recording | Pseudo-op tests must assert exact payloads and ordering | `builder::pseudo_ops_dont_panic` exact sequence assertion | `gbf-asm/src/builder.rs` |
| Lease pseudo-ops allowed stale/duplicate IDs and invalid bank ranges | Builder did not track authoring-state invariants | If builder tracks lease-like state, test duplicate, unknown, released, and range-error paths | `builder::builder_validates_lease_lifecycle_and_bank_ranges` | `gbf-asm/src/builder.rs` |
| Provenance scope could leak after caught panic | Temporary builder state was restored only on normal return | Scope helpers that mutate builder state must restore on normal return and caught panic | `builder::provenance_scope_restores_after_caught_panic` | `gbf-asm/src/builder.rs` |
| Effect/privilege expectations could be overclaimed | MachineEffect/PrivilegeClass has a separate owner bead | Move privilege/effect validation explicitly to `bd-1bw` until implemented | Closure moved-boundary section | `bd-1bw` |

Claim-to-gate matrix:

## F-H2: Kernel implementations (matvec, residual, norm, route, decode)

### What this is

The first wave of kernel implementations as `AsmIR` builders. Each tied to a `KernelSpecId` and (eventually) a `KernelCalibrationBundle`. Initial set: ternary matvec, residual add, clipped norm, top-1 router, greedy decode.

### Why this matters

These are the actual compute primitives the compiler emits. Quality of these directly determines tokens/sec.

### Design sketch

`gbf-kernel/src/impls/matvec_ternary.rs`, `residual_add.rs`, `norm_affine_clip.rs`, `top1_router.rs`, `decode_greedy.rs`. Each returns `Section`s buildable via `AsmIR`.

- F-H1 KernelSpec
- F-A1 gbf-asm builder
- F-A4 BankLease ABI (some kernels need lease patterns)

### Confidence: MEDIUM. Each kernel is real engineering work. Steady-state across milestones.

title: F-A2: gbf-hw — Verified Memory Map, MBC5, LCD/Timing, Calibration Schema
description: Single source of truth for hardware constants per planv0.md line 210. Fill gbf-hw (target, memory, mbc5, lcd, timing, interrupts, joypad, calibration). Includes TargetProfile + CartridgeProfile, the DMG/MBC5 memory map predicates, MBC5 register semantics, LCD modes + frame timing, IRQ vectors, joypad register, and calibration bundle SCHEMA (bundle production is Epic E).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-A2: `gbf-hw` — Verified Memory Map, MBC5, LCD/Timing, Calibration Schema

### Background

`gbf-hw` is the **single source of truth** for hardware constants, target profiles, and calibration schema. Compiler and runtime both import it. The plan (`planv0.md` line 210): "the compiler and runtime should both import these instead of carrying magic numbers."

The DMG/MBC5 facts that matter (`planv0.md` line 115): a fixed 16 KiB ROM bank at `$0000-$3FFF`, a single switchable 16 KiB ROM window at `$4000-$7FFF`, an 8 KiB external RAM window at `$A000-$BFFF` backed by up to 128 KiB across 16 banks, 8 KiB WRAM, 8 KiB VRAM, 127 bytes HRAM. DMG master clock 4.194304 MHz, system clock 1/4 of that, frame = 70224 dots ≈ 16.74 ms ≈ 17.5k normal-speed M-cycles. VBlank ≈ 59.7 Hz, lasts ~1.1 ms.

### How this serves overarching project goals

Every later pass derives from `gbf-hw`:

- `RomWindowPlan` (Epic B) uses the fixed-vs-switchable split.
- `SramPagePlan` (Epic B) uses the 8 KiB SRAM window + 16 banks.
- `gbf-runtime::banking` (F-A4) uses MBC5 register addresses.
- `video_commit` (F-A5) uses LCD modes + VBlank timing.
- `RuntimeChromeBudget` (`bd-1hv`) is sized against memory-map constants from here.
- `PlatformCalibrationBundle` schema lives here even though concrete bundles get measured in Epic E.

### The problem this solves

Without `gbf-hw`:
- Magic numbers proliferate: every module re-declares `0x4000` or `0xA000`.
- "Is this address in WRAM?" becomes a per-caller predicate, often wrong.
- MBC5 register semantics get rediscovered (and re-misunderstood) in three places.
- LCD-mode timing ends up as comments rather than typed predicates.

### Design

Module layout (matches the existing scaffold):

```
gbf-hw/src/
  target.rs        # TargetProfile, ConsoleModel, CartridgeProfile, CapabilitySet
  memory.rs        # MemoryMap: bank0 / switchable / sram / wram / vram / hram / oam / io
  mbc5.rs          # MBC5 register addresses ($0000, $2000, $3000, $4000) + RAM-enable value $0A
  lcd.rs           # LCD modes (HBlank, VBlank, OAMSearch, Drawing), LY=144 marker
  timing.rs        # M-cycles per frame, VBlank cycles, master clock constants
  interrupts.rs    # IE/IF register addresses + bit semantics + interrupt vector addresses
  joypad.rs        # P1/JOYP register semantics + button bit layout
  calibration.rs   # PlatformCalibrationBundle / KernelCalibrationBundle / RuntimeCalibrationBundle / CalibrationSetRef SCHEMA
```

`gbf-hw` is `no_std + alloc` capable per `planv0.md` line 2502.

### Children (T-A2.1 ... T-A2.7)

1. **T-A2.1** `TargetProfile` + `ConsoleModel` + `CartridgeProfile` + `CapabilitySet`.
2. **T-A2.2** Memory map constants and predicates.
3. **T-A2.3** MBC5 register semantics.
4. **T-A2.4** LCD modes + frame timing.
5. **T-A2.5** Interrupt vectors + IE/IF semantics.
6. **T-A2.6** Joypad register.
7. **T-A2.7** Calibration schema (types only — bundle production is Epic E).

### Confidence: HIGH

Verified against Pan Docs. The `TargetProfile` shape is the only minor choice; everything else is transcribing well-known constants.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T-A2.1–T-A2.7.

### Feature Definition of Done

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-hw                                          # All module tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- memory::predicates                     # is_wram / is_sram / is_rom_bank0 / is_rom_switchable correctness
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- mbc5::ram_enable_canonical_value       # RAM-enable register accepts $0A
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- timing::frame_cycles_match_pan_docs    # 17556 M-cycles per frame
cargo clippy -p gbf-hw -- -D warnings                          # Clean
```

title: T3.5: Independent ternary pack/unpack reference using TernaryWeightPlan
description: In gbf-verify, implement a slow independent ternary packer/unpacker that takes a canonical float tensor + TernaryWeightPlan and produces packed ternary bytes + scale bytes. This reference implementation must be structurally different from the production packer in gbf-model's ExportVisitor. Nightly tests compare production vs reference on randomized tensors. This prevents a shared bug in the packing logic from becoming self-validating.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-verify exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary passes; Reference packer agrees with production on 100 random tensors; Structurally different implementation
close_reason:
QAT Closure Checklist

Artifact contract
Implemented a verifier-only ternary byte oracle in `gbf-verify::ternary`.

- Weight packing consumes canonical ternary values (`-1`, `0`, `+1`) plus `TernaryWeightPlan` and emits deterministic packed bytes for `WeightEncoding::Ternary2`, `WeightEncoding::SparseTernaryBitplanes`, and `WeightEncoding::Binary1`.
- Scale packing consumes canonical raw Q8.8 scale payloads (`u16`) and emits little-endian bytes. It does not re-quantize production scale tensors at the packet boundary.
- Float projection is separate and requires explicit per-row thresholds because `TernaryWeightPlan` carries threshold strategy names, not concrete threshold values.
- The deployed weight byte conventions are documented on `WeightEncoding` in `gbf-artifact`.

Production byte agreement is not claimed in this bead: current `ExportVisitor` emits canonical `I8` ternary tensors and `U16` Q8.8 scale tensors, not packed production bytes. Randomized production-vs-reference agreement is moved to `bd-19b`/`bd-22o`, which already own production/reference packer agreement once comparable production bytes exist.

differentiable Burn path
Not applicable. This bead is verifier-only and does not change the scalar model core or Burn training adapters. The existing model/export path remains the owner of QAT projection and export state.

Tests proving it
- `cargo check -p gbf-verify`
- `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-verify -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --check --all`
- `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`

Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `project_reference_ternary_values` with explicit per-row thresholds | supported as verifier reference helper | not applicable | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_projects_float_weights_with_per_row_thresholds` |
| `WeightEncoding::Ternary2` reference packing/unpacking | not applicable | not applicable | supported as verifier oracle; production agreement moved to `bd-19b`/`bd-22o` | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_packs_known_ternary2_bytes` |
| `WeightEncoding::SparseTernaryBitplanes` reference packing/unpacking | not applicable | not applicable | supported as verifier oracle; production agreement moved to `bd-19b`/`bd-22o` | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_packs_sparse_bitplanes_independently` |
| `WeightEncoding::Binary1` reference packing/unpacking | zero values rejected | not applicable | supported as verifier oracle for sign-only values; production agreement moved to `bd-19b`/`bd-22o` | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_binary_round_trips_sign_values_and_rejects_zero` |
| Q8.8 scale byte packing from raw canonical `u16` scale values | not applicable | not applicable | supported as verifier oracle | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_packs_q8_8_scale_values_little_endian` |
| `ScaleFormat::Q4_4` and `ScaleFormat::Pow2` scale bytes | not applicable | not applicable | rejected in verifier scale-byte APIs; artifact support moved to `bd-38om` | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_rejects_non_q8_8_scales_until_artifact_encoding_exists` |

Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Reference weight bytes have deterministic known-vector layouts for current `WeightEncoding` variants. | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_packs_known_ternary2_bytes ternary_reference_packs_sparse_bitplanes_independently ternary_reference_binary_round_trips_sign_values_and_rejects_zero` | none | Known vectors pin code mapping, bit order, plane order, and padding behavior. |
| Reference weight unpackers round-trip randomized ternary inputs. | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_randomized_round_trips_supported_weight_encodings` | none | Uses a local deterministic LCG and does not call production packer code. |
| Reference scale byte packing consumes canonical Q8.8 raw scale payloads and rejects future scale formats. | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_packs_q8_8_scale_values_little_endian ternary_reference_rejects_non_q8_8_scales_until_artifact_encoding_exists` | none | `Q4_4`/`Pow2` artifact representation is moved to `bd-38om`. |
| Projection, quantization, and byte packing are separate verifier claims. | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_projects_float_weights_with_per_row_thresholds ternary_reference_rejects_invalid_projection_and_scale_numbers` | none | No public plan-aware projection/packing shortcut remains. |
| Reference API validates error paths instead of accepting malformed byte streams. | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_rejects_shape_and_length_mismatches ternary_reference_rejects_reserved_ternary2_codes_and_padding ternary_reference_rejects_sparse_signs_without_nonzero_values` | none | Covers shape, length, reserved code, padding, and sparse sign invariants. |
| QAT closure metadata and architecture constraints remain valid. | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Ran before closure. |

No-future

- `ScaleFormat::Q4_4` and `ScaleFormat::Pow2` are explicitly rejected by verifier scale-byte APIs until `bd-38om` defines artifact/export tensor representation and reconstruction tests.
- `WeightEncoding::Binary1` rejects zero-valued ternary weights because the one-bit encoding cannot represent them.
- No production-vs-reference byte agreement is claimed until production emits packed bytes; that acceptance remains with `bd-19b`/`bd-22o`.
- No `ThresholdPlan` variant is silently accepted for projection. Projection helpers take explicit thresholds and byte packers take canonical ternary values.

Acceptance movement

- Original criterion: “takes a canonical float tensor + `TernaryWeightPlan` and produces packed ternary bytes + scale bytes.” Implemented as separated projection plus canonical payload byte packing because the plan lacks concrete thresholds and scales. The implemented artifact-adjacent byte contract is canonical ternary values plus raw Q8.8 scale payloads.
- Original criterion: “reference packer produces same output as production packer on random inputs.” Moved to `bd-19b`/`bd-22o` because current production export emits canonical tensors, not packed production bytes.
- Non-Q8.8 scale byte support (`Q4_4`, `Pow2`) is rejected here and moved to `bd-38om`.

Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Plan-aware projection shortcut ignored `plan.threshold`. | Bead phrasing conflated strategy metadata with concrete thresholds. | Updated `qat-bead-closure`: reference packer beads must separate projection, quantization, and byte packing unless the plan carries concrete values. | Removed the shortcut; `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary_reference_projects_float_weights_with_per_row_thresholds ternary_reference_rejects_invalid_projection_and_scale_numbers`. | `.agents/skills/qat-bead-closure/SKILL.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary` |
| Production-vs-reference agreement was requested before production emits packed bytes. | Acceptance criterion assumed a materializer that does not exist yet. | Closure must mark this moved and name the owner bead. | QAT closure matrix plus `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; future owner gates in `bd-19b`/`bd-22o`. | `bd-19b`, `bd-22o` |
| Non-Q8.8 scale formats risked future-variant acceptance. | Public plan variants exist before artifact tensor encodings. | Existing no-future rule plus the new reference-packer guide. | `ternary_reference_rejects_non_q8_8_scales_until_artifact_encoding_exists`. | `bd-38om`; `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary` |
| Byte conventions were only implied by the verifier implementation. | `WeightEncoding` documented byte cost but not code mapping/bit order. | Document deployed weight byte conventions on the artifact enum. | Known-vector tests for each encoding. | `gbf-artifact/src/weight_plan.rs`; `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary` |

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:

title: F4: Phased Training with Dense Teacher and Explicit QAT Schedule
description: Training proceeds in 5 explicit phases (DenseTeacherWarmup -> RouterWarmup -> ExpertTernaryQat -> FullNumericQat -> HardenAndSelect) with per-phase QAT hardness control. Frozen dense teacher serves as distillation target and default ReferenceModelBundle.
acceptance_criteria: All T4.x tasks closed; Phase types, scheduler, teacher, distillation all tested

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F4: Phased Training with Dense Teacher and Explicit QAT Schedule

### Background

Ternary QAT training does not go from random initialization to fully hard-quantized model in a single training run. The literature (and practical experience with BitNet, 1-bit LLMs, etc.) shows that stability requires a careful phase schedule:

1. First train a decent model at higher precision
2. Then gradually introduce quantization constraints
3. Use the higher-precision model as a distillation target

Without this, ternary training tends to collapse: the STE gradients are noisy, the router can't find stable specialization when expert weights are simultaneously being quantized, and activation ranges swing wildly.

### The five phases

**Phase A (DenseTeacherWarmup)**: Train with no hard ternary, no activation fake quant. Router can be soft top-1. Goal: get a model with good quality and stable expert specialization. This phase produces the dense teacher checkpoint.

**Phase B (RouterWarmup)**: Freeze the basic model geometry. Turn on hard top-1 routing (with Gumbel or straight-through), load-balance loss, z-loss, expert dropout, and temporal switch regularization. Goal: make the router commit to stable, deployment-friendly patterns before quantization noise hits.

**Phase C (ExpertTernaryQat)**: Turn on hard/soft ternary projection for expert up/down projections only. Learn or anneal per-row thresholds. Fake-quantize the exported scales. Distill from the frozen dense teacher (logit-level KL divergence). Goal: ternarize expert weights while the teacher provides a stable gradient signal.

**Phase D (FullNumericQat)**: Turn on activation fake quant at expert input/output, residual boundaries, and deployable norm modules. Keep range penalties on. Goal: make the entire forward pass match the deployed numeric behavior.

**Phase E (HardenAndSelect)**: Run EMA export, shadow compile, small conformance suite, and frontier selection. Goal: pick the best checkpoint that balances quality, conformance, and deployment fit.

### Why the dense teacher matters for the architecture

The dense teacher checkpoint is not just a training aid — it becomes the default ReferenceModelBundle. This gives you a natural, high-quality denotational reference without needing a separate "reference model" training run. The approximation relation between DenotationalOracle (running the dense teacher) and ArtifactOracle (running the ternary artifact) is exactly the quantization gap, which is what conformance.json should measure.

### Confidence: HIGH with reservations

The phases themselves are textbook. The exact phase boundaries (which step to switch at) will need tuning once we have actual training data and loss curves. The types (TrainPhaseSpec, QuantHardness, RouterTrainMode) are clearly right even if the default phase schedule changes.

title: T-A5.4: Text renderer + font
description: 8x8 mono font (4096 bytes for 256 glyphs) + DMG-sized 20x18 layout. emit_text_print_glyph stages a UiCommitOp::PutGlyph through video_commit — never writes VRAM directly. Bank 0 cost is significant (~25%); revisit if budget tightens.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A5.4: Text renderer + font

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-runtime/src/text.rs`, plus a font asset (`gbf-runtime/assets/font_8x8.bin` or similar)
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 1626, line 1651 (font/assets in Bank 0).

### Background

The text renderer is what gets the model's tokens onto the screen. It writes glyph tiles into VRAM and updates the BG map. Crucially, all VRAM writes go through `video_commit` (T-A5.6) — text is a *producer* into the commit queue, not a direct VRAM writer.

### Background risk: Bank 0 budget

A typical 8×8 mono font has 256 glyphs × 16 bytes = 4096 bytes. That's a quarter of Bank 0. The minimal+UI shell budget assumes this, but if the budget is tight, font goes to a CommonBank instead. Default for v1: keep font in Bank 0 (simplest), accept the cost.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub fn build_text_section() -> Section;

pub fn font_bytes() -> &'static [u8];                            // 4096 bytes for 256 glyphs

pub struct TextLayout {
    pub bg_map_origin: u16,                                       // VRAM address
    pub columns: u8,                                              // 20 for DMG (160 / 8)
    pub rows: u8,                                                 // 18 for DMG (144 / 8)
}

/// Emit AsmIR: stage a glyph commit through video_commit::queue_glyph(x, y, glyph_id).
pub fn emit_text_print_glyph<B: Builder>(b: &mut B, x: u8, y: u8, glyph: u8);
```

Text never writes VRAM directly; it stages a `UiCommitOp::PutGlyph { ... }` into the commit queue. T-A5.6 owns the actual write at the next legal LCD-mode window.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-runtime
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- text::font_size                    # 256 glyphs * 16 bytes = 4096 bytes
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- text::layout_dmg                    # 20x18 DMG dimensions
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- text::print_glyph_stages            # Generates a UiCommitOp, never a direct VRAM write
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Text never writes VRAM directly — invariant enforced at the API boundary.
- §VI.1: Single text renderer.

title: T4.3b: Export frozen teacher as ReferenceModelBundle
description: After teacher weights are frozen (T4.3), export them as the default ReferenceModelBundle via ExportVisitor. Steps: (1) Run ExportVisitor on frozen teacher weights — this produces a ReferenceModelBundle with ReferenceProgram, ReferenceTensors, and ReferenceNumericProfile. (2) Validate the ReferenceProgram by evaluating it on named fixture prompts and comparing against the live frozen teacher's forward pass — outputs must be exact. (3) Store as sibling artifact alongside the eventual student ModelArtifact. This is the denotational truth baseline for conformance.json. Separating this from the teacher freeze (T4.3) allows distillation to begin immediately while the export machinery catches up.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- teacher::export passes; ReferenceModelBundle matches teacher forward on fixtures; Deterministic export

title: F-B3: GbInferIR (value/effect IR, Stage 3)
description: Hardware-aware value/effect IR. Storage-free but not effect-free: sequence-state mutation and RNG progression are explicit effect edges. InferOp covers Embedding/SequenceRead/SequenceWrite/RouteTop1/ExpertMatVec/CombineResidual/Norm/Classify/DecodeToken.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B3: GbInferIR (value/effect IR, Stage 3)

### What this is

The hardware-aware **value/effect IR** (`planv0.md` line 1181). Storage-free but **not effect-free**: sequence-state mutation and RNG progression are explicit `EffectId`s, not hidden in buffer aliasing. Typed by **value kind**, **quant format**, and **effect class**. Concrete address space, concrete buffers, tiling, accumulator scratch belong to later stages.

### Why this matters

This is the IR `ArtifactOracle` (Epic C) compares against op-for-op. Getting the value/effect split right is what lets schedule equivalence be reasoned about.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct GbNode {
    pub op: InferOp,
    pub inputs: Vec<ValueId>,
    pub effects_in: Vec<EffectId>,
    pub outputs: Vec<ValueId>,
    pub effects_out: Vec<EffectId>,
}

pub enum InferOp {
    Embedding { token_src: TokenSrc },
    SequenceRead { slot: StateSlotId },
    SequenceWrite { slot: StateSlotId },
    RouteTop1 { layer: LayerId },
    ExpertMatVec { layer: LayerId, expert: ExpertId },
    CombineResidual,
    Norm { plan: NormPlan },
    Classify,
    DecodeToken { plan: DecodeSpec },
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B1 QuantGraph (consumed)
- F-A3.6 SemanticCheckpointId (annotations)

### Confidence: HIGH

title: F-B11: GbSchedIR + ResourceStateValidation (Stages 10, 10.5)
description: How materializations are realized as loads/stores/tiles/reductions/resumable slices. SchedulePack keyed by RuntimeMode (multi-mode pending oracle question). ResourceStateValidation proves leases are balanced, no yield crosses non-resumable lease, no ISR-visible path depends on leased switchable state. Emits certs/resource_state.cert.json.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B11: GbSchedIR + ResourceStateValidation (Stages 10, 10.5)

### What this is

`GbInferIR` says **what**; `GbSchedIR` says **how** (planv0.md line 1361). Slices with `hard_cycles_to_safe_point`, `max_interrupt_latency`, `ResourceVector` (bank_switches/sram_page_switches/trace_bytes/persist_bytes/overlay_installs), `live_wram` / `live_sram`, `YieldKind`, `InterruptPolicy`, `required_leases`, `exit_kind`. A `CompiledBuild` may carry a `SchedulePack` keyed by `RuntimeMode` (multi-mode is the future-self switch question).

`ResourceStateValidation` (Stage 10.5) proves leases are balanced, no yield crosses a non-resumable lease, no ISR-visible path depends on leased switchable state, overlay/bank-shadow assumptions match declared residency.

### Why this matters

This is where coroutine ABI realism becomes fact. Slices have proven worst-case interrupt latency, proven liveness properties (`progress_epoch` advances at semantic-checkpoint granularity), proven lease lifecycle.

Emits `certs/resource_state.cert.json`.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct SchedSlice {
    pub id: SliceId,
    pub ops: Vec<SchedOp>,
    pub hard_cycles_to_safe_point: CycleBudget,
    pub max_interrupt_latency: CycleBudget,
    pub resources: ResourceVector,
    pub yield_kind: YieldKind,
    pub interrupt_policy: InterruptPolicy,
    pub required_leases: Vec<LeaseId>,
    pub exit_kind: ExitKind,
}

pub struct SchedulePack {
    pub modes: BTreeMap<RuntimeMode, GbSchedIR>,
    pub epochs: BTreeMap<RuntimeMode, Vec<ResidencyEpoch>>,
    pub checkpoint_schema_hash: Hash256,
    pub switch_policy: ModeSwitchPolicy,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B10 ArenaPlan
- F-A3.5 ResourceLease + InterruptPolicy
- F-B14 FeasibilityRefinementLoop (consumes proposals from here)

### Confidence: HIGH on shape, MEDIUM on multi-mode (oracle question pending)

title: F3: TernaryWeightPlan — Explicit Ternary Numeric Semantics
description: WeightEncoding::Ternary2 alone does not specify scale granularity, scale format, threshold schedule, or exported byte cost. Add TernaryWeightPlan/ScaleGranularity/ScaleFormat/ThresholdPlan to make the full ternary deployment contract explicit and computable.
acceptance_criteria: All T3.x tasks closed; cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- weight_plan passes; Reference packer agrees with production
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

**Artifact contract:** F3 makes ternary deployment metadata explicit instead of relying on `WeightEncoding::Ternary2` alone. `gbf-artifact` now owns `TernaryWeightPlan`, `ScaleGranularity`, `ScaleFormat`, `ThresholdPlan`, and `TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost`. `QuantSpec` records per-weight `WeightQuantEntry` metadata and `TernaryQuantEntry` details. Current deployable artifact export supports only Ternary2 weights with Q8_8 ternary scale tensors as first-class `CanonicalTensor` values with validated kind, element type, and shape. Threshold plans in the artifact are projection provenance/plan metadata, not runtime replay of concrete thresholds. Non-Ternary2 deployable encodings are rejected until `bd-nyen`; non-Q8_8 scale tensor encodings are rejected until `bd-38om`; learned per-group threshold state is rejected until `bd-2pg2`.

**differentiable Burn path:** No new differentiable Burn tensor path is introduced by the F3 feature closure. Burn-side train/export handoff for the supported Ternary2/Q8_8 ternary path is already covered by the F1 adapter gates. Burn-side evolution of the annealed threshold schedule remains with `bd-xpr7`; learned per-group threshold state remains with `bd-2pg2`; F3 only records and validates the current deployable plan metadata and byte math.

**Tests proving it:**

- `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- weight_plan`
- `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- quant`
- `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core`
- `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_ternary2_weight_encodings_until_tensor_encoding_exists`
- `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- artifact_core_rejects_learned_per_group_thresholds_until_state_is_exported`
- `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export`
- `cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget`
- `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test ternary_tests`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism`
- `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`

**Acceptance movement:** Yes. T3.1-T3.5 are closed. Runtime training schedule behavior for the annealed threshold plan remains with `bd-xpr7`. Learned per-group threshold state remains with `bd-2pg2`. Non-Ternary2 deployable weight encodings remain with `bd-nyen`. Non-Q8_8 scale tensor encodings remain with `bd-38om`. Real budget-source selection and full compiler Stage 2 reporting remain with `bd-t7x6` and `bd-2ps`. F3 closes only the explicit ternary plan contract, current Ternary2/Q8_8 artifact scale-tensor path, model-owned byte math, and independent verifier reference packer.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `TernaryWeightPlan` type contract and serde shape | supported | not applicable | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- weight_plan` |
| Ternary2 byte math with per-tensor/per-row/per-group scale overhead | supported | not applicable | supported | `ternary_weight_plan_computes_per_output_row_q8_8_cost`; `ternary_weight_plan_computes_scale_granularity_costs` |
| Default expert ternary plan in `QuantSpec` and model export | supported | moved | supported | `quant_spec_default_expert_plan_matches_training_contract`; `qat_ternary_default_plan_matches_artifact_expert_default`; `bd-xpr7` owns Burn schedule evolution |
| `ThresholdPlan::FixedQ8_8` / `ThresholdPlan::AnnealedGlobalThenPerOutputRow` projection provenance | supported | moved | supported | `qat_ternary_default_plan_matches_artifact_expert_default`; `bd-xpr7` |
| `ThresholdPlan::LearnedPerGroup` executable/exported threshold state | rejected | moved | rejected | `artifact_core_rejects_learned_per_group_thresholds_until_state_is_exported`; `bd-2pg2` |
| Q8_8 per-output-row scale tensors | supported | supported | supported | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism` |
| Q4_4/Pow2 scale-format byte-cost metadata | supported | moved | rejected | `ternary_weight_plan_computes_scale_granularity_costs`; `artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_q8_8_scale_formats_until_tensor_encoding_exists`; `bd-38om` |
| Non-Q8_8 deployable scale tensor encodings | rejected | moved | rejected | `artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_q8_8_scale_formats_until_tensor_encoding_exists`; `bd-38om` |
| `WeightEncoding::SparseTernaryBitplanes` / `WeightEncoding::Binary1` verifier oracle | not applicable | not applicable | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary` |
| `WeightEncoding::SparseTernaryBitplanes` / `WeightEncoding::Binary1` deployable artifact encoding | rejected | moved | rejected | `artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_ternary2_weight_encodings_until_tensor_encoding_exists`; `bd-nyen` |
| `StaticBudgetReport` single expert estimate from `TernaryWeightPlan` | supported | not applicable | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget`; `bd-t7x6`; `bd-2ps` |
| Independent verifier ternary pack/unpack oracle | not applicable | not applicable | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary` |
| Exported canonical ternary tensors are reference-packable | supported | not applicable | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test ternary_tests`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism` |
| Production/reference packed ternary byte agreement | not applicable | not applicable | moved | `bd-1pc6` |
| Exact compiler packed-layout budget and placement report | not applicable | not applicable | moved | `bd-2ps` |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| F3 implementation children T3.1-T3.5 are closed | `br show bd-1jl bd-qhu bd-209 bd-w80 bd-3ty` checked manually | none | All direct child tasks are closed |
| Ternary plan types and byte math are explicit and serde-compatible | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- weight_plan` | none | Covers byte cost, scale granularity, zero group rejection, monotonicity, and serde round-trip |
| QuantSpec references TernaryWeightPlan and validates summary/detail consistency | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- quant`; `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core` | none | Covers default plan, mixed precision, compatibility, missing metadata, and mismatch rejection |
| ArtifactCore rejects future deployable weight encodings and learned threshold state | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_ternary2_weight_encodings_until_tensor_encoding_exists artifact_core_rejects_learned_per_group_thresholds_until_state_is_exported` | none | Prevents public plan variants from becoming silently accepted artifact behavior |
| ExportVisitor emits Q8_8 scale tensors separately from ternary weights | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` | none | Checks deterministic names, tensor kind/type/shape, raw Q8_8 payload, and reconstruction from exported tensors |
| Expert byte estimates delegate to canonical TernaryWeightPlan math | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget` | none | Covers up/down projection delegation, structured report JSON, and invalid dimension rejection |
| Independent verifier pack/unpack supports current weight encodings and rejects unsupported scale formats | `cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary` | none | Uses a structurally separate verifier implementation with known vectors and randomized round trips |
| Exported canonical ternary tensors remain reference-packable and deterministic | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test ternary_tests`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test export_determinism` | none | Proves current canonical export can feed the independent reference packer and round-trip deterministically |
| QAT closure and architecture sensors remain clean | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Run after closure because the close reason is bead metadata |

## No-future

F3 accepts no future ternary scale, threshold, budget, or byte-packing behavior without an executable path and tests. Non-Ternary2 deployable weight encodings are rejected at the artifact boundary and remain with `bd-nyen`. Non-Q8_8 scale tensor encodings are rejected at the artifact boundary and remain with `bd-38om`. Learned per-group threshold state is rejected and remains with `bd-2pg2`. Burn-side annealed threshold schedule evolution remains with `bd-xpr7`. Runtime budget-source selection and full compiler Stage 2 reports remain with `bd-t7x6` and `bd-2ps`. Production/reference packed ternary byte agreement remains with `bd-1pc6`; current proof is canonical tensor determinism and reference-packability.

## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Public scale-format variants outpaced artifact tensor encodings | Plan metadata was broader than current tensor element support | Keep path-specific support rows and reject future artifact variants until encodings exist | `artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_q8_8_scale_formats_until_tensor_encoding_exists`; `ternary_tests_q8_8_scales_round_trip_and_future_scale_formats_are_rejected` | `bd-38om`; `qat-bead-closure` |

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F3: TernaryWeightPlan — Explicit Ternary Numeric Semantics

### Background

The plan defines WeightEncoding::Ternary2 as "2-bit packed ternary, four weights per byte." This is correct for storage but insufficient for specifying the deployed numeric behavior. Ternary quantization involves more than just {-1, 0, +1} packing:

1. **Scale granularity**: Are scales per-tensor, per-output-row, or per-group? This changes quality, byte cost, and accumulator behavior dramatically.
2. **Scale format**: Q8_8, Q4_4, or Pow2? This determines scale tensor size and accumulator precision requirements.
3. **Threshold schedule**: How are the ternary boundaries chosen? Fixed, globally annealed then per-row refined, or learned per group?

### The byte math

For a concrete example with the plan's default (d_model=128, d_ff=224, PerOutputRow scales, Q8_8):
- Expert up projection (128→224): ceil(128*224/4) = 7168 ternary bytes + 224*2 = 448 scale bytes = 7616 bytes
- Expert down projection (224→128): ceil(224*128/4) = 7168 ternary bytes + 128*2 = 256 scale bytes = 7424 bytes
- Total two-matrix expert: 7616 + 7424 = 15040 bytes + metadata
- This fits in a 16 KiB bank with ~1.3 KiB margin — but ONLY because we specified PerOutputRow Q8_8, not PerGroup(16) Q8_8 which would cost more

If you used per-tensor scales instead: 7168 + 2 = 7170 per projection, total ~14342 bytes — more margin but worse quality.
If you used per-group(16) Q8_8 scales: additional (128*224/16)*2 = 3584 scale bytes per projection — won't fit in one bank.

The TernaryWeightPlan makes these choices explicit so the byte formula is computable and the compiler can verify fit without guessing.

### Why this must be in the artifact, not just the compiler

The scale tensors and threshold parameters are part of the deployed model's identity. Two models with identical ternary weight bits but different scale granularity/format produce different inference results. Therefore TernaryWeightPlan must be referenced by QuantSpec inside ArtifactCore.

### Confidence: VERY HIGH

This is a mathematical necessity, not a design preference. Without it, the byte formulas are uncomputable and the export/compile agreement is unverifiable.

cargo test -p gbf-verify -- ternary                # Reference packer agrees with production
cargo test -p gbf-test -- ternary_tests            # All round-trip and hash stability tests pass

### Key Byte Math (from epic comment)

- PerOutputRow Q8_8 scale: `rows * 2` bytes

title: F-G3: Decode — DecodeMode/DecodePolicy/DecodeCapabilitySet/SessionProfile
description: Decode policy is session-level (SessionProfile). ArtifactCore declares DecodeCapabilitySet only. Oracles, compiler, runtime, harness all share decode + RNG semantics through SessionProfile.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-G3: Decode

### What this is

`DecodeMode`, `DecodePolicy`, `DecodeCapabilitySet`, `DecodeTransformSet`, `SessionProfile`. Decode policy is **session-level** declared in `SessionProfile`; `ArtifactCore` only declares supported modes via `DecodeCapabilitySet`.

### Why this matters

Per planv0.md line 703: oracles, compiler, runtime, and harness all need the same decode + RNG semantics at session/run level. `DecodeCapabilitySet` keeps `ArtifactCore` mode-agnostic.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub enum DecodeMode {
    Greedy,
    TopK { k: u16 },
    TopP { p: f32 },
    Temperature { t: f32 },
}

pub struct DecodePolicy { ... }
pub struct DecodeCapabilitySet { pub modes: BTreeSet<DecodeMode> }
pub struct SessionProfile {
    pub decode: DecodePolicy,
    pub rng_seed: u64,
    pub max_tokens: Option<u32>,
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- gbf-artifact (DecodeCapabilitySet in core, SessionProfile in aux/session)
- F-C1, F-C2 oracles (need shared decode semantics)
- F-D2 harness (SetSession sets SessionProfile)

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T6.1: Implement MoE-on-FFN-path-only with shared sequence-state
description: In gbf-model, the model topology struct should have a per-block config: MoeBlockConfig { has_moe_ffn: bool, shared_sequence_block: SharedSequenceConfig }. When has_moe_ffn is false, the block uses a shared dense FFN (non-expert). The sequence-state update path (attention/recurrent/linear) is always shared, never routed. This keeps routing complexity to one router call per MoE block and avoids doubling bank-switch cost.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- block passes; MoE only on FFN path; Sequence-state always shared
close_reason: Implemented topology/config contract with review fixes; see closure comment.

comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`

- `gbf-model/src/config.rs` (MoeBlockConfig)

### Implementation Notes
- Define per-block configuration:
```rust
pub struct MoeBlockConfig {
    pub has_moe_ffn: bool,
    pub shared_sequence_block: SequenceBlockConfig,
}
```
- Key architectural rule: **Sequence-state update path is ALWAYS shared, NEVER routed.**
  - The attention/state-mixing block is shared across all experts
  - Only the FFN path goes through MoE routing
- Block structure: `input -> shared_sequence_block -> [MoE FFN | dense FFN] -> output`
- Blocks without MoE (`has_moe_ffn: false`) use a standard dense FFN.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- block                  # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: MoE only applies to FFN path
# Verify: sequence-state update path is always shared regardless of MoE config
# Verify: blocks with has_moe_ffn=false use dense FFN
```

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
Implemented bd-a8k as a model-topology/configuration bead.

Scope owned by this bead:
- Backend-independent topology structs for per-block shared sequence state plus FFN-only routing.
- Dense-vs-MoE FFN selection through an enum-backed config, with `has_moe_ffn()` as a derived query.
- A topology oracle that emits `SharedSequence -> DenseFfn` for dense blocks and `SharedSequence -> Router -> MoeFfn` for MoE blocks.
- Constructor validation for nonzero dimensions, nonempty model block lists, matching sequence/FFN `d_model`, and consistent model `d_model` across blocks.

Scope explicitly not claimed:
- No tensor forward implementation or Burn module path.
- No actual LinearState or BoundedKv sequence-state math; `SharedSequenceKind` is a topology marker only.
- No block-selection policy for alternating/middle MoE blocks; that remains with bd-31r.
- No default expert MLP implementation; that remains with bd-x75.
- No optional shared dense branch inside MoE expert blocks; that remains separate from the dense fallback path here.
- No artifact/export serialization contract.

Subagent review disposition:
- Mode 1 intent review identified the bead as too easy to overread as runtime execution. Agreed; closure and `AGENTS.md` now state topology/config-only boundaries for topology beads.
- Mode 2 design review recommended keeping router nested under the FFN path and avoiding `Deserialize` on constructor-validated config. Agreed; `BlockTopologyPlan` now has `shared_sequence` plus `FfnExecutionPath::{Dense, Moe { router, ffn }}`, and serde derives were not added.
- Mode 3 semantic review flagged constructor bypass risk and mismatch with a raw bool model. Agreed; construction uses validated constructors and `FfnPathConfig` makes dense-with-router invalid through the public API.
- Mode 4 evidence review asked for dense mismatch and empty topology tests. Agreed; both are covered.

Feedback harness reflection:

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Topology bead could be read as owning runtime forward behavior | Closure did not separate topology from execution strongly enough | Added `AGENTS.md` guidance requiring topology/config beads to state whether they own only shape/topology or executable behavior | Closure scope section plus topology-stage tests | `AGENTS.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |
| Correctness oracle was implicit | Acceptance said “MoE only applies to FFN path” without a concrete observable | Use a topology oracle for config-only beads | `block_topology_routes_only_ffn_path_for_moe_blocks` and `block_topology_uses_dense_ffn_when_moe_is_disabled` assert exact stage order and router count | `gbf-model/src/block.rs`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |
| Raw bool could permit invalid dense-with-router combinations | The bead text named `has_moe_ffn: bool`, but the invariant needs a sum type | Added `AGENTS.md` guidance to prefer enum-backed topology choices over bool flags when invalid combinations must be unrepresentable | `FfnPathConfig::{Dense, Moe}` and tests for both public paths | `gbf-model/src/config.rs`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |
| Serde could bypass constructor invariants | Deriving `Deserialize` on private fields can materialize impossible configs | Added `AGENTS.md` guidance not to derive `Deserialize` for constructor-validated model config unless it validates through the same boundary | Compile-time absence of serde derives on these config types; constructor negative tests cover invalid states through the public API | `AGENTS.md`; `gbf-model/src/config.rs`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |
| Dense mismatch and empty model errors lacked direct proof | Initial tests covered MoE mismatch and zero values but missed adjacent branches | Test both dense and routed paths directly | Added `block_config_rejects_dense_sequence_and_ffn_model_dim_mismatch` and `block_model_topology_rejects_empty_block_sets` | `gbf-model/src/block.rs`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |

Claim-to-gate matrix:

| Claim | Gate |
| --- | --- |
| `gbf-model` compiles with the new topology/config modules | `cargo check -p gbf-model` |
| MoE routing is represented only on the FFN path | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |
| Dense blocks use the dense FFN path and have zero router invocations | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |
| The shared sequence-state path is always outside FFN routing in the topology oracle | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |
| Constructor validation rejects zero dimensions, empty models, and `d_model` mismatches | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |
| The model crate remains warning-free for this change | `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings` |
| Existing architecture boundaries still hold | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` |
| Patch has no whitespace errors | `git diff --check` |

Commands run:

- `cargo check -p gbf-model`
- `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings`

title: T-A1.6: Layout + branch relaxation
description: Section placement into banks under PlacementProfile (StrictOnePerBank | Budgeted | PackedExperts). Iterative-monotone branch relaxation: out-of-range JR -> JP, cross-bank -> far-call thunk. Convergence to fixed point asserted by test.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A1.6: Layout + branch relaxation

**Target crate**: `gbf-asm`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-asm/src/layout.rs`, `gbf-asm/src/relax.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` §"PlacedRom" (line 1536), §"Assembly eDSL".

### Background

Layout assigns concrete addresses to sections within ROM banks; relaxation rewrites branches when the chosen layout pushes targets out of range. On LR35902:
- `JR` is a signed 8-bit relative jump (`±127` bytes).
- `JP` is absolute 16-bit.
- Cross-bank calls require a far-call thunk because `CALL` is intra-address-space.

This task implements that legalization. The full PlacedRom pass (with placement profiles, bank packing, etc.) is Epic B; this is the gbf-asm-level mechanism.

### Implementation Notes

`layout.rs`:

```rust
pub struct LayoutPlan {
    pub sections: Vec<PlacedSection>,
}

pub struct PlacedSection {
    pub id: SectionId,
    pub bank: u16,                           // 0 for fixed, 1..=N for switchable
    pub start: u16,                          // address within bank
    pub size: u16,
}

pub fn layout_into_banks(
    sections: Vec<Section>,
    profile: PlacementProfile,
) -> Result<LayoutPlan, LayoutError>;
```

`PlacementProfile` is `StrictOnePerBank | Budgeted | PackedExperts` (`planv0.md` line 1540). The Bringup default is `StrictOnePerBank`.

`relax.rs`:

```rust
pub enum RelaxAction {
    KeepRelative,
    UpgradeToAbsolute,                       // JR -> JP
    InsertFarCallThunk { thunk_id: SymbolId, lease_chain: SmallVec<[LeaseId; 2]> },
}

pub fn relax_branches(plan: &LayoutPlan, sections: &mut [Section]) -> Vec<RelaxAction>;
```

The relaxation is iterative-monotone: each pass can only grow the encoded size. Iterate until fixed point or a configurable max iteration count.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-asm
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- layout::no_section_crosses_bank      # Hard invariant
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- layout::strict_one_per_bank           # Profile semantics
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- relax::out_of_range_jr_becomes_jp     # JR upgrade
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- relax::cross_bank_becomes_far_call    # Far-call thunk insertion
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- relax::reaches_fixed_point             # Iteration converges
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `LayoutError` enumerates exact failure modes (section too big, no fitting bank, etc.).
- §IV.3: Layout is deterministic for the same inputs.

title: F-C2: ArtifactOracle
description: Runs the frozen artifact in canonical logical form: same quantization, logical LUT semantics, decode semantics, sequence-state semantics. NO tiling, bank, or layout assumptions. Compares op-for-op against GbInferIR.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-C2: ArtifactOracle

### What this is

Runs the frozen artifact in canonical logical form (`planv0.md` line 295). Same quantization, same logical LUT semantics, same decode semantics, same `SequenceSemanticsSpec`. **No tiling, bank, or layout assumptions.** Compares op-for-op against `GbInferIR` (which is also storage-free).

### Why this matters

Proves "the deployed quantized model is what we exported." If `ArtifactOracle` and the compiler's `GbInferIR` disagree, export went wrong. If they agree but `ScheduleOracle` disagrees, scheduling went wrong.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct ArtifactOracle {
    core: ArtifactCore,
    decode: DecodeSpec,
}

impl ArtifactOracle {
    pub fn evaluate(&self, workload: &Workload, observation_policy: &ObservationPolicy)
        -> ArtifactObservations;
    pub fn compare_against(&self, other: &ArtifactObservations) -> ConformanceComparison;
}
```

### Cross-feature deps

- gbf-artifact ArtifactCore
- F-B1 QuantGraph (op-for-op comparison)
- F-A3.6 SemanticCheckpointId

### Confidence: HIGH on structure

title: T4.2: Implement phase scheduler in training loop
description: The training loop checks the current step against the phase schedule and adjusts QAT hardness accordingly. At phase transitions: log the transition, optionally save a checkpoint, and update the model's QAT modules via a set_hardness(expert_qat, activation_qat, norm_qat, router_mode) method on the model. Phase transitions should be logged to training metrics and visible in TensorBoard/equivalent.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler passes; Phase transitions at exact step boundaries; Idempotent; Structured log events emitted
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

**Artifact contract:** This bead implements a pre-export scheduler/control contract, not artifact agreement. `TrainingPhaseScheduler` performs pure step -> `TrainPhaseSpec` lookup, constructs `PhaseControls` with `expert_qat`, `activation_qat`, `norm_qat`, `router_mode`, and linear `soft_progress`, and applies those controls through a model boundary. Artifact export/runtime agreement remains outside this bead and is not claimed.

**differentiable Burn path:** No Burn math was introduced here. The scheduler is backend-neutral and supplies per-step `soft_progress` for `QuantHardness::Soft`; concrete Burn consumption of annealed threshold/blend schedules remains with Burn training owners such as bd-xpr7 and future training-loop coverage bd-14k/bd-1wd.

**Tests proving it:** Scheduler tests prove half-open boundaries, per-step Soft progress application, idempotent repeated calls, exact transition logging, `SoftTop1 -> HardTop1` selection at step 30, restart reconstruction from a bare step, out-of-range rejection, backward-step rejection, and missed/skipped live-boundary rejection. Logging tests prove the transition schema now carries norm hardness through both test collector and subscriber-captured tracing events.

**Acceptance movement:** Literal adoption by a production training loop is not claimed because `gbf-train` has no production loop entrypoint yet. This bead closes the implementable scheduler/control boundary named in the bead target (`gbf-train/src/scheduler.rs`). Full training-loop producer adoption remains with bd-1wd, phase-transition integration proof remains with bd-14k, and canonical structured logging adoption by future producers remains with bd-2sd7.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Canonical five-phase step lookup with half-open boundaries | supported | supported | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_current_phase_uses_half_open_boundaries` |
| Live phase control boundary carries expert/activation/norm QAT, router mode, and Soft progress | supported | moved | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_applies_soft_progress_on_in_phase_steps`; Burn consumption moved to bd-xpr7/bd-14k/bd-1wd |
| Phase transition side effects are idempotent for repeated steps | supported | supported | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_apply_step_is_idempotent_with_repeated_step` |
| Router dispatch mode remains separate from numeric QAT hardness | supported | moved | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_transition_applies_hard_router_at_exact_boundary`; full integration moved to bd-14k |
| Crash/restart can reconstruct current controls from schedule + step | supported | supported | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_can_reconstruct_current_controls_after_restart` |
| Missed, skipped, backward, and out-of-range live application states are rejected without effects | supported | supported | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_rejects_*` |
| Phase transition structured event includes before/after ternary, activation, norm, router, expert hardness and checkpoint flag | supported | moved | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-train --test structured_logging`; producer adoption moved to bd-2sd7 |
| Checkpoint persistence at transition | moved | moved | moved | Scheduler invokes a transition callback and logs the returned flag; dense teacher/checkpoint ownership remains bd-2hp/bd-7lu |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `gbf-train` exports a phase scheduler module | `cargo check -p gbf-train` | none | Verifies the public module compiles |
| Step lookup follows exact half-open phase boundaries and rejects the final end step | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_current_phase_uses_half_open_boundaries` | none | Covers 0, 9, 10, 49, and 50 for the default 5x10 schedule |
| Soft progress is applied on new in-phase steps and repeated same-step calls are idempotent | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_applies_soft_progress_on_in_phase_steps`; `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_apply_step_is_idempotent_with_repeated_step` | none | Proves scheduler-level Soft progress boundary without claiming concrete Burn temperature math |
| Transition logging and checkpoint callback happen only after a transition is planned | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_transition_applies_model_logs_event_and_uses_checkpoint_callback` | none | Avoids caller guessing whether a transition occurred |
| Router mode switches to `HardTop1` at the Phase C -> D boundary | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_transition_applies_hard_router_at_exact_boundary` | none | Guards the moved bd-2uw router owner note |
| Live scheduler state rejects backward, missed-boundary, skipped-phase, and out-of-range calls without model/log effects | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_rejects_backward_steps_without_side_effects`; `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_rejects_missed_or_skipped_live_phase_boundaries`; `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler_rejects_out_of_schedule_step_without_side_effects` | none | Keeps invalid live states explicit while restart reconstruction remains supported |
| Phase transition event schema carries norm hardness | `cargo test -p gbf-train`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --test structured_logging` | none | Unit collector and subscriber capture both cover the updated schema |
| Target acceptance commands pass | `cargo check -p gbf-train`; `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler`; `cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings` | none | All passed before close |
| QAT closure harness and architecture sensors still pass | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Both passed before close |

## No-future

No-future unsupported phase variants are accepted silently. The scheduler consumes the canonical five-phase `TrainingPhaseSchedule` already validated by bd-ptk and does not introduce alternate phase kinds, annealing curves, artifact encodings, or router hardness states. `QuantHardness::Soft` is represented as a concrete per-step linear progress value for the current phase; future non-linear schedules or Burn-specific threshold evolution remain moved to their owner beads rather than being claimed here.

## Review Feedback Addressed

| Feedback item | Resolution | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Soft progress was exposed but not applied | `PhaseControlledModel::apply_phase_controls` now receives `PhaseControls` on new in-phase steps, including `soft_progress` | `scheduler_applies_soft_progress_on_in_phase_steps` |
| `checkpoint_saved` made the caller guess transition state | Replaced with `apply_step_with_checkpoint` transition callback invoked only for planned transitions | `scheduler_transition_applies_model_logs_event_and_uses_checkpoint_callback` |
| Norm QAT was absent from transition logs | Added `norm_hardness`, `before_norm_hardness`, and `after_norm_hardness` fields | `cargo test -p gbf-train --test structured_logging` |
| `SoftTop1 -> HardTop1` boundary was untested | Added an exact step-30 transition assertion | `scheduler_transition_applies_hard_router_at_exact_boundary` |
| Backward or skipped live calls could smear transition effects | Added `NonMonotonicStep`, `MissedPhaseBoundary`, and `SkippedPhaseTransition` rejection paths | `scheduler_rejects_backward_steps_without_side_effects`; `scheduler_rejects_missed_or_skipped_live_phase_boundaries` |

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/scheduler.rs` or `gbf-train/src/loop.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "phase scheduler", "training loop", "phase transitions".

### Implementation Notes
- Training loop checks current step against phase schedule at each step.
- On phase transition:
  1. Log the transition as a structured event (tracing span)
  2. Call `model.set_hardness(phase.expert_qat, phase.activation_qat, phase.norm_qat)`
  3. Update router mode
  4. If transitioning to Phase B: activate router, begin z-loss/balance-loss
  5. If transitioning to Phase E: begin shadow compile pipeline
- Phase scheduler is a simple lookup: `fn current_phase(&self, step: u64) -> &TrainPhaseSpec`
- Must be idempotent — calling `current_phase(step)` multiple times returns the same result.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler              # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: phase transitions happen at exact step boundaries
# Verify: repeated calls at same step are idempotent
# Verify: structured log events emitted at transitions
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Phase scheduler is pure (step -> phase); side effects (logging, model updates) at the boundary
- §V.1: Phase transition logs are structured (tracing) with phase_from, phase_to, step fields
- §V.2: Crash-only — can restart training at any step and get correct phase

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Soft progress was exposed but not applied to the model/control boundary | The first implementation treated a pure progress query as enough evidence for scheduler-owned Soft annealing | Updated `.agents/skills/qat-bead-closure/SKILL.md`: F4 scheduler beads claiming `QuantHardness::Soft` progress must prove a new same-phase step applies a control plan | `scheduler_applies_soft_progress_on_in_phase_steps` | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler`; QAT skill |
| Transition logs dropped `norm_qat` | Logging schema had fewer fields than `TrainPhaseSpec`, and tests only asserted old fields | Updated QAT skill: F4 scheduler transition logs must cover `expert_qat`, `activation_qat`, `norm_qat`, and `router_mode` or move omissions explicitly | `phase_transition_records_before_after_hardness_and_checkpoint`; `canonical_event_helpers_are_captured_by_tracing_subscriber` | `cargo test -p gbf-train`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --test structured_logging` |
| `checkpoint_saved` let the caller guess transition state before the scheduler planned one | Side-effect API mixed transition detection with a caller-supplied postcondition | Updated QAT skill: checkpoint status should be produced from a planned transition boundary | `scheduler_transition_applies_model_logs_event_and_uses_checkpoint_callback` | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler` |
| Helper-only scheduler API risked claiming production training-loop adoption | Bead text was broader than the current crate surface; no production loop entrypoint exists | Updated QAT skill: if no production loop exists, close only the scheduler/control boundary and cite adoption owner beads | Closure support matrix names bd-1wd, bd-14k, and bd-2sd7 for future producer/integration adoption | QAT closure harness; bd-1wd/bd-14k/bd-2sd7 |
| `SoftTop1 -> HardTop1` exact boundary was untested | The first transition test stopped at Phase C where router mode remains soft | Guide covered by full phase-control field rule; exact router boundary now has a focused test | `scheduler_transition_applies_hard_router_at_exact_boundary` | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler` |
| Backward, missed-boundary, or skipped live calls could smear side effects | Stateful scheduler accepted non-monotonic or missed-boundary calls without making the invalid state explicit | No new guide beyond existing invalid-state closure discipline; this is now an executable scheduler invariant | `scheduler_rejects_backward_steps_without_side_effects`; `scheduler_rejects_missed_or_skipped_live_phase_boundaries` | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- scheduler` |

title: T12.1: Define SequenceSemanticsSpec enum and dispatch trait in gbf-model
description: Define SequenceSemanticsSpec { LinearState { state_bytes_per_layer }, BoundedKv { max_context, kv_bytes_per_token } } and a SequenceBlock trait that both implementations satisfy (forward, state_init, state_size, ExportFacts hooks). Wire SequenceSemanticsSpec into ArtifactCore via gbf-artifact (planv0.md ArtifactCore section). Schema + trait only.
close_reason:
Implemented bd-ctl as the sequence-state schema and dispatch contract bead.

What landed:
- `gbf-artifact::sequence` now defines the durable `SequenceSemanticsSpec`, `SequenceStateSize`, and validated `SequenceExportFacts`.
- `ArtifactCore` now requires sequence semantics, exposes `sequence_semantics()`, and includes the sequence contract in the domain-separated semantic hash.
- `gbf-model::sequence` re-exports the durable spec and adds backend-independent `SequenceActivation`, `SequenceState`, and `SequenceBlock`.
- `ModelTopologyConfig` now derives one authoritative sequence contract from its shared sequence blocks and rejects mixed sequence semantics across blocks.
- `ExportVisitor` now consumes `SequenceExportFacts` and writes the same sequence contract into both `ArtifactCore` and `ExportFacts`.
- `gbf-policy::compile::SequenceSemanticsRef` landed only as the lightweight profile selector; bd-ymo still owns `CompileProfile` and BringUp registry wiring.

Acceptance movement and scope:
- The bead text said the canonical spec lived in `gbf-model` and was referenced by `gbf-artifact`; the implemented direction is intentionally inverted to preserve the current crate graph. `gbf-artifact` owns the durable artifact schema, and `gbf-model` re-exports it for model-facing use.
- Executable `LinearStateBlock` behavior remains with bd-tnb.
- Executable `BoundedKvBlock` behavior remains with bd-1jk.
- Training scaffold dual-path A/B and shadow-compile reporting remain with bd-3jv.
- Full `CompileProfile` registry and BringUp default consumption of `SequenceSemanticsRef` remain with bd-ymo.
- No Burn dependency or differentiable sequence implementation is claimed here.

Four-mode review:
- Mode 1 flagged the ownership inversion and over-broad trait/profile scope. Fixed by documenting artifact-schema ownership, deriving sequence facts from model topology, and leaving executable/profile work with owner beads.
- Mode 2 flagged unconstrained `SequenceBlock` associated types and free-stamped export semantics. Fixed by adding project-native activation/state types and making `ExportVisitor` consume `SequenceExportFacts`.
- Mode 3 flagged false artifact semantics possible through `ExportVisitor::new(SequenceSemanticsSpec)`. Fixed by routing through validated sequence facts and topology-derived fixture export.
- Mode 4 flagged missing `SequenceExportFacts` wiring and weak serde evidence. Fixed by carrying sequence facts into `ExportFacts`, adding export visitor coverage, and adding stable JSON-shape coverage.

Feedback harness reflection:
| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Durable schema ownership contradicted the bead text. | Crate dependency direction prevents `gbf-artifact` from referencing `gbf-model`. | Added AGENTS sequence-state guidance to state ownership movement explicitly. | Closure note and `gbf-model::sequence` re-export. | bd-ctl |
| Export could stamp arbitrary sequence semantics. | Visitor accepted a raw enum instead of model/config facts. | AGENTS now requires export paths to consume `SequenceExportFacts`. | `qat_export_visitor_records_sequence_export_facts`; fixture export uses `config.topology().sequence_export_facts()`. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| `SequenceBlock` accepted arbitrary associated input/output/state types. | Trait shape was copied from the bead without a model-native tensor/state contract. | AGENTS now requires project-native activation/state types with validation. | `sequence_activation_validates_shape_and_finiteness`; `sequence_block_trait_can_be_implemented_without_burn_dependency`. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::spec` |
| Model topology could mix sequence variants while ArtifactCore has one sequence contract. | Existing `SharedSequenceConfig` and artifact spec were separate vocabularies. | One source of truth from topology to sequence export facts. | `block_model_topology_requires_consistent_sequence_semantics`. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |
| Policy selector ordering implied semantics that do not exist. | Derived `Ord` before any profile ordering policy. | AGENTS warns not to derive ordering for selector enums without executable meaning. | Removed `PartialOrd`/`Ord`. | `cargo clippy -p gbf-policy -- -D warnings` |

Claim-to-gate:
| Claim | Gate |
| --- | --- |
| Sequence semantics reject zero-sized contracts, round-trip through serde, have stable JSON shape, and validate export facts | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- sequence` |
| ArtifactCore carries sequence semantics and hashes LinearState/BoundedKv differently | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core::sequence_semantics`; `cargo test -p gbf-artifact` |
| Model sequence trait is backend-independent and uses validated activation/state types | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::spec` |
| Model topology has one authoritative sequence contract | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |
| ExportVisitor carries SequenceExportFacts into ArtifactCore and ExportFacts | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| Tiny fixtures derive artifact sequence facts from model topology | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures` |
| Burn remains out of gbf-model/artifact sequence work | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` |
| Touched crates compile and lint cleanly | `cargo check -p gbf-model -p gbf-artifact -p gbf-policy`; `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings`; `cargo fmt --check --all`; `git diff --check` |

Checks run:
- `cargo check -p gbf-model -p gbf-artifact -p gbf-policy`
- `cargo check -p gbf-model -p gbf-artifact`
- `cargo test -p gbf-artifact`
- `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- sequence`
- `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- core::sequence_semantics`
- `cargo test -p gbf-model`
- `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::spec`
- `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block`
- `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export`
- `cargo test -p gbf-policy -- compile`

- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`

- `cargo clippy -p gbf-artifact -p gbf-model -p gbf-policy -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --check --all`
- `git diff --check`
