title: T-A5.3: Joypad reader
description: Once-per-frame JOYP read with active-low decode. Caches ButtonState in WRAM; emit_joypad_read helper. Joypad ISR (0x0060) for STOP-mode wake.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A5.3: Joypad reader

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-runtime/src/joypad.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 1631, T-A2.6.

### Background

Joypad reads happen during the UI portion of each frame (or on Joypad interrupt). The reader does the standard "select directions, read, select buttons, read, OR them, invert active-low" dance and updates a `ButtonState` in WRAM.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub fn build_joypad_section() -> Section;

/// Helper that emits AsmIR for: read JOYP, debounce, update HRAM cached state.
pub fn emit_joypad_read<B: Builder>(b: &mut B);
```

The cached `ButtonState` (from T-A2.6) is stored in WRAM (not HRAM — 4 bytes is more than the HRAM hot-flag budget). Joypad ISR (vector `$0060`) is the path used for "wake from STOP"; the polling read happens once per frame from the scheduler.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-runtime
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- joypad::read_emits_expected_sequence
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- joypad::just_pressed_via_emu              # Run on emu with simulated input
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §VI.1: Single joypad reader; UI/keyboard layers consume the cached state.

title: T1.6: Implement ExportVisitor
description: Walks a trained gbf-model, extracts all canonical tensors (ternary weights, scales, embeddings, router weights, classifier, norm params, LUT contents), assembles them into ArtifactCore + TernaryWeightPlan + QuantSpec. Must produce byte-identical results for the same model weights regardless of training backend. This is the single most critical path for artifact/training agreement.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export passes; Deterministic export (same weights = same ArtifactCore hash); Scale tensors exported as first-class CanonicalTensors
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

**Artifact contract:** Implemented `ArtifactCore`, `CanonicalTensor`, `QuantSpec`, and `ExportFacts.activation_ranges` in `gbf-artifact`, plus `ExportVisitor` in `gbf-model`. The visitor emits deterministic `CanonicalTensor` entries for ternary weights, first-class ternary scale tensors, bias tensors, router weights/biases, dense/shared branch weights/biases, embeddings, classifier tensors, and norm LUT contents. `QuantSpec` references the ternary weight plans and tensor ids. `RangeDigest` records visited `ActFakeQuant` ranges in deterministic order.

**differentiable Burn path:** Not applicable to this bead. This is the backend-independent artifact/export boundary and has no Burn dependency. It consumes already-extracted model/QAT scalar state. Router/expert Burn adapters remain outside this export bead and are tracked separately by bd-1ptv.

**Tests proving it:** See the claim-to-gate matrix below.

**Acceptance movement:** None. This bead now owns and implements the artifact-side export contract it names. It does not claim compiler/runtime lowering agreement or ArtifactOracle agreement; those remain downstream validation work.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `ArtifactCore` assembly from QAT modules | supported | not applicable | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| `CanonicalTensor` schema and deterministic content hashes | supported | not applicable | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact` |
| Ternary projection export as separate weight and scale tensors | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` |
| Router weight/bias export | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_extracts_router_shared_dense_and_norm_lut_tensors` |
| Expert block traversal, including optional shared dense branch | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_records_all_qat_module_kinds_and_activation_ranges` |
| Activation range facts through `RangeDigest` / `ExportFacts.activation_ranges` | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_records_all_qat_module_kinds_and_activation_ranges` |
| Norm LUT contents | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_extracts_router_shared_dense_and_norm_lut_tensors` |
| Embedding and classifier raw tensor export helpers | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_builds_deterministic_artifact_for_same_modules` |
| Exhaustive QAT visitor enum dispatch through `QatModuleRef` | supported | not applicable | supported | `qat_export_visitor_builds_deterministic_artifact_for_same_modules` |
| Direct Burn module walking | not applicable | moved | not applicable | bd-1ptv |
| Target-specific packed tensor/lut lowering | not applicable | not applicable | not applicable | No `PackedTensor` or target lowering is emitted by this API |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `gbf-artifact` owns `ArtifactCore`, `CanonicalTensor`, `QuantSpec`, and `ExportFacts.activation_ranges` | `cargo test -p gbf-artifact` | none | Covers schema construction, tensor validation, duplicate ids, and deterministic core hashing |
| `ExportVisitor` compiles without Burn or backend dependencies | `cargo check -p gbf-model` | none | `gbf-model` depends only on artifact/foundation/hw plus serde |
| Export is deterministic for same weights independent of visit order | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_builds_deterministic_artifact_for_same_modules` | none | Compares `ArtifactCore` hash and serialized export bytes after different visitation order |
| All current QAT module types are visited | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_records_all_qat_module_kinds_and_activation_ranges` | none | Covers ternary, activation, norm, router, expert, expert block, and shared branch traversal |
| Scale tensors are first-class `CanonicalTensor` records, not embedded in packed repr | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_emits_scale_tensors_separately_from_ternary_weights` | none | Asserts separate `.weight`, `.scale`, and `.bias` tensor ids and `QuantSpec` references |
| Router/shared dense/norm LUT tensors are exported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_extracts_router_shared_dense_and_norm_lut_tensors` | none | Covers router matrices, shared dense alpha, and norm LUT payloads |
| Non-finite float export payloads are rejected | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_rejects_non_finite_raw_float_tensors` | none | Prevents NaN/Inf tensors from entering artifact state |
| Duplicate export paths are rejected | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export::tests::qat_export_visitor_rejects_duplicate_paths` | none | Prevents ambiguous tensor/fact identity |
| No direct Burn dependency leaks into model/artifact export | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Architecture test checks Burn import confinement and artifact/model boundary |
| QAT closure/sensor invariants still pass | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` | none | Harness passed after the implementation |
| Clippy is clean for the target crate | `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings` | none | Also checks the `gbf-artifact` dependency |

## No-future Acceptance

No future export encodings are accepted. `CanonicalTensorPayload` supports only implemented `F32`, ternary `I8` values constrained to -1/0/1, and `Q8_8` `U16` scales. The visitor emits target-independent canonical tensors only; it does not accept or pretend to lower packed target layouts, compiler schedules, or runtime-specific tensor encodings. `QatModuleRef` is exhaustive for the QAT module types present today and has no dynamic downcast fallback.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/qat/export.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "ExportVisitor", "ArtifactCore", "CanonicalTensor", "export visitation", "TernaryWeightPlan", "QuantSpec".

### Implementation Notes
- `ExportVisitor` walks a trained gbf-model and extracts ALL canonical tensors for artifact generation.
- This is the **single most critical path** for training/artifact agreement.
- Output: `ArtifactCore` containing:
  - One ternary weight `CanonicalTensor` + one scale `CanonicalTensor` per projection layer
  - Router weight tensors (at higher precision)
  - Embedding tensors
  - Norm parameters
  - `TernaryWeightPlan` per weight group
  - `QuantSpec` referencing the plans
- **MUST produce byte-identical results** for the same model weights regardless of training backend.
- Visitor pattern: walks each module type, calls `export_canonical()` on each QAT module.
- No Burn dependency in the export path — works on extracted tensors.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                          # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export             # Unit tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings           # No warnings
# Verify: determinism — same weights produce identical ArtifactCore hash
# Verify: all QAT module types visited (ternary, activation, norm, router, expert)
# Verify: scale tensors are first-class CanonicalTensors, not embedded in packed repr
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: Visitor must exhaustively match all module types — use enum dispatch, not dynamic downcasts
- §IV.3: Reproducible builds — byte-identical export is a hard requirement
- §III.4: Golden snapshot tests for export output

`bd-g90` explicitly owns the artifact side of the `bd-pk3` activation range contract: define/populate `RangeDigest` and `ExportFacts.activation_ranges` from visited `ActFakeQuant` modules, including deterministic ordering and a test under `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export`. `bd-pk3` only guarantees the pre-export module contract (`export_range` / `forward_spec`) and does not claim ArtifactCore or ExportFacts materialization.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Corrective scope note from bd-28f6

Follow-up review found that the bd-g90 closure overstated two pieces of scope:

- Cross-backend byte agreement is not proven by bd-g90 alone. bd-g90 provides the deterministic scalar/artifact export machinery. The end-to-end cross-backend agreement proof remains with bd-22o.
- Direct walking of trained Burn router/expert modules is not implemented by bd-g90. The Burn adapter/export handoff for Top1RouterQat and ExpertBlockQat remains with bd-1ptv.

bd-28f6 tightens the implemented artifact boundary: activation range and eval passthrough semantics are part of QuantSpec and ArtifactCore identity; ArtifactCore validates QuantSpec tensor references, tensor kinds, element types, and shapes; semantic_hash uses a domain-separated canonical byte encoder over tensor content hashes and quant metadata rather than serde JSON.

title: T11.4: Stub OverlayPlan IR placeholder in gbf-codegen
description: Add OverlayPlan placeholder pass output in gbf-codegen between RomWindowPlan and ArenaPlan (per planv0.md compiler-pipeline section 8.5). Types only for now: OverlayPlan { region_bytes, residents: Vec<OverlayResident>, reload_policy: OverlayReloadPolicy }. No allocator logic; compiler will fill in once expert kernel sets exist. Documents the IR slot so later passes can reason about overlay residency.
close_reason: Superseded by F-B9 (bd-140); see comment

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-codegen` (or `gbf-ir` if that owns the IR types).
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-codegen/src/overlay_plan.rs` (new) or equivalent IR module.

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — section 8.5 `OverlayPlan`. Also F11 design notes on bd-1i8.

### Implementation Notes

This task is intentionally a **stub**. The full overlay allocator depends on expert kernel sets that don't exist yet (F1/F6 land first). What we need *now* is the IR slot so later passes can refer to it and so T11.3's preflight has a place to read overlay demand from.

Define:

```rust
pub struct OverlayPlan {
    pub region_bytes: u16,                  // = CompileProfile.wram_layout.overlay_bytes
    pub residents: Vec<OverlayResident>,    // empty in v1
    pub reload_policy: OverlayReloadPolicy, // = CompileProfile.overlay_reload
}

pub struct OverlayResident {
    pub kind: OverlayResidentKind,          // ExpertKernel, MicroKernel, LutFragment
    pub size_bytes: u16,
    pub source_bank: SourceBankRef,
    pub estimated_reload_cost_cycles: Option<u32>,
}

pub enum OverlayResidentKind {
    ExpertKernel,
    MicroKernel,
    LutFragment,
}
```

Place `OverlayPlan` in the compiler-pipeline ordering after `RomWindowPlan` (planv0.md section 8) and before `ArenaPlan` (section 9), as planv0.md section 8.5 prescribes.

### What this task does NOT do

- It does not implement an allocator.
- It does not select residents.
- It does not estimate reload cost.

These all wait until expert kernels exist (F1/F6) and `gbf-bench` produces calibration data.

### What this task DOES do

- Declares the IR types.
- Adds a no-op pass that constructs an empty `OverlayPlan { region_bytes: profile.wram_layout.overlay_bytes, residents: vec![], reload_policy: profile.overlay_reload }`.
- Wires the pass into the pipeline so downstream consumers can reference it.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-codegen                              # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-codegen -- overlay_plan                # Empty OverlayPlan constructible from CompileProfile
cargo clippy -p gbf-codegen -- -D warnings               # No warnings
# Verify: OverlayPlan is reachable from the pipeline ordering
# Verify: region_bytes equals the active CompileProfile.wram_layout.overlay_bytes
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `OverlayResidentKind` is exhaustive; future kinds force compile errors at consumers.
- §VI.1: IR slot lives in the codegen pipeline, not invented ad hoc by later passes.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Superseded by F-B9 (bd-140) — 2026-04-26

This stub used a session-driven shape `{ region_bytes, residents, reload_policy }` that diverged from the canonical planv0.md `OverlayPlan`. F-B9 replaces it with the canonical shape `{ regions: Vec<OverlayRegion>, installs: Vec<OverlayInstall>, share_classes: Vec<OverlayShareClass> }`. For BringUp v1, F-B9 keeps `regions.len() == 1` and `share_classes.is_empty()` so the small-scope semantics of the original stub are preserved.

Closing as superseded. Implementation work is tracked under F-B9 and its (future) child tasks.

title: T11.1: Define WramLayoutPolicy, OverlayReloadPolicy, WramReserved types in gbf-policy
description: Add WramLayoutPolicy { overlay_bytes, continuation_bytes, stack_bytes, hot_arena_bytes_min }, OverlayReloadPolicy enum (PerExpertSwitch default + PerSlice + PerLayerStep variants), and WramReserved { overlay, hot_arena_floor, total } in gbf-policy. Restructure RuntimeChromeBudget.wram_reserved to use WramReserved. Schema-only — no logic.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-policy`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/budget.rs` (extend), `gbf-policy/src/profile.rs` (new — but only the WRAM-layout types here; `CompileProfile` itself is T11.2).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "WRAM hot arena", "WRAM overlay", "OverlayPlan", "ArenaPlan". Also see the 2026-04-26 session refinement comment on bd-yfv.

### Implementation Notes

Add to `gbf-policy`:

```rust
pub struct WramLayoutPolicy {
    /// Fixed-size region of WRAM reserved for overlay payloads (expert hot kernels copied
    /// from expert banks). Sized per CompileProfile so it is reproducible and preflightable.
    pub overlay_bytes: u16,
    /// WRAM bytes reserved for the resumable continuation record (gbf-abi).
    pub continuation_bytes: u16,
    /// WRAM bytes reserved for the call stack.
    pub stack_bytes: u16,
    /// Floor that ArenaPlan must satisfy after overlay/continuation/stack/runtime use.
    pub hot_arena_bytes_min: u16,
}

pub enum OverlayReloadPolicy {
    /// Default: reload the overlay only when the routed expert (or its kernel family)
    /// changes. Caches well under temporal smoothness regularization.
    PerExpertSwitch,
    /// Reload at every safe-point/yield. Predictable; may reload redundantly.
    PerSlice,
    /// Reload at each layer's expert dispatch. Worst per-token; trivially correct.
    PerLayerStep,
}

pub struct WramReserved {
    pub overlay: u16,
    pub hot_arena_floor: u16,
    pub total: u16,
}
```

Then **restructure `RuntimeChromeBudget`**:
- Replace `pub wram_reserved: u16` with `pub wram_reserved: WramReserved`.
- This is the field referenced in T2.1 (bd-yfv) refinement; coordinate so T2.1 lands the new shape and T11.1 owns the supporting types.

`OverlayReloadPolicy::PerExpertSwitch` is the default for the BringUp profile (T11.2). The other variants are named so future profiles can pick them; they are not implemented as alternative behaviors here, just declared.

Doc comments must explicitly note:
- `overlay_bytes` is per-CompileProfile and constant for that profile.
- `hot_arena_bytes_min` is a floor; actual `ArenaPlan` allocation may exceed it.
- `WramReserved.total <= 8192` (8 KiB WRAM is the absolute upper bound).

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-policy                                  # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- wram_layout                     # Round-trip + invariant tests
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- budget                          # Existing budget tests still pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-policy -- -D warnings                   # No warnings
# Verify: WramReserved serializes and total field equals overlay + runtime-internal use
# Verify: OverlayReloadPolicy is exhaustive over the three documented modes
# Verify: WramLayoutPolicy.overlay_bytes + continuation_bytes + stack_bytes + hot_arena_bytes_min <= 8192
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1 Correctness by Construction: `OverlayReloadPolicy` enum makes reload-cadence exhaustive; `WramReserved` field makes overlay-vs-arena explicit.
- §III.1 Compiler-first: WRAM upper bound is encoded as a debug assertion in constructors so violations are caught at construction.
- §VI.1 Single Source of Truth: WRAM split rules live in `gbf-policy` and are imported by `gbf-train` and `gbf-codegen`; no magic numbers elsewhere.

title: Use cryptographic digest for artifact semantic hashes
description: Follow-up from bd-28f6 review: ArtifactCore::semantic_hash and CanonicalTensor::content_hash currently use the local stable_digest helper to produce deterministic 256-bit values, but Hash256 should be backed by a real cryptographic digest such as SHA-256 or BLAKE3 before these hashes become content-addressed artifact identities. Evaluate dependency policy and replace the local FNV-derived helper with a cryptographic implementation.

title: T5.1: Implement temporal switch penalty (L_switch)
description: Implement L_switch = (1/LT) sum_{l,t} (1 - dot(p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1})) where p_{l,t} is the soft expert distribution. Requires access to consecutive token positions within a training batch. Must handle sequence boundaries (don't penalize switches across sequence boundaries). The gradient flows through the router's softmax output.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::switch passes; Identical consecutive dists -> loss=0; Orthogonal -> loss=1; Sequence boundaries handled

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/loss/switch.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "temporal switch penalty", "L_switch", "consecutive tokens", "bank-switch cost".

### Implementation Notes
- Temporal switch penalty: `L_switch = (1/LT) Σ_{l,t} (1 - ⟨p_{l,t}, p_{l,t-1}⟩)`
- Where p_{l,t} is the soft routing distribution at layer l, token t.
- Inner product between consecutive-token routing distributions penalizes switching.
- Must handle sequence boundaries: reset at start of each sequence (no penalty for first token).
- `pub fn temporal_switch_loss(routing_probs: &Tensor, sequence_mask: &Tensor) -> Tensor`
  - routing_probs shape: [batch, seq_len, n_layers, n_experts]
  - sequence_mask: marks sequence boundaries

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- loss::switch         # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings        # No warnings
# Verify: identical consecutive distributions -> loss = 0
# Verify: orthogonal consecutive distributions -> loss = 1
# Verify: sequence boundaries correctly handled (no cross-sequence penalty)
# Verify: gradient flows correctly
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.2: Pure function — tensor in, scalar out
- §III.2: Property test — loss is in [0, 1] range

title: T10.1: Create tiny model test fixtures and test helper utilities
description: Create a test fixtures module with: (a) A micro MoE model config: d_model=8, d_ff=16, n_experts=2, n_layers=2, vocab=32, BoundedKv sequence, one MoE block at layer 1. (b) Factory functions: make_tiny_model(), make_tiny_artifact(), make_tiny_compile_request(), make_tiny_workload(). (c) A deterministic random tensor generator seeded by name (for reproducible test data). (d) Assertion helpers: assert_tensor_close(actual, expected, atol, rtol) with detailed diff output on failure, assert_bytes_equal(a, b) with hex diff on first divergent byte, assert_artifact_valid(artifact) that checks all manifest invariants. (e) A tiny prompt corpus (8 prompts of 4-16 tokens each) for pipeline tests. All fixtures must be constructible without any training — they use hand-set or random weights.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-test exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures passes; tiny_moe_config produces valid model; deterministic_tensor with same seed = identical output
close_reason:
## Closure Summary

Implemented deterministic tiny fixture infrastructure in `gbf-test`:

- `tiny_moe_config()` builds the requested micro config: `d_model=8`, `d_ff=16`, `n_experts=2`, `n_layers=2`, `vocab=32`, BoundedKv sequence config, dense layer 0, and MoE layer 1.
- `make_tiny_model()` owns the same embedding, dense FFN, router, and expert-block state consumed by artifact export, so fixture factories no longer recreate private model truth.
- `make_tiny_artifact()` returns a validated `ArtifactCore` built from the currently exportable tiny model surfaces. Sequence-state manifest semantics are not claimed while those contracts are still stubs.
- `make_tiny_compile_request()` and `make_tiny_workload()` return explicitly named placeholder types until `gbf-policy` and `gbf-workload` define final request/manifest contracts.
- Deterministic tensor generation supports numeric and name seeds.
- Assertion helpers cover tensor shapes/values, raw `f32` slices, byte diffs, and artifact-core tensor/quant invariants including content-hash self-consistency.

## Review Feedback Applied

- Left the unrelated `history/planv0.md` changes out of scope and unstaged.
- Made `TinyModel` the single source of fixture state used by artifact generation.
- Added dense FFN layer-0 export coverage to match the tiny topology where current export APIs exist.
- Renamed compile/workload fixture structs as placeholders and recorded bd-209g for final contract factories.
- Narrowed artifact validation to `ArtifactCore` and added a stale `content_hash` regression test.
- Added slice-based assertion helpers plus negative tests that pin diagnostic panic messages.

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Tiny MoE config has the requested dimensions, two layers, BoundedKv sequence config, and one MoE block at layer 1. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_tiny_moe_config_is_micro_and_instantiable` | Also verifies model fixture owns embedding, dense FFN, router, and experts. |
| Deterministic tensor generation repeats by seed and name. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_deterministic_tensor_repeats_for_same_seed_and_name` | Checks same seed/name equality, different seed/name inequality, and finite values. |
| Tiny prompt corpus has 8 small vocab-bounded prompts. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_prompt_corpus_is_tiny_and_vocab_bounded` | Guards the 4-16 token range and vocab size. |
| Tiny artifact core is valid, deterministic, and covers embedding/dense/router/expert exportable surfaces. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_make_tiny_artifact_is_valid_and_deterministic` | Does not claim full manifest or sequence-state artifact semantics. |
| Placeholder compile/workload factories are deterministic and internally consistent. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_make_compile_request_and_workload_are_consistent` | Placeholder names are explicit. |
| Assertion helpers accept matching inputs and expose raw-slice adapters. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_assert_helpers_accept_matching_inputs` | Covers `TestTensor`, raw tensor values, raw `f32` slices, and bytes. |
| Assertion helper diagnostics identify the first bad value/byte. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_assert_helpers_report_first_bad_value_and_byte` | Guards failure-message usefulness. |
| Artifact-core validation rejects stale tensor content hashes. | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures_artifact_core_validation_rejects_stale_tensor_content_hash` | Guards against accepting corrupted deserialized/manual cores. |
| The crate compiles and lints cleanly. | `cargo check -p gbf-test`; `cargo clippy -p gbf-test -- -D warnings` | Passed after review fixes. |

## Non-Goals

- No executable end-to-end forward model is claimed.
- No final `CompileRequest`, `WorkloadManifest`, `ArtifactManifest`, or sequence-state artifact schema is claimed.
- No performance benchmark is claimed beyond the tiny constants and test runtime.

## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Tiny model config and artifact factory had competing truth. | Artifact generation recreated QAT state privately instead of consuming a model fixture. | Added AGENTS fixture guidance: one tiny fixture source of truth. | `fixtures_make_tiny_artifact_is_valid_and_deterministic`; `fixtures_tiny_moe_config_is_micro_and_instantiable` | `AGENTS.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures` |
| Compile/workload stand-ins looked like final contracts. | Policy/workload modules are still stubs, but fixture names did not say placeholder. | Added AGENTS fixture guidance: name placeholders explicitly and record later contract work. | Explicit `TinyCompileRequestPlaceholder` / `TinyWorkloadManifestPlaceholder` names; bd-209g | `AGENTS.md`; bd-209g |
| Artifact validation overclaimed manifest invariants. | Helper name and bead text blurred `ArtifactCore` with future manifest contracts. | Added AGENTS fixture guidance: scope artifact assertions to the existing artifact type. | `fixtures_artifact_core_validation_rejects_stale_tensor_content_hash` | `AGENTS.md`; `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures` |
| Assertion helper diagnostics were only happy-path tested. | The first implementation did not test failure-message quality. | Keep diagnostic helper tests negative as well as positive. | `fixtures_assert_helpers_report_first_bad_value_and_byte` | `cargo test -p gbf-test -- fixtures` |
| Unrelated plan edits polluted review context. | Pre-existing dirty `history/planv0.md` was visible in diff reviews. | No action in code; explicitly keep unrelated dirty files unstaged and call them out in closure. | `git status --short` before staging | Session workflow |

title: Epic E: Calibration & Bench — gbf-bench production
description: gbf-bench produces real measurements that populate the calibration schema from F-A2.7. PlatformCalibrationBundle (bank_switch_cost, sram_page_cost, timer_isr_cost), KernelCalibrationBundle (per-kernel measured profiles), RuntimeCalibrationBundle (scheduler_overhead, overlay_install_cost, trace_event_cost). Cycle-model drift reports + constrained autotune over tile/residency/slice knobs.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Epic E: Calibration & Bench (`gbf-bench`)

### What this is

`gbf-bench` per `planv0.md` line 318: workload manifests, cycle calibration, layered calibration bundle production (`PlatformCalibrationBundle`, `KernelCalibrationBundle`, `RuntimeCalibrationBundle`), constrained autotuning, cycle-model drift reporting.

The schema for these types lands in M0 (F-A2.7). This epic produces real *measurements* that populate them.

### Why this matters

`ScheduleCostAnalysis` (Epic B F-B12) is the only objective-facing cost producer in the compiler. Its inputs are calibration bundles. Without real measurements, cost estimates are heuristics with no `EvidenceClass::Measured` data, and `RiskPolicy::require_confidence_at_least` always fails.

`HardCap` switch budgets in `CompileProfile` (F11 / `bd-1i8`) graduate from hand-picked numbers to calibration-derived values once Epic E's `PlatformCalibrationBundle` exists with measured `bank_switch_cost`.

### Scope (six features)

- **F-E1 — Workload manifest pipeline**: `WorkloadManifest`, `WorkloadClass`, `AcceptanceMatrix`, `ExecutionMatrix`, `ObservationPolicy` types in `gbf-workload` plus the bench runner that consumes them.
- **F-E2 — `PlatformCalibrationBundle` production**: measure `bank_switch_cost`, `sram_page_cost`, `timer_isr_cost` against a target via emulator or hardware.
- **F-E3 — `KernelCalibrationBundle` production**: per-kernel measured profiles tied to `kernel_impl_hash` + `runtime_nucleus_hash`.
- **F-E4 — `RuntimeCalibrationBundle` production**: `scheduler_overhead`, `overlay_install_cost`, `trace_event_cost`.
- **F-E5 — Cycle-model drift reports**: compare `gbf-asm` cycle model predictions to measured kernel runs; produce drift reports.
- **F-E6 — Constrained autotune**: explicit knob set (tile sizes, kernel residency, slice ceilings); Pareto frontier; objective-driven selection.

### Out of scope

- Producing the artifacts themselves → Epic B.
- Running oracles in calibration mode → Epic C + this epic interface.

### Critical path

```
F-A2.7 (calibration schema) ──→ F-E1..F-E4
F-A1 (gbf-asm cycle model) ──→ F-E5 drift reports
F-H3 (emulator adapters) ────→ F-E2..F-E4 (bench runs against emulator)
```

### Confidence: MEDIUM

Bench infrastructure is straightforward. The risk is "what's a good baseline measurement context" — emulator vs hardware vs both, and how MeasurementContext encodes that.

title: Implement non-Ternary2 artifact weight encodings
description: Own artifact/export/compiler support for WeightEncoding::SparseTernaryBitplanes and WeightEncoding::Binary1, including tensor element representation, ArtifactCore validation, ExportVisitor materialization, independent verifier agreement, Binary1 zero rejection semantics, and removal or replacement of artifact_core_rejects_declared_non_ternary2_weight_encodings_until_tensor_encoding_exists.

title: Epic H: Kernel & Emulator — gbf-kernel + gbf-emu
description: gbf-kernel owns kernel contracts and the first-wave kernel implementations (matvec, residual, norm, route, decode) as AsmIR builders tied to KernelSpecId + KernelCalibrationBundle. gbf-emu wraps SameBoy and BGB as adapters with a shared trait (load_rom, run_until_breakpoint, read_mem, snapshot, dump_trace, power_cut, harness plumbing).

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Epic H: Kernel & Emulator (`gbf-kernel`, `gbf-emu`)

### What this is

Two crates that are infrastructure for the core compiler/runtime work:

- `gbf-kernel` per `planv0.md` line 189: "owns kernel contracts and kernel-family implementations; `gbf-codegen` selects them and `gbf-runtime` links them, but neither redefines kernel signatures." A kernel here is e.g., `matvec_int8`, `residual_add`, `top1_router`, `softmax_clipped`, etc. — the actual compute primitives.
- `gbf-emu`: emulator adapters (SameBoy / BGB), breakpoint orchestration, trace normalization, harness mode. Per planv0.md line 2552: "emulator accuracy solved by adapters around SameBoy/BGB rather than re-implementation."

### Why this matters

Without `gbf-kernel`, every compile request reinvents matvec. Without `gbf-emu`, every test that runs a ROM is bespoke shell-out to an emulator.

### Scope (three features)

- **F-H1 — `KernelSpec` + signatures + compatibility ids**: schema types only. `KernelSpec`, `KernelSpecId`, signatures, calling conventions, `KernelCompatSpec`, autotune dimensions.
- **F-H2 — Kernel implementations**: AsmIR builders for the first kernel families (matvec, residual, norm, route, decode). Each tied to a `KernelSpecId` and a `KernelCalibrationBundle`.
- **F-H3 — Emulator adapters**: SameBoy + BGB adapters with shared trait, breakpoint orchestration, normalized trace event format, harness command/result block plumbing.

### Critical path

```
F-A1 (gbf-asm builders) ──→ F-H2 (kernels are AsmIR sections)
F-A3 (gbf-abi traces)   ──→ F-H3 (adapters parse traces)
F-H1 ───→ F-B (compiler kernel selection in stages)
F-H3 ───→ F-E (bench needs adapters to run measurements)
```

### Confidence: MEDIUM

Adapters are real engineering — the harness-mode plumbing (breakpoints, memory inspection, trace dumping) varies between SameBoy and BGB. F-H2 kernel implementations are the steady-state work that gets refined over many milestones.

## F-F1: Build reports

### What this is

Emit the structured JSON + binary report set per `planv0.md` line 1577: `map.json`, `provenance.json`, `budget.json`, `slice_report.json`, `trace_schema.json`, `oracle_vectors.bin`, `semantic_checkpoint_schema.json`, `operational_probe_schema.json`, `artifact_lineage.json`, `workload_manifest.json`, `hint_consumption.json`, `compiler_feedback.json`, `reachability_report.json`. Plus per-stage `stages/<stage>.json` snapshots when StageCache is cold.

### Why this matters

Every regression in the system gets caught by diffing reports between builds. Without structured reports, debugging a regression is bisecting + cycle-counter staring.

### Design sketch

One JSON schema per report. Reports go into `artifacts/builds/<build_id>/`. CI tests round-trip each schema. Schema versioning via `gbf-migrate`.

### Cross-feature deps

- All compiler stages (F-B0..F-B13) emit their reports
- F-A6.1 gbf-store for archival
- F-A6.5 gbf-migrate for schema evolution

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T1.3: Implement ActFakeQuant module
description: Activation clipping/quantization at expert input/output boundaries. Clamps activations to a learned or fixed range, applies fake quantization matching the deployed integer format. Must export clip ranges as first-class metadata in ExportFacts. Drives the activation_range_penalty in the loss.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::activation passes; Activations clamped to configured range; STE gradient flows
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

- Artifact contract: pre-export activation contract implemented. `ActFakeQuant::export_range()` exposes the current clip range and `ActFakeQuant::forward_spec()` exposes range, quant format, and train/eval enablement for adapter/export callers. Full `ExportFacts.activation_ranges` materialization is not claimed here; existing dependent bead `bd-g90` owns `ExportVisitor` and artifact assembly.
- Differentiable Burn path: `gbf-train/src/qat/activation.rs` adds `ActFakeQuantBurnQat::fake_quant_forward`, using Burn tensors plus `ste_clamp`/`ste_round` under the `burn-adapter` feature.
- Tests proving it: model scalar tests, Burn activation forward/export tests, Burn autodiff gradient passthrough test, and clippy gates listed below.
- Acceptance movement: the original ExportFacts metadata wording is satisfied at this bead as the module-level pre-export `export_range` contract; first-class artifact placement moves to `bd-g90`, which already depends on this bead and owns `ExportVisitor`.

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Activation core validates range modes and scalar clamp/fake-quant behavior | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::activation` | none | Covers Fixed/Learned/EMA range updates, Int8/UInt8/Int4 scalar behavior, eval passthrough, invalid contracts, and range export. |
| Model crate exposes implemented adapter/export spec without depending on Burn | `cargo check -p gbf-model` | none | `ActivationFakeQuantSpec` is pure gbf-model data; Burn remains outside gbf-model. |
| Burn activation adapter matches scalar projection and preserves STE input gradients | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::activation` | `burn-adapter` | Includes a Burn autodiff test proving gradient passthrough through activation fake-quant. |
| Activation changes are warning-clean | `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings`; `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- -D warnings` | none / `burn-adapter` | Focused clippy gates for both sides of the activation contract. |

No-future-variant rule: no new range modes, quant formats, hardness modes, schedules, or artifact encodings were accepted. Existing `Fixed`, `Learned`, `Ema`, `Int8`, `UInt8`, and `Int4` paths have implemented behavior. Phase hardness remains owned by `bd-2uw`; full artifact `ExportFacts` lowering remains owned by `bd-g90`.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/qat/activation.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "ActFakeQuant", "activation clipping", "activation fake-quant", "expert input/output boundaries".

### Implementation Notes
- `ActFakeQuant` clamps activations to a learned or fixed range, then applies fake quantization matching the deployed integer format.
- Key API:
  - `forward(&self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor` — clamp + fake-quant in training, passthrough in eval if configured
  - `export_range(&self) -> ActivationRange` — for ExportFacts
- The clamp range can be:
  - Fixed (from config)
  - Learned (parameter updated during training)
  - EMA-tracked (running min/max with exponential moving average)
- Fake quantization: `round(clamp(x, lo, hi) * scale) / scale` with STE
- This sits at expert input/output boundaries to ensure activations fit in the deployed integer accumulator

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                           # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::activation          # Unit tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings            # No warnings
# Verify: forward produces output in clamped range
# Verify: gradients flow through via STE
# Verify: export_range returns correct bounds
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: Use an enum for range mode (Fixed/Learned/EMA) — no stringly-typed config
- §I.2: Pure quantization math, EMA state update at the boundary

comments.author: bkase

Do not close this bead until bd-1mv provides an actual Burn/autodiff adapter test proving gradient passthrough through activation fake-quant.

Learned/phase-hardness behavior remains open for later training-schedule tasks.

Bead remains open/blocked on bd-1mv for real Burn tensor/STE gradient proof.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Corrective review note: activation feedback 2026-04-26

The Burn-backed path is now intentionally narrower than the scalar gbf-model core:

- `gbf-model::ActFakeQuant` retains scalar/config support for Fixed, Learned, and Ema range modes.
- `gbf-train::ActFakeQuantBurnQat::from_core` rejects Learned and Ema until Burn owns trainable clip-bound params or live EMA observation updates. Follow-up bead `bd-1zv` owns that dynamic Burn range-state work.
- The proven differentiable Burn contract for this bead is Fixed-range activation fake-quant with clipped clamp gradients and STE round.
- `ExportFacts.activation_ranges` / `RangeDigest` materialization remains outside this bead and is now explicitly called out on `bd-g90`.
- `activation_range_penalty` is outside this bead and remains owned by `bd-1p3`.
- Actual expert input/output boundary placement is outside this bead and remains owned by `bd-ubm` / `ExpertBlockQat` composition.

Updated guards: `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::activation`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::activation`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- adapter::burn`.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Rounding parity scope note

Burn activation fake-quant runs on f32 tensors while the scalar core uses f64 intermediates for finite tiny-range safety. The Burn adapter is tested for representative scalar parity and finite tiny-range behavior, but `bd-pk3` does not claim bit-exact Burn/scalar equality at exact half-step rounding thresholds. Artifact/export numeric canonicalization remains owned by scalar/export code (`bd-g90`), not by the Burn training forward path.

title: F-B12: ScheduleCostAnalysis (Stage 11)
description: Single load-bearing producer of objective-facing cost envelopes. Per-mode EstimatedCostDelta with EvidenceClass (Measured/Transferred/Heuristic) + sample_count + refs. Feeds the FeasibilityRefinementLoop.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-B12: ScheduleCostAnalysis (Stage 11)

### What this is

The single load-bearing producer of objective-facing cost envelopes (`planv0.md` line 1484). Runs over `GbSchedIR` / `SchedulePack`, uses calibration bundles, produces per-mode cost envelopes that predict objective satisfaction and feed the refinement loop.

### Why this matters

Every other stage produces structural facts; F-B12 produces *predicted cost*. Centralizing keeps repair logic testable: a `RepairProposal::estimated_cost: EstimatedCostDelta` is meaningful only if there's a single oracle for cost.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct ScheduleCostReport {
    pub objective: CompileObjective,
    pub per_mode: BTreeMap<RuntimeMode, EstimatedCostDelta>,
    pub refs: Vec<EvidenceRef>,
}

pub struct EstimatedCostDelta {
    pub cycles: Option<CostEstimate>,
    pub bank_switches: Option<CostEstimate>,
    pub sram_page_switches: Option<CostEstimate>,
    pub bytes: Option<CostEstimate>,
}
```

Each `CostEstimate` carries `EvidenceClass::{Measured, Transferred, Heuristic}` + `sample_count` + `refs` so risk policies can reject low-confidence estimates.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B11 GbSchedIR
- Epic E calibration bundles (`PlatformCalibrationBundle` etc.)

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T4.1: Define training phase types in gbf-train
description: Define TrainPhaseKind (DenseTeacherWarmup/RouterWarmup/ExpertTernaryQat/FullNumericQat/HardenAndSelect), TrainPhaseSpec (kind + start/end step + per-component QAT hardness + router mode), QuantHardness (Off/Soft/Hard), RouterTrainMode (SoftTop1/HardTop1). These are config-driven; the phase schedule is declared in a TOML config file, not hardcoded.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-train -- phase passes; TOML round-trip; Validation rejects gaps in phase boundaries; 5-phase default is constructible
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

- Artifact contract: not implemented in this bead. `bd-ptk` defines the pre-export training phase config contract only: the canonical five-phase schedule, validated TOML shape, per-phase `QuantHardness`, and model-owned `RouterTrainMode` selection. No artifact bytes, export facts, or ReferenceModelBundle behavior are claimed here.
- Differentiable Burn path: not implemented in this bead. This is schema/config validation only. Executable training-loop phase selection is moved to bd-cmf, and per-module Off/Soft/Hard application is moved to bd-2uw.
- Tests proving it: `cargo test -p gbf-train -- phase`, `cargo test -p gbf-model -- router`, `cargo check -p gbf-model -p gbf-train`, `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -p gbf-train -- -D warnings`, `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`, and `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`.
- Acceptance criterion movement: scheduler behavior is moved to bd-cmf; executable phase hardness control is moved to bd-2uw; dense teacher freezing is moved to bd-2hp; logit distillation is moved to bd-18h; ReferenceModelBundle export is moved to bd-7lu; phase transition integration coverage is moved to bd-14k.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Canonical five-phase schedule config | supported | moved to bd-cmf | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- phase_schedule_parses_from_training_toml_and_round_trips` |
| Schedule range/order validation | supported | moved to bd-cmf | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- phase_schedule_rejects` |
| Default five-phase config helper | supported | moved to bd-cmf | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- default_five_phase_schedule` |
| `QuantHardness::{Off,Soft,Hard}` config values | supported as config | moved to bd-2uw | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- phase` |
| `RouterTrainMode::{SoftTop1,HardTop1}` | supported by gbf-model router mode type | moved to bd-cmf/bd-2uw | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- router` |
| Teacher freeze / distillation / ReferenceModelBundle | not applicable | moved to bd-2hp and bd-18h | moved to bd-7lu | owner beads named here |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Phase schedules parse from `[[training.phases]]` TOML and round-trip through the public adapter | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- phase_schedule_parses_from_training_toml_and_round_trips` | none | Pins the literal snake_case TOML shape and expected parsed phase rows. |
| Schedule validation rejects wrong phase count, non-zero start, noncanonical order, gaps, overlaps, and zero-length phase ranges | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- phase_schedule_rejects` and `cargo test -p gbf-train -- phase_specs_reject_zero_length_ranges` | none | This is config validation only, not training-loop execution. |
| The default five-phase config helper is constructible and uses checked step arithmetic | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- default_five_phase_schedule` | none | Overflow is rejected instead of silently truncating phase ranges. |
| Router phase mode reuses the model-owned router mode enum instead of duplicating a training-only enum | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- router` and `cargo check -p gbf-model -p gbf-train` | none | The executable routing behavior remains in gbf-model; this bead only serializes the selected mode. |
| QAT closure format and architecture guardrails are satisfied | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` and `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Required local sensors for QAT bead closure. |

## No-future Variant Acceptance

This bead accepts no future phase names, phase counts, or router modes beyond the executable variants present today. `TrainingPhaseSchedule` rejects schedules that are not exactly the five canonical F4 phases starting at step 0 with contiguous half-open ranges. `QuantHardness::Soft` is accepted only as a config value; its executable model/Burn semantics are not claimed here and are owned by bd-2uw.

No pre-patch red/green transcript was captured; before this change `gbf-train/src/phase.rs` was a stub, so the new phase tests are post-patch evidence only.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-train`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-train/src/phase.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "TrainPhaseKind", "TrainPhaseSpec", "QuantHardness", "RouterTrainMode", "DenseTeacherWarmup".

### Implementation Notes
Define these types:
```rust
pub enum TrainPhaseKind {
    DenseTeacherWarmup,    // Phase A: train dense model, no QAT
    RouterWarmup,           // Phase B: activate router, soft top-1, no weight QAT
    ExpertTernaryQat,       // Phase C: hard ternary weights, soft activations
    FullNumericQat,         // Phase D: hard everything
    HardenAndSelect,        // Phase E: freeze, export, shadow compile, select
}

pub struct TrainPhaseSpec {
    pub kind: TrainPhaseKind,
    pub start_step: u64,
    pub end_step: u64,
    pub expert_qat: QuantHardness,
    pub activation_qat: QuantHardness,
    pub norm_qat: QuantHardness,
    pub router_mode: RouterTrainMode,
}

pub enum QuantHardness { Off, Soft, Hard }
pub enum RouterTrainMode { SoftTop1, HardTop1 }
```
- Config-driven: phase schedule declared in training TOML config.
- Parse from TOML: `[[training.phases]]` array.
- Validate: phases must be contiguous (no gaps), start_step[i+1] == end_step[i].

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-train -- phase                  # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-train -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: TOML round-trip — serialize/deserialize phase schedule
# Verify: validation rejects gaps in phase boundaries
# Verify: default 5-phase schedule is constructible
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: TrainPhaseKind enum makes the 5 phases exhaustive — can't invent a phase at runtime
- §III.3 Spec-Driven: Phase schedule in TOML IS the spec

title: T-B14.3: Define ConstraintDelta + KnobDelta + ResourcePressureUpdate + admissibility primitives
description: 14 KnobDelta variants (one per repair lever). ResourcePressureUpdate covers wram_hot/hram_hot/bank0_rom/switchable_rom_window/sram_window/slice_cycles/interrupt_latency/trace_bytes/persist_bytes/overlay_installs/bank_switches/sram_page_switches with PressureLimit<T> { soft, hard }. Pure check_delta_admissible function returns DeltaRejection (KnobLocked, PolicyToggleDisabled, BeyondBounds, NotMonotone, EffectfulRecompute, InvariantObservabilityViolation). Loop controller (T-B14.6) calls but does not duplicate.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-B14.3: Define `ConstraintDelta` + `KnobDelta` + `ResourcePressureUpdate`

**Target crate**: `gbf-policy`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/knobs/delta.rs`
**Reference**: F-B14 oracle resolution. T-B14.1 (knobs), T-B14.2 (overrides) must land first.

### Background

`RepairProposal::tighten: ConstraintDelta` — the typed mutation a pass proposes. The loop controller (T-B14.6) decides admissibility and applies. Each `KnobDelta` variant maps 1:1 to one repair lever; no untyped extension points.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct ConstraintDelta {
    pub changes: Vec<KnobDelta>,
}

pub enum KnobDelta {
    AdvancePlacementProfile { to: PlacementProfile },
    SetTraceDemotion { to: TraceDemotionLevel },
    DisableOptionalProbes { probes: BTreeSet<TraceProbeId> },
    RaiseReductionCeiling { selector: Option<ReductionSelector>, to: ReductionPlanCeiling },
    PromoteRecomputeLevel { to: RecomputePromotionLevel },
    ForceRecompute { values: BTreeSet<ValueSelector> },
    AdvanceSramPageAggression { to: SramPageAggression },
    AdvanceKernelResidencyBias { to: KernelResidencyBias },
    AdvanceKernelDuplicationBias { to: KernelDuplicationBias },
    ForceKernelResidency { selector: KernelSelector, to: KernelResidency },
    PromoteOverlay { to: OverlayPromotionLevel },
    NarrowTileClasses { selector: TileSelector, remaining: BTreeSet<TileCandidateClass> },
    SetSliceCoarsening { to: SliceCoarseningLevel },
    UpdatePressureThreshold { update: ResourcePressureUpdate },
}

pub enum ResourcePressureUpdate {
    WramHot(PressureLimit<ByteBudget>),
    HramHot(PressureLimit<ByteBudget>),
    Bank0Rom(PressureLimit<ByteBudget>),
    SwitchableRomWindow(PressureLimit<ByteBudget>),
    SramWindow(PressureLimit<ByteBudget>),
    SliceCycles(PressureLimit<CycleBudget>),
    InterruptLatency(PressureLimit<CycleBudget>),
    TraceBytesPerFrame(PressureLimit<u16>),
    PersistBytesPerFrame(PressureLimit<u16>),
    OverlayInstallsPerFrame(PressureLimit<u8>),
    BankSwitchesPerToken(PressureLimit<u16>),
    SramPageSwitchesPerToken(PressureLimit<u16>),
}
```

### Admissibility-check primitives

Provide pure functions that the loop controller (T-B14.6) calls — they don't mutate state, they answer "is this delta legal against current `CompileKnobs` + `RepairPolicy`?".

```rust
pub fn check_delta_admissible(
    delta: &KnobDelta,
    current: &CompileKnobs,
    policy: &RepairPolicy,
    observability: ObservabilityMode,
) -> Result<(), DeltaRejection>;

pub enum DeltaRejection {
    KnobLocked { knob: CompileKnobId },
    PolicyToggleDisabled { knob: CompileKnobId, toggle: &'static str },
    BeyondBounds { knob: CompileKnobId, attempted: String, max: String },
    NotMonotone { knob: CompileKnobId, current: String, attempted: String },
    InvariantObservabilityViolation { knob: CompileKnobId },
    EffectfulRecompute { value: ValueSelector },     // recompute promotion of an effectful value
}
```

`DeltaRejection::EffectfulRecompute` is the special case: the loop controller validates that `ForceRecompute` targets are pure (no RNG, no sequence write, no persistence commit, no trace effect). T-B14.6 wires this against `GbInferIR` effect edges.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-policy
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- delta::serde_round_trip
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- delta::admissibility_locked          # Locked knob -> KnobLocked
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- delta::admissibility_policy_toggle    # allow_* false -> PolicyToggleDisabled
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- delta::admissibility_bounds           # Beyond max -> BeyondBounds
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- delta::admissibility_monotone         # Backward step -> NotMonotone
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- delta::invariant_blocks_demotion      # Invariant + SetTraceDemotion -> InvariantObservabilityViolation
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `KnobDelta` enum is closed; new repair levers require new variants.
- §V.3: `DeltaRejection` carries actionable diagnostic info.

title: T-A1.8: Listing (.lst) emitter
description: Human-readable annotated assembly listing — one line per encoded instruction with address, bytes, mnemonic, source-stage provenance. Byte-stable across runs so diffs are meaningful. Optional fields per ListingOptions.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A1.8: Listing emitter

**Target crate**: `gbf-asm`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-asm/src/listing.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` §"EncodedRom" (line 1565: emits `.gb`, `.sym`, `.lst`).

### Background

A `.lst` file is human-readable annotated assembly listing — one line per encoded instruction with address, bytes, mnemonic, source-stage provenance. It exists for debugging compiler/runtime output without reverse-engineering the ROM. Listings must be byte-stable across runs so a diff between two listings is meaningful.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct ListingOptions {
    pub show_provenance: bool,
    pub show_cycle_costs: bool,
    pub show_bytes: bool,
}

pub fn emit_listing(
    section: &Section,
    encoded: &EncodedSection,
    placed: &PlacedSection,
    symbols: &SymbolTable,
    opts: &ListingOptions,
) -> String;
```

Format:

```
; section: kernel.matvec.tile_8x8 (CommonBank)
; bank=03 origin=$4000 size=0x123
$4000: CD 34 80    call $8034            ; provenance: stage=Backend op=far_call_thunk
$4003: 21 00 C0    ld   hl, $C000        ; provenance: stage=ArenaPlan op=load_input_tile
...
```

Listings concatenate naturally per build into one `.lst` blob. Tests assert: same `(Section, EncodedSection, ListingOptions)` produces the same string twice in a row.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-asm
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- listing::byte_stable                     # Same input -> same output bytes
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- listing::all_options_render               # Each ListingOptions flag changes output
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- listing::provenance_visible               # Stage labels appear in output
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §IV.3: Listing determinism asserts at test level.
- §V.3: Listings carry provenance so a failing slice's listing is self-explaining.

title: T3.2: Update QuantSpec to reference TernaryWeightPlan
description: QuantSpec should carry an Option<TernaryWeightPlan> for each weight group (experts, router, classifier, embedding). The default expert plan is { encoding: Ternary2, scale_granularity: PerOutputRow, scale_format: Q8_8, threshold: AnnealedGlobalThenPerOutputRow }. Non-expert weights may have different plans or no ternary plan (staying at higher precision).
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-artifact exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- quant passes; QuantSpec supports mixed precision per weight group; Default expert plan matches spec
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

- Artifact contract: `QuantSpec` now carries explicit `WeightQuantEntry` records keyed by typed `ArtifactPath`/`CanonicalTensorId`. Each deployable weight tensor must have exactly one entry. `ternary_plan: Some(TernaryWeightPlan)` requires matching `TernaryQuantEntry` scale/bias metadata; `ternary_plan: None` is accepted only for current full-precision deployable weight tensors. `ArtifactCore::semantic_hash()` includes the weight-quant surface.
- Differentiable Burn path: not implemented in this bead. This bead updates artifact/model quant metadata and scalar export. The Burn-side runtime schedule implied by `ThresholdPlan::AnnealedGlobalThenPerOutputRow` is explicitly moved to `bd-xpr7`; current scalar/model code records and exports explicit threshold state and does not claim optimizer schedule evolution.
- Tests proving it: `cargo test -p gbf-artifact -- quant`, `cargo test -p gbf-artifact`, `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat`, `cargo clippy -p gbf-artifact -- -D warnings`, `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings`, `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`.
- Acceptance movement: no bd-qhu artifact acceptance was moved. The hidden runtime-training schedule implied by the annealed threshold plan is moved to `bd-xpr7` because bd-qhu is the QuantSpec/artifact mapping bead, not a Burn training scheduler bead.

## Review Feedback Disposition

- Intent review: clarified implementation as per-deployable-weight/per-projection artifact metadata keyed by existing typed artifact paths, not a new `WeightGroupId` enum.
- Design and semantic review: fixed bidirectional consistency so `WeightQuantEntry { ternary_plan: Some(_) }` cannot exist without `TernaryQuantEntry`, and every deployable weight tensor requires weight quant metadata.
- Semantic review: aligned `TernaryLinearQat::canonical_weight_plan()` and exported default expert projections with `QuantSpec::default_expert_ternary_plan()`.

title: T2.6: Synthetic reference RuntimeChromeBudget for pre-runtime training (M1-M2)
description: Provide a deterministic synthetic RuntimeChromeBudget for use before a real runtime shell exists. Same schema as the real emission path (T2.2) so trainer is not branchy. Hash field uses a stable, explicitly-marked sentinel (e.g., runtime_nucleus_hash with a known SYNTHETIC_REFERENCE prefix and reference_shell_modules = minimal+UI set). Stored as a fixture under fixtures/calibration/ or similar. Real shell emission supersedes it.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate(s)**: `gbf-train` (loader + fixture path), `gbf-policy` (helper to construct), `fixtures/calibration/` (the actual JSON).
**Target file(s)**: `fixtures/calibration/synthetic_reference_budget.json` (new), `gbf-policy/src/synthetic.rs` (new — constructs / validates), `gbf-train/src/preflight.rs` (uses).

**Reference**: bd-1hv 2026-04-26 session ("Pre-runtime: synthetic budget path"), T2.2 refinement (bd-1g9), T2.4 (bd-37r).

### Implementation Notes

This task lets training begin at M1–M2, before a real `gbf-runtime` shell exists, by providing a deterministic synthetic `RuntimeChromeBudget`.

1. **Schema parity with real emission.**
   - The synthetic budget MUST round-trip through the same `serde` types as the real one (no parallel struct).
   - `reference_shell_modules` is populated with the minimal+UI set from `pinned_reference_shell()` (T2.4).
   - `runtime_nucleus_hash` uses a stable, explicitly-marked sentinel:
     ```
     0x53594E54__48455449435F5345544348__... // "SYNT" "HETI" "C_SE" "TCH..."
     ```
     Or simpler: `Hash256::from_str("synthetic-reference-shell-v1").into_marked()`. The point is that the value is recognizable in logs and impossible to confuse with a real hash.

2. **Concrete byte estimates.**
   - Bank0Free: estimate 8 KiB after minimal+UI nucleus (Bank 0 is 16 KiB; UI/scheduler/etc. estimated at 8 KiB).
   - CommonBank: 1 slot × 16 KiB usable_bytes − reserved_slack=512.
   - ExpertBank: 8 slots × 16 KiB usable_bytes − reserved_slack=384 each.
   - WramReserved: overlay=512, hot_arena_floor=4096, total=1536 (overlay + continuation + stack + small runtime slack).
   - sram_reserved: 1024 (sequence-state + commit metadata reservation under T2.4).
   - Future-module reservations from T2.4 are subtracted from the relevant slots and recorded.

   These numbers must be reviewed and updated as the real shell forms; they are CONSERVATIVE starting points so a model trained against them likely fits the eventual real shell.

3. **Loader.**
   - `gbf-train::preflight` falls back to the synthetic budget when no real `RuntimeChromeBudget` is available.
   - Trainer emits a structured log line at run start: `{ "synthetic_reference_budget": true, "hash": "0x...", "fixture": "fixtures/calibration/synthetic_reference_budget.json" }`.
   - If the user explicitly passes a real budget path, the synthetic is ignored (real wins).

4. **Marked nature.**
   - The CI drift gate (T2.5) MUST treat synthetic-marked hashes as a separate cohort — drift in synthetic is a fixture update (low ceremony), drift in real is a release-note-required promotion.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-train                                            # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- synthetic::round_trip                    # JSON round-trips
cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight::synthetic_fallback             # Trainer uses synthetic when no real budget present
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings                              # No warnings
# Verify: synthetic budget passes the same preflight as a real one (schema parity)
# Verify: `runtime_nucleus_hash` for synthetic is detectably synthetic (a structured check, not a string match)
# Verify: trainer log line clearly indicates synthetic-mode at run start
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Same `RuntimeChromeBudget` type for synthetic and real eliminates "two paths" drift.
- §IV.3: Reproducible — checked-in JSON fixture means every run sees the same synthetic budget.
- §V.1: Structured log indicates synthetic-mode (machine-readable for CI).
- §VI.1: Fixture is in-repo at `fixtures/calibration/`.

title: F-A1: gbf-asm — Typed LR35902 eDSL + Encoder + Cycle Model + Layout
description: Fill the ten module stubs in gbf-asm (isa, section, provenance, symbols, builder, cycle_model, layout, relax, encoder, listing) plus a small ROM builder. AsmIR is the only authoring layer for executable code per planv0.md engineering rule 1. ReachabilityValidation later relies on MachineEffect + PrivilegeClass typing introduced here.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-A1: `gbf-asm` — Typed LR35902 eDSL + Encoder + Cycle Model + Layout

### Background

`gbf-asm` is the Rust eDSL that authors **all** generated executable code in the system. Per the constitution-grade rule from `planv0.md` line 2492 ("All generated executable code originates from `AsmIR` / `Instr` / audited runtime builders, never from ad hoc byte pushes"), there is no other path to bytes-on-cartridge. `Raw(Vec<u8>)` exists only as a tightly audited escape hatch.

This feature fills the ten module stubs in `gbf-asm/src/`: `isa`, `section`, `provenance`, `symbols`, `builder`, `cycle_model`, `layout`, `relax`, `encoder`, `listing`, plus a small ROM-builder wrapper.

### How this serves overarching project goals

- Compiler-generated code (Stage 12 backend in Epic B) emits `AsmIR` and **must not** make raw MBC writes — the legal path is `BankLease` pseudo-ops that go through F-A4.
- The runtime nucleus (F-A5) is itself authored as Rust builders that emit `AsmIR` sections (`planv0.md` line 305: "this is the key interpretation of 'end-to-end Rust' on Game Boy").
- `ReachabilityValidation` (Epic B, deferred) computes residency / privilege classes by walking `AsmIR`'s `MachineEffect` and `PrivilegeClass` annotations. Without those types here, the validation pass can't be written later.
- `gbf-bench` (Epic E) reads cycle annotations from `AsmIR` to ground its calibration.

### The problem this solves

Without `gbf-asm`:
1. Anyone emitting bytes invents their own ad hoc encoder, which silently disagrees with anyone else's encoder.
2. There is no single place where "is this MBC write legal here?" can be asked.
3. Cycle models live as comments in random files.
4. Provenance — which compiler stage emitted which byte — is lost the moment bytes leave the compiler.

### Design

Module-level layout (matching the existing scaffold):

```
gbf-asm/src/
  isa.rs          # legal LR35902 instruction shapes, registers, addressing modes
  section.rs      # SectionRole, SectionId, MachineEffect, PrivilegeClass
  provenance.rs   # InstrProvenance — which compile stage emitted what
  symbols.rs      # symbol table, label resolution, .sym output
  builder.rs      # typed Builder eDSL emitting Instr + Db/Dw + pseudo-ops
  cycle_model.rs  # per-instruction M-cycle costs (taken/not-taken branches)
  layout.rs       # section placement, address assignment
  relax.rs        # branch relaxation (rel-jump <-> jp <-> far-call thunk)
  encoder.rs      # the only thing that turns Instr into bytes
  listing.rs      # human-readable .lst emitter
```

Plus a small `rom.rs` (or a top-level helper) that takes encoded sections + cartridge header and produces a `.gb` blob.

### Pseudo-ops the eDSL must support (planv0.md line 1511)

- `BankLease { bank, mode }` → consumed by F-A4.
- `BankRelease { lease }`
- `FarCall { target, lease_chain }`
- `Yield { kind: YieldKind }` — consumed by Epic B's slice scheduler.
- `TraceProbe { id, level }` — consumed by Epic D's trace pipeline.
- `AssertBank { expected }` — debugging assertion.
- `Db(u8)`, `Dw(u16)` — data directives. `Raw(Vec<u8>)` exists but is a code smell.

### Children (T-A1.1 ... T-A1.9)

1. **T-A1.1** ISA types + register/operand modeling.
2. **T-A1.2** SectionRole + SectionId + symbol table + provenance.
3. **T-A1.3** Builder eDSL (instructions + pseudo-ops + Db/Dw).
4. **T-A1.4** `MachineEffect` + `PrivilegeClass` typing on instructions/sections.
5. **T-A1.5** Cycle model.
6. **T-A1.6** Layout + branch relaxation.
7. **T-A1.7** Encoder.
8. **T-A1.8** Listing (.lst) emitter.
9. **T-A1.9** ROM builder (.gb assembler with cartridge header).

### Confidence: HIGH

Pure types and a deterministic encoder. The hardest part is getting `MachineEffect` / `PrivilegeClass` right because they are consumed by ReachabilityValidation later — the typing must be expressive enough that "this MBC write is privileged" / "this load is bank-dependent" can be asked locally.

## Agent Instructions (Feature)

**This is a feature bead.** Implement via child tasks T-A1.1–T-A1.9. Tasks should land roughly in the dep order; T-A1.7 (encoder) is the integration point.

### Feature Definition of Done

```bash
cargo test -p gbf-asm                                       # All module tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- encoder::round_trip                 # Encode then decode (where decoders exist) round-trips
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- cycle_model::known_instructions     # Cycle costs match Pan Docs for a curated set
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- layout::branch_relaxation           # Out-of-range rel-jump becomes jp; cross-bank becomes far-call thunk
cargo test -p gbf-asm -- listing::stable                     # Listings are byte-stable across runs (no nondeterminism)
cargo run -p gbf-asm --example tiny_rom                      # Produces a 32 KiB .gb that boots in an emulator
```

title: F1: Reframe Burn as Training Front-End, gbf-model Owns Deployed Numerics
description: Burn provides backend portability, autodiff, optimizers, checkpoints, and metrics. gbf-model owns ternary projection, activation fake-quant, norm approximation, and export visitation via qat::{ternary, activation, norm, export} modules. Burn-native PTQ remains as baseline/ablation. Pin exact Burn version.
acceptance_criteria: All T1.x tasks closed; cargo check -p gbf-model -p gbf-train exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat passes; gbf-model does NOT depend on burn
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

**Artifact contract:** F1 establishes that `gbf-model` owns deployable QAT numerics and artifact-facing export contracts. The implemented artifact boundary is `ExportVisitor` producing `ArtifactCore`, `QuantSpec`, `ExportFacts`, artifact metadata, and canonical tensors where applicable for ternary weights/scales, dense weights/biases, embeddings, classifiers, router/shared-dense branches, and norm LUTs. Activations export as range/quant facts, not tensors. Sequence-state semantics are owned by F12; F1 only ensures the QAT/export boundary composes with model-owned artifact types. Target compiler/runtime lowering and ArtifactOracle agreement are not claimed by F1; those remain downstream validation.

**differentiable Burn path:** Burn is contained in `gbf-train` behind the adapter boundary. Implemented Burn-backed paths cover finite-input ternary linear, activation fake-quant for fixed ranges, norm approximation, router, and expert block adapters with finite owned adapter state. The supported adapters use Burn tensors/autodiff for training while exporting back through model-owned scalar cores and `ExportVisitor`. Global non-finite error-boundary parity with scalar code is not claimed by F1 except where focused tests exist. Learned/EMA activation range state is intentionally not accepted on the Burn path yet; constructors reject it and `bd-1zv` owns that future behavior.

**Tests proving it:**

- `cargo check -p gbf-model -p gbf-train`
- `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat`
- `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -p gbf-train -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`
- `./scripts/check_burn_pin.sh`
- `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`
- `if grep -q '^burn' gbf-model/Cargo.toml; then exit 1; fi`

**Acceptance movement:** Yes. The original F1 T1.1-T1.8 scope is complete. The later follow-up `bd-1zv` remains open for Burn-backed `ActivationRangeMode::Learned` and `ActivationRangeMode::Ema` state. Current Burn activation adapters reject those dynamic modes with a focused test, so F1 closes with no future dynamic range behavior accepted.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| QAT module namespace and model-owned scalar numerics | supported | not applicable | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat`; export support is via model-owned contracts |
| Finite-input `TernaryLinearQat` scalar forward/export | supported | supported | supported | `qat_ternary_forward_and_export_share_projection`; `burn_ternary_forward_export_and_gradients_share_owned_tensors` |
| Finite-input `ActFakeQuant` fixed activation ranges | supported | supported | supported | `qat_activation_forward_clamps_and_quantizes_in_range`; `burn_activation_forward_matches_core_scalar_projection`; export emits activation quant/range facts |
| `ActivationRangeMode::Learned` scalar/export support and Burn rejection | supported | rejected | supported | `burn_activation_rejects_dynamic_range_modes_until_state_is_owned`; export support is scalar range only; `bd-1zv` owns Burn state |
| `ActivationRangeMode::Ema` scalar/export support and Burn rejection | supported | rejected | supported | `burn_activation_rejects_dynamic_range_modes_until_state_is_owned`; export support is scalar range only; `bd-1zv` owns Burn state |
| Finite-input `NormApproxQat` scalar/Burn/export behavior | supported | supported | supported | `qat_norm_affine_clip_lut_forward_and_export_match`; `burn_norm_export_matches_artifact_contract_from_owned_tensors`; export is metadata or LUT tensor where applicable |
| Finite-input `Top1RouterQat` scalar/Burn behavior | supported | supported | supported | `qat_router_hard_top1_returns_single_expert_and_aux_losses`; `burn_router_forward_matches_scalar_router_outputs`; `qat_export_visitor_extracts_router_shared_dense_and_norm_lut_tensors` |
| Finite-input `ExpertBlockQat` scalar/Burn behavior | supported | supported | supported | `qat_expert_composes_up_activation_down_and_residual`; `burn_expert_forward_matches_scalar_expert_block`; `qat_export_visitor_records_all_qat_module_kinds_and_activation_ranges` |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| F1 T1.1-T1.8 implementation children are closed | `br show bd-2q4 bd-3p5 bd-pk3 bd-2fi bd-ubm bd-g90 bd-1mv bd-1ptv bd-2fy` checked manually | none | `bd-1zv` is a later P3 dynamic-range follow-up, not accepted as part of T1.1-T1.8 |
| `gbf-model` owns QAT scalar numerics and export-facing contracts | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat` | none | Covers ternary, activation, norm, router, expert, and export visitor paths |
| Burn training logic is behind `gbf-train` adapters and remains out of `gbf-model` | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`; `if grep -q '^burn' gbf-model/Cargo.toml; then exit 1; fi` | none | Architecture test also checks direct source imports |
| Implemented Burn adapters execute and differentiate where expected | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat` | `burn-adapter` | Covers finite-path ternary, activation, norm, router, and expert adapters; global non-finite parity is not claimed |
| Burn adapter code is warning-clean under the feature gate | `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- -D warnings` | `burn-adapter` | Plain clippy without the feature does not lint adapter implementation |
| Fixed activation range Burn adapter matches scalar projection | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::activation::tests::burn_activation_forward_matches_core_scalar_projection` | `burn-adapter` | Also covered by full QAT train adapter gate |
| Learned/EMA activation range Burn state is not silently accepted | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat::activation::tests::burn_activation_rejects_dynamic_range_modes_until_state_is_owned` | `burn-adapter` | Future implementation remains with `bd-1zv` |

| QAT closure and architecture sensors remain clean | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Run before closure |

## No-future

F1 accepts no future QAT/Burn variants without an executable path and tests. Learned and EMA activation ranges exist in the scalar model contract and artifact range export, but Burn-owned learned params/live EMA state are rejected today and moved to `bd-1zv`. Target-specific lowering, ArtifactOracle agreement, production packed bytes, and train-to-export pipeline proof are outside F1 and remain owned by downstream beads.

## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| F1 has a later open child (`bd-1zv`) despite T1.1-T1.8 being complete | Follow-up dynamic range work was added after the original feature scope | Close F1 only with an explicit support matrix row that rejects/moves dynamic Burn range state | `burn_activation_rejects_dynamic_range_modes_until_state_is_owned` | `bd-1zv`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- qat` |
