| Risk of closing architecture feature without proving Burn containment | Feature definition is architectural, not just test-count based | Include both runtime tests and static architecture gates for direct Burn dependency containment | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`; direct Cargo grep | F1 closure gate |
| Future feature closures can miss lower-priority open follow-ups | `br ready` prioritization shows feature beads before P3 follow-ups | Before closing a feature, list child statuses and classify open children as blocking or moved | Manual child status check; no cheap general sensor yet | Beads workflow |
| Closure wording overclaimed activation tensors and sequence-state ownership | Artifact terminology from downstream export work leaked into the F1 ownership claim | Say "artifact metadata plus canonical tensors where applicable" and keep F12 sequence-state semantics out of F1 closure | Review packet; QAT closure checklist | `qat-bead-closure` |
| Burn/scalar parity was broader than the tests prove | Finite-path Burn tests do not prove every scalar non-finite error boundary | Scope Burn adapter claims to finite inputs and finite adapter state unless rejection guards exist | Future non-finite Burn adapter tests if parity is required | Burn adapter hardening; `bd-1zv` for dynamic range state |

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F1: Reframe Burn as Training Front-End

### Background

The original plan said "Burn-fronted with custom fake-quant / STE layers because built-in QAT is currently not supported." This is true but incomplete — it doesn't say WHERE those custom modules live or WHO owns the numeric contracts that cross the training/artifact boundary.

Burn's docs position it as a Rust training framework with swappable backends. Its value is: autodiff, optimizer implementations (Adam, AdaFactor, etc.), checkpoint serialization, metric logging, and backend portability (wgpu, tch, ndarray, candle). What Burn does NOT currently offer is QAT, ternary/binary projection, or hardware-aware fake quantization.

### The problem this solves

Without this reframing, the first implementer faces an ownership ambiguity: does `gbf-model` contain Burn `Module` types that happen to have quantization, or does `gbf-train` contain quantization logic that wraps Burn modules? The answer matters because:

1. The export boundary (what goes into ArtifactCore) must be defined by whoever owns the quantization modules
2. The fake-quant forward pass (used during training) must match the real quantization (used during export) — if they live in different crates, drift is inevitable
3. The ExportVisitor pattern (walk the model, extract canonical tensors + scales + metadata) is tightly coupled to the quantization module structure

### Design decision

`gbf-model` owns a `qat` module namespace containing:
- `TernaryLinearQat` — wraps a Burn Linear, adds learnable/annealed thresholds and per-row scales, provides both a fake-quant forward (for training) and an export method (for artifact generation)
- `ActFakeQuant` — activation clipping/quantization at expert input/output boundaries
- `NormApproxQat` — deployable norm approximation (AffineClipLut or TileRmsThenAffineClip)
- `Top1RouterQat` — low-rank top-1 router with z-loss and export support
- `ExpertBlockQat` — composes the above for a complete MoE expert block
- `ExportVisitor` — walks a trained model, extracts all canonical tensors, scales, metadata, and emits ArtifactCore + TernaryWeightPlan

Burn stays as the training host: you derive `Module` on your model struct, Burn gives you `.forward()`, `.backward()`, `.step()`, `.save()`. But the numeric semantics of what gets exported are defined in gbf-model's qat modules.

### Why Burn-native PTQ is kept as ablation path

Even though QAT is the production path, Burn's native PTQ (post-training quantization, if/when available) should remain as a baseline for:
- Shared modules (embedding, router, classifier) that may stay at higher precision
- Ablation experiments comparing PTQ vs QAT quality
- Quick iteration when you want to test a model architecture change without running the full QAT schedule

### The adapter layer

`gbf-train` should contain a thin adapter layer that:
- Pins the exact Burn version (e.g., `burn = "=0.16.0"`)
- Wraps any Burn API that might change between versions
- Provides a stable internal interface that gbf-model's QAT modules program against
- Includes a CI check that warns on Burn version bumps

### Confidence level: VERY HIGH

This is the single best framing correction in the whole patch. The ownership ambiguity it resolves would otherwise cause real implementation paralysis.

title: T-A5.5: Keyboard input + on-screen layout
description: On-screen keyboard with cursor + chord input. KeyboardLayoutSpec is data-driven from LexicalSpec.charset (Epic G). SpecialKey covers Backspace/Submit/Shift/Cancel. Reads cached joypad state.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A5.5: Keyboard input + layout

**Target crate**: `gbf-runtime`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-runtime/src/keyboard.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 1626 + `KeyboardLayoutSpec` (line 703).

### Background

The on-screen keyboard is how users input prompts. It's a grid of glyphs with a cursor; D-pad moves the cursor, A presses, B backspaces, Start submits. The layout is data-driven from `LexicalSpec.charset` (Epic G) so character set changes don't touch keyboard code.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub fn build_keyboard_section() -> Section;

pub struct KeyboardLayoutSpec {
    pub rows: u8,
    pub columns: u8,
    pub characters: &'static [u8],                  // grid laid out row-major
    pub special_keys: &'static [SpecialKey],
}

pub enum SpecialKey {
    Backspace,
    Submit,
    Shift,                                          // toggles charset slice (e.g., letters <-> punct)
    Cancel,
}

pub struct KeyboardState {
    pub cursor_row: u8,
    pub cursor_col: u8,
    pub charset_slice: u8,
}

/// Emit AsmIR: read joypad cached state, advance cursor / commit character.
pub fn emit_keyboard_step<B: Builder>(b: &mut B);
```

Keyboard state lives in WRAM. Each step:
1. Read joypad (T-A5.3 cached state).
2. If just-pressed direction: move cursor (text re-render via T-A5.4).
3. If just-pressed A: emit current character into the prompt buffer.
4. If just-pressed B: backspace.
5. If just-pressed Start: signal `HarnessOp::SetSession` or whatever the prompt protocol is.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-runtime
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- keyboard::default_layout            # Sane DMG-sized layout exists
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- keyboard::cursor_movement            # D-pad moves cursor; bounds clamp
cargo test -p gbf-runtime -- keyboard::special_keys               # Backspace/Submit work
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: SpecialKey is enumerated.

title: F-G1: Corpus pipeline — gbf-data governance
description: CorpusManifest with source provenance, deterministic normalization, dedup policy, contamination check vs WorkloadManifests, license metadata, deterministic train/val/test splits. Data lineage as reproducibility peer of build lineage.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-G1: Corpus pipeline

### What this is

`gbf-data` corpus governance: `CorpusManifest` with source provenance, deterministic normalization, dedup policy, contamination check vs `WorkloadManifest`s, license/usage metadata, deterministic train/val/test splits.

### Why this matters

Engineering rule + planv0.md line 2552: data lineage is a reproducibility peer of build lineage. Without governance, training corpus drift masquerades as model or compiler regressions.

### Design sketch

```rust
pub struct CorpusManifest {
    pub id: CorpusId,
    pub sources: Vec<CorpusSource>,
    pub normalization: NormalizationPolicy,
    pub dedup_policy: DedupPolicy,
    pub split: TrainValTestSplit,
    pub canary_prompts: Vec<PromptCase>,
}

pub fn check_contamination(corpus: &CorpusManifest, workloads: &[WorkloadManifest])
    -> ContaminationReport;
```

### Cross-feature deps

- F-E1 WorkloadManifest (contamination target)
- F-G2 LexicalSpec (normalization)

### Confidence: HIGH

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Normalization rules — locked to Tier 2 charset

Decision date: 2026-04-27. Owner: bkase. Charset spec lives on bd-2ym0 (F-G2); this bead owns the deterministic pipeline that turns raw corpus into the 80-token stream.

### Deterministic normalization order

Apply in exactly this order — order is part of `NormalizationSpec`'s identity:

1. **Unicode NFC** normalize the input.
2. **Strip combining accents** via NFD-decompose-and-filter (`café` → `cafe`, `naïve` → `naive`).
3. **Preserve case as-is** — mixed case is load-bearing.
4. **Fold quotation/dash variants** to ASCII:
   - `“` `”` `«` `»` → `"`
   - `‘` `’` `‚` → `'`
   - `—` (em-dash) → `--`  (TranscriptSpec maps `--` back to em-dash on render)
   - `–` (en-dash) → `-`
   - `…` (ellipsis) → `...`
5. **Whitespace**: tab → single space; collapse runs of internal ASCII spaces to one; preserve `\n` exactly; trim trailing spaces on each line; CRLF → LF.
6. **Unmappable codepoint handling**: if any character outside the 79-printable-token set survives 1–5, replace with `<unk>`. If > 2 % of the post-normalization characters in a single training example are `<unk>`, drop the example.

### Drop policy

Drop reason recorded in CorpusManifest's per-source provenance:
- `unmappable_density` — too many `<unk>`s.
- `length_below_min` / `length_above_max` — bounds set by NormalizationSpec.
- `dedup_collision` — handled separately by DedupPolicy (still owned here).

### Sampling / splits

- Deterministic train/val/test split keyed by stable hash of the source-record id (NOT by a content hash, which would shift on normalization changes).
- `split_hashes: BTreeMap<SplitName, Hash256>` exported with the artifact for reproducibility.
- Canary prompts are an explicit list in CorpusManifest, NOT auto-sampled.

### Acceptance for closure

- A reference normalization run on a small fixture (~100 paragraphs covering accents, smart quotes, em-dashes, ellipses, tabs, CRLF, unmappables) with byte-exact expected output committed under fixtures/.
- Idempotence test: `normalize(normalize(x)) == normalize(x)` over the fixture.
- Determinism test: same input + same NormalizationSpec → byte-identical output across two runs.
- Drop-policy test: example with > 2 % unmappable density is dropped, recorded with reason.
- CorpusManifest serde + content-hash regression test.
- Contamination check vs WorkloadManifest is **not** part of v1 closure — moved to F-E1 (bd-1rao) integration. State this explicitly in closure if the cross-check is stubbed.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-G2 (bd-2ym0) — LexicalSpec / charset (this bead consumes it).
- F-E1 (bd-1rao) — WorkloadManifest (contamination check target). Stubbed in v1.
- F-G3 (bd-a4du) — Decode policy is independent.

### Genre intent

Hard-sci-fi corpus, Stross / *Accelerando* register. Pre-1928 public-domain hard-sci-fi (Asimov, Clarke, etc. once out of copyright) and license-clean modern equivalents are acceptable v1 sources. License metadata is mandatory in CorpusManifest's per-source provenance.

title: T12.2: Implement LinearState sequence block under SequenceSemanticsSpec
description: Implement a recurrent / linear-attention-like sequence block satisfying SequenceBlock. State fits in WRAM ping-pong, no growth with context. Includes QAT-compatible numerics (activation fake-quant at boundaries; norm via NormApproxQat). Config-gated; tiny-model fixture instantiates it.
close_reason:
Implemented `LinearStateBlock` as the backend-independent scalar sequence block under `gbf-model::sequence::linear_state`.

QAT closure questions:

- Artifact contract: `SequenceSemanticsSpec::LinearState(state_bytes_per_layer)` is carried into `SequenceState`, `SequenceExportFacts`, and `ArtifactCore`. `ExportVisitor::visit_linear_state_block` now walks the block and emits real `CanonicalTensor`s for the input norm LUT plus both ternary projections; sequence tensor handles are recorded from the emitted tensor IDs and tested against `ArtifactCore`. This does not claim compiler/runtime byte execution agreement.
- Differentiable Burn path: moved to `bd-2bm4`. `gbf-model` remains Burn-free; the new bead owns the LinearState Burn adapter, recurrence gradient smoke, and TrainPhase hardness propagation once `bd-ptk`/`bd-2uw` exist.
- Tests: see claim-to-gate matrix below.
- Moved acceptance: gradient flow through the recurrence and `TrainPhaseSpec`/`QuantHardness::Soft` tiny instantiation are moved to `bd-2bm4`/`bd-ptk`/`bd-2uw`; training scaffold and A/B fixture adoption remain owned by `bd-3jv`.

Support matrix:

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `LinearStateBlock` fixed-state recurrence | supported | moved to `bd-2bm4` | supported for `ArtifactCore` tensor materialization | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| Single shared `SequenceState` buffer | supported for `batch == 1` | moved to `bd-2bm4` | n/a | `linear_state_forward_rejects_multi_batch_until_state_is_batch_shaped` |
| `batch > 1` forward | rejected | moved to `bd-2bm4` | n/a | `linear_state_forward_rejects_multi_batch_until_state_is_batch_shaped` |
| Norm/activation/ternary QAT boundaries | supported with scalar QAT kernels | moved to `bd-2bm4`/`bd-2uw` | supported via visitor for norm/projection metadata | `linear_state_constructor_rejects_incompatible_norm_tile_width`; `qat_export_visitor_walks_linear_state_block_and_records_real_sequence_handles` |
| F4 Off/Soft/Hard `TrainPhaseSpec` hardness | not implemented in this bead | moved to `bd-ptk`, `bd-2uw`, `bd-2bm4` | n/a | owner beads exist and name the behavior |

Claim-to-gate matrix:

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| LinearState has fixed per-layer state and `bytes_per_token == 0` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state` | none | `linear_state_block_reports_fixed_layer_state_size` pins 64 bytes and zero token growth. |
| LinearState implements `SequenceBlock` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence_block::linear_state` | none | Trait-filter test exercises `SequenceBlock` dispatch. |
| Recurrence update has a literal scalar oracle | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state` | none | Two-token oracle pins output `[1.0, 0.0, 0.5, 1.0]` and final state `[0.5, 1.0]`. |
| Invalid contracts are rejected before silent bad execution | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state` | none | Covers projection shape, norm tile width, wrong `d_model`, wrong state spec, non-finite state, and batch rejection. |
| Failed forward does not advance sequence state | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state` | none | Overflowing output path leaves state bytes unchanged. |
| LinearState export emits real artifact tensors and sequence handles resolve to them | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` | none | Visitor test checks handle strings and membership in `ArtifactCore.tensors()`. |
| QAT/backend boundary remains clean | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Harness and architecture tests pass. |

Feedback-harness reflection:

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Batch rows shared one state buffer implicitly | `SequenceState` had one byte buffer while `SequenceActivation` allowed batches | Added AGENTS sequence-state rule: reject `batch > 1` unless state is batch-shaped | `linear_state_forward_rejects_multi_batch_until_state_is_batch_shaped` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state` |
| Export facts could fabricate handles | Runtime config carried artifact naming | Added AGENTS sequence-state rule: handles must come from an ExportVisitor path emitting matching tensors | `qat_export_visitor_walks_linear_state_block_and_records_real_sequence_handles` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::export` |
| Scalar recurrence proof was too weak | Tests checked mutation, not the update law | Added AGENTS sequence-state rule requiring a literal multi-token oracle | `linear_state_forward_matches_literal_two_token_recurrence_oracle` | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state` |
| Gradient/TrainPhase acceptance had no owner | Bead mixed scalar `gbf-model` work with future Burn/phase work | Added AGENTS sequence-state rule separating scalar kernels, Burn adapters, phase hardness, and A/B scaffold claims | New `bd-2bm4`; existing `bd-ptk`, `bd-2uw`, `bd-3jv` | owner beads |

Commands run:


comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/sequence/linear_state.rs` (new).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "LinearState", "fixed-state recurrent", "linear-attention". Also F12 (bd-144) and T12.1 (bd-ctl).

### Implementation Notes

Implement a `LinearStateBlock` that satisfies `SequenceBlock`:

- **State shape**: fixed-size per layer, no growth with context.
- **Update rule**: a recurrent / linear-attention-like update. Initial implementation can mirror a simple linear-attention or RWKV-lite variant — concrete choice can be revisited; the contract is that state size is bounded and per-token compute is O(state_size).
- **Numerics**: QAT-compatible. Use the QAT modules from F1 (TernaryLinearQat, ActFakeQuant, NormApproxQat) at the appropriate boundaries — expert input/output, residual boundaries, deployable norm. Hardness controlled by `TrainPhaseSpec`.
- **State location at deploy**: WRAM ping-pong (no SRAM paging required). State is checkpointed to SRAM at semantic checkpoint boundaries via the persistence record protocol; this is checkpoint behavior, not hot-path behavior.
- **Export facts**: populate `SequenceExportFacts` with measured `state_bytes_per_layer`, the canonical tensor handles for any per-block parameters, and the SequenceSemanticsSpec::LinearState variant.

### What "good enough for v1" looks like

- Tiny tests instantiate it with `state_bytes_per_layer = 64` and run a few forward passes.
- Gradient flows through the recurrence (smoke test).
- ExportVisitor (T1.6) can walk it and produce CanonicalTensors for its parameters.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                                   # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence::linear_state           # Forward + grad smoke tests
cargo test -p gbf-model -- sequence_block::linear_state     # Implements SequenceBlock trait
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings                    # No warnings
# Verify: state_size().bytes_per_token == 0 (state does not grow with tokens)
# Verify: state_size().bytes_per_layer matches the configured constant
# Verify: A tiny LinearStateBlock instantiates under a TrainPhaseSpec with QuantHardness::Soft
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: Implementing `SequenceBlock` means the type system enforces all required operations are present.
- §III.1: Bounded-state invariant is a type-level guarantee (bytes_per_token == 0).
- §VI.1: Block lives in `gbf-model::sequence::linear_state`, not scattered across training code.

title: F-F2: Certificates — range, arena, window, reachability, resource_state
description: Machine-checkable certificates per engineering rule 23. Each cert carries pass version, inputs hash, outputs hash, and structured proof. gbf-verify re-checks the structured claim independently.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## F-F2: Certificates

### What this is

Machine-checkable certificates per planv0.md correction: `certs/range.cert.json`, `certs/arena.cert.json`, `certs/window.cert.json`, `certs/reachability.cert.json`, `certs/resource_state.cert.json`. Each consumed by `gbf-verify` as proof.

### Why this matters

Engineering rule 23: "Critical safety passes emit machine-checkable certificates consumed by `gbf-verify`; human-readable reports are not the only source of truth for pass validity." Without certificates, "ReachabilityValidation passed" is just a log line.

### Design sketch

Each certificate carries the pass version, inputs hash, outputs hash, and a structured proof artifact. `gbf-verify` re-checks the structured claim independently.

### Cross-feature deps

- F-B5 RangePlan -> certs/range
- F-B8 RomWindowPlan -> certs/window
- F-B10 ArenaPlan -> certs/arena
- F-B11 ResourceStateValidation -> certs/resource_state
- F-B13 ReachabilityValidation -> certs/reachability
- F-F3 gbf-verify checker

### Confidence: HIGH

title: T1.5: Implement Top1RouterQat and ExpertBlockQat
description: Top1RouterQat: low-rank top-1 router with z-loss, expert dropout, temporal smoothness. ExpertBlockQat: composes TernaryLinearQat (up + down projections), ActFakeQuant, residual connection, optional shared dense branch. Both must support the phased QAT hardness control from F4.

close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

- Artifact contract: this bead implements the pre-export scalar/model contract in gbf-model. Top1RouterQat owns the higher-precision low-rank router core, top-1 expert selection, routing weights, explicit dropout/jitter inputs, previous-token temporal smoothing state, and typed RouterAuxLosses. ExpertBlockQat owns validated two-matrix expert composition, TernaryLinearQat up projection -> ActFakeQuant boundary -> TernaryLinearQat down projection, residual addition, and an optional alpha-gated shared dense branch. Final ArtifactCore export materialization is moved to bd-g90, and first-class tensor materialization for exported scale tensors is moved to bd-209.
- Where is the differentiable Burn path: existing differentiable Burn paths for TernaryLinearQat and ActFakeQuant remain in gbf-train QAT adapters. The concrete router/expert Burn adapter path is owned by bd-1ptv; this bead exposes backend-independent scalar cores and phase-control options.
- Tests proving it: cargo check -p gbf-model; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat; cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings; python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py; cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture.
- Acceptance moved: the bead text's Tensor-shaped forward signature is implemented here as the repository's current backend-independent slice API, matching the existing TernaryLinearQat and ActFakeQuant scalar cores. The richer temporal ring-window and sequence-boundary training policy is moved to bd-122. Export/artifact lowering is moved to bd-g90 and bd-209; router/expert Burn adapters are moved to bd-1ptv. This closure does not claim exact compiler/runtime lowering agreement.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Low-rank Top1RouterQat projection with nonzero default rank | supported | moved | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router`; bd-1ptv; bd-g90 |
| HardTop1 router selection | supported | moved | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router`; bd-1ptv; bd-g90 |
| SoftTop1 router distribution | supported | moved | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router`; bd-1ptv |
| Router dropout mask and logit jitter | supported | moved | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router`; bd-1oc |
| Router z-loss, token balance proxy, and temporal smoothness loss components | supported | moved | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router`; bd-1ptv; bd-122 |
| ExpertBlockQat hard ternary two-matrix path | supported | moved | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert`; bd-1ptv; bd-g90 |
| ExpertBlockQat full-precision phase path | supported | moved | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert`; bd-1ptv |
| Optional shared dense branch | supported | moved | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert`; bd-wsd; bd-g90 |
| Canonical artifact tensor export | moved | moved | moved | bd-g90; bd-209 |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Top1RouterQat validates model dimension, expert count, rank, projection shapes, finite weights, and nonzero default rank. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router` | none | Covered by router invalid-contract and default-rank tests. |
| HardTop1 returns one expert index with one-hot routing weights and typed aux losses. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router` | none | Covered by hard-top1 and weighted-aux tests. |
| SoftTop1, explicit dropped-expert masks, and logit jitter are available without embedding RNG policy in gbf-model. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router` | none | Stochastic policy integration is left at the training boundary. |
| Temporal smoothing state updates only at the forward boundary and can be reset at sequence boundaries. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router` | none | Full contiguous-window policy is moved to bd-122. |
| ExpertBlockQat validates expert dimensions and composes TernaryLinearQat -> ActFakeQuant -> TernaryLinearQat with residual addition. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert` | none | Covered by expert composition and activation-boundary tests. |
| ExpertBlockQat supports hard-quantized and full-precision phase paths. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert` | none | Scalar phase control only; Burn wrappers remain at the adapter boundary. |
| Optional shared dense branch is explicit, alpha-gated, and can initialize to expert-only behavior. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert` | none | Configuration/profile evolution remains with bd-wsd. |
| gbf-model remains free of direct Burn imports and test-only QAT backend seams. | `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Architecture sensor passed. |
| QAT closure shape and moved-acceptance ownership satisfy the harness. | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py` | none | Harness passed after bd-1ptv replaced the old generic Burn owner. |
| The gbf-model QAT implementation is formatted, compiles, and has no clippy warnings. | `cargo fmt --check --all`; `cargo check -p gbf-model`; `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings` | none | All passed. |

## No-future Variant Acceptance

- Accepted public router modes are only SoftTop1 and HardTop1, and both are covered by scalar tests.
- Accepted public expert phase modes are only FullPrecision and HardQuantized, and both are covered by scalar tests.

## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**:
- `gbf-model/src/qat/router.rs` — Top1RouterQat
- `gbf-model/src/qat/expert.rs` — ExpertBlockQat

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "Top1RouterQat", "ExpertBlockQat", "top-1 routing", "z-loss", "expert dropout", "temporal smoothness", "low-rank router".

### Implementation Notes

**Top1RouterQat**:
- Low-rank top-1 router: `d_model -> rank -> n_experts` (rank = min(n_experts/4, 8))
- Both projection matrices stay at higher precision (not ternary)
- Training features: z-loss, expert dropout with jitter, temporal smoothness regularization
- `forward(&self, x: Tensor) -> (expert_index: Tensor, routing_weights: Tensor, aux_losses: RouterAuxLosses)`
- Must track previous-token routing distribution for temporal smoothness (buffer)

**ExpertBlockQat**:
- Composes: TernaryLinearQat (up proj) -> ActFakeQuant -> TernaryLinearQat (down proj)
- Plus: residual connection, optional shared dense branch
- `forward(&self, x: Tensor, expert_id: usize) -> Tensor`

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                       # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::router          # Router tests pass
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert          # Expert tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings        # No warnings
# Verify: Top1RouterQat returns single expert index (top-1)
# Verify: ExpertBlockQat composes TernaryLinearQat + ActFakeQuant correctly
# Verify: aux losses (z-loss, balance) are computed and returned
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: RouterAuxLosses as a struct, not loose floats — typed loss components
- §I.2: Pure routing math; temporal buffer state update at boundary
- §III.1: Type-check that router output matches expert dispatch expectations

comments.text: PARALLELISM NOTE: This task bundles Top1RouterQat and ExpertBlockQat. These are in separate files (router.rs, expert.rs) and could be split into T1.5a and T1.5b for parallel execution. T7.5 (low-rank router) only needs Top1RouterQat, so splitting would let T7.5 start earlier. Consider splitting if agent throughput is a bottleneck on this path.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## QAT Closure Checklist

- Artifact contract: `StaticBudgetReport` is structured, serde-compatible model-side budget data for one expert shape and one already-selected byte limit. It is not an `ArtifactCore` contract and it does not claim compiler placement, `RuntimeChromeBudget`, or `DeployabilityEnvelope` integration.
- differentiable Burn path: not applicable. This bead is pure byte accounting over `TernaryWeightPlan`; no Burn tensor, optimizer, or gradient path is introduced.
- Tests proving it: see the Claim-To-Gate matrix below.
- Acceptance movement: real budget-source selection from `RuntimeChromeBudget` versus `DeployabilityEnvelope` is moved to `bd-t7x6`, with a handoff comment added there. Full compiler Stage 2 reporting, including multi-expert reports, total ROM usage, per-bank margins, slot identity, placement caps, WRAM/SRAM/HRAM projections, accumulator maxima, and bank-switch estimates, is moved to `bd-2ps`, with a handoff comment added there. Exact packed metadata bytes remain estimated here and final layout metadata belongs to `bd-2ps`.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Two-matrix expert byte estimate from `TernaryWeightPlan` | supported | not applicable | moved | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget` |
| Estimated expert metadata bytes | supported as estimated constants | not applicable | moved for exact compiler packing | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget`; `bd-2ps` |
| Single selected byte-limit fit or reject report | supported | not applicable | moved for real compiler/preflight source wiring | `cargo test -- static_budget`; `cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight` |
| Structured JSON budget report | supported | not applicable | moved for compiler build reports | `cargo test -- static_budget` |
| `RuntimeChromeBudget` and `DeployabilityEnvelope` source selection | moved out of `gbf-model` | moved | moved | `bd-t7x6`; `bd-yfv`; `bd-27n` |
| Multi-expert and per-bank `StaticBudgetReport` | moved | not applicable | moved | `bd-2ps` |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Expert byte math delegates to `TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost()` for up and down projections. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget_compute_expert_bytes_delegates_to_ternary_weight_plan` | none | Proves both projection calls use the canonical plan helper. |
| The default 128x224 two-matrix expert byte estimate includes ternary weights, scales, and explicitly estimated metadata. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget_known_default_expert_matches_bank_warning_scale` | none | Metadata is estimated, not a compiler packing claim. |
| `StaticBudgetReport` flags fit and over-budget cases for a selected byte limit. | `cargo test -- static_budget` | none | The limit is already selected by the caller; source selection is moved. |
| `StaticBudgetReport` is structured data that serializes and deserializes. | `cargo test -- static_budget` | none | Guards the JSON report shape for the current model-owned fields. |
| Invalid expert dimensions are rejected by checked report/preflight APIs. | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget_zero_dimensions_do_not_charge_metadata`; `cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight_rejects_zero_expert_dimensions` | none | Raw arithmetic still returns zero for zero dimensions, but checked APIs reject. |
| Training preflight still delegates to the model budget formula. | `cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight` | none | This is not real CLI or RuntimeChromeBudget wiring. |
| QAT closure and architecture metadata remain valid. | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Harness run required after closing because bead metadata is part of the gate. |

## No-future

No `RuntimeChromeBudget`, `RomBudgetSlot`, `BudgetSlotClass`, or `DeployabilityEnvelope` schema is accepted in `gbf-model`. The model layer only computes byte math and a fit/reject result for a byte limit supplied by another layer. Runtime-source preference, per-bank margins, total ROM usage, and exact component-header/alignment metadata are not silently accepted here; they are moved to `bd-t7x6` and `bd-2ps`.

## Review Feedback Applied

- Removed the premature model-local `RuntimeChromeBudget` and `DeployabilityEnvelope` source-selection surrogate.
- Kept the patch narrow: serde support for the existing model-side budget report plus a focused static-budget JSON test.
- Renamed the focused report test so `cargo test -- static_budget` runs real checks.
- Added handoff comments to `bd-t7x6` and `bd-2ps` for the moved source-selection and compiler-report responsibilities.
- Updated the QAT bead-closure guide to prevent future budget beads from creating shadow policy/runtime schemas in `gbf-model`.

## Feedback Harness Reflection

| Feedback item | Root cause | Guide | Sensor | Owner/gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| RuntimeChromeBudget and DeployabilityEnvelope source selection was modeled inside `gbf-model`. | I let a stale broad bead pull policy/runtime concepts across the model boundary. | Updated `qat-bead-closure` to say model computes byte math and policy/train/compiler own source selection and per-bank diagnostics. | Removed the surrogate API; `rg "ExpertBudgetLimit|RuntimeChromeExpertSlotBudget|select_expert_budget_limit" gbf-model/src/budget.rs` finds no matches. | `.agents/skills/qat-bead-closure/SKILL.md`; `bd-t7x6`; `bd-2ps` |
| The documented `cargo test -- static_budget` command originally exercised too little of the new behavior. | Test names did not match the bead acceptance filter. | Closure claim-to-gate rows must name commands that run relevant tests, not zero-test passes. | Renamed the report test to `static_budget_report_uses_canonical_compute_function`; `cargo test -- static_budget` now runs 2 `gbf-model` tests. | `gbf-model/src/budget.rs`; `cargo test -- static_budget` |
| The bead asked for full compiler report behavior that current inputs cannot prove. | `bd-w80` predates `bd-2ps` and overlaps the real compiler Stage 2 owner. | Move broad acceptance explicitly and enrich the owner bead before closing. | Added handoff comment to `bd-2ps`. | `br show bd-2ps` |
| Exact metadata bytes were not proven. | Current model constants are estimates and final packed layout metadata needs compiler context. | Name estimated metadata as estimated and cite the exact owner for final packing. | Constants remain `ESTIMATED_*`; closure marks exact metadata as moved. | `gbf-model/src/budget.rs`; `bd-2ps` |
| Review surface included unrelated dirty plan/oracle files. | Existing worktree contained unrelated changes before this bead. | Closure and commit should stage only bead-owned files. | `git status -sb`; staged file list before commit. | repo workflow |

Commands run so far:

- `cargo fmt --all`
- `cargo test -p gbf-model -- budget`
- `cargo test -- static_budget`
- `cargo test -p gbf-train -- preflight`
- `cargo clippy -p gbf-model -p gbf-train -- -D warnings`
- `git diff --check`

title: T6.5: Optional shared dense FFN branch (stability profile)
description: Each MoE block may optionally include a small shared dense FFN in common banks. The expert output is added to the shared output: final = shared_ffn(x) + router_weight * expert_ffn(x). The shared branch has d_ff_shared << d_ff_expert (e.g., d_ff_shared = d_model/2 = 64). This costs ~4-8 KiB of common-bank space per MoE block but provides a stable gradient path. Config-gated, off by default until quality experiments show it helps.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; Disabled by default (None config); When enabled output = expert_out + alpha*dense_out; Alpha initializes to 0
close_reason: Implemented optional shared dense FFN branch stability profile

comments.text:
## QAT Closure Checklist

Final `ArtifactCore`/`ArtifactOracle` deployable agreement and exact common-bank residency accounting are moved to `bd-12c`/F6 integration coverage, with `bd-2qj` covering model config/instantiation tests.
- differentiable Burn path: `gbf-train --features burn-adapter` owns the differentiable adapter. Shared dense up/down projections and alpha are Burn params; alpha gets gradient even at the default zero-alpha initialization.
- Tests proving it: `cargo test -p gbf-model -- shared_dense`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- expert`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test qat_tests shared_branch`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture`; `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`.
- Acceptance moved: exact common-bank byte/residency accounting and final ArtifactOracle agreement are not claimed here. Relative shared branch width is enforced at `MoeFfnConfig`; exact export/compiler accounting remains downstream.

## Support Matrix

| Public behavior or variant | Scalar/model core | Burn training path | Export/artifact path | Guard |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Optional shared dense branch config is absent by default and represented on `MoeFfnConfig`/`MoeFfnStage` | supported | not applicable | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- shared_dense`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` |
| `SharedDenseBranchConfig` requires nonzero, budget-sized dims and `d_ff_shared < d_ff` at MoE config boundary | supported | not applicable | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- shared_dense` |
| Scalar shared branch forward is `residual + selected_expert_delta + alpha * shared_dense_delta` | supported | supported | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- shared_dense`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- expert` |
| Zero-alpha initialization gives expert-only output while alpha remains trainable | supported | supported | not applicable | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- shared_dense`; `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- zero_alpha` |
| Pre-export visitor records shared dense up/down/activation/alpha | supported | moved | supported | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat_export_visitor_extracts_router_shared_dense_and_norm_lut_tensors`; Burn export handoff guarded by `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- expert` |
| Final artifact/common-bank byte residency agreement | not applicable | not applicable | moved | `bd-2qj` for F6 config/instantiation coverage; `bd-12c` for ExportVisitor-to-ArtifactOracle agreement |

## Claim-To-Gate

| Closure claim | Guarding test or command | Feature gate | Notes or deviation |

| Shared dense branch is config-gated and off by default at the model topology surface | `cargo test -p gbf-model -- shared_dense`; `cargo test -p gbf-model -- block` | none | Proves `MoeFfnConfig::new` has `None` and configured branch appears in `MoeFfnStage`. |

| Burn path is differentiable through selected expert and shared branch | `cargo test -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- expert`; `cargo clippy -p gbf-train --features burn-adapter -- -D warnings` | `burn-adapter` | Includes zero-alpha alpha-gradient proof and existing nonzero-alpha shared-branch gradient proof. |

| QAT closure metadata remains harness-compatible | `python3 scripts/qat_harness_checks.py`; `cargo test -p gbf-test --test architecture` | none | Harness and architecture guard pass before closure. |

## No-future

No-future: this bead accepts only the optional two-matrix shared dense branch paths implemented today: scalar/model core, Burn adapter, and pre-export visitor structure. It does not accept GLU execution, final ArtifactCore materialization, exact common-bank byte accounting, or compiler/runtime residency proof. Those paths are moved/rejected as listed above rather than silently implied by the public config.

title: T6.4: Default two-matrix expert MLP with clipped nonlinearity
description: Default expert: TernaryLinearQat(d_model -> d_ff) -> ClippedActivation -> TernaryLinearQat(d_ff -> d_model). ClippedActivation is ReLU, GELU-clip, or SiLU-clip with the clip bound declared in the TernaryWeightPlan's activation range. GLU variants (three-matrix) are behind an explicit config flag and a big warning: 'GLU experts at d_model=128 d_ff=224 will not fit in a single ExpertBank slot under Ternary2 PerOutputRow Q8_8.'
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-model exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert passes; Default expert has exactly 2 TernaryLinearQat layers; GLU requires explicit opt-in with bank-fit warning
close_reason:
## QAT Closure Checklist

- Artifact contract: supported expert activation semantics are part of export identity. `ExportVisitor` now records the expert clipped nonlinearity in `ActivationQuantEntry.nonlinearity`, and `ArtifactCore::semantic_hash()` includes that field. The activation clip range has one source of truth: the wrapped `ActFakeQuant::export_range()`.
- differentiable Burn path: not implemented in this bead. bd-2c8z tracks the `gbf-train` adapter for `ExpertBlockQat`, including scalar parity, clipped activation gradients, activation phase options, and current rejection of GLU execution.
- Tests proving it: see the Claim-To-Gate matrix below.
- Acceptance movement: the bead text said the clip bound came from `TernaryWeightPlan`; implementation uses activation quant range instead because `TernaryWeightPlan` only describes weight encoding. The GLU warning is a structured returned event with byte-cost math, not a runtime logger. Actual ExpertBank fit enforcement remains outside this bead; bd-3bb covers the budget formula work.

comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-model`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-model/src/qat/expert.rs` (update ExpertBlockQat implementation)

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "two-matrix expert MLP", "clipped nonlinearity", "GLU", "d_model -> d_ff -> d_model".

### Implementation Notes
- Default expert architecture: `TernaryLinearQat(d_model -> d_ff) -> ClippedActivation -> TernaryLinearQat(d_ff -> d_model)`
- **Two matrices only** (not three as in GLU/SwiGLU). This is critical for bank budget math.
- ClippedActivation: a hardware-friendly nonlinearity (e.g., clipped ReLU or hardswish) that stays within the accumulator's safe integer range.
- GLU variants are behind an **explicit config flag** with a bank-fit warning (GLU needs 3 matrices → 50% more bytes per expert).
- Expert byte cost = `TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost(d_model, d_ff) + TernaryWeightPlan::compute_byte_cost(d_ff, d_model)` for two-matrix.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-model                         # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-model -- qat::expert            # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-model -- -D warnings          # No warnings
# Verify: default expert has exactly 2 TernaryLinearQat layers
# Verify: GLU variant requires explicit opt-in and logs a bank-fit warning
# Verify: forward produces correct output shape [batch, d_model]
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: Expert variant (TwoMatrix vs GLU) is an enum, not a bool
- §V.3: GLU bank-fit warning is a structured log event, not a println

title: T-A2.7: Calibration schema (PlatformCalibrationBundle / KernelCalibrationBundle / RuntimeCalibrationBundle / CalibrationSetRef)
description: Layered calibration types per planv0.md line 221. Schema only — bundle production is Epic E. Includes CycleDistribution (mean/p50/p90/p99), CalibrationConfidence with sample_count + class (Strong/Reasonable/Weak), ValidityEnvelope tying validity to compiler version + runtime_nucleus_hash.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## T-A2.7: Calibration schema (types only)

**Target crate**: `gbf-hw`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-hw/src/calibration.rs`
**Reference**: `planv0.md` line 221.

### Background

Calibration is layered (`PlatformCalibrationBundle`, `KernelCalibrationBundle`, `RuntimeCalibrationBundle`) so platform facts (bank-switch cost, timer ISR cost) don't get invalidated when only kernel implementations change. This task defines the **types** for those bundles plus the `CalibrationSetRef` that `CompileRequest` uses to point at them.

The actual *production* of bundles (running gbf-bench, measuring real cycles) is Epic E. M0 needs the schema available so the rest of the system (Epic B compiler, F-A2 calibration consumers) can compile against typed inputs.

### Implementation Notes

```rust
pub struct CalibrationConfidence {
    pub class: CalibrationConfidenceClass,
    pub sample_count: u32,
    pub stddev: Option<f32>,
}

pub enum CalibrationConfidenceClass {
    Strong,                                  // >= 1000 samples, low stddev
    Reasonable,
    Weak,                                    // < 100 samples or high stddev
}

pub struct ValidityEnvelope {
    pub valid_until_compiler_version: SemVer,
    pub valid_until_runtime_nucleus_hash: Option<Hash256>,
}

pub struct CycleDistribution {
    pub mean: f32,
    pub p50: u32,
    pub p90: u32,
    pub p99: u32,
}

pub struct PlatformCalibrationBundle {
    pub id: PlatformCalibrationId,
    pub target: TargetProfileId,
    pub measurement_context: MeasurementContext,
    pub bank_switch_cost: CycleDistribution,
    pub sram_page_cost: CycleDistribution,
    pub timer_isr_cost: CycleDistribution,
    pub confidence: CalibrationConfidence,
    pub valid_for: ValidityEnvelope,
    pub cohort: CalibrationCohortId,
}

pub struct KernelCalibrationBundle {
    pub id: KernelCalibrationId,
    pub target: TargetProfileId,
    pub kernel_impl_hash: Hash256,
    pub runtime_nucleus_hash: Hash256,
    pub kernel_profiles: Vec<MeasuredKernelProfile>,
    pub confidence: CalibrationConfidence,
    pub valid_for: ValidityEnvelope,
}

pub struct RuntimeCalibrationBundle {
    pub id: RuntimeCalibrationId,
    pub target: TargetProfileId,
    pub runtime_nucleus_hash: Hash256,
    pub scheduler_overhead: CycleDistribution,
    pub overlay_install_cost: CycleDistribution,
    pub trace_event_cost: CycleDistribution,
    pub confidence: CalibrationConfidence,
    pub valid_for: ValidityEnvelope,
}

pub struct CalibrationSetRef {
    pub platform: PlatformCalibrationId,
    pub kernel: Option<KernelCalibrationId>,
    pub runtime: Option<RuntimeCalibrationId>,
}

pub struct MeasurementContext {
    pub emulator: Option<EmulatorAdapter>,
    pub hardware: Option<HardwareIdentifier>,
    pub measured_at: chrono::DateTime<chrono::Utc>,
    pub gbllm_version: SemVer,
}
```

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-hw
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- calibration::serde_round_trip
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- calibration::confidence_class_ordering    # Strong > Reasonable > Weak
cargo test -p gbf-hw -- calibration::set_ref_optional_layers      # Kernel/Runtime layers are independently optional
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `CalibrationConfidenceClass` is enumerated; `RiskPolicy::require_confidence_at_least` can match exhaustively.
- §VI.1: Calibration schema is single-sourced here.

title: Implement annealed ternary threshold schedule in Burn training
description: Own the runtime training schedule implied by ThresholdPlan::AnnealedGlobalThenPerOutputRow. The current artifact/model path records and exports the plan with explicit per-row thresholds, but Burn-side schedule evolution from global annealing to per-output-row refinement needs a concrete adapter/training implementation and tests before training behavior can claim scheduler support.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text: Referenced from bd-qhu: this bead owns Burn-side training scheduler behavior for ThresholdPlan::AnnealedGlobalThenPerOutputRow. It does not replace the artifact/model metadata guard qat_ternary_default_plan_matches_artifact_expert_default; that test remains the bd-qhu guard proving default metadata is applied before scheduler behavior exists.

title: T0.2: Set up pre-commit hook with cargo fmt, clippy, and test gates
description:
Set up a git pre-commit hook that runs automated verification before every commit. This is critical for linux-kernel-style concurrent development where multiple agents commit independently. The hook must be fast (<2 min per CONSTITUTION §II.1) and catch all regressions locally before they reach other contributors.

Hook stages (fail-fast order):
1. cargo fmt --check --all (formatting — instant)
2. cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings (lint — seconds)
3. cargo test --workspace (full test suite — must be <2 min)

Implementation: use a shell script at .git/hooks/pre-commit (or a Rust-based hook runner if faster). Include a setup script (scripts/install-hooks.sh) that symlinks or copies the hook. AGENTS.md should document that all agents must run this setup script before their first commit.

For large workspaces where full cargo test is slow, support an escape hatch: GBLLM_SKIP_SLOW_TESTS=1 runs only cargo check + clippy + fast unit tests.

Per CONSTITUTION §I.4 (Verifiable Rewards): every commit must pass automated verification. Per §II.1 (Latency is Tech Debt): the hook must complete within 2 minutes.
acceptance_criteria: .git/hooks/pre-commit exists and is executable; scripts/install-hooks.sh exists and works; Hook runs cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy, cargo test in fail-fast order; Hook exits 1 on formatting violations, clippy warnings, or test failures; Hook completes in <2 minutes; GBLLM_SKIP_SLOW_TESTS=1 skips slow tests
close_reason: Pre-commit hook, installer, and AGENTS setup documentation are in place.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

- `scripts/install-hooks.sh` (installer)
- `AGENTS.md` (document hook requirement)

**Reference**: CONSTITUTION.md §I.4 (Verifiable Rewards), §II.1 (Latency is Tech Debt).

### Implementation Notes
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Pre-commit: formatting ==="
cargo fmt --check --all

echo "=== Pre-commit: lint ==="
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings

echo "=== Pre-commit: tests ==="
if [ "${GBLLM_SKIP_SLOW_TESTS:-0}" = "1" ]; then
    cargo test --workspace --lib  # unit tests only
else
    cargo test --workspace        # full suite
fi
```

- Install script should be idempotent (safe to run multiple times).
- Add to AGENTS.md: "Before first commit, run: ./scripts/install-hooks.sh"
- Consider `cargo nextest` for parallel test execution if available.
- For agent workflows: the hook ensures every commit is self-certifying.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
./scripts/install-hooks.sh                     # Installs hook
test -x .git/hooks/pre-commit                  # Hook is executable
# Create a deliberate formatting violation:
echo "fn   bad(){}" > /tmp/test.rs && ! cargo fmt --check  # Would fail
# Verify hook rejects bad commits (functional test)
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.4: The hook IS the verifiable reward — every commit passes or is rejected
- §II.1: <2 minutes total — if tests grow past this, split into fast/slow
- §V.3: Silence on success — only output on failure

title: T2.1: Define RuntimeChromeBudget types in gbf-policy
description: Define RuntimeChromeBudget, RomBudgetSlot, BudgetSlotClass in gbf-policy. These are schema types only — no logic. BudgetSlotClass has three variants: Bank0Free (remaining space in Bank0 after runtime nucleus), CommonBank (typical common-bank slot), ExpertBank (typical expert-bank slot). Each RomBudgetSlot carries usable_bytes, reserved_slack, and supported PlacementProfile set.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check -p gbf-policy exits 0; cargo test -p gbf-policy -- budget passes; RuntimeChromeBudget JSON round-trip; BudgetSlotClass has Bank0Free, CommonBank, ExpertBank

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-policy`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/budget.rs`

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search for "RuntimeChromeBudget", "RomBudgetSlot", "BudgetSlotClass", "chrome".

### Implementation Notes
Define these types:
```rust
pub struct RuntimeChromeBudget {
    pub target: TargetProfileId,
    pub profile: CompileProfileId,
    pub runtime_nucleus_hash: Hash256,
    pub rom_slots: Vec<RomBudgetSlot>,
    pub wram_reserved: u16,
    pub sram_reserved: u32,
}

pub struct RomBudgetSlot {
    pub id: BudgetSlotId,
    pub class: BudgetSlotClass,
    pub usable_bytes: u32,
    pub reserved_slack: u16,
    pub placement_caps: BTreeSet<PlacementProfile>,
}

pub enum BudgetSlotClass {
    Bank0Free,
    CommonBank,
    ExpertBank,
}
```
- Schema types only — no behavior beyond construction and serialization.
- "Chrome" = the runtime/UI shell that occupies ROM space before model data is placed.
- Derive: `Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize`
- Serializable to/from `chrome_budget.json`.

### Acceptance Criteria
```bash
cargo check -p gbf-policy                        # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- budget                # Tests pass
cargo clippy -p gbf-policy -- -D warnings         # No warnings
# Verify: RuntimeChromeBudget round-trips through JSON serialization
# Verify: BudgetSlotClass has all three variants
```

### Constitution Checkpoints
- §I.1: BudgetSlotClass enum makes bank classification exhaustive
- §VI.1: JSON schema is the single source of truth for budget format

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Refinement from 2026-04-26 model/runtime interface session

The original type sketch is correct in spirit but two fields need restructuring before this task is implemented. Adopt these refinements as part of T2.1; do not defer them.

### 1. `wram_reserved` becomes structured `WramReserved`

WRAM is split into a **fixed-size overlay region** (per-`CompileProfile` constant, used to host expert hot kernels copied from expert banks) and a **computed remainder** that `ArenaPlan` allocates for hot arena (activations, accum, route, decode, continuation, stack). Preflight needs both numbers, not their sum.

Replace `pub wram_reserved: u16` with:

```rust
pub struct WramReserved {
    /// Fixed overlay region size (per CompileProfile.wram_layout.overlay_bytes).
    pub overlay: u16,
    /// Floor on hot-arena bytes available to ArenaPlan after overlay + runtime use.
    /// Preflight rejects models whose ArenaPlan demand exceeds this floor.
    pub hot_arena_floor: u16,
    /// Total WRAM bytes consumed/reserved by the runtime (overlay + runtime-internal).
    /// `8192 - total` is the absolute upper bound on additional arena.
    pub total: u16,
}
```

The `WramLayoutPolicy` and `OverlayReloadPolicy` types referenced in the overlay design live on `CompileProfile` and are owned by T11.1 (F11). T2.1 imports them.

### 2. `RuntimeChromeBudget` gains `reference_shell_modules`

To detect drift between the shell that emitted the budget and the shell actually shipping, the budget records which runtime modules were present at emission time. Add:

```rust
pub struct RuntimeChromeBudget {
    pub target: TargetProfileId,
    pub profile: CompileProfileId,
    pub runtime_nucleus_hash: Hash256,
    pub reference_shell_modules: BTreeSet<RuntimeShellModule>,
    pub rom_slots: Vec<RomBudgetSlot>,
    pub wram_reserved: WramReserved,
    pub sram_reserved: u32,
}

pub enum RuntimeShellModule {
    Boot,
    Interrupts,
    Scheduler,
    Banking,
    Joypad,
    Text,
    Keyboard,
    VideoCommit,
    Panic,
    Trace,
    Harness,
    Persistence,
}
```

The pinned reference shell for pre-training (per F2 architectural decisions) populates this set with `{Boot, Interrupts, Scheduler, Banking, Joypad, Text, Keyboard, VideoCommit}`. Production shells add modules; the set is what T2.5 / T2.7 diff against.

### 3. `reserved_slack` semantics: named, not opaque

`RomBudgetSlot.reserved_slack` is not a magic margin — it covers (a) bank header / stub overhead, (b) named reservations for known-future modules. T2.4 owns the spec (`ReferenceShellSpec`) listing each contributor. T2.1 keeps the field as `u16` but documents the decomposition convention in the doc comment so emitters do not collapse it into a fudge factor.

### Acceptance updates

Add to existing acceptance:
- `WramReserved` round-trips through JSON.
- `RuntimeShellModule` enum is exhaustive over the modules listed in `gbf-runtime/src/`.
- `reference_shell_modules` participates in `runtime_nucleus_hash` pre-image (so adding a module changes the hash even if byte sizes happen to match).

title: T11.2: Define CompileProfile registry and BringUp profile defaults in gbf-policy
description: Add CompileProfile struct with id, wram_layout: WramLayoutPolicy, overlay_reload: OverlayReloadPolicy, max_bank_switches_per_token: u16, sequence_state: SequenceSemanticsRef, plus a static registry. Encode the BringUp profile defaults (concrete WRAM bytes, PerExpertSwitch reload, hand-picked switch cap). Document switch-cap provenance and the path to calibration-derived ceilings once gbf-bench is online.

comments.author: bkase
comments.text:
## Agent Instructions

**Target crate**: `gbf-policy`
**Target file(s)**: `gbf-policy/src/profile.rs` (new), `gbf-policy/src/profile/bringup.rs` (new — registry entry).

**Reference**: `history/planv0.md` — search "CompileProfile", "BringUp", "max_bank_switches_per_token". Also the 2026-04-26 session decisions (bd-1hv, bd-1rb).

### Implementation Notes

Define the `CompileProfile` type and a static registry:

```rust
pub struct CompileProfile {
    pub id: CompileProfileId,
    pub display_name: &'static str,
    pub wram_layout: WramLayoutPolicy,
    pub overlay_reload: OverlayReloadPolicy,
    pub max_bank_switches_per_token: u16,
    pub sequence_state: SequenceSemanticsRef,
    pub provenance: SwitchCapProvenance,
}

pub enum SwitchCapProvenance {
    /// Hand-picked starting value documented in the profile body. Replace once
    /// gbf-bench produces a PlatformCalibrationBundle with bank_switch_cost.
    HandPicked { rationale: &'static str },
    /// Derived from PlatformCalibrationBundle.bank_switch_cost and a target
    /// tokens/sec. Carries the calibration id used.
    CalibrationDerived { calibration: PlatformCalibrationId, target_tps: f32 },
}

pub fn registry() -> &'static [CompileProfile];
```

### Encode the `BringUp` profile

Initial concrete values (subject to refinement once shadow_compile data exists):

```rust
CompileProfile {
    id: CompileProfileId::BringUp,
    display_name: "BringUp",
    wram_layout: WramLayoutPolicy {
        overlay_bytes: 512,
        continuation_bytes: 256,
        stack_bytes: 256,
        hot_arena_bytes_min: 4096,
    },
    overlay_reload: OverlayReloadPolicy::PerExpertSwitch,
    // Hand-picked. Allows ~1 inter-expert switch per MoE layer per token at
    // n_moe_layers <= 4 for tiny bring-up models. Refine via gbf-bench.
    max_bank_switches_per_token: 8,
    sequence_state: SequenceSemanticsRef::Unspecified,
    provenance: SwitchCapProvenance::HandPicked {
        rationale: "Bring-up only; tiny model, low quality bar. Refine when \
                    PlatformCalibrationBundle.bank_switch_cost is measured \
                    and a tokens/sec target exists.",
    },
}
```

`SequenceSemanticsRef::Unspecified` is the placeholder until F12 (sequence-state experimentation) defines it. BringUp does not yet pin a sequence-state choice; that lands as production profiles after F12.

### Production profiles are explicitly deferred

Document in module doc comment that no `Production-LinearState` or `Production-BoundedKv` profile exists yet by design. They are added after F12 / T12.4 reports a comparison. Any agent that wants to add one must first reference data from the SequenceStateComparisonReport.

### Acceptance Criteria

```bash
cargo check -p gbf-policy                                # Compiles
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- profile::registry             # Registry has exactly BringUp
cargo test -p gbf-policy -- profile::bringup_invariants   # WRAM total <= 8192, switch_cap > 0
cargo clippy -p gbf-policy -- -D warnings                 # No warnings
# Verify: CompileProfileId is exhaustive (only BringUp for now)
# Verify: SwitchCapProvenance variant for BringUp is HandPicked with non-empty rationale
```

### Constitution Checkpoints

- §I.1: `SwitchCapProvenance` makes the source of every cap value explicit; no naked numbers.
- §VI.1: BringUp lives in code, not in a config file or comment.
- §I.4: A profile with provenance is a verifiable reward — CI can refuse a HandPicked cap older than N days.

title: Add measured logging-overhead benchmark gate for training producers
description: Follow-up from bd-3mrj review. Define a representative tiny training/preflight workload, baseline-vs-instrumented measurement policy, warmup/sample-count policy, and CI or bench command. Feed measured values into LoggingOverheadGate::constitution_one_percent().require_pass before making any CONSTITUTION §II.1 <1% logging-overhead claim. Record uncertainty/noise handling in the evidence packet. Non-goal: bd-3mrj only adds the explicit measured-value gate type and does not claim measured overhead.
